blurb: Sloan Peak is a 7,835-ft summit in the North Cascades, the highest point in the Henry M. Jackson Wilderness (Mount Baker-Snoqualmie National Forest), rising between the North and South Forks of the Sauk River about 40 miles east of Everett and 12 miles southwest of Glacier Peak. Its sheer, knobby diorite/granitic upper faces and isolated, sharp profile have earned it the nickname "The Matterhorn of the Cascades." It was first climbed July 30, 1921 by Harry Bedal and Nels Skaar via what is now the Corkscrew Route.
hierarchyNote: The supplied parentArea "Glacier Peak Wilderness" appears to be incorrect. Multiple sources (PeakVisor's Henry M. Jackson Wilderness page, USFS) identify Sloan Peak as the highest peak within the Henry M. Jackson Wilderness, not the Glacier Peak Wilderness — the two wildernesses are adjacent (Glacier Peak Wilderness borders Henry M. Jackson Wilderness to the north/east), and Sloan Peak sits on the Henry M. Jackson side of that boundary. Name, coordinates (48.04145, -121.340261 ≈ Wikipedia's 48°02'29"N 121°20'25"W), and elevation (7,835 ft) all check out and unambiguously identify the correct, real peak.

routes.overview: The mountain's easiest and most popular line: from a high camp on the north side, climbers cross a small glacier, then 'corkscrew' around the south ridge and up onto the Upper West Face to reach the true summit via short, improbable Class 3 terrain.
routes.beta: Rated 3.5 stars on Mountain Project with the most recorded ascents of any route on the peak. From the north-side basin (5,000-5,500 ft) climbers ascend snow (early season) or the ridge to climber's left (late season) to a saddle around 5,800 ft, then traverse under the East Face on the glacier before curling around the south buttress/ridge and finishing up the Upper West Face on 3rd-class rock. This is a glacier + rock scramble, not a pitched rock climb.
routes.approach: Two trailhead options. Bedal Creek (more popular): drive the Mountain Loop Highway to 18.25 mi south of Darrington (~1 mi south of Bedal Campground) or 7 mi north of Barlow Pass, turn onto Forest Road 4096, and drive 3 mi to the trailhead (2,800 ft) — high-clearance/4WD recommended for a short steep section between the 5th and 6th switchbacks; parking fits fewer than 10 cars. Hike the Bedal Creek Trail (No. 705) about 1.2 miles to 3,560 ft, then travel off-trail through trees/brush to a 5,300 ft saddle and on to the 5,000-5,500 ft staging basin. Cougar Creek (alternate): from 17 mi south of Darrington, turn onto North Fork Sauk River Road, drive 4.5 mi to a pullout past Garnet Creek (1,900 ft); this approach requires fording the North Fork Sauk River, which can be difficult to impossible at high water, via a brushy trail with log crossings to an old logging-road switchback system reaching the same staging area.
routes.descent: Descend the same Corkscrew line back to the north-side basin, or alternately via the SE Shelf.
routes.descentText: Retrace the ascent route: downclimb the Upper West Face scramble terrain, curl back around the south ridge/buttress, and re-cross the glacier to the north-side basin/camp. An alternate quieter descent (or ascent) is the SE Shelf — a tilted slab ramp on the SE side that avoids the more heavily crevassed north-side glacier; by August the small SE-basin glacier arm recedes to expose polished, sometimes wet/grit-covered low-5th-class slabs where many parties choose to rope up, especially descending, and scattered rappel webbing is visible from past parties.
routes.itinerary: Typically a 2-day trip: hike in to a high camp in the north-side basin (roughly 4-5 hrs from the trailhead), then a summit day of roughly 5-6 hrs round-trip climbing from camp, followed by the descent back to camp and out.
routes.bail: Retreat back down the ridge/glacier the way you came; turn back before committing to the glacier traverse if visibility is poor or crevasses look unmanageable, and before the final Class 3 scramble in wet or snow-covered conditions.
routes.turnaround: Begin the descent early enough to be off the glacier and summit ledges before typical Cascades afternoon cloud buildup and before morning-firm snow softens into the afternoon, which increases both crevasse risk and rockfall/loose-rock hazard on the scramble.
routes.bestSeason: Late June through August is the optimal window: early enough that the glacier is still mostly snow-covered (easier, safer travel) but late enough that the upper heather benches and summit ledges are snow-free enough to route-find and climb safely. Too early and snow buries the route-finding on the upper mountain; by September the glacier crossing becomes increasingly broken and technical, sometimes impassable.
gear: crevasse-rescue kit (pickets, pulley, prusiks, chock pick)
whatToBring: rain jacket and full-zip rain pants
bivy/camp gear for the overnight approach
routes.detailedRack: No rock rack is needed for the standard line — this is a glacier-and-scramble route, not a pitched rock climb.
routes.proNeeds: A full crevasse-rescue kit (rope, pickets or screws, prusiks/ascenders, rescue pulley, chock pick) plus a mountaineering axe and crampons (light aluminum crampons are adequate for the low-angle glacier, though morning snow can be firm). A rope for the glacier crossing is recommended even for confident parties, particularly late season as crevasses open up.
proTips: Cache glacier/snow gear (rope, harness, pickets, axe, crampons) at the top of the snow section before the final Class 3 scrambling to move faster on the ridge and summit block.
Get a current glacier-conditions report before a late-season (September) attempt — crevasses open up significantly as the season progresses.
The SE Shelf offers a quieter alternative to the crevassed north-side glacier, at the cost of some low 5th-class slab climbing.
watchOut: Crevasses on the glacier traverse worsen as the season progresses.
Loose, exposed Class 3 rock on the final summit scramble.
Afternoon cloud buildup and thunderstorm potential typical of the Cascades.
knownHazards: The glacier below the corkscrew ramp is crevassed and becomes more broken through summer, especially by September.
Rockfall is possible on the loose upper ledges, particularly with multiple parties on route.
Snow-covered upper heather benches before roughly late June obscure route-finding and can force a turnaround.

road.name: Forest Road 4096 (Bedal Creek) / North Fork Sauk River Road (Cougar Creek)
road.status: Bedal Creek approach via FR 4096 is the more commonly used option; Cougar Creek requires fording the North Fork Sauk River, a serious obstacle at high water.
road.driveNote: High-clearance/4WD recommended for a short, steep, narrow section of FR 4096 between the 5th and 6th switchbacks; the Bedal Creek trailhead parking area is very small (fewer than 10 cars).

climate.typical: Marine west coast climate; Pacific frontal systems bring orographic precipitation, and snow is often wet and heavy, elevating avalanche danger in winter/spring.
climate.spring: Heavy, wet snowpack often persists into June on the approach and upper mountain; avalanche danger can remain elevated into late spring.
climate.summer: Best climbing window; July-August offers the most reliable balance of snow-free ledges and manageable (though still crevassed) glacier travel.
climate.fall: By September the glacier crossing becomes increasingly broken and can turn impassable; the technical difficulty of the route rises quickly as the season progresses.
climate.winter: Heavy snow and avalanche hazard largely preclude routine winter ascents of the standard route; a handful of technical winter lines exist elsewhere on the peak (e.g., Full Moon Fever, IV 5.8 AI4 R, on the West Face).

routes.fa: Harry Bedal & Nels Skaar, July 30, 1921 (also the peak's first ascent)
routes.face: Curls from the north-side glacier around the South Ridge/buttress onto the Upper West Face
objHaz: loose rock on the final scramble

routes.overview: The longest route on the mountain: a sustained 9-pitch buttress climb up steep, featured alpine rock, with several bivy options along the way.
routes.beta: Rated IV 5.9+, 1,800 ft, 9 pitches. A mix of a few really good pitches and a few lesser ones (2 stars). Best attempted mid-to-late season (July-September) to avoid snowmelt complications.
routes.approach: Same Bedal basin approach used for the West Face routes: hike to the meadows below the broad west face. The route begins on a corner of darker rock at the lowest, leftmost part of the face.
routes.descent: Scramble 3rd/4th class terrain to a sub-summit, downclimb briefly, then continue to the main summit; descend via the Corkscrew Route.
routes.descentText: From the top of the buttress, scramble 3rd/4th class rock to a sub-summit, make a brief downclimb, then continue up to Sloan's true summit. Descend the standard Corkscrew Route (glacier + ridge scramble) back to the north-side basin.
routes.bail: Retreat is difficult once established on the upper buttress pitches given the sustained, sometimes poorly protected climbing; easiest bail points are from ledges below the crux corner/chimney system.
routes.turnaround: Plan for a full day (9 pitches plus a scramble finish and descent) — start early enough that you are not still on the upper pitches or the descent scramble as afternoon weather typical of the Cascades builds.
routes.bestSeason: Mid-to-late season, roughly July through September, is recommended; earlier-season snowmelt on the approach and lower buttress can complicate the climb.
gear: medium alpine rock rack to 3+ inches
whatToBring: bivy gear if splitting the route over two days
approach shoes for the Bedal basin hike
routes.detailedRack: A medium alpine rock rack up to 3+ inches with 8-10 runners covers the route; a helmet is essential given loose rock in places.
routes.proNeeds: Protection is variable — the opening pitch (5.8+) is not greatly protected, and the crux corner/chimney relies in part on a single fixed pin.
proTips: Expect 4-5 genuinely good pitches mixed with a few less inspiring ones — move efficiently through the easier heather/tree terrain to save time for the crux.
Bivy spots exist partway up the buttress if you want to split the route over two days.
watchOut: The first pitch is funky, balancy climbing on not-greatly-protected rock (5.8+ish) heading for a dead tree near the crest.
Rock quality varies, including a rotten-rock band on the northern face traverse.
The crux corner/chimney partly relies on a single fixed pin for protection.
knownHazards: Loose/rotten rock exists off-route on the north-face traverse below the crux.
Late-season snowmelt can complicate the approach and lower buttress.

pitchDetail.notes: Funky, balancy climbing on not-greatly-protected rock, heading for a dead tree near the crest.
Left past rotten rock onto the north face via easy rock, heather, and small trees; passes a grassy gully and a large rock pillar toward a black rock section.
Obvious right-facing corner/chimney, starting around 5.7 and getting harder toward the top; crux involves climbing straight up via cracks with a fixed pin and maneuvering onto a small sandy ledge.
Nice short hand crack leading to the upper slopes below the summit.

road.name: Forest Road 4096 (Bedal Creek)
road.status: Same Bedal basin trailhead access as the standard route.
road.driveNote: High-clearance/4WD recommended for a short, steep section of FR 4096; very limited trailhead parking.

climate.typical: Marine west coast climate with orographic precipitation; snow tends to be wet and heavy.
climate.spring: Snow often lingers on the lower buttress and approach into June.
climate.summer: Best window, July-September, once snowmelt has cleared the lower rock.
climate.fall: Workable into September; weather becomes less reliable later in fall.

routes.fa: M. Preiss & M. Bunker, 2000

routes.overview: An 8-pitch, sustained 5.10d alpine rock line up the Southwest Face on featured diorite, finishing with a short Class 3 scramble to the true summit via the Upper Corkscrew Route.
routes.beta: 1,100 ft (333 m) of technical rock across 8 pitches, plus roughly 500 ft of 3rd-class scrambling to the summit. Climbs wild knobs and diorite fins reminiscent of California granite. Pitches are steep, long, and relatively sustained for alpine climbing; rock quality is generally very good but can be lichen-y/dirty in places from low traffic. Generally very good protection, especially at the cruxes, with a couple of runout sections (pitches 2 and 5).
routes.approach: From the Bedal Creek Trailhead (end of FR 4096), hike the Bedal Creek Trail (No. 705) about 2.5 miles, first through old-growth forest then along Bedal Creek's steep banks, to where the trail ends at a washout. Ascend the washout, exit via a cairn onto a climber's path into the basin, then cross the ridge separating the west-facing and southwest-facing basins to reach the base of the SW Face.
routes.descent: Descend via the Upper Corkscrew Route (~500 ft of Class 3 scrambling) from the summit, then continue on the standard Corkscrew descent or drop via the SE Shelf.
routes.descentText: From the summit, downclimb roughly 500 ft of 3rd-class terrain via the Upper Corkscrew Route, then either descend the standard Corkscrew line back toward the north-side basin, or return via the SE Shelf (polished granite slabs, then snow) back toward the approach basin; optional rappels are used by some parties, plus down-climbing to snow and a traverse back to the approach.
routes.itinerary: Can be done car-to-car in a big day from Seattle, or with a bivy at the base of the route where running water is available.
routes.bail: Retreat from the lower pitches is possible with a single rope by rappelling the route's crack systems; above the mid-route ledges it is generally easier to continue up and descend via the Corkscrew scramble than to reverse the technical pitches.
routes.turnaround: Given the route is a full technical day (8 pitches plus a scramble to the true summit and a multi-option descent), start early enough to be off the upper pitches well before dark, allowing a margin for the descent's routefinding and any rappels.
routes.bestSeason: Late season is recommended, as snow lingers on Sloan's large ledges earlier in the summer and complicates the route; the FA and repeat ascents were done in August-October.
gear: optional ice axe/crampons for snow traverses depending on season
whatToBring: bivy gear if not going car-to-car
water treatment (running water available at the base in season)
approach shoes for the basin traverse
routes.detailedRack: Doubles of cams from roughly #0.2 to #2, a single #3, a full set of stoppers, and lots of alpine draws (doubles of slings recommended).
routes.proNeeds: Generally very good protection, especially at the pitch cruxes, but with runout sections on pitch 2 and pitch 5.
proTips: Can be climbed car-to-car in a big day from Seattle, or with a bivy at the base (running water available).
Wait for late season — snow on Sloan's large ledges earlier in the year complicates the route.
Finish with the ~500 ft Corkscrew scramble to reach the true summit rather than stopping at the top of the technical rock.
watchOut: Runout sections on pitch 2 and pitch 5 despite generally solid protection.
Weather is unpredictable on the SW Face with low clouds common.
Approach crosses streams that can be higher/harder earlier in the season.
knownHazards: Low route traffic means lichen, moss, and dirt can be present in cracks.
Descent ledges/glacier remnants can hold snow into midsummer, complicating the scramble finish.

pitchDetail.notes: The technical crux: left-facing chimney (5.9) to a mid-pitch ledge, finishing with splitter finger cracks (5.10d) up a steep face.
Excellent pitch: hand crack, featured face, off-width, then a hand traverse beneath a roof.
Right-facing corner to a heather ledge.
Left-trending crack system into a clean dihedral with an off-width, ending on a pedestal.
Knobs and fins (5.8R, runout) leading to a right-facing corner.
Featured face climbing to a major heather ledge.
Navigate around a gendarme via hand cracks and a leftward traverse.
Left traverse on flakes to easier ground.

climate.typical: Marine west coast climate; unpredictable weather with low clouds common on the SW Face.
climate.summer: Snow on the large upper ledges can complicate the route earlier in summer.
climate.fall: Late season (Aug-Oct) is the recommended window once ledges have cleared of snow.

routes.corrections: First-ascent year is given as 2009 by Mountain Project's route data, but the AAC/American Alpine Journal write-up is filed under 2010 (likely a publication-year offset, common for AAJ reports of late-season climbs) — otherwise the route facts (grade, pitch count, length, FA party) are consistent across sources.

routes.overview: A 9-pitch, sustained 5.10/5.11- alpine rock line on the Southwest Face, established in 2011 roughly in the vicinity of Fire on the Mountain.
routes.beta: 1,100 ft, Grade III, nine pitches. Mountain Project lists the route at 5.11- (2 stars); the original first-ascent trip report describes it as 5.10, Grade III. Details beyond length, pitch count, and first-ascent party are sparse in available sources.

routes.fa: Brandon Workman & Rad Roberts, September 11, 2011
routes.corrections: Mountain Project's area page lists this route at 5.11- (2 stars); the original 2011 first-ascent trip report (CascadeClimbers.com) describes it as graded 5.10, Grade III at the time of the FA. Both figures are reported here since the discrepancy could not be resolved from available sources — the FA account is likely the initial (possibly soft) grade, with 5.11- reflecting later consensus.

blurb: Table Mountain is a broad, flat-topped 5,744-foot summit in the Skagit Range of the North Cascades, within the Mount Baker Wilderness (Mount Baker-Snoqualmie National Forest), northeast of Mount Baker and west of Mount Shuksan. The mountain is a single massive Pleistocene andesite lava flow with distinctive columnar jointing, thought to have been constrained by canyon walls (possibly glacial ice) as it cooled. It sits directly above Artist Point at the end of the Mount Baker Highway (SR 542) and is reached by one of the most popular short trails in the North Cascades — a non-technical but exposed path up cliff-cut switchbacks to the summit plateau.

routes.overview: A short, steep, non-technical trail (Forest Service Trail #681) climbs a series of switchbacks blasted into the cliff face of Table Mountain's southeast side from the Artist Point parking lot, gaining the mountain's broad, flat summit plateau in under a mile, with sweeping views of Mount Baker, Mount Shuksan, Mount Herman, and Mount Larrabee.
routes.beta: From Artist Point (5,100 ft), the trail climbs gently before switchbacking steeply up an exposed cliff on Table Mountain's southeast face to reach the plateau on top, then continues across easy, mostly flat terrain to the summit and beyond to viewpoints over the Chain Lakes basin and the Table Mountain Glacier remnant. It is technically an easy Class 1-2 hike with no roped climbing, but the switchback section has real cliff-edge exposure and is not recommended for anyone uneasy with heights, small children, or dogs (prohibited). A separate, unofficial scramble trail continues north from the tarns near the summit and drops steeply (700+ ft) onto/around the Table Mountain Glacier remnant to connect with the Chain Lakes Trail below; this connector is loose, exposed, and not part of the standard out-and-back route.
routes.approach: From I-5 Exit 255 (Bellingham), follow the Mount Baker Highway (SR 542) east about 34 miles to the town of Glacier, then continue roughly another 24 miles (past Heather Meadows/the ski area) to the highway's end at the large Artist Point parking lot (room for roughly 150-250 vehicles, plus an overflow lot just below). The trailhead is on the west side of the lot opposite the restrooms. A Northwest Forest Pass or America the Beautiful/Interagency pass is required to park.
routes.descent: Retrace the same switchback trail back down to the Artist Point parking lot.
routes.descentText: Descent is simply a reversal of the ascent: from the summit plateau, walk back to the head of the switchbacks and downclimb the same cliff-cut trail to Artist Point. Take particular care on the switchbacks if snow, ice, or loose gravel is present, and watch footing near the unfenced drop-offs. Do not attempt the unofficial northern scramble trail down onto the Table Mountain Glacier remnant toward Chain Lakes as a 'shortcut' unless you are an experienced off-trail navigator comfortable with steep, loose, exposed terrain and route-finding — hikers have gotten cliffed-out or badly disoriented trying to find the connector.
routes.itinerary: This is a half-day trip: drive to Artist Point (allow a full day if combining with the ~150-mile round trip from Puget Sound), hike the ~1.4 miles and ~700 ft of gain up to the summit plateau (30-60 min), spend time exploring the plateau and viewpoints, then descend the same way (20-40 min). Total car-to-car time is typically 1.5-2 hours for the standard summit hike; add 1-2+ hours if continuing on to explore toward Chain Lakes.
routes.bail: The route is a simple out-and-back from a single trailhead — retreat is possible from any point simply by turning around and descending the switchback trail back to the Artist Point parking lot; there is no technical descent or rappelling required.
routes.turnaround: Because the exposed switchback section becomes genuinely hazardous when wet, icy, or snow-covered, turn around if you encounter unconsolidated snow or ice on the switchbacks without traction devices, if thunderstorms are building (the summit plateau is fully exposed with no shelter), or if any member of the party is uncomfortable with the sustained cliff-edge exposure — the trail offers no technical protection.
routes.bestSeason: Late July through September is the reliable snow-free window; the road to Artist Point itself is normally only open roughly June 21 to mid-October, and snow can linger on the steep switchbacks into July even after the road opens (the flatter summit plateau holds patchy snow longer but is safe to cross without spikes once the switchbacks are clear). Early-season and late-season visits should be prepared for snow/ice on the switchback section. It is one of the most popular short hikes in the North Cascades, so a weekend start by ~8 AM is recommended to find trailhead parking.
routes.comms: No reliable cell service at Artist Point itself; coverage on SR 542 is spotty past Maple Falls and generally absent from the Kendall area onward, with only occasional pockets for some carriers near Glacier and at the Mt. Baker Ski Area base. Do not rely on a cell phone for emergency communication on this route.
gear: sturdy hiking boots or trail runners with good traction
trekking poles (recommended for the steep switchbacks)
layered clothing for wind and exposure
sun protection - hat, sunglasses, sunscreen (the plateau has zero shade)
all the water you'll need (none available on route)
Northwest Forest Pass or Interagency (America the Beautiful) pass for parking
microspikes/traction if snow or ice is present on the switchbacks (common into July)
whatToBring: extra insulating layer (weather changes fast at 5,700 ft)
camera - the summit plateau has some of the best views of Mt. Baker and Mt. Shuksan in the range
proTips: Arrive at the Artist Point lot by ~8 AM on summer weekends; the lot (150-250+ cars) fills early and this is one of the most popular short hikes in the North Cascades.
There is no shade anywhere on the route, so sun protection and extra water matter even for this short hike.
Trekking poles help a lot on the steep, sometimes loose switchback section.

Don't try to freelance a route down toward Chain Lakes via the glacier remnant unless you're experienced with off-trail scrambling and route-finding — the connector is not obvious and has stranded hikers.
watchOut: Sustained exposure with unfenced drop-offs on the cliff-cut switchback section - not suitable for those uneasy with heights, small children, or pets.
Snow and ice commonly linger on the switchbacks into July, turning an easy trail into a real slip-and-fall hazard.
Loose gravel and rock on the switchbacks and near the summit edges.
No water sources on route - carry everything you need.
The open summit plateau offers no shelter from sudden weather or lightning.
The unofficial scramble connector to Chain Lakes/the Table Mountain Glacier remnant is steep, loose, and easy to get cliffed-out on - it is not part of the maintained standard trail.
knownHazards: Cliff-edge exposure along the switchback section poses a serious fall hazard, especially with wet rock, snow, or ice.

Winter and shoulder-season snowpack on the north/northeast slopes below the summit is real avalanche terrain used by backcountry skiers - this is outside the scope of the standard summer hiking route.
The trail and summit plateau are fully exposed to weather with no tree cover, raising lightning and hypothermia risk in storms.
Remnants of the Table Mountain Glacier below the north side of the summit create additional fall and crevasse-adjacent hazard for anyone leaving the maintained trail.

pitchDetail.pitch: Artist Point to base of switchbacks
pitchDetail.notes: Gentle, gradual climb on a wide, well-built trail across the lower slope.
Cliff-cut switchbacks gain most of the route's elevation in under a mile; unfenced drop-offs, can hold snow/ice into July.
Broad, mostly flat plateau walking to the high point and viewpoints; can hold patchy snow into summer but is not exposed.

road.name: Mount Baker Highway (SR 542)
road.status: Paved to Artist Point; open seasonally only
road.seasonalGate: The final 2.7 miles to Artist Point typically open around June 21 and close around mid-October to November 1 (snow-dependent); the highway remains open further down to Heather Meadows (MP 54) for a longer season.
road.driveNote: From I-5 Exit 255 near Bellingham, take SR 542 east about 34 miles to Glacier, then continue roughly 24 more miles to the highway's end at Artist Point.

climate.forecastZone: West Slopes North (NWAC)
climate.typical: Cool, wet, and heavily snowed-in much of the year - the Mount Baker area records some of the heaviest annual snowfall in North America.
climate.spring: Deep snowpack typically persists on the switchbacks and plateau into June; the access road usually doesn't open until around June 21.
climate.summer: Warm and mostly dry July-September, the primary hiking window; the open plateau has no shade and high UV exposure.
climate.fall: Cool with the first significant snowfall often closing the access road between mid-October and early November.
climate.winter: Road closed beyond Heather Meadows; Table Mountain becomes serious avalanche terrain and a backcountry ski/mountaineering objective rather than a hiking destination.

access.landManager: Mount Baker-Snoqualmie National Forest, Mount Baker Ranger District, within the Mount Baker Wilderness
access.fees: Trailhead parking requires a Northwest Forest Pass or America the Beautiful/Interagency federal recreation pass
access.permit: No permit is required for day hiking
access.passRequired: Northwest Forest Pass or Interagency (America the Beautiful) pass
access.closures: No camping in the Austin Pass/Artist Point/Chain Lakes area May 15-November 14; dogs are prohibited on the Table Mountain and Chain Lakes trails; the access road to Artist Point is closed seasonally roughly mid-October through late June.

timing.recommendedStart: Early morning (by about 8 AM) on summer weekends to secure parking at the popular Artist Point trailhead

emergency.rangerStation: Glacier Public Service Center, 10091 Mt. Baker Hwy, Glacier, WA 98244 - (360) 599-2714
emergency.nearestHospital: PeaceHealth St. Joseph Medical Center, Bellingham, WA (roughly 55-60 miles / ~1.5 hr drive via SR 542)
emergency.notes: Cell coverage is unreliable to nonexistent at Artist Point and along most of upper SR 542; do not count on a phone call for help. This is a very high-traffic trail in summer, so other hikers are usually nearby.

objHaz: avalanche terrain in winter (off standard route)
routes.corrections: Given elevation of 5,744 ft is consistent with sourced figures (USGS/Wikipedia lists 5,742 ft; WTA cites a trail high point of ~5,700 ft) - the small variance reflects normal differences between survey datums and is not a data error.

blurb: The Brothers is a twin-summited peak in the eastern Olympic Mountains, overlooking Hood Canal and visible from Seattle across Puget Sound; its higher south summit (Mount Edward, ~6,866-6,868 ft) and slightly lower north summit (Mount Arthur) sit within the Forest Service's Brothers Wilderness on the boundary with Olympic National Park. The mountain is composed of Eocene basalt and was named in 1856 by surveyor George Davidson for two brothers of his fiancée, Ellinor Fauntleroy. The north peak was first climbed in 1908 (C. Hill and W. Hill) and the higher south peak in 1912 (I. Collier, O. Corkenill, W. Dehn, W. Fish, E. Goldsmith, H. Trumbull).

routes.overview: The standard, non-technical line up The Brothers' south peak: a long forest/creek approach to a climbers' camp, then a steep scree-and-snow couloir on the peak's south side broken by a class 3-4 rock narrows ("the Hourglass") just below the summit ridge.
routes.beta: From the Brothers Base Camp (~3,000-3,600 ft) the route follows the west fork of Lena Creek up through an old avalanche/burn area, crosses into a forested gully, and breaks out onto open scree around 5,000 ft. Climbers make an ascending rightward traverse near cliffs to reach the couloir proper, which holds sustained snow (~30 degrees) in early season above the Hourglass, a class 3-4 rock chokepoint; above it, scree and easy rock lead to the summit. Route character shifts seasonally from a snow climb (ice axe/crampons, sometimes pickets) in spring/early summer to a loose class 3-4 scree scramble once the couloir melts out, typically by late August.
routes.approach: Drive US-101 to the Hamma Hamma River Road (Forest Road 25) turnoff (~14 mi north of Hoodsport, near milepost 318) and follow it about 8 miles west to the Lena Lake Trailhead. Hike Lena Lake Trail #810 about 3 miles to Lena Lake (1,800 ft), then continue onto The Brothers Trail #821 through the aptly named "Valley of the Silent Men," crossing Lena Creek to reach informal camps around 3,000-3,600 ft (roughly 5.5-6 miles from the trailhead) — the usual base camp for a summit push. Trail marking beyond the Lena Lake basin is inconsistent (flagging/cairns through old 1999/2003 burn windfall); multiple trip reports warn not to rely solely on GPX tracks and to watch for tape where the route swings toward the Brothers drainage.
routes.descent: Descend the same South Couloir/Hourglass line back to base camp, then retrace the approach trail to the Lena Lake Trailhead.
routes.descentText: Reverse the ascent route: downclimb or glissade (when snow is consolidated and temperatures allow) the couloir back past the Hourglass to the scree slopes at ~5,000 ft, then drop back into the forested gully to base camp. At least one trip report found a variant that avoids the main upper gully on the way down and offers friendlier footing; in cold conditions the snow can stay too firm to glissade safely. From base camp, hike back out via the Brothers Trail and Lena Lake Trail to the trailhead (about 8-9 miles).
routes.itinerary: Typical 2-day plan: Day 1 — hike from Lena Lake Trailhead to the Brothers Base Camp (~3,000-3,600 ft, 5.5-6 miles) and camp. Day 2 — pre-dawn alpine start, climb the South Couloir and Hourglass to the south summit (6,866-6,868 ft), descend to camp, break camp, and hike out. Strong parties occasionally do it as a single very long push directly from the trailhead (reported starts as early as 1:45 AM with a return the following afternoon), but this is an aggressive, full-value day given the ~16-18 mile round trip and ~6,050-6,200 ft of gain.
routes.bail: Below the Hourglass, the route can be abandoned at almost any point by reversing the scree/couloir and forest path back to the Brothers Base Camp — there is no technical commitment. Above the Hourglass there is no established rappel line; retreat is by downclimbing the same class 3-4 terrain, so parties should not commit to the upper couloir if conditions (snow, rockfall, weather) make that downclimb doubtful.
routes.turnaround: Because evacuation from the upper mountain is a multi-hour downclimb followed by a 6+ mile single-track hike out with no helicopter landing zone near the summit, parties should set a firm decision point below the Hourglass and turn back for deteriorating weather, whiteout, unconsolidated/icy snow in the couloir, or a start too late to be off the upper mountain with a comfortable daylight margin.
routes.bestSeason: Best climbed roughly June through September. Early in the season the South Couloir is a genuine snow climb requiring ice axe, crampons, and often a helmet for rockfall through the Hourglass narrows; by late summer (commonly by late August) the couloir melts out to loose scree and becomes a scramble climbable without snow gear but with more rockfall/loose-rock exposure. Snow conditions vary significantly year to year.
routes.comms: No reliable cell coverage is reported along the approach or route; Jefferson County Search and Rescue has responded to multiple satellite-messenger (inReach) SOS activations from the climbers' camp and standard route in recent years, and parties commonly carry a satellite communicator given the lack of cell service and the multi-mile, no-helicopter-access evacuation.
gear: Rope and 2-3 snow pickets (optional, for less experienced parties on firm snow)
routes.detailedRack: No technical rock rack is needed for the standard line. When the couloir holds snow, carry an ice axe (or whippet on the lower approach), crampons if the snow is firm, and a helmet for rockfall through the Hourglass narrows; parties with less-experienced members sometimes bring a 30 m glacier/climbing rope and a few pickets to protect the steeper snow sections. Once the couloir has melted to scree (typically by late summer), no snow or rock protection is used and the route is climbed as an unroped class 3-4 scramble.
routes.proNeeds: Protection is snow-dependent rather than rock-dependent: pickets/a hand line in the couloir for weaker parties on consolidated early-season snow; none needed on the dry, melted-out scree/scramble version.
whatToBring: Gaiters (approach shoes get soaked at creek crossings)
Extra water — creek crossings and water sources vary through the day
proTips: Trail marking through the old burn/windfall area beyond Lena Lake is inconsistent — watch closely for flagging tape where the route swings toward the Brothers drainage, and don't rely solely on a GPX track.
The couloir is usually more enjoyable, and the scree less loose underfoot, when there is still snow cover; by late season it becomes a tedious loose-scree scramble.
Cold overnight temperatures can keep the snow too firm to glissade safely on descent — plan for a slower downclimb in those conditions.
A base camp near ~3,000-3,600 ft (informal sites, some with a firepit) is the standard staging point for a summit push rather than climbing round-trip from the road in one day.
watchOut: Extensive deadfall/windfall from the 1999 and 2003 wildfires makes the trail hard to follow in places.
Class 3-4 rock scrambling with loose rock through the Hourglass narrows just below the summit.
Sustained ~30-degree snow slopes above the Hourglass in early season require ice axe/crampons and careful footwork.
Creek crossings (Lena Creek and its west fork) can become more difficult later in the day as flows change.
Mountain goats are regularly encountered near camp and on-route.

No cell coverage and no helicopter access near the summit — evacuation is a long single-track carry-out; multiple SAR call-outs (broken ankle, heat injury, a serious fall) have originated from this route, and at least one fatal accident has been reported on it.
knownHazards: Avalanche-prone terrain is crossed low on the approach in an old avalanche runout area.
Rockfall and loose rock are a persistent hazard through the Hourglass and upper scree slopes, especially with multiple parties on-route.
Steep, sustained snow above the Hourglass presents a slip-and-fall hazard without proper snow travel gear.
Remote, multi-mile evacuation with no helicopter landing near the summit has factored into several documented rescues, including a fatality reported by at least one source.
routes.face: South Couloir / south side of the south peak (Mount Edward)

road.name: Hamma Hamma River Road (Forest Road 25)
road.status: Open seasonally; Lena Lake Trailhead and FS-25 day-use sites were confirmed open in 2025
road.seasonalGate: Road is typically snowed in and impassable through winter; a July 2025 fire-related closure order on FR-25 (issued 7/29/25) was rescinded 8/3/25
road.driveNote: From Hoodsport, drive ~14 miles north on US-101 to the Hamma Hamma River Road turnoff near milepost 318, then follow FR-25 west about 8 miles to the Lena Lake Trailhead.

climate.forecastZone: Olympics (NWAC avalanche/mountain-weather zone)
climate.typical: Wet maritime climate typical of the eastern Olympics; the South Couloir can hold snow well into summer in heavy-snow years.
climate.spring: Couloir usually snow-covered and climbed as a snow route; avalanche hazard exists on approach slopes.
climate.summer: Prime climbing window (Jun-Sep); the couloir commonly melts out to scree by August.
climate.fall: Increasing rain and snow with shorter daylight; conditions become more winter-like later in the season.
climate.winter: Access road typically snowed in; not a standard winter objective.

access.landManager: USDA Forest Service, Olympic National Forest (Hood Canal Ranger District), within The Brothers Wilderness; the upper mountain lies on/near the boundary with Olympic National Park.
access.fees: Recreation Pass required for day-use parking at the Lena Lake Trailhead (Northwest Forest Pass / Interagency "America the Beautiful" pass or a Digital day pass are honored).
access.permit: No permit needed for Wilderness day use. The Brothers Wilderness has group-size and campfire restrictions (no fires above 3,500 ft; groups over 12 prohibited). A separate NPS wilderness permit applies only if a party's route crosses into Olympic National Park proper via this trail system.
access.passRequired: Northwest Forest Pass (or equivalent interagency/day pass) at the trailhead
access.closures: FR-25 and the Lena Lake Trailhead are seasonal; expect the road to be snowed in through winter. Check current Olympic National Forest alerts before driving, as the corridor has had short-notice fire closures (e.g., the rescinded July-August 2025 Hamma Fire closure order).

timing.recommendedStart: A pre-dawn alpine start from base camp (parties commonly leave between roughly 1:45 AM and 4:45 AM) to cross the avalanche/creek terrain and the Hourglass before the day heats up and to leave a full daylight margin for the long round trip.

emergency.county: Jefferson County, WA (near the Mason County line; both counties' SAR teams have responded on this peak)
emergency.sheriffDispatch: Jefferson County Sheriff's Office, supported by Jefferson Search and Rescue (JSAR) and Olympic Mountain Rescue for incidents on The Brothers
emergency.rangerStation: Hood Canal Ranger District (Quilcene Office), Olympic National Forest — (360) 765-2200, 295142 US-101, Quilcene, WA
emergency.nearestHospital: Jefferson Healthcare Hospital, Port Townsend, WA (nearest for the Brinnon/Hood Canal side); Mason General Hospital in Shelton, WA is also commonly used as the nearer facility from the Hamma Hamma/Hoodsport side of the trailhead.
emergency.notes: No cell coverage is reported on-route; recent rescues (broken ankle, heat injury, and a fatal accident reported by one trip account) were initiated via satellite SOS devices (e.g., inReach) rather than cell phone. There is no helicopter landing zone near the summit — evacuation requires a multi-mile single-track carry-out.

routes.corrections: The given elevation (6,868 ft) and coordinates (47.653799, -123.140923) closely match independent sourcing: Wikipedia's coordinates (47.653766, -123.140952) are within ~5 m, and Mountain Project lists the summit at 6,866 ft (essentially the same figure, within normal survey/GPS variance) — no change made. Note Wikipedia's infobox separately cites an older USGS figure of 6,842 ft for the same summit; sources vary by ~25 ft depending on survey vintage.

blurb: Mount Stone (6,612 ft) is a three-summited peak in the southeastern Olympic Mountains of Washington, straddling the boundary between Olympic National Park (Mount Skokomish/Olympic Wilderness) and Olympic National Forest; it is the highest point in Mason County. Like most of the eastern Olympics it is built of Crescent Formation pillow basalt — rock Mountain Project describes as of "questionable quality" — and its South (main), Middle, and North summits are reached by class 2-4 scrambling above the alpine cirque of Lake of the Angels. No documented first-ascent party/date appears in standard references (SummitPost, Mountain Project, Wikipedia); the only dedicated guidebook coverage is in Olympic Mountain Rescue's "Climber's Guide to the Olympic Mountains."

routes.routeName: South Route via Lake of the Angels
routes.rockGrade: Class 3, one class-4 move near the summit
objHaz: Lingering snow and avalanche-prone terrain near St. Peter's Gate into early summer
Steep, unmaintained trail with an exposed handline section
Fog/whiteout navigation near St. Peter's Gate
Black bear activity in the Lake of the Angels basin
routes.season: Jun-Oct trail season; best climbing window roughly Jul-Sep once the headwall and west-ridge notch clear of snow
routes.overview: The standard route on Mount Stone: a long, steep trail approach up the Putvin Trail to the alpine cirque of Lake of the Angels, followed by a class-3 scramble (one 4th-class move near the top) up the mountain's south side and west ridge to the true (South) summit.
routes.beta: From Lake of the Angels, a rough but cairned climbers' path climbs the headwall to a small meadow around 4,400 ft, then continues north to St. Peter's Gate, an obvious notch in the SE ridge at 5,950 ft. From the gate, skirt (don't descend into) the cliffs to the west to reach a notch on the west ridge — the more direct-looking gully lines are actually harder. Scramble class 3 up a couple hundred feet to a shallow hanging basin, traverse it, then climb the east side of the ridge to the summit block, marked by a large cairn; the final stretch includes one section of 4th-class scrambling. Rock is typical Olympic pillow basalt of variable quality.
routes.approach: Drive US 101 to Hamma Hamma River Road (Forest Road 25) at milepost 318 (about 14 miles north of Hoodsport). Follow FR 25 west; it is paved for roughly 7.5-8 miles to the Lena Lake Trailhead, then turns to gravel (occasional potholes, sometimes washed out — high clearance recommended) for another ~4.3-4.5 miles to the Putvin Trailhead pullout (roadside parking for about 30 cars). The Putvin Trail (#813) starts across the road on the north side and is steep and largely primitive, gaining nearly 3,000 ft in under 4 miles. It passes the 'Pond of the False Prophet' (Frog Pond) in the 'Valley of Heaven,' then a short, steep headwall section near mile 2.8 with an exposed fixed handline on slick rock, before entering Olympic National Park and reaching the alpine basin at Lake of the Angels (about 4,888-4,950 ft) at roughly 3.6 miles.
routes.descent: Reverse the ascent: down the east side of the west ridge, through the hanging basin, back past St. Peter's Gate to Lake of the Angels, then down the Putvin Trail. Snow slopes above the lake can sometimes be glissaded early season.
routes.descentText: From the summit, downclimb the 4th-class block to the cairned notch, then reverse the traverse along the base of the cliffs (staying high, west of the gullies) back to the west-ridge notch. Descend the class-3 slope into the hanging basin, then back down past St. Peter's Gate to the meadow at ~4,400 ft. Drop through the headwall climbers' path to Lake of the Angels and retrace the Putvin Trail — using extra care on the handline/headwall section near mile 2.8, which is more exposed descending than ascending — back to the trailhead.
routes.itinerary: Commonly done as a single long day (car-to-car reports of roughly 7.5 hours for a fast party up to 10+ hours) or as an overnight with camp at Lake of the Angels (wilderness permit required), summiting the next morning and hiking out.
routes.bail: The route can be abandoned at nearly any point by reversing the way in; the main commitment is the 4th-class summit block, which can be downclimbed. St. Peter's Gate and the headwall/handline section on the Putvin Trail need the most care retreating in wet or icy conditions.
routes.turnaround: Because the final scrambling and the exposed Putvin Trail sections are far easier and safer in daylight and on dry rock, set a firm turnaround time that gets you off the summit block and back through the headwall/handline section before dark or before afternoon thunderstorms build.
routes.bestSeason: Best from mid-summer into early fall (roughly July-September per multiple trip reports) once seasonal snow has cleared the headwall and west-ridge notch. SummitPost lists June-October as the general trail season; earlier-season (May-June) ascents typically involve full snow cover, postholing, and avalanche-terrain exposure near St. Peter's Gate.
routes.detailedRack: Not a technical rope climb for most parties. If protecting the short 4th-class section, Mountain Project recommends a couple of mid-to-large nuts and a few triple-length slings to sling rock horns, since the crumbly basalt takes gear poorly.
routes.proNeeds: Minimal — the crux is a short 4th-class scramble on loose basalt; a light rack of nuts/slings is optional insurance rather than a requirement.
gear: Optional light rack (nuts + slings) for the 4th-class summit block
whatToBring: Map/compass or GPS track for the St. Peter's Gate route-finding
Trekking poles for the steep Putvin Trail
Wilderness permit if camping at Lake of the Angels
proTips: Skirt the cliffs to the west of St. Peter's Gate rather than dropping into the tempting gullies below it — the more direct-looking lines are actually harder.
The summit block is marked by a large cairn on its east side; approach from the east, not from the west/gullies.
Lake of the Angels makes a scenic overnight base and shortens summit day considerably.
watchOut: The Putvin Trail's headwall section (~mile 2.8) has an exposed, slick-rock stretch with a fixed handline — treat it with care, especially descending or when wet.
Basalt on the route is described by Mountain Project as of 'questionable quality'/'crumbly' — test holds before weighting them.
Fog is common at St. Peter's Gate and has caused navigation problems for parties on this side of the mountain.
knownHazards: Loose, crumbly pillow-basalt rock throughout the route.
Steep, unmaintained trail with an exposed handline section on the approach.
Lingering snow and avalanche-prone terrain near St. Peter's Gate into early summer.
Black bear activity reported in the Lake of the Angels basin.

pitchDetail.notes: ~3.6 mi, ~3,300-3,400 ft gain, Putvin TH to Lake of the Angels
Climbers' path through the headwall to a ~4,400 ft meadow
North to the 5,950 ft notch in the SE ridge
Skirt cliffs west of the gate, avoid the gullies
~200 ft scramble to a shallow hanging basin
East side of the ridge to the cairned summit

climate.spring: Deep lingering snowpack on approach and summit slopes into June; avalanche and postholing hazard
climate.summer: Warm, mostly dry; the best climbing window

access.landManager: Olympic National Forest (Hood Canal Ranger District) for the Putvin Trail approach; Olympic National Park (Mount Skokomish/Olympic Wilderness) above the park boundary near Lake of the Angels
access.fees: The Forest Service's own Putvin Trailhead page states no fee is required; some third-party trail guides list a Northwest Forest Pass as required for the trailhead — sources conflict.
access.permit: Wilderness permit required for overnight camping at Lake of the Angels, via the Olympic National Park Wilderness Information Center
access.closures: Firearms, pets, and campfires above 3,500 ft are prohibited once inside Olympic National Park

timing.recommendedStart: Early / pre-dawn start recommended for a long day

emergency.sheriffDispatch: Mason County Sheriff / MACECOM dispatch: (360) 426-4441 non-emergency; 911 for emergencies
emergency.rangerStation: Olympic National Park Wilderness Information Center, Port Angeles: (360) 565-3100; Olympic National Forest Hood Canal Ranger District

routes.corrections: The given peak elevation (6,612 ft) and coordinates (47.60632, -123.266607) match Wikipedia's cited 47°36'23"N 123°16'00"W and 6,612 ft figure closely; no correction needed. This route corresponds to Mountain Project's 'South Couloir' route on Mount Stone (Grade II, 4th class).

routes.routeName: Putvin Trail / Lake of the Angels Scramble
objHaz: Steep, loose, often muddy trail tread
Exposed handline/headwall scramble section around mile 2.8
Lingering snow into early summer on upper trail sections
routes.overview: A shorter, non-technical objective than the full Mount Stone summit climb: the popular Putvin Trail hike-and-scramble up to the alpine cirque of Lake of the Angels itself, without continuing to Stone's true summit. Very steep, partly primitive trail with one exposed handline section.
routes.beta: The Putvin Trail (#813) is rated 'Hard'/'Difficult' by WTA and Hiking Project. It climbs steeply from the Hamma Hamma valley floor through forest, gaining roughly 3,300-3,400 ft in under 4 miles, passing the marshy 'Pond of the False Prophet' in the 'Valley of Heaven' before a short, near-vertical headwall section (fixed handline) around mile 2.8, then enters Olympic National Park and opens into the rocky alpine cirque holding Lake of the Angels (high point ~4,914-4,950 ft).
routes.approach: Drive US 101 to Hamma Hamma River Road (Forest Road 25) at milepost 318 (about 14 miles north of Hoodsport). Follow FR 25 west; paved for roughly 7.5-8 miles to the Lena Lake Trailhead, then gravel for another ~4.3-4.5 miles to the Putvin Trailhead pullout (roadside parking, ~30 cars, ~1,600 ft). The Putvin Trail begins across the road on the north side.
routes.descent: Reverse the Putvin Trail; use extra caution at the handline/headwall section, which many hikers find more difficult descending than ascending.
routes.descentText: From Lake of the Angels, drop back through the headwall climbers' path, then down the steep, primitive Putvin Trail past the Pond of the False Prophet in the 'Valley of Heaven,' watching footing on the exposed handline/headwall section near mile 2.8, back to the Putvin Trailhead.
routes.itinerary: Popular as a long day hike (~7 miles round trip) or an overnight backpack with a permitted campsite at Lake of the Angels.
routes.bail: Retreat is simply reversing the trail at any point; the only technical difficulty is the handline headwall section near mile 2.8.
routes.turnaround: Given the steep, loose, and partly exposed trail, plan to be off the headwall section well before dark, and turn back if thunderstorms threaten given the exposed rock sections.
routes.bestSeason: June through October per Washington Trails Association, though the trail typically still holds snow into June and the basin often keeps patches into July.
whatToBring: Awareness of bear activity in the basin
Water treatment (Lake of the Angels / Pond of the False Prophet)
proTips: The trail is unsigned and easy to lose in spots — carry a GPS track.
Camping is available at Lake of the Angels but sites are limited and popular; go midweek if possible.
watchOut: The headwall section near mile 2.8 requires a near-vertical scramble with a fixed handline — not for those uncomfortable with exposure.
The trail is steep enough that it is rated among the harder maintained-adjacent trails in the Olympics.
knownHazards: Steep, loose, and often muddy tread on the Putvin Trail.
Exposed handline/headwall scramble section around mile 2.8.
Lingering snow into early summer on upper trail sections.

pitchDetail.notes: Trail gains nearly 3,000 ft in under 4 mi overall
~mile 2.8, slick rock

routes.corrections: The two Putvin-Trail-based entries on this peak appear to describe two related but distinct objectives sharing the same trailhead and lower approach: this entry matches the widely-documented standalone hike/scramble to the Lake of the Angels basin (~7 mi RT, ~3,280-3,400 ft gain, high point ~4,900-4,950 ft, per WTA/Hiking Project), while 'South Route via Lake of the Angels' matches Mountain Project's technical 'South Couloir' line that continues from the lake to Mount Stone's true 6,612 ft summit (~10 mi RT, ~5,000+ ft gain, class 3-4). No source describes them as two separate summit routes.

routes.routeName: Lena Lake to Mt Stone traverse
objHaz: Extended cross-country/way-trail travel with navigation risk in fog
Steep snow on St. Peter's Ramp
Cliff-band descent sections on the Putvin Trail (handline, tree-root handholds)
Loose pillow-basalt rock on the Mount Stone summit side-trip
routes.season: Best Jul-Sep once the high traverse clears of persistent snow
routes.aspect: SE approach (Lena Lake side) transitioning to S/SW on Mt Stone's west ridge
routes.face: West Ridge (summit side-trip); full traverse crosses Mt Stone's SE flank via St. Peter's Gate
routes.overview: A demanding 3-day, ~15-mile point-to-point alpine traverse across Mount Skokomish Wilderness backcountry, linking Lena Lake, Scout Lake/Basin, St. Peter's Gate, and Lake of the Angels, with an optional side-trip to Mount Stone's summit, finishing down the Putvin Trail to a different trailhead than the one you started from.
routes.beta: Day 1 follows the well-graded Lena Lake Trail 3 miles to Lena Lake, then a steep, primitive trail up West Fork Lena Creek to Upper Lena Lake (6.5 mi, 4,000 ft gain from the trailhead). Day 2 leaves the trail entirely: climb southwest across meadows/heather toward the ridge south of Mt Lena, follow intermittent way trails west past Scout Lake, over Scout Pass (5,200 ft) and Upper Scout Lake/Scout Basin, over Ullin's Pass (5,000 ft, with views of the 'Ullin's Bathtubs' tarns), then up the steep snow/dirt 'St. Peter's Ramp' to St. Peter's Gate (5,950 ft) — the high point of the traverse and an obvious notch in Mt Stone's SE ridge. Lake of the Angels lies half a mile southwest of the gate in a grassy bowl. The optional Mt Stone summit side-trip climbs a class 3-4 gully on the mountain's south face to a rock bowl, joins the standard west-ridge route, and finishes with a class 2-3 scramble on solid pillow basalt to the summit (about 1,100 ft gain round trip, ~3 hrs). Day 3 descends the Putvin Trail — historic and primitive, with cliff sections (one with a fixed handline, one requiring tree roots) past Whitehorse Basin and the Pond of the False Prophet — to the Putvin Trailhead, about 4 miles by road from the Lena Lake Trailhead where the trip began.
routes.approach: Start at the Lena Lake Trailhead on Hamma Hamma River Road (~750 ft), reached via US 101 to Hamma Hamma Road (FR 25) at Hoodsport, then west on the paved section of FR 25. The trip finishes at the Putvin Trailhead about 4 miles further up (west on) the same road; a car shuttle or road walk between trailheads is required.
routes.descent: From Lake of the Angels, descend the Putvin Trail back to the Putvin Trailhead — a different trailhead than the Lena Lake start, requiring a shuttle.
routes.descentText: The Putvin Trail from Lake of the Angels drops through the alpine basin past Whitehorse Basin, then descends steeply through cliff-band sections — one equipped with a handline, another requiring careful use of tree roots for handholds — and past the Pond of the False Prophet in the 'Valley of Heaven,' losing about 100 ft net over the final 3.3 miles to the Putvin Trailhead on Hamma Hamma Road.
routes.itinerary: Documented 3-day itinerary: Day 1 — Lena Lake TH to Upper Lena Lake (6.5 mi, 4,000 ft gain, ~4.3 hrs); Day 2 — Upper Lena Lake over Scout Pass/Ullin's Pass/St. Peter's Gate to Lake of the Angels with the Mount Stone summit side-trip (5.3 mi plus side-trip, 3,300 ft gain, ~9.8 hrs total including the summit); Day 3 — Lake of the Angels down the Putvin Trail to the Putvin Trailhead (3.3 mi, ~100 ft loss, ~3 hrs).
routes.bail: Once past Upper Lena Lake there is no maintained trail and no easy bailout until St. Peter's Gate/Lake of the Angels, where the Putvin Trail is the only real descent option; parties should be prepared to complete the full traverse to Lake of the Angels once committed past Scout Basin.
routes.turnaround: Given the long, remote, way-trail nature of Day 2 and poor-visibility navigation risk near St. Peter's Gate, set a firm turnaround for the Mount Stone summit side-trip that still allows reaching Lake of the Angels and making camp well before dark.
routes.bestSeason: Best in late summer/early fall (the documented trip was mid-September) once persistent snow on the high traverse and St. Peter's Ramp has melted or consolidated; earlier-season attempts should expect full snow cover on the Scout Basin-to-St. Peter's Gate section.
routes.detailedRack: No rope typically carried; ice axe and crampons are the main technical items and are used minimally outside of early-season snow.
routes.proNeeds: None beyond an ice axe for occasional steep snow; this is a cross-country/scrambling traverse, not a technical roped climb.
whatToBring: Wilderness permits for Upper Lena Lake and Lake of the Angels camps
Navigation aids for foggy, way-trail sections
proTips: Way trails come and go across Scout Basin — carry a GPS track, since fog on this traverse has caused real navigation trouble for parties.
Stash overnight packs about 300 ft below St. Peter's Gate at the base of a basalt cliff before doing the Mount Stone summit side-trip, to climb it faster and lighter.
A car shuttle (or a ~4-mile road walk) is required between the Lena Lake and Putvin trailheads.
watchOut: Cross-country travel from Upper Lena Lake to St. Peter's Gate has no maintained trail and limited reference points in fog.
The Putvin Trail descent has cliff sections requiring a fixed handline and, at another point, tree roots as handholds.
St. Peter's Ramp is steep snow/dirt and can be icy or loose depending on conditions.
knownHazards: Extended cross-country/way-trail travel with real navigation risk in fog, particularly around Scout Basin and St. Peter's Gate.
Steep snow on St. Peter's Ramp.
Cliff-band descent sections on the Putvin Trail requiring a handline and tree-root handholds.
Loose pillow-basalt rock on the Mount Stone summit side-trip.

climate.summer: Warm, mostly dry; the best traverse window
climate.fall: Cooling temperatures, increasing storm/fog frequency by October
climate.winter: Heavy snow; not a winter objective

access.closures: Firearms, pets, and campfires above 3,500 ft prohibited within Olympic National Park

emergency.sheriffDispatch: Mason County Sheriff / MACECOM dispatch: (360) 426-4441 non-emergency; 911 for emergencies

emergency.nearestHospital: Mason General Hospital, Shelton, WA
emergency.notes: Cell coverage is unreliable to absent through most of the traverse; this is a remote, mostly off-trail route with limited rescue access.

routes.corrections: Mountain Project describes this as a '16-mile loop' with 'nearly 9,000 ft' of gain, but the only detailed trip report found (Jim Brisbine/trailcatjim.com) documents it as a 15.1-mile point-to-point through-hike (Lena Lake TH to Putvin TH, different trailheads requiring a shuttle) with 7,400 ft gain / 6,550 ft loss over 3 days; the discrepancy likely reflects Mountain Project measuring a loop that road-walks back to the start. gainFt/lossFt/distKm here follow the detailed point-to-point report.

blurb: Mount Rainier is a 14,406-ft andesite/dacite stratovolcano in the Cascade Range southeast of Seattle-Tacoma, the most heavily glaciated peak in the contiguous US (25 major glaciers) and the centerpiece of Mount Rainier National Park. Its documented first ascent was by Hazard Stevens and Philemon Van Trump on August 17, 1870 via Gibraltar Ledges (an earlier 1852 ascent is claimed but unverified); the mountain's steep icefalls, seracs, and rapid weather changes make it one of the most consequential glacier-mountaineering objectives in North America. Note: a 2024 resurvey suggests the Southwest Rim, not the historical Columbia Crest ice dome, may now be the true high point as the crater ice has thinned — official USGS/NPS elevation is still generally cited as 14,406-14,411 ft depending on source/survey vintage.

routes.overview: The technical crux of the Kautz Glacier line: a roughly 300-ft alpine-ice headwall near 12,000 ft, split into two rope-length pitches up to about 50 degrees, just below Camp Hazard.
routes.beta: Climbed as part of the broader Kautz Glacier route rather than as an independent summit line. After crossing the Nisqually and Wilson Glaciers and gaining the 'turtle' snowfield, parties reach the ice headwall around 11,500-12,000 ft: roughly 200 ft of low-angle ice followed by a steeper ~300-ft, two-pitch section (35-50 degrees) that can be climbed with a 60m rope and a handful of screws.
routes.approach: From Paradise (5,400 ft) take the paved trail to Glacier Vista, drop off-trail west to cross the Nisqually Glacier, then climb a boulder-filled gully to the left side of the Wilson Glacier and up rolling snowfields to a turtle-shaped snowfield (~9,000 ft, lower camp) and rock camp near 11,000 ft (upper camp / Camp Hazard vicinity).
routes.descent: Continue to the false summit then the true summit and descend via the Disappointment Cleaver or Ingraham Direct route; downclimbing the headwall is an option in good conditions but exposes the party to the Kautz Ice Cliff for longer.
routes.descentText: Most parties climbing the headwall are already committed to the summit; from the crater rim, descend via Disappointment Cleaver or Ingraham Direct to Camp Muir and Paradise rather than reversing the icefall-exposed Kautz side.
routes.bail: Below the headwall, retreat is a straightforward reversal of the approach; above it, the safest option is usually to continue to the summit and descend the DC/Ingraham side rather than reverse through the icefall zone.
routes.turnaround: Turn around before the ice pitches soften in full sun or if icefall activity is heard/observed from the Kautz Ice Cliff above — minimize time spent directly beneath it.
routes.bestSeason: June through August, once the ice headwall is well filled in but before it turns to bare, rockfall-prone ice late season.
routes.iceGrade: AI2 (occasionally rated harder depending on conditions)
routes.face: South (Kautz Glacier headwall below Camp Hazard)
routes.detailedRack: A handful of ice screws (roughly 6-8) for the two pitches of the headwall, 2-3 pickets for the surrounding snowfields, two ice tools per climber, and standard crevasse-rescue kit (prusiks or mechanical ascenders, pulley, cordelette).
routes.proNeeds: Screw placements protect the two ice pitches; pickets suffice on the moderate snow above and below.
proTips: A 60m rope lets you link the low-angle first 200 ft with minimal gear before belaying the steeper upper pitch
Start early to be through the headwall and past Camp Hazard before the ice softens
watchOut: This name is often used interchangeably with the ice-climbing crux of the Kautz Glacier route rather than a fully separate summit line — treat route facts (approach, camps, descent) as shared with Kautz Glacier
Camp Hazard earned its name from serac fall off the Kautz Ice Cliff — minimize time there
knownHazards: Icefall from the Kautz Ice Cliff directly above the headwall/Camp Hazard
Rockfall in the approach gully off Glacier Vista
Crevasse hazard crossing the Nisqually and Wilson Glaciers

pitchDetail.notes: ~200 ft, low-angle, minimal screws needed
~300 ft main headwall, climbable in two pitches with a 60m rope

road.name: Paradise-Longmire Road (SR 706, Nisqually entrance)
road.status: Paved, plowed to Paradise year-round
road.driveNote: Enter via the Nisqually (southwest) entrance and drive to the Paradise upper parking lot trailhead (5,400 ft).

climate.forecastZone: NWAC / Mount Rainier alpine forecast zone
climate.typical: Exposed south-side glacier terrain fully open to Pacific frontal weather; conditions can change from clear to whiteout within hours.
climate.spring: Deep snowpack still covers crevasses; cold, ice pitches often firm.
climate.summer: Primary climbing season; ice can be bare and rockfall risk rises as snow cover retreats.
climate.fall: Route quickly deteriorates as snow bridges fail and ice becomes brittle/rockfall-prone.
climate.winter: Not a standard winter objective due to serac and avalanche hazard.

access.landManager: National Park Service - Mount Rainier National Park
access.fees: $82 per-person annual climbing cost-recovery fee (2026) for all travel above 10,000 ft or on any glacier, plus park entrance fee.
access.permit: Climbing/wilderness permit via Recreation.gov; must register in person at a ranger station/Wilderness Information Center
access.passRequired: Mount Rainier annual climbing fee (not covered by America the Beautiful pass)

timing.recommendedStart: Pre-dawn (roughly midnight-2am) alpine start from high camp to be off the headwall before the ice softens

emergency.sheriffDispatch: After-hours: Lewis County Sheriff (360) 740-1105, then prompt 0
emergency.rangerStation: Mount Rainier NP Dispatch (360) 569-2211 ext. 0 (5:30am-11pm summer hours); Wilderness Information Center (360) 569-6641
emergency.notes: Climbers registered above 10,000 ft who are overdue trigger an automatic NPS search; the park runs its own high-altitude SAR team.

routes.corrections: 'Kautz Headwall' is documented in primary sources as the named ice-climbing crux pitch of the Kautz Glacier route (near Camp Hazard, ~12,000 ft) rather than a fully independent summit line with its own approach/descent — data here largely mirrors the Kautz Glacier route.

routes.overview: A classic mixed line of terraced buttresses and snow/ice ramps (not a true knife-edge) up Rainier's north side between the Mowich and Carbon drainages, with sustained 40-55 degree climbing and superb position — a Grade IV alternative to Liberty Ridge.
routes.beta: From either the Mowich Lake or White River side, parties gain the lower ridge around 9,300-9,800 ft on the Mowich Glacier, then follow terraced ledges and buttresses rather than a continuous crest, climbing progressively steeper snow/ice (40-55 deg) with mixed rock sections, to join the upper mountain near Liberty Cap.
routes.approach: Option 1 (Mowich Lake side): ~3 miles via the Wonderland Trail to Spray Park, then a climbers' path up lower Ptarmigan Ridge (subject to the current SR165/Fairfax Bridge closure blocking vehicle access to Mowich Lake). Option 2 (White River side): Glacier Basin Trail ~4 miles to St. Elmo Pass (7,400 ft), then traverse the Winthrop and Carbon Glaciers to reach the ridge.
routes.descent: Standard descent via the Emmons-Winthrop Glacier route to Camp Schurman.
routes.descentText: From the summit plateau, descend past Liberty Cap onto the upper Emmons-Winthrop route, following wands/the boot track down through the Corridor to Camp Schurman, then out via St. Elmo Pass and Glacier Basin to White River.
routes.bail: Below the steepest sections, retreat down the approach glaciers is possible in good visibility; once committed above the terraced buttresses, continuing over the top to descend via Emmons-Winthrop is usually safer than reversing.
routes.turnaround: Turn back before deteriorating weather or softening snow makes the terraced rock/snow transitions unsafe to downclimb — the remote north-side location means self-rescue is the primary option.
routes.bestSeason: Route conditions are typically excellent by May; the Mowich Lake road historically doesn't open until early July, and access via that side is currently blocked indefinitely by the SR165 Fairfax Bridge closure outside the park.
routes.face: North side, between the Mowich and Carbon Glacier drainages
routes.detailedRack: Early season: 3-4 pickets often suffice. Later in the season expect to need ice screws, a light pin rack, and a few small cams for verglassed rock on the terraced sections.
routes.proNeeds: Highly conditions-dependent, from almost no gear needed on solid early-season snow to a fuller ice/mixed rack late season.
gear: Light rock rack (cams/pitons) for late-season rock
whatToBring: Bivy gear for a multi-day ascent
proTips: Camp high near Liberty Cap or Columbia Crest to set up an efficient, low-risk summit day
The White River/St. Elmo Pass approach avoids depending on the Mowich Lake road
watchOut: Route character swings hugely with season — early-summer snow vs. late-season verglas are very different climbs
Mowich Lake Rd has no vehicle access as of 2026 due to the SR165 Fairfax Bridge closure outside the park; verify current status before planning that approach
knownHazards: Crevasse crossings on the Mowich, Winthrop, and Carbon Glacier approaches
Cornices along the ridge crest
Rockfall/icefall on the terraced rock bands, worse late season

road.name: Mowich Lake Road (SR 165) or White River Road
road.status: Mowich Lake Rd: no current vehicle access (SR165 Fairfax Bridge closed indefinitely outside the park); White River Rd is the seasonal NE-side alternative.
road.seasonalGate: Typically opens ~July, closes mid-October
road.driveNote: Check the NPS road-status page before choosing an approach side.

climate.forecastZone: NWAC / Mount Rainier alpine forecast zone
climate.typical: Ridge sits above 9,000 ft, fully exposed to Pacific frontal systems with rapid weather changes and whiteout potential.
climate.spring: Best snow conditions typically by May.
climate.summer: Rock bands melt out and become more exposed/loose as the season progresses.
climate.fall: Route generally not recommended; crevasse bridges deteriorate and rock is bare.
climate.winter: Not a standard objective.

access.landManager: National Park Service - Mount Rainier National Park

access.passRequired: Mount Rainier annual climbing fee

timing.recommendedStart: Multi-day approach with a pre-dawn summit push from a high camp near Liberty Cap

emergency.sheriffDispatch: After-hours: Lewis County Sheriff (360) 740-1105, then prompt 0
emergency.rangerStation: Mount Rainier NP Dispatch (360) 569-2211 ext. 0; Wilderness Information Center (360) 569-6641
emergency.notes: Remote north-side route — self-rescue and satellite communication are strongly advised given the multi-hour response time to this side of the mountain.

routes.overview: A direct line up the 45-degree headwall of the Ingraham Glacier from Camp Muir, functioning as the winter/early-season standard route and a more direct alternative to the Disappointment Cleaver.
routes.beta: From Camp Muir, cross to the Ingraham Glacier via Cathedral Gap (or the steeper Cadaver Gap) and rope up. The route ascends the glacier's 45-degree headwall on a switchbacked boot track (easing the effective angle to 35-40 degrees), reaching Ingraham Flats (~11,100 ft), then trends east above Disappointment Cleaver to avoid a serac band near 12,500 ft before low-angle switchbacks lead to the crater rim (~14,100 ft) and a 20-30 minute walk to the summit.
routes.approach: Standard Paradise (5,400 ft) to Camp Muir (10,080 ft) approach via the Skyline Trail and Muir Snowfield, same as the Disappointment Cleaver route.
routes.descent: Descend the same route.
routes.descentText: Reverse the ascent line back down the Ingraham Glacier headwall to Ingraham Flats and Camp Muir, staying east of the serac band near 12,500 ft; continue down the Muir Snowfield and Skyline Trail to Paradise.
routes.bail: Retreat is straightforward down the same glacier track to Camp Muir in reasonable visibility; in whiteout, a GPS track or wands are recommended on the featureless Muir Snowfield below.
routes.turnaround: Turn back before the serac band near 12,500 ft becomes active in full sun, and before deteriorating weather removes visibility on the crevassed upper glacier.
routes.bestSeason: This is the standard route during winter and early season (roughly December through end of May); by mid-summer the Disappointment Cleaver route typically becomes the more established track.
routes.detailedRack: Standard glacier-travel rack: pickets and ice screws carried 'just in case' along with crevasse-rescue gear; the boot track is usually well established so heavy protection is rarely placed.
routes.proNeeds: Pickets/screws as needed for crevasse crossings; route is typically climbed on an established boot track.
proTips: Serving as the de facto winter standard route, Ingraham Direct sees a well-packed track in season — but conditions and the exact line through the crevasses change year to year
watchOut: Huge seracs on the west side of the Ingraham Glacier near 12,500 ft pose ongoing icefall hazard; the route trends east above Disappointment Cleaver specifically to avoid them
knownHazards: Serac band around 12,500 ft with a history of large ice blocks crossing the route
Extensive crevasse fields, especially near 13,000 ft
Altitude and rapid weather changes

road.status: Paved, plowed to Paradise year-round
road.driveNote: Enter via the Nisqually (southwest) entrance to the Paradise upper lot.

climate.winter: Primary season for this route; storms can be severe and approach conditions icy.

routes.overview: A historic one-off line pioneered straight up the broken, heavily crevassed Nisqually Icefall on Rainier's southwest flank; not an established modern route.
routes.beta: Ten hours of sustained, exhausting ice work through the icefall's breakups and seracs, following a roughly 500-ft, ~40-degree approach slope into the fractured glacier above.
routes.approach: Approached from the Nisqually Glacier below Glacier Vista/the Kautz-Fuhrer Finger approach area, on Rainier's southwest side.
routes.bestSeason: No established season — the route has not become a standard climb since its 1948 first ascent.
watchOut: A large avalanche swept across the first-ascent party's path within 24 hours of their climb, and surveyors afterward strongly recommended against using the icefall as a regular route to the summit
knownHazards: Active seracs and icefall throughout the broken glacier
Documented avalanche path directly over the climbing line
Extremely complex, shifting crevasse terrain

road.driveNote: Enter via the Nisqually (southwest) entrance to Paradise.
