routes.fa: Bob Craig and party, July 15, 1948
routes.corrections: Sources describe this as a historic first-ascent line, not a maintained or repeated modern route; a large avalanche crossed the exact path within 24 hours of the 1948 first ascent, and the route was subsequently discouraged as a climbing objective. Treat quantitative fields as sparse/historical rather than current route beta.

routes.overview: An 8-ft boulder with a distinctive 'bread loaf'-shaped top hold, located along the Silver Falls Loop Trail near Ohanapecosh Campground — a bouldering problem, not an alpine summit route on Mount Rainier.
routes.beta: A short V0 problem: dyno sit-start or a techy stand-start up the left side of the boulder's face to the loaf-shaped top hold.
routes.approach: About 1 mile in (clockwise) on the Silver Falls Loop Trail from Ohanapecosh Campground, directly adjacent to the trail.
routes.descent: Down-climb or hop off; a crash pad is typically used for landings.
routes.bestSeason: Accessible whenever Ohanapecosh Campground/the Stevens Canyon Road corridor is open, roughly late spring through fall.
proTips: A good warm-up boulder for parties camped at Ohanapecosh
watchOut: This is a low-elevation bouldering problem at a front-country campground, unrelated to Mount Rainier's alpine summit routes

road.name: Stevens Canyon Road / SR 123 (Ohanapecosh entrance)
road.status: Seasonal, typically open late May through fall
road.seasonalGate: Closed in winter
road.driveNote: Access via the Ohanapecosh/Stevens Canyon (southeast) entrance.

access.landManager: National Park Service - Mount Rainier National Park
access.fees: Standard park entrance fee; no separate climbing permit required for bouldering.

emergency.sheriffDispatch: After-hours: Lewis County Sheriff (360) 740-1105, then prompt 0
emergency.rangerStation: Ohanapecosh Visitor Center / Mount Rainier NP Dispatch (360) 569-2211 ext. 0

routes.fa: Patricia P., July 22, 2020 (per Mountain Project route log)
routes.corrections: This entry is documented in primary sources as a V0 boulder problem at Ohanapecosh Campground (Silver Falls Loop Trail), not an alpine route up Mount Rainier itself. It is included here per the given route list but is a fundamentally different type of climb (low-elevation front-country bouldering) than the other 17 summit-oriented routes on this peak.

routes.overview: A committing, rarely-climbed Grade IV/V line up the ridge separating the Willis Wall from the Carbon Glacier, technically harder, more confusing, and more serious than either Liberty Ridge or Ptarmigan Ridge nearby.
routes.beta: From the 10,300-ft ridge point where technical climbing begins, the route follows steep snow/ice (up to 55 deg) and short rock steps (4th class to low 5th, historically including A2 on the original line) to gain the upper mountain near 13,800 ft, with a mandatory ~70-ft rappel encountered along the way.
routes.approach: From White River Campground, follow the trail to Glacier Basin, then continue straight (rather than turning toward the Inter Glacier) to St. Elmo Pass. Cross the Winthrop Glacier, diverge from the Liberty Ridge approach around 7,200 ft, and ascend Curtis Ridge, trending left to the 10,300-ft point where technical climbing starts.
routes.descent: Descend via the Emmons Glacier route to Camp Schurman, either continuing over the summit or traversing left to join the standard descent.
routes.descentText: From the top of the technical section, join the upper Emmons-Winthrop route and descend through the Corridor to Camp Schurman, then out via St. Elmo Pass to White River.
routes.bail: Above the 10,300-ft technical start, retreat is difficult — a mandatory rappel makes reversing costly once the rope is pulled; continuing to join the Emmons-Winthrop descent is usually the safer option.
routes.turnaround: Given the rockfall-prone nature of the route, plan to be through the technical rock bands during cold, stable conditions and turn back if warming temperatures begin loosening rock overhead.
routes.bestSeason: Cold conditions are strongly preferred to minimize rockfall; a narrow early-summer window when the route is still well-frozen is typical.
routes.rappels: 1 mandatory ~70-ft rappel
routes.face: North side, between Willis Wall and the Carbon Glacier
routes.detailedRack: A light alpine rack: a couple of pitons, a few nuts, and about 4 cams, plus cord for the mandatory rappel station.
routes.proNeeds: Light trad rack for the rock steps; ice screws/pickets for the snow-and-ice sections.
gear: Light alpine rock rack (pitons, nuts, 4 cams)
whatToBring: Bivy gear for a multi-day ascent
proTips: Climb in cold, stable conditions — many parties consider this a 'rockfall death route' in warm weather
Confirm the rappel anchor before committing, since pulling the rope removes the option to reverse
watchOut: More confusing route-finding and more committing than neighboring Liberty Ridge or Ptarmigan Ridge
Multiple rock bands and cliff sections present ongoing rockfall danger
knownHazards: Rockfall through multiple rock bands, worsening in warm conditions
A mandatory 70-ft rappel with no easy reversal
Avalanche terrain on the approach slopes

road.name: White River Road (SR 410 corridor)
road.status: Open seasonally
road.seasonalGate: Typically opens late May/June, closes mid-October
road.driveNote: Access via the White River (northeast) entrance to White River Campground.

climate.forecastZone: NWAC / Mount Rainier alpine forecast zone
climate.typical: North-side terrain stays colder and holds ice/snow longer than the south side, which is part of why cold conditions are sought here.
climate.summer: Narrow early-summer window preferred for frozen rock.

access.fees: $82 per-person annual climbing cost-recovery fee (2026), plus park entrance fee.
access.permit: Climbing/wilderness permit via Recreation.gov.
access.passRequired: Mount Rainier annual climbing fee

emergency.sheriffDispatch: After-hours: Lewis County Sheriff (360) 740-1105, then prompt 0
emergency.rangerStation: Mount Rainier NP Dispatch (360) 569-2211 ext. 0; Wilderness Information Center (360) 569-6641
emergency.notes: Remote, seldom-climbed line — self-rescue capability and a satellite communicator are strongly advised.

routes.fa: Gene Prater and Marcel Schuster (year not documented in available sources)

routes.overview: One of North America's classic alpine climbs — a sustained ridge of steep snow and ice splitting the Willis and Liberty Walls on Rainier's North Face, listed among the '50 Classic Climbs of North America.'
routes.beta: From Thumb Rock (10,500 ft), stay on or near the ridge crest, climbing progressively steeper slopes (up to 50-55 degrees) through several pitches of 40-60-degree ice, negotiating the bergschrund near 13,500 ft to reach the Liberty Cap Glacier and the 14,112-ft Liberty Cap summit.
routes.approach: From White River Campground, follow the Glacier Basin Trail; around 6,700 ft break right off the trail and ascend snow slopes to St. Elmo Pass (7,400 ft). Cross the lower Winthrop Glacier toward Curtis Ridge, then access the Carbon Glacier around 7,300 ft, where bivy sites are available before continuing to Thumb Rock.
routes.descent: Most parties descend via the Emmons-Winthrop route to Camp Schurman, then traverse the Interglacier back to the White River trailhead.
routes.descentText: From Liberty Cap, traverse to the main summit area if desired, then descend the Emmons-Winthrop glacier route via the Corridor to Camp Schurman; from there, descend through St. Elmo Pass and the Interglacier back to Glacier Basin and White River Campground.
routes.bail: Below Thumb Rock, retreat down the Carbon Glacier/St. Elmo Pass approach is possible; above Thumb Rock the route is largely committing, with descent typically requiring completion of the climb and descent via Emmons-Winthrop.
routes.turnaround: Because the ridge becomes progressively harder to reverse once above Thumb Rock, commit only with a stable multi-day forecast and be prepared to continue over the top rather than retreat from high on the route.
routes.bestSeason: Spring through early summer, for firm snow conditions and stable crevasse bridges; the route becomes increasingly rockfall- and icefall-prone as the season progresses.
routes.face: North Face, ridge between Willis Wall and Liberty Wall
routes.detailedRack: 4-6 ice screws and a few pickets covers the technical ice pitches and bergschrund crossing; a light rock rack is sometimes carried for the Thumb Rock area.
routes.proNeeds: Screws and pickets for the steep snow/ice sections and bergschrund; minimal rock protection needed.
whatToBring: Bivy gear for Thumb Rock camp
Extra fuel/food for a 2-3 day ascent
proTips: Thumb Rock (10,500 ft) is the standard, relatively protected bivy before the technical ridge
Move fast through the Carbon Glacier approach, which is exposed to icefall from the surrounding walls
watchOut: Reputation as the hardest and most dangerous of Rainier's 'standard' technical routes due to commitment, remoteness, and sustained steep angle
The approach across the Carbon Glacier and beneath Liberty Wall carries real icefall exposure
knownHazards: Icefall/serac hazard on the Carbon Glacier approach beneath Liberty Wall
Sustained 40-60 degree ice pitches high on the ridge
Bergschrund crossing near 13,500 ft
Rockfall on the lower ridge in warm conditions

pitchDetail.notes: Progressively steeper slopes on/near the ridge crest
Several pitches of steep alpine ice
Crux crossing onto the Liberty Cap Glacier

climate.summer: Increasing rockfall/icefall risk as the route dries out.
climate.fall: Not typically climbed.

routes.fa: Ome Daiber, Arnie Campbell, and Jim Burrow, 1935

routes.overview: The standard route on Rainier's northeast side, a long, mostly non-technical glacier ascent that is the second most-climbed line on the mountain and among the least technically demanding.
routes.beta: From Camp Schurman (9,440 ft), navigate through longitudinal crevasses onto Emmons Flat, then up a 1,500-ft snowy ramp called the Corridor (roughly 10,000-11,200 ft), which threads between seracs, icefalls, and crevasses. Above the Corridor, the route steepens and trends right to a bergschrund crossing before easier upper-mountain terrain leads to the summit.
routes.approach: From White River Campground (4,320 ft), follow the Glacier Basin Trail to just below the Interglacier, cross it, and continue to Camp Curtis (8,500 ft) on the ridge above St. Elmo Pass, then descend slightly onto the Emmons Glacier to Camp Schurman (9,440 ft) at Steamboat Prow.
routes.descent: Descend the same route back to Camp Schurman and out via the Interglacier and Glacier Basin Trail.
routes.descentText: Reverse the Corridor and Emmons Flat back to Camp Schurman, then descend via Camp Curtis, the Interglacier, and Glacier Basin Trail to White River Campground.
routes.bail: Retreat to Camp Schurman is generally straightforward in reasonable visibility; the featureless upper Corridor can be disorienting in whiteout, so wands or GPS tracking are recommended.
routes.turnaround: Turn back before crevasse bridges become unreliable in the heat of the day, and well before any approaching frontal weather removes visibility on the open glacier.
routes.bestSeason: Late spring through summer; most parties take 3-4 days including a rest day and a full descent day.
routes.face: Northeast side, Emmons and Winthrop Glaciers
routes.detailedRack: Standard glacier-travel kit: pickets and snow protection as needed for crevasse crossings, full crevasse-rescue rigging per rope team.
routes.proNeeds: Pickets/snow anchors for crevasse and bergschrund protection; technical ice gear rarely needed.
whatToBring: Sun protection for the exposed glacier
proTips: Camp Schurman has a ranger hut and outhouse and can accommodate roughly 50 climbers a night — arrive with a backup camp plan in busy periods
The Corridor is the safest line through the icefall/serac zone — stay on the established track
watchOut: Longitudinal crevasses require careful route-finding through Emmons Flat
Bergschrund crossing above the Corridor can be tricky depending on the year's conditions
knownHazards: Crevasse fields on the Interglacier and Emmons Glacier
Seracs and icefall bordering the Corridor
Bergschrund crossing near the top of the Corridor

routes.fa: J. W. Fobes, G. James, and R. O. Wells, 1884 (early ascent via this general line)

routes.overview: A remote north-side line up the North Mowich Glacier headwall to Liberty Cap, featuring a steep rock/ice hourglass entry followed by roughly 2,000 ft of ~45-degree snow to a rock band and the summit ice cap.
routes.beta: From a bivy around 10,000 ft, ascend a steep hourglass section of mixed rock and ice (40-50 ft), then climb roughly 2,000 ft of ~45-degree snow to the base of a rock band; clearing the band can require 5th-class climbing depending on conditions, after which an easy walk across the ice cap leads to Liberty Cap.
routes.approach: Traditionally approached via Carbon Creek and Spray Park, though the current preferred approach is from Mowich Lake (subject to the ongoing SR165/Fairfax Bridge closure), ascending the North Mowich Glacier to a bivy around 10,000 ft; typically planned as a two-day objective.
routes.descent: Standard descent via the Emmons-Winthrop Glacier route to Camp Schurman, or the Disappointment Cleaver route to Camp Muir.
routes.descentText: From Liberty Cap, cross to the main summit area or descend directly via Emmons-Winthrop to Camp Schurman.
routes.bail: Below the rock band, retreat down the North Mowich Glacier is possible; above it, continuing over the top is generally the practical option.
routes.turnaround: Given the lack-of-snow-year variability noted for the rock band, be prepared to turn back if the band is bare and looks unstable rather than forcing 5th-class climbing in mountaineering boots.
routes.bestSeason: Early-to-mid summer, when the hourglass and rock band still hold enough snow/ice to ease the crux sections.
routes.face: Northwest side, North Mowich Glacier headwall below Liberty Cap
routes.detailedRack: Screws, pickets, and a light rock rack for the hourglass entry and rock band.
routes.proNeeds: Mixed protection — ice screws for the entry hourglass, pickets on the snow slope, a few rock pieces for the upper band.
proTips: A great, varied climb with 'a bit of everything' — mixed, snow, and rock in one line
Plan as a two-day objective with a bivy on the North Mowich Glacier
watchOut: Remote northwest-side location requires full self-sufficiency
Rock band difficulty varies significantly by season/snow coverage
knownHazards: Rockfall on the entry hourglass and upper rock band
Crevasse hazard crossing the North Mowich Glacier
Long, remote approach with limited bail options

routes.fa: Mike Heath, Dan Davis, Mead Hargis, and Bill Cockerham, July 1968

routes.overview: A steep snow-and-ice face route on Rainier's northwest side above Mowich Lake, with two variations: an original Grade III line and a harder Grade IV ice/mixed variation.
routes.beta: Both variations climb the North Mowich Glacier to around 12,500 ft. The original Grade III line cuts left through an hourglass of snow, ice, and rock to a ledge and a 40-50-degree snow ramp to the top. The harder Grade IV variation cuts right instead, encountering ice steps (AI2-3) and 4-6 pitches of 50-60-degree snow leading to the Liberty Cap Glacier.
routes.approach: Via the Wonderland Trail from Mowich Lake to Spray Park; before the trail descends toward the Carbon River, take a climbers' path up the lower part of Ptarmigan Ridge, leaving it around 8,300 ft to descend ~1,000 ft to the North Mowich Glacier.
routes.descent: Carry over the summit and descend via the Emmons-Winthrop or Disappointment Cleaver route.
routes.descentText: From Liberty Cap or the main summit, descend via Emmons-Winthrop to Camp Schurman, or via Disappointment Cleaver to Camp Muir, depending on party preference and conditions.
routes.bail: Below 12,500 ft, retreat down the North Mowich Glacier is the main option; above the split, committing to one of the two variations and continuing to the top is typical.
routes.turnaround: Choose the Grade III or IV variation based on conditions at the 12,500-ft decision point, and be ready to retreat to the glacier below if neither line looks safe.
routes.bestSeason: Summer, once the North Mowich Glacier approach is passable and before the face becomes overly bare/rockfall-prone.
routes.detailedRack: A couple of ice screws for the ice steps and 2-4 pickets for the snow sections; light rock gear for the hourglass on the Grade III variation.
routes.proNeeds: Screws for the ice steps (Grade IV variation); pickets for the broader snow sections on either line.
whatToBring: Bivy gear for a multi-day ascent
Extra food/fuel for the remote approach
proTips: Decide between the Grade III and Grade IV variation at the 12,500-ft split based on current ice/snow coverage
watchOut: Remote northwest-side approach demands full self-sufficiency
Loose rock in the hourglass/rock-band sections on both variations
knownHazards: Rockfall in the hourglass and rock-band terrain
Crevasse hazard on the North Mowich Glacier approach
Icefall exposure near the glacier

pitchDetail.notes: Right-hand variation at ~12,500 ft
4-6 pitches leading to Liberty Cap Glacier

routes.fa: Dee Molenaar, Gene Prater, Jim Wickwire, and Dick Pargeter, 1966

routes.overview: A classic, moderately technical route up Rainier's south side via the Kautz Glacier, historically the standard guided route before Disappointment Cleaver took over; features a two-pitch ice headwall around 12,000 ft.
routes.beta: From Paradise, cross the Nisqually Glacier and climb to a turtle-shaped snowfield, then to a rock camp near 11,000 ft. The technical section is two ~400-ft pitches (35-40 deg, then 45-50 deg) of alpine ice near 12,000 ft, encountered near Camp Hazard beneath the Kautz Ice Cliff, above which slopes ease toward the false summit and the true summit.
routes.approach: From Paradise (5,400 ft), follow the paved trail to Glacier Vista, then a climbers' path descending west to the Nisqually Glacier; cross it and scramble a boulder-filled gully to the left side of the Wilson Glacier, following rolling snowfields to a turtle-shaped snowfield.
routes.descent: Head toward the false summit, then to the true summit; descend via Disappointment Cleaver or Ingraham Direct rather than reversing the icefall-exposed Kautz side.
routes.descentText: From the crater rim, most parties descend via the Disappointment Cleaver or Ingraham Direct routes to Camp Muir and Paradise, avoiding re-exposure to the Kautz Ice Cliff.
routes.bail: Below the ice headwall, reverse the approach; above it, continuing to the summit and descending via DC/Ingraham is generally preferred over downclimbing past Camp Hazard.
routes.turnaround: Move quickly through the Camp Hazard/ice-cliff zone and turn back if there is active icefall or if the ice pitches are softening dangerously in the sun.
routes.bestSeason: Summer, once the ice headwall is well filled in.
routes.detailedRack: A few ice screws and 1-2 pickets for the two ice pitches; standard glacier/crevasse-rescue kit otherwise.
routes.proNeeds: Screws on the two ice pitches; pickets suffice elsewhere.
proTips: The main ice headwall (~300 ft) climbs easily in two pitches with a 60m rope and a minimum of screws
Minimize time at Camp Hazard, which sits directly beneath the Kautz Ice Cliff
watchOut: Significant icefall hazard from the Kautz Ice Cliff, especially in the descent gully from Camp Hazard
Prior glacier/alpine climbing experience is expected before attempting this route
knownHazards: Icefall from the Kautz Ice Cliff near Camp Hazard
Crevasse hazard on the Nisqually and Wilson Glaciers
Rockfall in the approach gully

pitchDetail.notes: ~200 ft, moderate angle
Steepest section, ~300-400 ft, two pitches with a 60m rope

routes.fa: Hans Fuhrer, Heinie Fuhrer, Roger Toll, and Harry Myers, 1920 (first documented ascent via this line)

routes.overview: Rainier's historic first-ascent line: an exposed traverse of ledges along the south flank of Gibraltar Rock connecting to a steep chute and the upper southeast slopes, now mainly a winter/spring route due to rockfall hazard.
routes.beta: From Camp Muir, ascend the upper Cowlitz Glacier to the Cowlitz Cleaver and Gibraltar Rock's ledge system; the ledges include a short, very exposed downclimb onto a steep snow slope, then a traverse to the Gibraltar Chute, a 50-degree slope connecting to the Nisqually Glacier. Above 'Camp Comfort' saddle, rope teams continue roughly 2,000 ft to the east crater rim.
routes.approach: From Paradise, take the Skyline Trail to Pebble Creek (7,200 ft), then ascend the Muir Snowfield to Camp Muir (10,080 ft); the snowfield can be difficult to navigate in whiteout, and map/compass bearings are recommended.
routes.descent: Typically via Disappointment Cleaver or Ingraham Direct; the climbing route itself is only occasionally descended in favorable conditions.
routes.descentText: From the summit, descend via Disappointment Cleaver or Ingraham Direct to Camp Muir and back to Paradise, rather than reversing the rockfall-exposed ledges.
routes.bail: Retreat along the exposed ledges is possible but adds rockfall exposure; continuing to the summit and descending via DC/Ingraham is generally preferred once past the ledges.
routes.turnaround: This route can only really be recommended in winter/spring, when firm snow and ice cover the ledges and chute — turn back if rock is exposed and loose underfoot.
routes.bestSeason: Winter or spring, when snow/ice cover minimizes the route's significant rockfall hazard.
routes.face: South flank of Gibraltar Rock, southeast side
routes.detailedRack: Standard crevasse-rescue gear, a picket each, and helmets; some parties carry minimal rock gear though it's not essential.
routes.proNeeds: Pickets for the chute and upper slopes; rock protection rarely used.
whatToBring: Map and compass for the Muir Snowfield in poor visibility
proTips: Move quickly across the ledges and Gibraltar Chute to minimize rockfall exposure time
watchOut: Significant rockfall hazard along the ledges and in Gibraltar Chute — this is why the route is now mainly recommended only in winter/spring
Some short sections of loose 4th-class rock may be exposed depending on snow coverage
knownHazards: Rockfall along Gibraltar Rock and in Gibraltar Chute
Exposed downclimb section on the ledges
Muir Snowfield whiteout navigation hazard on the approach

routes.fa: Hazard Stevens and Philemon Van Trump, August 17, 1870 (first documented ascent of Mount Rainier, via this line)

routes.overview: A remote, moderate glacier route up the largest glacier on Rainier's west side, quiet and rarely climbed, with route-finding through significant crevasse fields as the main challenge.
routes.beta: Two approach options: early season, ascend directly up the lower Tahoma Glacier from Emerald Ridge; later in the season, follow the Puyallup Cleaver to St. Andrews Rock and drop via a ramp onto the Tahoma Glacier proper at 9,600 ft. The ascent zigzags across the glacier, trending south to ~7,200 ft, then diagonally to ~9,000 ft and again to ~10,000 ft, navigating major crevasse systems near 10,700 ft and 12,000 ft before steeper zigzagging terrain above 11,500 ft.
routes.approach: Accessed via West Side Road on Rainier's west side; camping is available at 9,800 ft below St. Andrews Rocks on the later-season approach.
routes.descent: Reverse the ascent route back down the Tahoma Glacier and Puyallup Cleaver/Emerald Ridge to West Side Road.
routes.descentText: Descend the same zigzag line back down the glacier, taking care to reverse the crevasse-field route-finding from 12,000 ft and 10,700 ft, then exit via St. Andrews Rock/Puyallup Cleaver or Emerald Ridge.
routes.bail: Retreat down the glacier is generally straightforward in good visibility given the moderate-angle terrain; the main risk is crevasse route-finding in whiteout.
routes.turnaround: Turn back before crevasse bridges soften in the afternoon sun, especially through the 10,700-ft and 12,000-ft crevasse zones.
routes.bestSeason: Early to late season depending on approach chosen; the west-facing glacier gets limited morning sun (until roughly 10am), which can leave snow hard early in the day.
routes.face: West side, between Tahoma Cleaver and Sunset Ridge
routes.detailedRack: Standard glacier-travel gear: pickets and crevasse-rescue rigging per rope team; no technical ice/rock gear typically needed.
routes.proNeeds: Pickets for crevasse-bridge protection through the two major crevasse zones.
whatToBring: Bivy gear for a multi-day ascent
proTips: The largest glacier on Rainier's west side is remote and quiet — expect solitude compared to the south-side routes
Because the face is west-facing, snow stays hard until late morning; time your crux crevasse crossings accordingly
watchOut: Significant, shifting crevasse systems around 10,700 ft and 12,000 ft require careful route-finding
Remote west-side location with limited quick-bail options
knownHazards: Extensive crevasse fields on the mid and upper glacier
Hard, west-facing snow before late-morning sun softens it

routes.fa: Philemon Van Trump, Alfred Drewry, and Warren Riley, 1891

routes.overview: The most popular and least technical route to Rainier's summit, the standard line used by the guide services, following a well-worn track over the Ingraham Glacier and up the rock/scree cleaver that gives the route its name.
routes.beta: From Camp Muir, traverse the upper Cowlitz Glacier to Cathedral Rocks Ridge, cross the Ingraham Glacier (Ingraham Flats, ~11,100 ft) to the base of Disappointment Cleaver, climb the cleaver to its top (~12,300 ft), then follow the upper Ingraham/Emmons snowfields as directly as crevasses allow to the east crater rim, finishing with a walk to the true summit (14,406 ft).
routes.approach: From Paradise (5,420 ft), take the Skyline Trail and Muir Snowfield route past Panorama Point (6,800 ft) and Pebble Creek (7,200 ft) to Camp Muir (10,080 ft).
routes.descent: Descend the same route back to Camp Muir and Paradise.
routes.descentText: Reverse the ascent: down the upper snowfields to the top of the Cleaver, down the Cleaver to Ingraham Flats, back across the Ingraham Glacier and Cathedral Rocks Ridge to Camp Muir, then down the Muir Snowfield and Skyline Trail to Paradise.
routes.bail: Straightforward retreat to Camp Muir or Ingraham Flats camp in reasonable visibility; alternate camps on the Muir Snowfield and at Ingraham Flats offer options if Camp Muir is full.
routes.turnaround: Turn back before crevasse bridges become unreliable in the afternoon heat, and before deteriorating weather removes visibility on the exposed upper snowfields.
routes.bestSeason: Roughly late May through September, the primary guided climbing season, once the route is established over the Cleaver.
routes.face: South side, via the Ingraham Glacier and Disappointment Cleaver
routes.detailedRack: Standard glacier-travel kit: crampons, ice axe, rope, and crevasse-rescue gear; pickets/screws carried but rarely placed on the well-established track.
routes.proNeeds: Minimal — the route follows a maintained boot track; pickets carried as a backup for crevasse crossings.
whatToBring: Headlamp for the pre-dawn summit push
Sun protection for the glare-heavy upper mountain
proTips: This is the normal route of the guide services and the most-climbed line on the mountain — expect a well-defined track but also crowding and fixed-line sections on the Cleaver in busy periods
watchOut: Rockfall on the loose Disappointment Cleaver itself, especially with other parties above
Route through the Ingraham Glacier crevasses is re-established every season and can shift
knownHazards: Crevasse crossings on the Ingraham and Cowlitz Glaciers
Rockfall on Disappointment Cleaver
Altitude effects above 12,000 ft
Serac exposure near Ingraham Flats

emergency.rangerStation: Camp Muir has a seasonal ranger presence; Mount Rainier NP Dispatch (360) 569-2211 ext. 0; Wilderness Information Center (360) 569-6641
emergency.notes: As the most-traveled route, DC also sees the most guided-party traffic and rescue call volume on the mountain.

routes.overview: Described by climbers as 'the most fearsome alpine face in the lower 48,' Willis Wall is a huge, serac-capped north-side wall between Liberty Ridge and Curtis Ridge, not technically extreme by modern standards but exceptionally dangerous due to constant rock and icefall.
routes.beta: Multiple ribs and lines have been climbed up the wall since the 1962 first ascent, typically staying on one of several rock ribs (Central Rib, East Rib, or variations) to avoid the worst of the crowning ice cliff, exiting high on Curtis Ridge or through breaks in the summit ice cliff.
routes.approach: Approached via the Carbon Glacier from the White River side (Glacier Basin Trail to St. Elmo Pass, then across the Winthrop and Carbon Glaciers beneath Liberty Wall), the same general approach used for Liberty Ridge and Curtis Ridge.
routes.descent: Descend via the Emmons-Winthrop Glacier route to Camp Schurman.
routes.descentText: From the summit area, descend via the standard Emmons-Winthrop line through the Corridor to Camp Schurman, then out via St. Elmo Pass to White River.
routes.bail: Retreat off the wall itself is extremely limited once committed due to the overhanging ice cliff and active serac fall; success rates are reported below 30%, and most retreats happen before committing to the face.
routes.turnaround: Climb only in cold, stable conditions and be prepared to abandon the attempt at the base if serac or rockfall activity is heard/observed — this is not a route to push through deteriorating conditions on.
routes.bestSeason: Cold, stable periods in late spring/early summer when the wall is well frozen; success is inherently conditions-dependent and rare.
routes.face: North Face, between Liberty Ridge and Curtis Ridge
routes.detailedRack: Pins, screws, nuts, and cams for the mixed rock ribs; full glacier/crevasse-rescue kit for the approach.
routes.proNeeds: A mixed rack (pins, screws, nuts, cams) is needed for the rock-rib sections; approach protection is standard glacier gear.
whatToBring: Bivy gear for a multi-day ascent
proTips: Success is heavily conditions-dependent; watch the wall from a safe distance before committing to assess active serac/rockfall
watchOut: Very active, continuous serac fall off the summit ice cliff strafes the wall regularly
Reported success rate below 30% for parties attempting the wall
knownHazards: Continuous serac fall from the crowning ice cliff
Constant rockfall off the wall's rock ribs
Large bergschrund at the base, among the largest in the lower 48 states

routes.fa: Ed Cooper and Mike Swayne, June 1962 (first ascent of the wall, via a line staying left of the summit ice cliff, exiting high on Curtis Ridge)
routes.corrections: Mountain Project's listed first-ascent credit (Charles Bell / Jens Klubberud and Loren Campbell) appears to reference a specific later variation or linkup rather than the wall's original 1962 first ascent, which multiple independent sources (AAC Publications, Wikipedia) attribute to Ed Cooper and Mike Swayne.

routes.overview: A west-side ridge route above Sunset Amphitheater featuring steep snow leading to a crux of loose volcanic rock, then easier ground with notable nieve penitente up to Liberty Cap.
routes.beta: From the West Side Road, climb the ridge past Tokaloo Rock, then traverse left across glaciers to a bivouac around 8,500 ft. Above the bivy, steep snow climbing leads to loose volcanic rock (the crux), followed by easier-angled snow — including spectacular nieve penitente — up to Liberty Cap.
routes.approach: From the West Side Road, climb the ridge past Tokaloo Rock and traverse left across the glaciers below to reach the bivy site.
routes.descent: Standard descent via the Emmons-Winthrop or Disappointment Cleaver route.
routes.descentText: From Liberty Cap, descend via Emmons-Winthrop to Camp Schurman or via Disappointment Cleaver to Camp Muir, depending on party plans.
routes.bail: Below the rock crux, retreat down the snow and glacier traverse to the West Side Road is possible; above it, continuing to easier ground and over the top is generally preferred.
routes.turnaround: Loose volcanic rock at the crux is the main hazard — turn back if the rock is wet or if rockfall risk from parties above is a concern.
routes.bestSeason: Summer, once the West Side Road approach and glacier traverse are passable.
routes.detailedRack: Minimal protection available — some slings for boulders and a few small cams have been used at the loose-rock crux.
routes.proNeeds: Very limited natural protection at the crux; largely a run-out rock/snow scramble.
whatToBring: Careful footwear choice for the loose volcanic rock crux
proTips: The nieve penitente on the upper snow slopes is a notable, photogenic feature of this route
watchOut: Extremely loose rock (volcanic mud and cobbles) at the crux with minimal natural protection
Exposed climbing on the ridge with limited anchor options
knownHazards: Loose volcanic rock at the crux, prone to rockfall
Crevasse crossings on the glacier traverse to the bivy
Minimal fixed or natural protection along exposed sections

routes.overview: A classic, enjoyable snow-couloir route up Rainier's south side, named for the Fuhrer brothers who made its first ascent; features roughly 2,200 ft of 40-45-degree snow climbing before merging with the upper Kautz Glacier route.
routes.beta: From Wapowety Cleaver's high camp, the route climbs the 'Finger,' a snow couloir above the Wilson Glacier, gaining about 2,200 ft at 40-45 degrees before joining the upper Kautz Glacier route around 13,000 ft, above which slopes ease and some crevasse crossings lead to the crater rim.
routes.approach: From Paradise (5,400 ft), hike the Skyline Trail to Glacier Vista (6,300 ft), the rope-up point; descend several hundred feet to cross the Nisqually Glacier, then climb a snow gully angling up and right, gaining 700 ft to a rest area, and continue to Wapowety Cleaver for the high camp on the Wilson Glacier's edge.
routes.descent: Three options: descend the climbing route (higher rockfall risk), descend via the Kautz Glacier (requires comfort with 50-degree alpine ice), or carry over the summit and descend via Ingraham Glacier or Disappointment Cleaver.
routes.descentText: Many parties prefer to summit and descend via Disappointment Cleaver or Ingraham Direct to avoid re-exposure to rockfall in the Finger couloir; descending the Kautz Glacier side requires competence on 50-degree alpine ice.
routes.bail: Below the couloir, retreat down the approach gully and across the Nisqually Glacier to Glacier Vista is straightforward; within the couloir, downclimbing is the main option given limited anchors.
routes.turnaround: A very early start is essential — the upper mountain gets early sun here, so turn around or move fast if rockfall risk in the couloir is rising with warming temperatures.
routes.bestSeason: Summer, snow-climbing season, once the couloir is well filled in.
routes.face: South side, the 'Finger' couloir above the Wilson Glacier
routes.detailedRack: A few pickets and runners; helmets highly recommended given rockfall in the couloir.
routes.proNeeds: Pickets for the snow couloir; minimal additional protection typically needed.
whatToBring: Bivy gear for the Wapowety Cleaver high camp
proTips: A very early start is essential to minimize rockfall danger in the couloir as the sun hits the upper mountain
watchOut: Rockfall hazard in the couloir, worsening as the day warms
The upper mountain here catches early sun, accelerating snow/ice loosening
knownHazards: Rockfall in the Finger couloir
Avalanche potential on the steep snow slopes
Crevasse crossings above 13,000 ft near the crater rim

routes.fa: Hans Fuhrer, Heinie Fuhrer, Joseph Hazard, and Thomas Hermans (or Peyton Farrer per one source), July 2, 1920

routes.overview: The southernmost of the three Mowich Face sections above Mowich Lake — a smooth, steep snow-and-ice slope its whole length, first climbed in 1957 and generally the most straightforward of the Mowich-side lines.
routes.beta: A sustained, mostly uniform snow/ice slope (roughly Grade III-IV) on the northwest side of the mountain, distinct from the more complex, rock-band-capped Central Mowich Face and the mixed North Mowich Headwall/Face to either side.
routes.approach: Via the Wonderland Trail from Mowich Lake to Spray Park, then a climbers' path up toward the Mowich Glacier area (subject to the current SR165/Fairfax Bridge closure limiting vehicle access to Mowich Lake).
routes.descent: Carry over the summit and descend via the Emmons-Winthrop or Disappointment Cleaver route.
routes.descentText: From the summit area, descend via Emmons-Winthrop to Camp Schurman or via Disappointment Cleaver to Camp Muir.
routes.bail: Retreat down the uniform snow/ice slope is more straightforward here than on the rockier Central or North Mowich lines, given good visibility and stable snow.
routes.turnaround: Turn back before the slope becomes rockfall-prone in full sun; climb in the morning where possible.
routes.bestSeason: Summer, once the Mowich Glacier approach is passable.
routes.face: Southernmost section of the Mowich Face, above Mowich Lake
routes.detailedRack: Ice screws and pickets for the sustained snow/ice slope; helmet mandatory given rockfall exposure.
routes.proNeeds: Screws/pickets along the length of the uniform snow-ice slope.
whatToBring: Bivy gear for a multi-day ascent
Early start gear to climb before rockfall risk rises
proTips: Climb in the morning before the sun hits the face to reduce rockfall potential — this does not eliminate the hazard entirely
This is a rarely attempted route; expect solitude and a remote, self-sufficient experience
watchOut: Rockfall exposure throughout the climb, even with an early start
Remote approach requires full self-sufficiency and rescue gear
knownHazards: Rockfall on the exposed snow/ice slope
Crevasse hazard on the Mowich Glacier approach

routes.corrections: 'Mowich Face' is used generically in some sources for the whole three-part Mowich Face complex (south/central/north). This entry represents the south section specifically, first climbed in 1957 (first-ascent party not documented in available sources); the central section is catalogued separately in this database as 'Central Mowich Face' (FA 1966) and the north section as 'North Mowich Headwall/Edmunds Headwall' (FA 1968).

blurb: Kendall Peak is a rocky, multi-summit ridge (5,799 ft, 383 ft prominence) in the Alpine Lakes Wilderness of the central Washington Cascades, straddling the King–Kittitas county line about 2 miles northeast of Snoqualmie Pass between Commonwealth and Gold Creek basins. Its rock formed from Eocene-age volcanic/igneous activity later sculpted by Pleistocene glaciation, and its northern (highest) summit rises directly above the Pacific Crest Trail just before the exposed "Kendall Katwalk." Routes in the area were first explored informally by The Mountaineers club roughly between 1915 and 1925 as members sought first ascents of peaks near the old Snoqualmie Pass Lodge; no documented technical first ascent exists for the peak's easy scramble line.
hierarchyNote: None — verified: Wikipedia gives Kendall Peak's coordinates as 47°26'35"N 121°23'05"W (≈47.4431, -121.3847) and elevation 5,799 ft, both matching the given data closely; the peak sits ~2 mi NE of Snoqualmie Pass as stated, so the given id/coordinates/elevation/parent area are all correct. Note only a minor cross-source inconsistency: WTA's trip page lists the summit at 5,784 ft (vs. the 5,799 ft in Wikipedia and the given/authoritative data) — not a correction, just a ~15 ft discrepancy between secondary sources.

routes.overview: An easy, unmarked Class 2 off-trail scramble that leaves the Pacific Crest Trail below Kendall Katwalk and climbs open talus/dirt slopes on the peak's north ridge to Kendall Peak's highest (north) summit.
routes.beta: The standard line follows the maintained PCT north from the Snoqualmie Pass trailhead for roughly 4.6-5 miles (some sources report up to 5.5 mi one-way depending on exact trailhead/summit points used), then breaks off onto a faint, unsigned boot path near 5,200 ft that climbs directly uphill to the ridge and summit. No technical gear is required in summer conditions; it is popular as a long day hike/scramble combo with the nearby Kendall Katwalk viewpoint.
routes.approach: From I-90 eastbound, take Exit 52 (Snoqualmie Pass West Summit), turn left/north and cross under the freeway, then take the first right into the Pacific Crest Trail (PCT) North Trailhead parking lot (day-use fee or Northwest Forest Pass required; free self-issued Alpine Lakes Wilderness permit available at the trailhead kiosk). Follow the PCT north, climbing steadily through forest with views into Commonwealth Basin, for roughly 4.6-5 miles. Around 5,200 ft, watch for a large stump partially obstructing the left side of the trail; about 15 feet past it, a faint boot path breaks off to the right and climbs straight uphill off-trail toward the ridge.
routes.descent: Reverse the ascent line: down-scramble the boot path back to the PCT junction at the stump landmark, then follow the PCT south back to the trailhead.
routes.descentText: From the summit, retrace the Class 2 scramble line back down to the boot-path junction on the PCT (marked by the large trailside stump), rejoin the maintained trail tread, and hike the PCT south/west roughly 4.6-5 miles back to the Snoqualmie Pass PCT North trailhead. No rappels or technical descent gear are needed in dry summer conditions; carry an ice axe and consider microspikes if snow lingers on the upper slopes into early summer.
routes.itinerary: Typically done as a single long day trip: drive to the PCT North Trailhead at Snoqualmie Pass, hike the PCT north, break off onto the boot path near the ~5,200 ft stump landmark, scramble to Kendall Peak's summit, then reverse the entire route to the trailhead — roughly 9-11 miles round trip with about 5 hours of hiking/scrambling time per trip-planning sources.
routes.bail: Retreat is straightforward at any point: simply reverse course back down the scramble slope to the PCT and follow the maintained trail south back to the trailhead. There is no technical terrain committing you past a safe walk-off.
routes.turnaround: Because the boot-path section is unmarked, off-trail, and crosses avalanche/loose-rock terrain, set a firm turnaround time before starting based on your pace and weather, and honor it rather than pushing for the summit against fading daylight, incoming thunderstorms, or deteriorating snow conditions on the upper slopes.
routes.bestSeason: Late June through early October is the typical window per WTA and trip-planning sources; the upper boot-path slopes often hold snow into June, and late August through September offers the most consistently snow-free scrambling. Afternoon thunderstorms are common in summer, so an early start is recommended.
routes.detailedRack: No technical rack is needed — this is a non-technical Class 2 scramble on dirt trail, open slopes, and talus/rock.
routes.proNeeds: No fixed or placed protection is used on this route; a helmet is recommended for loose rock underfoot, and an ice axe for any lingering snow on the upper slopes.
whatToBring: Extra water (limited/no reliable sources on the upper mountain)
Layers for wind and afternoon storms
Map/compass or GPS app as backup for the unmarked boot path
proTips: Carry a GPS track (Gaia or Halfmile PCT app) — the boot-path turnoff from the PCT is unsigned and easy to miss.
Bring an ice axe and consider microspikes before mid-to-late June.
Start early to secure trailhead parking, beat PCT foot traffic, and be off exposed terrain before afternoon thunderstorms.
Self-issue the free wilderness permit at the trailhead kiosk before starting.
watchOut: Easy to overshoot or undershoot the unmarked boot-path turnoff — watch for the large trailside stump around 5,200 ft as the landmark.
Loose rock underfoot on the scramble section, especially with other parties above you.
Snow/ice can persist on the upper mountain well into early summer.
Don't confuse the standard scramble line with crossing the exposed Kendall Katwalk section of the PCT itself.
knownHazards: The unmaintained boot path below Kendall Peak's summit crosses loose rock and talus prone to rockfall.
Snow can linger on the upper slopes into June, creating avalanche and postholing hazard on what is otherwise a summer scramble.
The adjacent Kendall Katwalk section of the PCT is a narrow, exposed ledge blasted into a cliff face and has been the site of a fatal fall (September 2018).
Afternoon thunderstorms are common in summer and increase lightning and exposure risk on the open ridge.
objHaz: loose rock/talus on the boot path
exposed nearby PCT section (Kendall Katwalk) with a documented fatality

pitchDetail.notes: ~4.6-5 mi one-way on maintained PCT tread from the trailhead (3,000 ft) to the boot-path junction near 5,200 ft; watch for the large trailside stump landmark.
Leave the PCT about 15 ft past the stump on a faint boot path climbing directly uphill through open slopes and talus to Kendall Peak's north (highest) summit.

road.name: PCT North Trailhead access road / I-90 Exit 52 spur
road.status: Paved; trailhead open and accessible year-round, 24 hours a day
road.driveNote: From I-90 eastbound, take Exit 52 (Snoqualmie Pass West Summit), turn left under the freeway, then take the first right into the PCT North Trailhead lot; suitable for any passenger vehicle.

climate.forecastZone: NWAC Snoqualmie Pass zone
climate.typical: Maritime Cascade-crest climate — Snoqualmie Pass averages roughly 400+ inches of snowfall per winter with heavy snowpack Nov-May; summers are mild and mostly dry with afternoon thunderstorm potential.
climate.spring: Heavy lingering snowpack on north-facing and upper slopes into June; avalanche and postholing hazard common on the off-trail scramble section.
climate.summer: Warm, mostly dry days with possible afternoon thunderstorms; the best window for the scramble, especially late Aug-Sep.
climate.fall: Cooling temperatures and increasing rain/snow chance by October; early season snow can return to the upper mountain.
climate.winter: Heavy snow and avalanche terrain; the off-trail scramble section is not a winter/spring objective without full mountaineering gear and skills.

access.landManager: Mount Baker-Snoqualmie National Forest, Snoqualmie Ranger District (Alpine Lakes Wilderness)
access.fees: $5/vehicle/day (or $30 annual) Northwest Forest Pass fee at the PCT trailhead lot, payable via Recreation.gov Scan & Pay or a displayed valid pass
access.permit: Free self-issued Alpine Lakes Wilderness permit at the trailhead kiosk
access.passRequired: Northwest Forest Pass or America the Beautiful interagency pass
access.closures: No seasonal road or trailhead closure — the trailhead is open year-round, 24 hours; however the off-trail summit slopes carry avalanche hazard and are effectively unsafe to travel during winter/spring snowpack.

timing.recommendedStart: An early morning start is recommended to secure trailhead parking, complete the roughly 9-11 mile round trip in daylight, and be off exposed terrain before afternoon thunderstorms build.

emergency.county: King County (trailhead/approach side); the summit sits directly on the King-Kittitas county line
emergency.sheriffDispatch: King County Sheriff's Office non-emergency dispatch: (206) 296-3311 — call 911 for active emergencies
emergency.rangerStation: Snoqualmie Ranger District, Mt. Baker-Snoqualmie National Forest, 902 SE North Bend Way Bldg 1, North Bend, WA 98045 — (425) 888-1421
emergency.nearestHospital: Snoqualmie Valley Hospital, 9801 Frontier Ave SE, Snoqualmie, WA 98065 — (425) 831-2300; 24/7 ER, Level 5 trauma / Level 2 cardiac / Level 3 stroke facility, roughly 25 miles / 30 min from the trailhead via I-90
emergency.notes: Cell coverage is inconsistent along the PCT/Commonwealth Basin corridor. King County Sheriff's Office coordinates backcountry search and rescue for this area via volunteer SAR teams.

blurb: Northwest Mox Peak ("Easy Mox"), 8,407 ft, is the smaller of the two Mox Peaks "Twin Spires" in the Chilliwack Range of North Cascades National Park, about four miles south of the Canada-US border. Built of loose, discordant gneiss/metaigneous rock, it is separated from its higher, harder neighbor Southeast Mox Peak (8,504 ft, "Hard Mox") by a deep notch nicknamed "the Col of the Wild." Fred and Helmy Beckey made the first ascents of both spires in June 1941, climbing the NW peak first and completing the SE peak the following day.

routes.fa: Fred Beckey & Helmy Beckey, June 1941 (first ascent of the NW spire; the pair climbed the SE spire the following day)
routes.rappels: ~2 single-rope rappels to a ledge below the summit, then a longer double-rope rappel from the highest anchor down the headwall to the glacier (some parties carry 2-3 ropes to combine single- and double-rope raps)
objHaz: wet or slick slabs on the headwall
sudden rain squalls even under a sunny forecast
high exposure on the ridge crest
difficult route-finding on the Redoubt Glacier in poor visibility
routes.face: Northeast ridge headwall and upper ridge crest
routes.overview: A long, remote glacier approach via the Redoubt Glacier leads to NW Mox's northeast-facing headwall, where two pitches of loose class 5 rock gain the ridge crest; an exposed class 3-4 ridge traverse then leads to a short final class 5 step and one more pitch to the summit.
routes.beta: From a high camp on the Redoubt Glacier saddle, cross roughly 2 miles of glacier eastward to the base of NW Mox's northeast-facing headwall. Climb two pitches of unpleasant, loose class 5 rock (rock shoes help on wet slabs) to gain the northeast ridge crest, then follow the crest for about half a mile of exposed class 3-4 simul-climbing on more solid metaigneous rock, using a roughly 40-ft chimney on the left to bypass ridge pinnacles. A short class 5 step past a notch between a pinnacle and the summit tower leads to one long class 3-4 pitch to the true summit.
routes.approach: From Chilliwack, BC, drive Chilliwack Lake Road/the Depot Creek Forest Service Road to the Depot Creek trailhead (roughly 90 minutes from Chilliwack); the final ~6 miles are pothole-infested dirt requiring a 4x4, high-clearance vehicle, with minor washed-out/repaired sections reported around km 2.5-3. From the parking area follow the left-hand roadway fork about 1 mile, then climb to the US-Canada border at Monument 65 (about 2 miles total). Beyond the monument, follow the unmaintained Depot Creek climbers' path through old-growth forest for about 3 miles to the base of Depot Creek Falls (one of Washington's tallest, roughly 967-1,000 ft of drop). Scramble alongside the falls on an exposed class 3 climbers' path with a fixed handline/rope for roughly 800-1,000 ft of gain, then cross the outwash plain to Ouzel Lake (~5,600 ft) for a first camp. From Ouzel Lake, scramble ledgy rock and rope up for the Redoubt Glacier, navigating (GPS strongly recommended in poor visibility) to a high camp on the broad Redoubt Glacier saddle (~7,150 ft).
routes.descent: Reverse the northeast ridge: two single-rope rappels down to a ledge below the summit, then a double-rope rappel from the highest anchor back down the headwall to the Redoubt Glacier, followed by re-crossing the glacier and retracing the approach.
routes.descentText: From the summit, downclimb/scramble back to the notch below the summit tower and make two single-rope rappels to a wide ledge below the summit. Downclimb/simul-climb the class 3-4 ridge crest back toward the headwall (reversing the chimney bypass around the pinnacles), then make a double-rope rappel from the highest available anchor down the headwall to the Redoubt Glacier - parties often carry 2-3 ropes so single- and double-rope rappels can be combined efficiently. From the glacier, rope back up and retrace the route to high camp, then down to Ouzel Lake and out the Depot Creek trail to the trailhead.
routes.itinerary: Day 1: Drive to the Depot Creek trailhead and hike in via Monument 65 and Depot Creek Falls to camp at Ouzel Lake (~9 hrs, ~3,550 ft gain). Day 2: Move camp up onto the Redoubt Glacier to a high camp on the glacier saddle (~7,150 ft). Day 3: Alpine start; cross the Redoubt Glacier (~2 mi) to the NE ridge headwall, climb the ridge to the summit (~1,900 ft gain, roughly 9 hrs round trip to/from high camp) and return to high camp. Days 4-5: Descend to the trailhead - many parties combine Easy Mox with Hard Mox and/or Mount Redoubt and Mount Spickard from the same Ouzel Lake/Redoubt Glacier camps.
routes.bail: The northeast ridge and headwall can be reversed by rappel from nearly any point above the glacier. Retreat from the glacier back to Ouzel Lake and the Depot Creek trail is straightforward in good weather, but route-finding on the glacier becomes difficult in poor visibility, so a GPS track is valuable for a low-visibility retreat.
routes.turnaround: Given the multi-hour glacier crossing, loose rock, and rappel-dependent descent, set a firm turnaround time that leaves enough daylight to complete all rappels and re-cross the glacier before dark or before afternoon weather typically deteriorates.
routes.bestSeason: Best climbed July through September, once the Depot Creek Falls scramble and Redoubt Glacier are largely snow-free; even in summer, trip reports describe sudden rain squalls appearing despite a sunny forecast, and the already-loose rock becomes markedly more hazardous when wet.
routes.detailedRack: A light alpine rock rack (small-to-medium cams and stoppers) for sparse placements on the headwall and ridge, plus a full glacier travel kit (crampons, ice axe, rope, harness, crevasse-rescue gear) for the Redoubt Glacier crossing; rock shoes are commonly carried for extra friction on the wet, slabby headwall pitches.
routes.proNeeds: Protection on the headwall and ridge is sparse and the rock is loose in sections, so slings for horns and chockstones supplement a standard light alpine rack; 2-3 ropes are recommended so single- and double-rope rappels can be combined efficiently on descent.
whatToBring: Multi-day bivy/camp gear (typically a 3-5 day trip)
Navigation - map, compass, and GPS with a pre-loaded track for the glacier crossing
Food storage per NPS backcountry regulations
Extra layers for sudden rain squalls despite forecast
proTips: Many parties combine Easy Mox with Hard Mox, Mount Redoubt, and Mount Spickard in a single multi-day trip from the same Ouzel Lake/Redoubt Glacier camps.
Rock shoes add useful friction on the wet, slabby sections of the northeast ridge headwall.
A pre-loaded GPS track is strongly recommended for the Redoubt Glacier crossing, which parties have navigated in poor visibility using 'map, GPS, and the previous climbers' footprints.'
watchOut: The Depot Creek Falls scramble is exposed, wet class 3 terrain even with a fixed handline in place.
Rock on the peak is loose, discordant gneiss/metaigneous rock - test holds carefully, especially near the ridge pinnacles.
Weather can turn quickly, with rain squalls reported even under a sunny forecast, making the headwall rock more dangerous.
knownHazards: Loose rock on the northeast ridge headwall and near the summit pinnacles - large blocks have pulled loose on the neighboring peak (Hard Mox) with near-miss consequences historically.
Crevasses on the Redoubt Glacier; the glacier is reported as relatively flat with low crevasse hazard in normal conditions but still requires roped travel.
Long, remote, cross-border, multi-day approach with limited options for a quick rescue.

pitchDetail.notes: First of two headwall pitches from the Redoubt Glacier up the NE-facing headwall to gain the ridge crest; wet, slabby rock.
Second headwall pitch continuing to the ridge crest.
~0.5 mile of exposed simul-climbing on more solid metaigneous rock along the ridge crest; a ~40-ft chimney on the left bypasses ridge pinnacles.
Short class 5 step past a notch between a pinnacle and the summit tower, then one long class 3-4 pitch to the true summit.

road.name: Chilliwack Lake Road / Depot Creek Forest Service Road (BC)
road.status: Unmaintained, rough dirt/gravel with erosion; passable to 4x4 high-clearance vehicles with care
road.driveNote: About 90 minutes from Chilliwack, BC; the final ~6 miles are pothole-infested dirt, with a washed-out section around km 2.5-3 that has been informally, non-officially repaired - 4x4 strongly recommended.

climate.typical: Marine west coast climate with heavy orographic winter snowfall from Pacific storms and a short, weather-dependent summer climbing window
climate.summer: Prime climbing season (Jul-Sep) once the approach and glacier are mostly snow-free, but rain squalls can appear even under a sunny forecast
climate.winter: Deep snowpack and avalanche-prone conditions make the peak effectively inaccessible in winter; a documented first winter ascent of neighboring Hard Mox underscores how serious a winter attempt on this massif would be.

access.landManager: North Cascades National Park Complex / Stephen Mather Wilderness (US, summit side); BC Parks - Chilliwack Lake Provincial Park and adjacent Crown land (Canada, approach side)
access.fees: NPS backcountry/wilderness camping permit fee (reported at roughly $10/person plus a $6 non-refundable reservation fee); no fee reported on the BC approach road/trail itself
access.permit: A mandatory NPS wilderness permit is required for overnight backcountry camping within North Cascades National Park; obtained at the Wilderness Information Center in Marblemount, WA
access.closures: No specific route or seasonal closure found; North Cascades National Park enforces a fixed-anchor moratorium in wilderness areas (clean climbing techniques required, no new bolts/pitons).

emergency.county: Whatcom County, WA (US side); Fraser Valley Regional District, BC (Canada approach side)
emergency.sheriffDispatch: 911 for emergencies; Whatcom County Sheriff's Office (non-emergency) on the US side, or NPS non-emergency line 360-854-7255
emergency.rangerStation: North Cascades National Park Wilderness Information Center, Marblemount, WA - (360) 854-7245 (park headquarters/visitor info: 360-854-7200)
emergency.nearestHospital: PeaceHealth United General Medical Center, 2000 Hospital Dr, Sedro-Woolley, WA (US side, ~360-856-6021); Chilliwack General Hospital, Chilliwack, BC (nearer to the Canada-side trailhead)
emergency.notes: The approach is entirely via Canada (Chilliwack Lake Rd/Depot Creek FSR from Chilliwack, BC); the summit itself sits just south of the border inside North Cascades National Park. A rescue could involve coordination between Chilliwack Search & Rescue (BC, tasked via RCMP) and NPS/Whatcom County SAR (US).

routes.corrections: Mountain Project's own 'Northwest Mox Peak' area page lists an elevation of 7,586 ft, which conflicts with the 8,407 ft figure already in this database. Peakbagger.com and WTA both independently support 8,407 ft (with 527 ft of prominence), so the existing 8,407 ft value is retained as correct and the Mountain Project figure appears to be an error on that site.

blurb: North Twin Sister (6,644 ft) is the northern and slightly lower of the two principal summits of the Twin Sisters Range, a jagged sub-range of the North Cascades roughly 10 miles southwest of Mount Baker in Whatcom County, Washington. The range is composed almost entirely of dunite — an ultramafic, olivine-rich rock and the largest exposed body of olivine in the Western Hemisphere — giving the mountain's rock a distinctive orange-yellow weathered surface, unusually solid holds, and grippy friction. It is a popular scrambling objective via its class 3-4 west ridge, though the outing is complicated by a long, gated logging-road approach across private timberland.

routes.overview: The classic line up North Twin Sister: a striking, craggy ridge of golden-orange dunite climbed near its crest for roughly 1,500 vertical feet of sustained class 3-4 scrambling to the summit, with an optional short mixed (M1b) step on a variation start.
routes.beta: The West Ridge is the standard and by far the most popular route on North Twin Sister. After a long logging-road and trail approach to treeline near Dailey Prairie, the route follows the ridge crest (staying generally on the climber's-right/west side to avoid the hardest moves) past two named rock features — the 'Obelisk' and the 'Cockscomb' — with a notch around 6,300 ft ('Katie Gap') that bypasses a cliff band where the crest is blocked. Most of the climbing is class 3 on excellent, high-friction rock with frequent holds; a handful of class 4 (and, on the crest itself, occasional class 5) moves are encountered, and some parties rope up for a short M1b mixed step on a variation start. Multiple false summits complicate routefinding near the top. Descent is either back down the ridge or, early season, a steep glissade down snow on the north face.
routes.approach: From Acme, WA, follow Mosquito Lake Road north/east roughly 9 miles to a steel bridge over the Middle Fork Nooksack River, continue about 0.75 mile, then turn onto Forest Road 38. The road is gated roughly 5-6 miles below the climbing route; park at the gate/bridge (do not park past it, as it may be locked behind you). From the gate, bike or walk the logging roads: cross the bridge and climb Road 9000 for about 2.5 miles, taking the right fork at the first junction (the left/main road leads to a quarry) and continuing through clearcut about 1.5 miles to a second fork, where you take the left fork. Around 5 miles from the car a use trail begins (marked by a dirt mound, dead tree, and cairn), switchbacking up an old road spur through clearcut to a fire ring at roughly 6.4 miles, after which the route simply follows the ridge crest through brush and timber to alpine terrain.
routes.descent: Descend the west ridge as climbed, or glissade steep snow on the north face when conditions allow (ice axe/crampons required early season).
routes.descentText: Most parties reverse the west ridge, downclimbing the crest and staying below class 3 the whole way; this is straightforward but slow. In early season (roughly through mid-June), a steep snowfield on the north face offers a quick glissade descent back toward treeline, cutting real time off the return, but requires an ice axe and crampons and firm snow-travel judgment given the exposure above. From treeline, retrace the logging-road approach; a bike makes the multi-mile road descent dramatically faster than walking.
routes.itinerary: Single long day: pre-dawn start from the gate on FR 38, bike/hike the logging roads (~2-3 hrs) to the base of the ridge near Dailey Prairie, scramble the west ridge to the summit (~3-4 hrs), then descend by the same ridge or a north-face glissade (~2-3 hrs) back to the logging roads and out to the car, for a roughly 9-12 hour car-to-car day.
routes.bail: Retreat is straightforward at almost any point by downclimbing the ridge crest back toward Dailey Prairie and the logging roads; there are no fixed rappel stations on the standard line, so bailing means reversing climbed terrain rather than descending with rope work.
routes.turnaround: Given the long, exposed ridge and the long approach/exit, set a firm turnaround time before starting that accounts for the full round trip plus a safety margin, and commit to it regardless of how close the summit feels — deteriorating weather or fading daylight on this route means a long, brushy, routefinding-heavy exit in the dark.
routes.bestSeason: Best from roughly mid-June, once seasonal snow has melted off the ridge and the north-face descent option is no longer avalanche terrain, through September; some parties climb into fall in dry years. Winter and early spring are not viable — FR 38 is gated and the approach and ridge are snow-covered.
routes.detailedRack: Not a roped rock route for most parties: sturdy scrambling boots and a helmet are the core kit. Early-season parties carry an ice axe and crampons for lingering snow on the ridge and for the optional north-face glissade descent. A handful of parties bring a light rack and a short rope for the optional M1b mixed variation start or for the class 5 moves near the Cockscomb if climbing with less-experienced partners.
routes.proNeeds: Protection needs are minimal on the standard class 3-4 line; a light rack (a few cams/nuts and slings) covers the short M1b variation start or any roped class 5 moves near the Cockscomb for parties who choose to belay them.
gear: Ice axe and crampons (early season / north-face descent)
Optional light rack and short rope for the variation start
whatToBring: Mountain bike (strongly recommended for the multi-mile logging-road approach)
Extra water — the approach is long and dry
GPS track or detailed map (routefinding through clearcuts and road forks is confusing)
Work/gardening gloves for handling sharp dunite
proTips: Bring a mountain bike for the logging-road miles — it turns a grueling approach into a fast one, especially on the return.
Stay generally on the climber's-right/west side of the ridge crest rather than directly on top of it to avoid the hardest class 5 moves.
Watch for the notch known as 'Katie Gap' around 6,300 ft, where the ridge crest is blocked by a cliff band and a short bypass is needed.
Wear old gloves — the rock is famously grippy but abrasive on skin.
watchOut: Multiple false summits along the upper ridge cause routefinding confusion near the top.
Active logging operations can temporarily block, gate, or reroute the access road without notice.
Wet or lichen-covered dunite becomes slick despite its usual excellent friction.
Loose rock exists off the main ridge crest and in bypass gullies.
knownHazards: Sustained exposure along the narrow ridge crest with little natural protection for unroped travel.
Early-season snow and ice on the ridge and north face require an ice axe, crampons, and solid snow-travel skills.
A long, dry, brushy approach with genuine navigational complexity through clearcuts and multiple logging-road forks.
Loose rock in bypass gullies and off-route terrain.
Cell coverage is unreliable to absent through much of the Twin Sisters Range interior.

pitchDetail.notes: Gated logging roads (bike-friendly) to roughly 4,900 ft, then a use trail through clearcut and timber to a fire ring near treeline
Ridge-crest scrambling through scattered fir stands on solid, high-friction dunite, marked by occasional cairns

Exposed scrambling past the Obelisk and Cockscomb rock features to the summit; some parties rope up for a short mixed step on the variation start, and there is a bypass notch ('Katie Gap') around 6,300 ft

road.name: Forest Road 38 (Middle Fork Nooksack Road)
road.status: Gated roughly 5-6 miles below the route on Forest Service/private timberland; frequently locked
road.seasonalGate: Typically gated through winter and into early summer (roughly December-June) and subject to additional closures for active logging
road.driveNote: From Acme, WA, take Mosquito Lake Road north/east about 9 miles to a steel bridge over the Middle Fork Nooksack, continue about 0.75 mile, then turn onto FR 38; park at the gate/bridge rather than risk being locked in

climate.forecastZone: Mount Baker Wilderness / North Cascades west-slope zone (NWS Seattle mountain zone forecasts)
climate.typical: Wet, mild, maritime Cascades climate with heavy annual snowfall at elevation; conditions can shift quickly
climate.spring: Significant snow lingers on the approach roads and ridge into June; the north-face descent is avalanche terrain early season
climate.summer: The best climbing window — dry rock and the road most likely to be open, typically mid-June through September
climate.fall: Generally good conditions into September/early October in dry years; approach brush can be wet from rain
climate.winter: Not a winter objective — FR 38 is gated and the ridge is under heavy snow

access.landManager: Mt. Baker Ranger District, Mount Baker-Snoqualmie National Forest (Mount Baker Wilderness); the lower approach crosses private timberland
access.fees: No specific day-use fee documented for this trailhead; Northwest Forest Pass/Interagency passes are honored at fee sites elsewhere in the forest
access.permit: No permit required for day use; Mount Baker Wilderness regulations cap party size at 12
access.closures: FR 38 is gated seasonally (roughly December-June) and subject to additional temporary closures for active logging operations

timing.recommendedStart: Pre-dawn (roughly 5-6 AM) given a typical 9-12 hour car-to-car day

emergency.sheriffDispatch: 911 for emergencies; Whatcom County Sheriff's Office non-emergency line (360) 676-6911
emergency.rangerStation: Mt. Baker Ranger District, Mount Baker-Snoqualmie National Forest, (360) 856-5700
emergency.nearestHospital: PeaceHealth St. Joseph Medical Center, Bellingham, WA
emergency.notes: Search and rescue is coordinated through the Whatcom County Sheriff's Office with Whatcom County Search & Rescue (WCSAR); cell coverage is unreliable to absent through much of the Twin Sisters Range interior

objHaz: Early-season snow and ice, including on the north-face descent
Active logging operations on the approach road
routes.corrections: One trip-report source (Julia Goes Mountaineering, 2019) states the summit elevation as 7,401 ft and elevation gain as roughly 3,500-4,000 ft; this conflicts with the summit elevation of 6,644 ft corroborated by Wikipedia/USGS and Mountain Project (6,640 ft), and with the 5,000-5,300 ft round-trip gain reported by multiple GPS-tracked trip reports (StephAbegg.com, Steven's Peak-bagging Journey). The 7,401 ft figure is treated as an error/outlier and was not used; the given peak elevationFt of 6,644 ft is confirmed correct.

routes.overview: The standard non-technical scrambling ascent of North Twin Sister on the range's signature golden-orange dunite (olivine) rock — the class 3 walk-up/scramble description of the same west-ridge line, staying off the hardest ridge-crest moves that the roped 'West Ridge' entry documents.
routes.beta: No source was found documenting an independently named 'Olivine Scramble' route; every reputable account of climbing North Twin Sister (Mountain Project, SummitPost, The Mountaineers, StephAbegg, and numerous trip reports) describes a single standard line — the class 3-4 west ridge on the mountain's famously grippy, orange-weathered dunite. This entry is treated as the general scrambling description of that same route (the walk-up-oriented, unroped experience most parties actually have), while the sibling 'West Ridge' entry carries the more technical framing (mixed variation, roped sections). Expect sustained class 3 scrambling on exceptionally solid, high-friction rock, with short class 4 steps and multiple false summits near the top.
routes.approach: Identical to the West Ridge approach: from Acme, WA, Mosquito Lake Road to the steel bridge over the Middle Fork Nooksack, then Forest Road 38 to its gate roughly 5-6 miles below the route. From the gate, logging roads (bike-friendly) lead about 5 miles to a use trail marked by a dirt mound, dead tree, and cairn, which climbs through clearcut to a fire ring near treeline before the ridge scramble begins.
routes.descent: Reverse the ridge scramble, or glissade steep snow on the north face early season (ice axe/crampons required).
routes.descentText: Downclimb the ridge, staying on class 3 terrain, back toward the treeline use trail and logging roads; in early season a steep north-face snowfield offers a faster glissade descent for parties carrying an ice axe and crampons.
routes.itinerary: Single long day: early start from the FR 38 gate, bike/hike the logging roads to treeline, scramble the ridge to the summit, and descend the same way (or via the north-face snowfield), for a roughly 9-12 hour car-to-car outing.
routes.bail: Retreat by downclimbing the ridge crest at any point back toward the treeline use trail and logging roads; no technical rappel gear is needed on the standard scrambling line.
routes.turnaround: Set a firm, conservative turnaround time before starting given the long approach and exposed upper ridge, and honor it regardless of proximity to the summit.
routes.bestSeason: Mid-June through September, once the ridge has shed its seasonal snow; a dry-rock summer window is most reliable.
routes.detailedRack: No roped rock gear is used on the standard scrambling line; a helmet, sturdy scrambling boots, and (early season) an ice axe and crampons are the core kit.
routes.proNeeds: Minimal — the scrambling line is unroped for essentially all parties; the technical M1b variation and any roped class 5 moves belong to the 'West Ridge' entry, not this scramble line.
whatToBring: Mountain bike for the logging-road approach
GPS track or detailed map for the clearcut/road routefinding
Work gloves for the abrasive rock
proTips: This is the same mountain and same general line as the 'West Ridge' entry — treat the two as different framings of one route rather than two separate objectives.
The dunite's grip is excellent but abrasive; gloves help on long ridge sections.
A bike cuts hours off the logging-road miles in both directions.
watchOut: Multiple false summits near the top cause routefinding confusion.
The access road is on active timberland and can be gated or rerouted without notice.
Wet rock is noticeably more slippery than its dry-day reputation suggests.
knownHazards: Sustained ridge exposure on an unroped scramble.
Long, brushy, routefinding-heavy approach through logging roads and clearcuts.
Early-season snow/ice on the ridge and north face.
Unreliable cell coverage in the range interior.

road.name: Forest Road 38 (Middle Fork Nooksack Road)
road.status: Gated roughly 5-6 miles below the route; frequently locked
road.seasonalGate: Typically gated December-June, plus additional logging-related closures
road.driveNote: From Acme, WA via Mosquito Lake Road to the Nooksack bridge, then FR 38; park at the gate

climate.forecastZone: Mount Baker Wilderness / North Cascades west-slope zone
climate.typical: Wet, mild maritime Cascades climate
climate.spring: Snow lingers on the ridge into June
climate.summer: Best window, mid-June through September
climate.fall: Generally good into September/early October in dry years
climate.winter: Not viable — road gated, heavy snow

access.landManager: Mt. Baker Ranger District, Mount Baker-Snoqualmie National Forest (Mount Baker Wilderness); lower approach on private timberland
access.fees: No specific fee documented for this trailhead
access.permit: No permit required for day use; 12-person wilderness party limit
access.closures: FR 38 gated seasonally and for active logging

timing.recommendedStart: Pre-dawn given a long car-to-car day

emergency.sheriffDispatch: 911 for emergencies; Whatcom County Sheriff's Office non-emergency (360) 676-6911
emergency.rangerStation: Mt. Baker Ranger District, (360) 856-5700
emergency.nearestHospital: PeaceHealth St. Joseph Medical Center, Bellingham, WA
emergency.notes: SAR coordinated through Whatcom County Sheriff's Office / WCSAR; cell coverage unreliable in the range interior

objHaz: Logging operations on the approach road
routes.corrections: No source documents a route specifically named 'Olivine Scramble' on North Twin Sister; all located references (Mountain Project, SummitPost, The Mountaineers, StephAbegg, and independent trip reports) describe one standard class 3-4 west-ridge line. This entry is filled as the general/non-technical scrambling description of that same route rather than as an independently verified separate line — flagging in case the catalog intends it to represent a genuinely distinct route (e.g. north or northeast ridge, mentioned by some sources as a secondary, snow-covered approach) that this research could not separately confirm.

hierarchyNote: Confirmed genuine: coordinates (47.9907, -121.470477) match Wikipedia's 47°59'27"N 121°28'14"W for Del Campo Peak in Snohomish County, and "Monte Cristo Group" as parent area is geographically correct (the Mountaineers/other sources place it in the "Monte Cristo / Barlow Pass Area," directly above Gothic Basin near the historic Monte Cristo mining district). No mismatch found. Minor data point: elevation is most commonly sourced as 6,610 ft (Wikipedia, WTA, Peakbagger-derived) vs. the given 6,615 ft — a 5 ft discrepancy, noted in the route's corrections field.
blurb: Del Campo Peak (6,610 ft) is a rugged summit in the Monte Cristo Group of the western North Cascades, rising above Foggy Lake at the head of Gothic Basin near Barlow Pass on the Mountain Loop Highway. Unlike the crumbly volcanic breccia common on neighboring Cascade summits, Del Campo is built of conglomerate sandstone and shale, giving it comparatively solid rock. The peak was named for a mining claim on its slopes (submitted 1917, officially adopted 1918) and was first climbed in 1912 by a party led by J.A. Juleen.

routes.overview: From Foggy Lake in upper Gothic Basin, the standard route climbs south-facing heather and snow slopes to a shoulder above the lake, ascends class 3 ledges left of an obvious gully to a notch between the South Buttress and the summit, then works up and over a second, smaller notch on the SE buttress (bypassing a 30-ft step) to finish on class 3 rock on the peak's NE side.
routes.beta: This is the standard, easiest line up Del Campo Peak, reached via the Weden Creek/Gothic Basin trail to Foggy Lake. It is a sustained, exposed class 3-4 scramble on generally solid rock (better than much of the surrounding Cascade volcanic rock), not a roped technical climb, but the exposure and loose rock make a helmet mandatory and a fall highly consequential. The crux is route-finding: the correct entry gully off the lake and the correct line through the summit notch are both easy to get wrong, and whiteout/fog is common in the basin.
routes.fa: J.A. Juleen party, 1912 (first recorded ascent of the peak; this standard line is understood to follow essentially the original route of ascent, though no source confirms a route-specific first-ascent party distinct from the peak FA)
routes.aspect: S/SE (approach face), summit finish on NE side
objHaz: exposure near the notch and summit block
snow-filled gullies persisting into early summer
routes.corrections: Given peak elevation of 6,615 ft is not matched by any source checked; Wikipedia, WTA, and Peakbagger-derived data consistently give 6,610 ft (Mazamas lists 6,613 ft, likely a rounding variant of the same figure). Recommend correcting to 6,610 ft. Note also: willhiteweb.com's page appears to transpose the two peaks' elevations (listing Del Campo at 6,213 ft and Gothic Peak at 6,610 ft) — this is almost certainly an error on that page, since every other source agrees Del Campo is the higher (6,610 ft) and Gothic Peak is 6,213 ft; that figure was not used here.
routes.approach: From the Verlot Public Service Center (11 mi east of Granite Falls) or from Granite Falls itself (~31 mi total), follow the Mountain Loop Highway east to Barlow Pass (elev. ~2,360 ft) and park at the Barlow Pass trailhead (privy available).
