Walk south past the gate on the (typically closed-to-vehicles) Monte Cristo Road for about 1 mile to the Weden Creek (Gothic Basin) Trail #724 trailhead on the right, just before a Sauk River crossing. The trail climbs steadily through forest (crossing from Mt. Baker-Snoqualmie National Forest into the WA DNR Morningstar Natural Resources Conservation Area) for about 3.3 miles to Foggy Lake at the top of Gothic Basin, gaining roughly 2,700-2,900 ft along the way; the final approach to the basin follows an old, steep, switchback-free miner's track. Snow can obscure the trail well into early summer.
routes.descent: Reverse the ascent line exactly — down-climb the notches and gully-side ledges back to the shoulder above Foggy Lake, then descend to the lake and return via the Weden Creek Trail.
routes.descentText: Downclimb is via the same class 3-4 terrain used on ascent; there is no separate rappel or walk-off descent documented. Because the correct line through the notches and the ledge system beside the gully is easy to lose, especially in fog (common in the basin) or under snow, parties are advised to mark or GPS-track their ascent line for the return. From the shoulder above Foggy Lake, descend the snow/heather slopes back to the lake's east side and follow the Weden Creek Trail back down through Gothic Basin and the forest to the Monte Cristo Road and Barlow Pass trailhead.
routes.itinerary: Typically done car-to-car in a single long day: trailhead to Foggy Lake (roughly 2.5-3 hrs), Foggy Lake to summit via the notch scramble (roughly 20 min to 2 hrs depending on conditions/route-finding, per multiple trip reports), then reverse for the descent. Some parties backpack in and camp near Foggy Lake to split the trip over two days and climb Del Campo and/or Gothic Peak together the next morning.
routes.bail: There are no established bail anchors or rappel stations on this route; retreat means reversing the ascent line back down through the notches to the shoulder above Foggy Lake. Turn back before committing to the gully/notch section if weather, visibility, or party ability are in question, since down-climbing exposed class 3-4 terrain in poor visibility is the route's main hazard.
routes.turnaround: Given the long approach and the difficulty of relocating the correct descent line through the notch system, especially in the fog that is common in Gothic Basin, parties should set a firm turnaround time before starting the summit scramble and be willing to retreat rather than push through deteriorating visibility or late-day fatigue.
routes.bestSeason: Best climbed July through September; the basin and its gullies hold snow late (often into early July), and NWAC-adjacent trip reports describe needing an ice axe and traction for snow travel into early summer. By fall, shortening days and early snow can return quickly at this elevation.
routes.detailedRack: No roped rock gear is used on the standard line — it is an unroped class 3-4 scramble. A climbing helmet is considered mandatory given rockfall and loose rock. In early season, an ice axe and traction device (microspikes) are commonly carried for the snow-filled gullies and the snowfield on the south slope.
gear: map/compass or GPS with a pre-loaded track
whatToBring: extra water (no reliable water past Foggy Lake)
Northwest Forest Pass for the trailhead
proTips: Pre-load a GPS track from a previous ascent (e.g., via GaiaGPS/Peakbagger data) — the entry point off Foggy Lake into the scramble is genuinely hard to locate on sight, and starting in the wrong spot can cliff you out.

At the notch between the South Buttress and the summit, an obvious, friendly-looking gully invites you in but is a poor choice — the less inviting-looking scramble line beside it is the correct, safer way and is easier than it looks from a distance.
Del Campo's conglomerate/sandstone rock is noticeably more solid than the volcanic breccia found on many neighboring Cascade scrambles, which helps offset the exposure somewhat.
watchOut: Loose talus and rockfall risk from other parties above you on the summit block — helmet up and stay alert.
Snow can persist in the ascent gullies and on the south-slope snowfield into July, requiring an ice axe.
Fog/whiteout is common in Gothic Basin and can make relocating the descent line very difficult.
Car break-ins have been reported at the Barlow Pass trailhead — don't leave valuables visible in your vehicle.
knownHazards: Loose rock and rockfall hazard throughout the class 3-4 summit block
High-consequence exposure near the notch and final summit scramble, where a fall would be fatal
Snow-filled gullies and a large snowfield beneath the summit crags persisting into early summer
Frequent fog/whiteout complicating route-finding, particularly on the descent

pitchDetail.notes: From the east side of Foggy Lake, ascend a shoulder/southwest ridge area north of the lake on heather and snow slopes.
Climb a snowfield on Del Campo's south slope, then work up left of an obvious gully (which itself cliffs out) on ledges to a notch between the South Buttress and the summit.
From just above the notch, move up the face to a second, smaller notch on the SE buttress; avoid the inviting-looking gully here and take the less-obvious scramble line beside it.
Bypass a roughly 30-ft step and scramble the final terrain to the summit on the peak's NE side.

road.name: Mountain Loop Highway to Monte Cristo Road
road.status: Mountain Loop Highway is paved/maintained to Barlow Pass; Monte Cristo Road beyond the Barlow Pass gate is closed to public vehicles and must be walked (or biked).
road.seasonalGate: Permanent vehicle gate on the Monte Cristo Road at Barlow Pass; the trailhead for Weden Creek Trail #724 is about 1 mile past this gate on foot.
road.driveNote: From Granite Falls, follow the Mountain Loop Highway east about 31 miles (or from the Verlot Public Service Center, about 19.5 miles) to Barlow Pass. Park at the Barlow Pass trailhead (privy on site), then walk south past the gate on the Monte Cristo Road for about 1 mile to the signed Weden Creek/Gothic Basin trailhead on the right, just before a Sauk River crossing.

climate.typical: Fog is frequently reported in Gothic Basin, and weather in the western North Cascades foothills is generally wet and changeable.
climate.spring: Heavy lingering snowpack; ascent gullies and the south-slope snowfield are typically still snow-covered, requiring an ice axe.
climate.summer: The recommended climbing window (July-September); snow can still linger in shaded gullies into early July.
climate.fall: Cooling temperatures with the possibility of early-season snow returning by October.
climate.winter: Deep snowpack and avalanche-prone terrain; not documented as a winter climbing objective.

access.landManager: Mount Baker-Snoqualmie National Forest, Darrington Ranger District (lower approach); upper Gothic Basin (near Foggy Lake) falls within the WA DNR Morningstar Natural Resources Conservation Area.
access.fees: A day-use recreation pass is required at the trailhead; a Recreation.gov scan-and-pay QR option has been available since 2025.
access.passRequired: Northwest Forest Pass or an Interagency (America the Beautiful) Pass.
access.closures: Campfires are prohibited in the basin; Leave No Trace camping is permitted near Foggy Lake.

timing.recommendedStart: Early/dawn start recommended given the long approach and the need to be off the exposed summit block before afternoon fog or storms build.

emergency.sheriffDispatch: Snohomish County Sheriff's Office non-emergency: 425-407-3999 (dial 911 for emergencies); Snohomish County Volunteer Search and Rescue (SCVSAR): 425-388-3328
emergency.rangerStation: Darrington Ranger District, Mt. Baker-Snoqualmie National Forest: (360) 436-1155
emergency.nearestHospital: Cascade Valley Hospital, Arlington, WA (nearest); Providence Regional Medical Center Everett, Everett, WA (regional trauma center)
emergency.notes: This is a remote backcountry trailhead with no documented cell coverage information found; frequent fog in Gothic Basin can significantly delay or complicate any rescue response.

hierarchyNote: Peak identity is solid: Wikipedia's coordinates for Cathedral Rock, WA (47°33'19"N 121°08'08"W ≈ 47.5553,-121.1356) and its 6,724 ft elevation match the given data almost exactly, and it is a well-documented, unambiguous summit (not to be confused with Cathedral Peak in the Pasayten, or Cathedral Rocks in Yosemite/Colorado). However, the given parentArea "Snoqualmie Pass" is geographically off: Cathedral Rock sits above the Cle Elum River valley near Salmon la Sac/Roslyn, accessed via FR-4330 and the Cathedral Pass Trail out of the Cle Elum Ranger District (Okanogan-Wenatchee National Forest, Alpine Lakes Wilderness, Kittitas County) — roughly 15-20 road miles from the actual Snoqualmie Pass (I-90 summit). Mountain Project itself files it under "Central-East Cascades, Wenatchee & Leavenworth," not the Snoqualmie Pass Area. Both are part of the broader Central Cascades/Alpine Lakes region, so this may be an intentional coarse grouping, but the parent label does not reflect the real trailhead/approach geography and may be worth revisiting.
blurb: Cathedral Rock is a 6,724-foot andesite volcanic plug in the Alpine Lakes Wilderness of Washington's Central Cascades, standing above the Cle Elum River valley near Salmon la Sac and guarding the ridge north of Mount Daniel. Formed as an intrusive andesite complex during the Oligocene and later sculpted by Pleistocene glaciation, it is a striking, easily recognized formation with views toward Stevens Pass and the northern Alpine Lakes region. It was first climbed by local settler James Grieve sometime between 1882 and 1896, according to the Cascade Alpine Guide, and its Class 4 Southwest Face is now one of the most popular scramble/rock outings out of the Cle Elum Ranger District.

routes.overview: The historic and easiest line up Cathedral Rock: hike to Cathedral Pass, scramble up the south ridge/rounded spur to the base of the summit formation, then climb a loose gully and the southwest face to the ridge crest before a short, exposed scramble to the summit.
routes.beta: From Cathedral Pass (or via the Peggy's Pond Trail), scramble up the south ridge/rounded spur to about 6,400 ft where it meets the summit formation. Climb the obvious gully on its upper-left side, left of the South Buttress, then continue up the southwest face to the ridge crest. Most parties choose to rope up for one short, awkward-angle 5th-class pitch off the ridge crest; from there it's a class-4 scramble to the true summit. Rockfall in the gully is the defining hazard of the route, especially once it melts out of snow later in the season.
routes.approach: Drive WA-903 N through Roslyn along Cle Elum Lake to Salmon la Sac (~16-17 miles from Roslyn), then continue on gravel Forest Road 4330 (Cle Elum River Road) roughly 12 miles to the road-end trailhead (shared by the Cathedral Rock and Deception Pass trails, near Tucquala Meadows). Hike the Cathedral Rock Trail past Squaw Lake (~2.5 mi) and subalpine meadows to Cathedral Pass / the PCT junction (~4 mi, ~2,300 ft gain to ~5,550 ft). Continue cross-country/scramble up the south ridge toward the base of the rock formation (~6,400 ft) — about 4 miles and 3,400 ft of total gain from the trailhead to the base of the technical climbing.
routes.descent: Reverse the route or descend via the east/southeast gully.
routes.descentText: From the summit, most parties reverse the roped pitch with one or two double-rope rappels down the climbing gully to natural (tree/horn) anchors, keeping the rope to climber's left to avoid dropping loose rock; some parties instead downclimb the gully. An alternative is to cross to the main east gully and scramble down to the meadows east of the peak. Loose rock is the primary descent hazard — helmets and careful rope management are strongly advised.
routes.itinerary: Car-to-car day trip: drive to the FR-4330 road-end trailhead, hike ~4 miles/2,300 ft to Cathedral Pass, scramble/climb ~300-400 additional vertical feet to the summit (roped pitch + class-4 scrambling), then descend by rappel/downclimb and reverse the approach. Total time is commonly reported as 5-6 hours trailhead-to-summit-to-trailhead for a fit, efficient party.
routes.bail: The route can be backed off from the ridge crest or partway up the gully by downclimbing the class-4/low-5th terrain already climbed, or by building a natural-anchor rappel off a tree/horn/large block; there are no fixed/bolted rappel stations reported.
routes.turnaround: Begin early enough to be off the gully and ridge before the day warms significantly — rockfall risk rises as overnight-frozen rock and any lingering snow patches loosen through the day, and the route offers little shelter from afternoon thunderstorms common to the Cascades in summer.
routes.bestSeason: Climbed spring through summer, snow-and-melt-out dependent; the approach trail is noted as best from late summer into fall for drier footing, open meadows, and (per WTA) huckleberries and late-season wildflowers, but the gully itself carries the least rockfall hazard earlier in the season while still holding snow.
routes.rappels: 1-2 double-rope rappels down the climbing gully to natural (tree/horn) anchors, or downclimb the route
routes.fa: James Grieve, sometime between 1882 and 1896 (per the Cascade Alpine Guide) — this is the historic original/easiest line up the peak, though the FA is documented for the peak overall rather than pitch-by-pitch for this specific route.
routes.detailedRack: A light rack covers the single roped pitch: a small set of nuts and cams (finger-to-hand sizes) is sufficient for the awkward-angle 5th-class section off the ridge crest. Bring extra slings/webbing for a natural-anchor (tree, horn, or large block) rappel on descent, since no fixed anchors are reported.
routes.proNeeds: Minimal — one short technical pitch protects with a small rack; the bigger protection concern is helmet use and careful movement through the loose gully rather than sustained gear placement.
gear: small rack of nuts and cams
whatToBring: Sturdy approach shoes or light boots
Map/compass or GPS for the cross-country scramble section above Cathedral Pass
Extra water — limited reliable water sources above Squaw Lake
Layers for exposed ridge crest and afternoon weather
proTips: Rope up for the ridge-crest pitch even though it is short — the angle is awkward and the exposure is real.
Climbing early season while the gully still holds some snow can reduce rockfall hazard compared to a fully melted-out late-summer gully.

The cross-country route-finding from Cathedral Pass up the south ridge to the base of the formation is as much a part of the route as the climbing — take note of the line on the way up for the descent.
watchOut: Serious rockfall hazard in the main gully, worse as the season progresses and snow melts out.
Loose, friable rock on the ridge crest and summit scramble — test holds carefully.
No fixed rappel anchors; natural anchors (trees/horns/blocks) must be assessed on the spot.
Afternoon thunderstorms are common in the Cascades in summer with little shelter on the exposed upper mountain.
knownHazards: The gully used to gain the southwest face funnels rockfall and is described by multiple sources as seriously hazardous, particularly once snow-free.
Loose rock persists through the ridge-crest pitch and summit scramble.
The approach above Cathedral Pass to the base of the formation is cross-country scrambling with real route-finding and fall-hazard exposure, not maintained trail.

pitchDetail.notes: South ridge/rounded spur from Cathedral Pass area to ~6,400 ft, where it meets the base of the summit rock formation.

pitchDetail.pitch: Gully + Southwest Face to ridge crest
pitchDetail.notes: Climb the gully on its upper-left side, left of the South Buttress, then the southwest face to the ridge crest; most parties rope up for an awkward-angle move here. Rockfall hazard is highest in this section.
Short scramble from the ridge crest to the true summit; watch for loose rock.

road.name: Cle Elum River Road / Forest Road 4330
road.status: Gravel/dirt, maintained to the road-end trailhead near Tucquala Meadows
road.seasonalGate: Higher-elevation Cascades forest road; typically inaccessible or snow-covered in winter, opening as snow melts in late spring — check current Cle Elum Ranger District conditions before driving.
road.driveNote: From Roslyn, follow WA-903 N along Cle Elum Lake roughly 16-17 miles to Salmon la Sac (staying right past the guard station/campground), then continue on gravel FR-4330 about 12 miles to the road-end trailhead shared by the Cathedral Rock and Deception Pass trails.

climate.spring: Trail and gully often still hold snow; can reduce rockfall risk but adds snow-travel hazard.
climate.summer: Primary climbing season; warm and generally dry with a real chance of Cascades afternoon thunderstorms.
climate.fall: WTA notes late-summer-into-fall as good hiking conditions on the approach trail (drier footing, huckleberries, late wildflowers); nights get cold at this elevation.
climate.winter: Not a winter climbing objective; access road is high-elevation gravel and typically snow-affected.

access.landManager: Okanogan-Wenatchee National Forest, Cle Elum Ranger District — Alpine Lakes Wilderness
access.fees: Northwest Forest Pass (or America the Beautiful/National Park Pass) required for trailhead parking
access.permit: Free, self-issue wilderness permit at the trailhead
access.passRequired: Northwest Forest Pass / America the Beautiful Pass

timing.recommendedStart: Early morning start recommended to minimize afternoon thunderstorm exposure and catch the gully while rock/snow are still cold and more stable.

emergency.sheriffDispatch: Kittitas County Sheriff's Office, (509) 962-7525; 911 dispatch handled by KITTCOM (Kittitas County Emergency Communications)
emergency.rangerStation: Cle Elum Ranger District, 803 W. 2nd Street, Cle Elum, WA 98922, (509) 852-1100
emergency.nearestHospital: Kittitas Valley Healthcare (full ER), 603 S Chestnut St, Ellensburg, WA 98926, (509) 962-9841 — roughly 30-40 min drive from Cle Elum/Roslyn; KVH also runs an urgent care (non-ER) in Cle Elum at 214 W 1st St.
emergency.notes: No cell coverage should be assumed once off FR-4330 and into the Alpine Lakes Wilderness; this is a remote, cross-country alpine rock objective and rescues from the summit block have occurred (documented Kittitas County SAR responses in this immediate area).

blurb: Mount Pugh (native name Da Klagwats, 7,201 ft) sits near the western edge of the North Cascades above the confluence of the White Chuck and Sauk Rivers, within the Mt. Baker-Snoqualmie National Forest near Darrington, Washington. The North Cascades here are built from accreted terranes and heavily glacially scoured metamorphic/volcanic rock, and Pugh's summit is renowned for one of the range's best panoramas — Glacier Peak, Sloan Peak, Three Fingers, Monte Cristo, and (on clear days) Baker, Shuksan, Rainier, and the Olympics. Nels Bruseth made the first recorded ascent in 1916 while serving as a fire lookout; the mountain's dramatic knife-edge northwest ridge trail was hand-built in 1918-19 by Harry Bedal and Nels Skaar and remains the standard line to the top.

routes.overview: The standard and only established route on Mount Pugh: an old-growth forest trail (built 1918-19 as a fire-lookout supply route) climbs past Lake Metan to Stujack Pass, then a steep, exposed Class 2-3 scramble follows the razorback northwest ridge to the true summit.
routes.beta: Trail #644 gains roughly 5,300 ft in about 5.5-5.7 miles one-way. The first ~1.5-1.8 miles switchback through dense old-growth forest to Lake Metan (3,260 ft), the last reliable water. Beyond the lake, longer switchbacks climb through subalpine meadows to Stujack Pass (5,700-5,750 ft), a common turnaround point for hikers uncomfortable with exposure. From the pass, an unroped scramble follows the knife-edge NW ridge — including a permanent snowfield, a narrow 'knife's edge' section, and an iron ring bolted into the rock at one exposed move — to the 7,201-ft summit and former fire-lookout site.
routes.approach: From Darrington, drive the Mountain Loop Highway (SR-530) east approximately 12.5-14 miles to Forest Road 2095 (signed 'Mt. Pugh Trail'), turn left/east, and continue about 1-1.5 miles to trailhead parking on the right at a sharp switchback. Parking is limited to a few small turnouts; there are no facilities. The trail (#644) climbs steeply through old-growth forest, passing two creek crossings in the first mile, to Lake Metan around 1.5-1.8 miles.
routes.descent: Reverse the ascent line: downclimb the ridge scramble back to Stujack Pass, then descend Trail #644 to the trailhead.
routes.descentText: There is no separate descent route — down-climb the same NW ridge scramble back to Stujack Pass, taking care on loose rock and the snowfield/knife-edge section, which is often more intimidating in descent. From the pass, follow the maintained trail back down through the meadows and old-growth forest past Lake Metan to the trailhead. Do not follow cairns above Stujack Pass on the descent; several documented trip reports describe cairns leading hikers toward vertical cliff bands requiring Class 4 downclimbing to regain the correct trail — stay on the obvious, worn climbers' path instead.
routes.itinerary: Almost always done car-to-car in a single long day; a strong party can do it in under 8 hours, but 9-10+ hours is typical given the sustained 5,300 ft of gain and exposed ridge scrambling. Some parties camp at Lake Metan to split the effort.
routes.bail: Stujack Pass is the natural and commonly used bailout/turnaround point — below it the route is a straightforward maintained trail with no exposure. Above the pass, the ridge scramble has limited safe bail options once committed to the knife-edge section; retreat the way you came rather than attempting to find an alternate line.
routes.turnaround: Set a firm turnaround time before starting the exposed ridge scramble above Stujack Pass — afternoon thunderstorms, wet rock, or lingering snow can make the knife-edge section far more dangerous later in the day, and the long descent still requires daylight and energy in reserve.
routes.bestSeason: Best climbed July through September, once the upper snowfield has consolidated or melted out and the ridge scramble is dry. Snow can persist above ~4,500-5,000 ft into early summer, and the route is notably harder and more dangerous with snow or in wet/adverse weather, when it is 'best left to those with mountaineering experience.' Access is also gated seasonally (see road notes).
objHaz: loose rock on the upper ridge
unreliable cairns leading toward cliff bands
avalanche debris crossing the trail in spring
cornices/wind-loaded snow lingering into early summer
routes.detailedRack: No technical rock gear is needed for the standard summer scramble — it is entirely unroped Class 2-3 terrain. When snow lingers on the upper ridge and summit snowfield (common through June and sometimes into July), carry an ice axe and know how to self-arrest; light crampons or microspikes help on firm morning snow. A helmet is commonly recommended for the loose, exposed final push above Stujack Pass.
routes.proNeeds: Not a roped or protected route: no rack, cams, or fixed anchors are used on the standard line. Protection above Stujack Pass is essentially route-finding, sound footwork on exposed terrain, and — in early season — an ice axe/self-arrest skills on the snowfield.
whatToBring: extra water — Lake Metan/the spring just below it is the last reliable source
sun protection — little shade above treeline and on the open ridge
map/GPS track — cairns above Stujack Pass are unreliable and have led parties toward cliffs
layers for wind exposure on the ridge
microspikes or light crampons in early-season snow conditions
proTips: Start very early — the 5,300 ft of gain plus exposed ridge scrambling go much better in cool morning temperatures and with a safety margin before afternoon storms.
Don't trust cairns above Stujack Pass; several documented routes of cairns lead toward vertical cliff bands. Stay on the obvious, well-worn climbers' trail.
Fill water at Lake Metan (or the spring just below it) — it's the last dependable water on the route.
Descend the exact line you ascended; the upper ridge has false leads and cliff bands that are easy to wander into, especially in descent or low visibility.
watchOut: The razorback/knife-edge sections above Stujack Pass carry real exposure — a slip could mean a long, consequential fall.
A permanent snowfield sits just below the summit; it can be firm/icy in the morning and is a common site of slips.
An iron ring is bolted into the rock partway up the final scramble, marking one of the more exposed hand-and-foot moves.
Stujack Pass is the recommended turnaround for hikers, those with pets, or anyone uncomfortable with unroped exposure — the terrain above is meaningfully harder than the trail below.
knownHazards: Avalanche debris paths cross parts of the trail in spring and early summer.
Cornices and wind-loaded snow can linger on the summit ridge into early summer.
Forest Road 2095 is a narrow, single-lane unpaved spur with very few pullouts, adding driving risk in poor visibility or when meeting oncoming traffic.
The Mountain Loop Highway has experienced washouts in recent years that can add significant driving distance or close access entirely — check current road status before heading out.

pitchDetail.notes: ~1.5-1.8 mi, steep switchbacks through old-growth forest; two creek crossings in the first mile; Lake Metan (3,260 ft) is the last reliable water.
Long, gradual switchbacks through subalpine meadow to the pass (~4.5-4.6 mi, 5,700-5,750 ft); good turnaround for those not continuing to the summit.

Unroped scramble along the exposed NW razorback ridge, including a 'knife's edge' section, a permanent snowfield, and an iron ring marking an exposed move; roughly the final 1-1.5 miles and ~1,450-1,500 ft of gain.

road.name: Forest Road 2095 (Mt. Pugh Spur) off the Mountain Loop Highway (SR-530)
road.status: Narrow, unpaved, single-lane road with very few pullouts
road.seasonalGate: Seasonally gated near the Mountain Loop Hwy junction to protect wildlife, roughly mid-October through early June (exact dates vary; confirm current status with Darrington Ranger District before driving)
road.driveNote: From the Darrington Ranger Station, drive Mountain Loop Highway roughly 12.5-14 miles to FR 2095 (signed 'Mt. Pugh Trail') on the left/east side, then continue about 1-1.5 miles to trailhead parking on the right at a sharp switchback.

climate.typical: Wet, temperate maritime Cascades climate at the trailhead transitioning to alpine conditions on the upper ridge; the route can hold snow well into early summer.
climate.spring: Significant snow typically still covers the upper mountain; avalanche debris can cross the trail and the ridge scramble becomes a snow/ice route requiring mountaineering skills.
climate.summer: Prime season (Jul-Sep) once the upper snowfield has melted out or firmed up; still expect a lingering snow patch near the summit into early summer.
climate.fall: Generally dry and stable through September, with increasing chance of early-season snow on the ridge as fall progresses.
climate.winter: Not a standard winter objective; FR 2095 is typically gated and access/avalanche conditions make the route a serious undertaking.

access.landManager: Mt. Baker-Snoqualmie National Forest (Darrington Ranger District); the upper route lies within the Glacier Peak Wilderness
access.fees: Northwest Forest Pass (or America the Beautiful pass) required for trailhead parking
access.permit: No overnight/wilderness permit required, though standard Glacier Peak Wilderness regulations apply above Stujack Pass
access.passRequired: Northwest Forest Pass
access.closures: FR 2095 is seasonally gated for wildlife protection; the Mountain Loop Highway has been subject to washout closures in recent years — check current Forest Service road status before driving.

timing.recommendedStart: Pre-dawn/first light, given the sustained 5,300 ft of gain and the need to be off the exposed ridge before afternoon weather builds

emergency.sheriffDispatch: Snohomish County Sheriff's Office non-emergency: 425-388-3428 / 911 for emergencies
emergency.rangerStation: Darrington Ranger Station, 1405 Emens Avenue North, Darrington, WA 98241, (360) 436-1155
emergency.nearestHospital: Cascade Valley Hospital (Skagit Regional Health), 330 South Stillaguamish Ave, Arlington, WA 98223, (360) 435-2133 — Level IV Emergency Department; nearest hospital to Darrington/Mountain Loop Hwy
emergency.notes: Remote trailhead with no cell service reported in most trip logs; carry a satellite communicator/PLB. Access via FR 2095 and the Mountain Loop Highway can be affected by seasonal gating and washouts — confirm road status before relying on it for evacuation.

routes.corrections: Mountain Project lists Mount Pugh's elevation as 7,208 ft and catalogs the area's only formal route as 'Pika Slab' (a short roped 5.0 rock route near the trail), distinct from this Stujack Pass scramble line. WTA, Wikipedia, and Peakbagger all corroborate the given 7,201 ft elevation and 48.143/-121.374 coordinates, so no correction was made to the peak's core stats. Trailhead elevation is reported as either ~1,900 ft or ~1,950 ft depending on the source; 1,900 ft (the WTA/Forest-Service-consistent figure) was used here.

blurb: Morning Star Peak (6,020 ft) rises above the Stillaguamish River valley in the Mount Baker-Snoqualmie National Forest along the Mountain Loop Highway corridor of the western North Cascades, near the Glacier Peak Wilderness boundary and grouped with Vesper and Sperry Peaks around Headlee Pass. Its rocky sub-summits — Vega Tower and Eros Tower (aka Vega North Tower) — carry a cluster of modern, fully-bolted multi-pitch sport-alpine routes established between 2015 and 2020, while the main summit itself was first climbed by Sam Heller in 1940 via easy Class 3 scrambling.

routes.overview: The peak's non-technical line: a brushy trail approach followed by a steep Class 3 scramble up the SE ridge to a notch, then a final push (often snow/ice-choked) to the summit.
routes.beta: Widely referred to in guidebooks and by The Mountaineers as the 'South Ridge' route. It follows the Sunrise Mine Trail, then leaves the trail to climb steep talus, brush, and slabs to a notch on Morning Star's southeast ridge (~5,600 ft), crosses through a steep gully on the far side, and finishes on Class 3 rock/scree to the true summit. Rated a true scramble (T5/S4 on The Mountaineers' scale) — comfort with steep exposed rock and snow is required.
routes.approach: From the Verlot Public Service Center (11 mi east of Granite Falls), drive the Mountain Loop Highway east ~17.1 mi, turn right (south) on Sunrise Mine Road (FR 4065), and continue ~2.2 mi to the Sunrise Mine Trailhead. Hike the Sunrise Mine Trail; there is a difficult, sometimes hazardous ford of the South Fork Stillaguamish River around 0.5 mile (an unofficial route on the east bank can sometimes avoid it). Continue toward Headlee Pass, then leave the maintained trail to climb steep talus/brush/slabs toward the notch in the SE ridge (~5,600 ft); the lower section has a reputation for thick brush and route-finding difficulty.
routes.descent: Reverse the ascent route: down-climb through the notch and steep gully, then retrace the brush/talus descent to the Sunrise Mine Trail.
routes.descentText: Descend by reversing the scramble line back through the SE-ridge notch and down the steep gully (commonly holding firm snow/ice well into summer — many parties down-climb facing in with an axe or descend adjacent dry rock instead), then continue down the brushy talus slopes back to the Sunrise Mine Trail and out via the ford.
routes.bail: Retreat is straightforward below the notch by simply reversing the ascent line; above/at the notch, down-climbing the steep gully is the only realistic option since there is no fixed rappel infrastructure on this route.
routes.turnaround: Set a firm turnaround time before committing to the steep gully above the notch — down-climbing it wet, in the dark, or with parties dislodging rock from above adds serious risk; turn around if timing or conditions don't leave a comfortable safety margin.
routes.bestSeason: July through September offers the most favorable weather; the approach brush and the summit gully/notch typically hold snow and ice well into midsummer, so many parties still carry an axe and crampons even in July.
routes.comms: No reliable cell coverage should be expected along the Mountain Loop Highway corridor or anywhere on the mountain; carry a satellite communicator or PLB.
routes.fa: Sam Heller, 1940 (first ascent of the peak, via this easiest Class 3 line)
knownHazards: Difficult, sometimes hazardous ford of the South Fork Stillaguamish River near 0.5 mile
Thick brush and poor route-finding on the lower approach
Loose rock throughout the upper ridge, worsened when other parties are above
A steep (~45°) snow/ice gully above the ~5,600 ft notch that often holds firm neve or ice into summer
Exposed Class 3 scrambling near the summit
watchOut: Ice can lurk under fresh snow near the notch even in season — crampons help avoid slipping
The gully on the far side of the notch is frequently steep and icy
Loose rock is common near the top, especially with other climbing parties above
whatToBring: Northwest Forest Pass for trailhead parking
Map/compass or GPS — brush and route-finding are the crux of this line
Trekking poles for the brush and river ford
Extra layers — the notch/gully can hold snow into summer
proTips: An unofficial trail on the east bank of the South Fork Stillaguamish can sometimes be used to avoid the harder ford near 0.5 mile
Some parties bypass the icy gully above the notch by climbing adjacent dry rock instead

access.landManager: Mount Baker-Snoqualmie National Forest, Darrington Ranger District
access.permit: Northwest Forest Pass required to park at the Sunrise Mine Trailhead
access.passRequired: Northwest Forest Pass / America the Beautiful interagency pass

road.name: Sunrise Mine Road (FR 4065)
road.driveNote: From Verlot Public Service Center, drive Mountain Loop Highway east ~17.1 mi, turn right (south) onto FR 4065, and continue ~2.2 mi to the Sunrise Mine Trailhead.

climate.forecastZone: NWS 'West Slopes North Central Cascades and Passes' zone (WAZ568)
climate.typical: Marine west-coast climate with heavy winter precipitation; Pacific fronts drop moisture via orographic lift on the west-slope Cascades.
climate.spring: Trail, ford, and summit gully typically remain snow-covered into midsummer.
climate.summer: Best window; still expect a steep snow/ice gully above the notch into July.
climate.fall: Cool and increasingly wet; shortening daylight for a long scramble day.
climate.winter: Full winter snowpack; not a standard-route season.

emergency.sheriffDispatch: Snohomish County Sheriff's Office, supported by Snohomish County Volunteer Search & Rescue (SCVSAR)
emergency.rangerStation: Darrington Ranger District, 1405 Emens Ave N, Darrington, WA 98241, (360) 436-1155
emergency.nearestHospital: Cascade Valley Hospital (Skagit Regional Health), 330 S Stillaguamish Ave, Arlington, WA 98223, (360) 435-2133, 24-hr ED
emergency.notes: No cell coverage expected along the Mountain Loop Highway or on-mountain; carry a satellite communicator/PLB.

routes.corrections: The DB route name 'Standard Scramble' corresponds to what guidebooks and The Mountaineers formally call the 'South Ridge' (also described as the southeast ridge) route — same line, different naming convention.

routes.overview: A modern classic: seven pitches of sustained, fully-bolted face and arête climbing up the sun-drenched southwest arête of Eros Tower (Vega North Tower), a 5,280 ft sub-summit just north of Morning Star Peak proper.
routes.beta: Excellent, solid rock, an outstanding summit position, and a quick, easy approach/descent have made this the area's most-climbed line (250+ logged ascents). Fully bolted — a rack of quickdraws and a rope is all that's needed. Grades run 5.8-5.10a across 7 pitches on the crest of the pillar.
routes.approach: From the Sunrise Mine Trailhead, hike the Sunrise Mine Trail toward Headlee Basin, crossing creeks along the way. Leave the trail at the last major switchback (~4,300 ft) before it zigzags up to Headlee Pass, and make an easy, cairned traverse northeast across talus and heather to the base of the Mile High buttress — about 2.5-2.8 miles one-way from the trailhead.
routes.descent: Eight rappels down the route on a single 60m rope (or two 60m ropes/70m single for larger step-downs).
routes.descentText: Descend via 8 rappels using the route's bolted anchors — one intermediate station is set partway down Pitch 3. Follow the documented anchor sequence carefully to avoid crossing the active climbing line or triggering rockfall onto parties below; carrying a printed description of the rap sequence is recommended.
routes.bail: Any belay/rap anchor along the route can be used to bail with a single rope; the fully-bolted nature of the line makes retreat straightforward from nearly any point.
routes.turnaround: Given the ~8-10 hour car-to-car day, set a turnaround time that keeps the 8-rappel descent and talus descent out of full darkness.
routes.bestSeason: Best after the spring snowpack has receded; approach shoes are fine for the talus approach after roughly July 4th or once the gully snow is gone. A small seep flows through mid-season and for a few days after rain.
routes.comms: No reliable cell coverage expected along the Mountain Loop Highway or on the mountain; carry a satellite communicator.
routes.face: Southwest arête, Eros Tower (Vega North Tower)
routes.fa: Darin Berdinka and Rad Roberts, September 12, 2015
routes.rappels: 8 rappels, single 60m rope
knownHazards: Loose rock on ledges, especially Pitch 7
Rockfall risk in the approach gully/talus
Steep spring snow on the approach before it melts out
watchOut: Falls are undesirable given the ledgy terrain throughout
Pitch 7 has notably loose rock near the top
proTips: Approach shoes work fine on the talus after early July once snow is gone
A couple of alpine draws cut down on rope drag on the wandering upper pitches
Bring the published rappel-anchor sequence to avoid crossing the live climbing line on descent
routes.detailedRack: Fully bolted sport-alpine route — no cams or nuts required. Carry about a dozen quickdraws plus 2-3 alpine draws for drag management, and a single 60m rope (sufficient for all 8 raps) or two 60m/one 70m rope for faster double-rope rappels.
routes.proNeeds: Bolts only; a dozen quickdraws covers all 7 pitches.
whatToBring: 3-4L water (only a seasonal seep en route)
Ice axe/crampons in early season for approach snow

routes.overview: A longer, harder, more sustained alpine sport line running straight up the West Face of Eros Tower (Vega North Tower) for 11 pitches (8 of them 5.10 or harder), finishing on the same summit as Mile High Club.
routes.beta: Significantly longer, harder, and more committing than its neighbor Mile High Club, with sustained 5.10 climbing across varied terrain — faces, corners, roofs, and slabs — and outstanding mountain views throughout.
routes.approach: Follow the Sunrise Mine Trail approximately 2.5 miles to the last major switchback (~4,300 ft), then traverse northeast across talus toward Eros/Vega North Tower, following cairns. The route starts on a dark gray face above the largest boulders in the talus field; a fresh-water seep is available near the red rock in the Mile High gully.
routes.descent: Recommended: rappel the neighboring Mile High Club with a single 60m rope rather than the route itself.
routes.descentText: Because Pitch 1 alone is longer than 50m, rappelling Beyond Redlining directly is awkward and would require two ropes or careful single-rope angled rappels; most parties instead descend via the 8 bolted rappels on Mile High Club.
routes.bail: Retreat is complicated below the upper pitches given the long, runout first pitch — descending via the Mile High Club anchors (once reached) is the standard escape.
routes.turnaround: This is a long, committing day (11 pitches plus a shared descent) — start early enough that the Mile High Club rappel descent isn't pushed into darkness.
routes.bestSeason: Snow and wet conditions are possible in spring but the west face dries quickly after winter; the face stays shaded/cold until roughly 1 PM, so plan for a late-morning warm-up on route.
routes.comms: No reliable cell coverage expected; carry a satellite communicator.
routes.face: West Face, Eros Tower (Vega North Tower)
routes.fa: Rad Roberts and Kurt Hicks, July 2020
knownHazards: Loose rock in places
Steep snow possible on the approach in spring

watchOut: Pitch 1 is over 50m long, making a route rappel impractical — descend via Mile High Club instead

routes.detailedRack: 17 quickdraws plus 3-6 alpine draws to manage rope drag on the wandering line; a 60m rope suffices to climb, but 70m is recommended if any retreat off-route is anticipated.
routes.proNeeds: Fully bolted; no trad gear required, but bring enough draws (17+) given the bolt-dense, sustained pitches.
whatToBring: Water (seep available in the Mile High gully)

routes.overview: A seven-pitch, fully-bolted sport-alpine line up the skyline (west) ridge of Vega Tower, working through small roofs and overlaps before becoming increasingly exposed as it skirts the tower's north face in the upper pitches.
routes.beta: 800 feet, Grade III 5.10b, with well-protected cruxes though bolts are spaced further apart on easier (5.9-and-under) terrain. Prior to this route, only one other technical line (Starshot Ridge, 5.8, established 1981) existed on Vega Tower, and the west ridge itself was unclimbed until 2016/2017.
routes.approach: Follow the Sunrise Mine Trail about 2.5 miles to the last major switchback (~4,300 ft), then make the easy, cairned traverse northeast across talus toward the Mile High buttress/Vega Tower base, passing through trees and talus fields.
routes.descent: Rappel the route using a 70m rope; bolted anchors are spaced roughly 30m apart.
routes.descentText: Descend via the route's bolted rap line with a single 70m rope; watch for rock pinches on the ropes and loose terrain around some of the anchor stances.
routes.bail: Any bolted anchor can serve as a bail point given the fully-bolted nature of the climb.
routes.turnaround: Plan around a full climbing day plus the shared talus approach/descent; retreat via the bolted anchors rather than pushing on if conditions or time run short.
routes.bestSeason: Prime midsummer through early fall once approach snow has cleared.
routes.comms: No reliable cell coverage expected; carry a satellite communicator.
routes.face: West ridge (skyline ridge) of Vega Tower, skirting the north face in the upper pitches
routes.fa: Morgan Zentler and Shelia Zentler, September 16, 2017 (per the American Alpine Journal; Mountain Project lists September 2016 — the AAJ date is treated as authoritative here)

blurb: Mount Goode (9,220 ft) is the highest peak within North Cascades National Park, rising in the range's remote core between the Skagit River and Lake Chelan — invisible from any road and reached only by long, multi-day backcountry approaches. Built of solid alpine gneiss, it carries the fourth-highest non-volcanic summit in Washington and offers three major established lines of very different character: the class-4 Southwest Couloir (the 1936 first-ascent route), the classic Northeast Buttress rock route (Beckey/Stewart, 1966), and the long, rarely repeated Megalodon Ridge (Herrington/Wertkin, 2007).
hierarchyNote: None — Mount Goode's name, coordinates (48.4829, -120.9109), and 9,220 ft elevation all match the well-documented peak in North Cascades National Park (Chelan County), and "North Cascades Core" is a geographically plausible parent grouping for it. No mismatch found.

routes.overview: A spring snow-and-ice line up the broad Northeast Face of Goode via the Goode Glacier — steep (50-55°), largely unroped snow/névé climbing below and beside the rocky crest of the adjacent Northeast Buttress rock route — typically paired with a ski or snowboard descent of the South Face into Park Creek.
routes.beta: Documented mainly through ski-mountaineering trip reports rather than a rock-climbing guidebook line. Parties skin/climb the Goode Glacier onto the face proper, transitioning to crampons on firm, steep snow or alpine ice (roughly 2,000 ft at 50-55°) as the angle steepens toward the upper mountain and the Southeast Peak. It is a distinct, less-traveled line from the summer rock Northeast Buttress route on the same general aspect.
routes.approach: Two documented approaches: (1) from the Rainy Pass trailhead on SR 20 (elev. 4,880 ft), follow the PCT south and the North Fork Bridge Creek trail toward the Goode Glacier basin; or (2) via the Lake Chelan passenger ferry to Stehekin, vehicle/shuttle up the Stehekin River Road to High Bridge, then trail travel (~5 miles) to a camp near 3,200 ft below the East and North Faces, continuing up toward the glacier and face.
routes.descent: South Face, dropping through amphitheaters, chutes, and ridges toward the Park Creek drainage — a long, complex descent that does not reverse the ascent line.
routes.descentText: Parties have descended the South Face by skiing/riding through a series of amphitheaters, ridges, rollers, chutes, and trees down to the Park Creek valley bottom. This descent is long and route-finding-intensive; at least one documented party bivied unplanned in Park Creek when the descent ran into darkness before exiting the following day.
routes.bail: Retreat down the ascent line is more straightforward low on the face but becomes harder once established on the steeper upper snow/ice; downclimbing or rappelling back the way you came is generally a safer bail than committing to the unfamiliar South Face descent if conditions deteriorate.
routes.turnaround: Set a firm turnaround time before departure based on the steep, avalanche- and icefall-exposed terrain and the long, complex descent — being caught on the face or in the Goode Glacier icefall zone after the snow softens or weather turns is the primary risk driver.
routes.bestSeason: Best in spring (roughly April-May) when the face is loaded with consolidated snow rather than bare, rockfall-prone summer scree or hard late-season ice; a stable, cold weather window is important to avoid rapid icing or wet-slide hazard on the face.
objHaz: Crevasses and seracs on the Goode Glacier
Rapid icing of the face with changing weather
Avalanche hazard on the approach and face
Cliff bands requiring careful route-finding on the South Face descent
routes.detailedRack: Minimal rack — this is primarily a snow/ice line rather than a rock route. A short section of rope plus a few pickets or ice screws for glacier crevasse crossings or belaying the steepest ice sections is typical; most of the face is soloed on good snow.
routes.proNeeds: Protection needs are mainly for glacier travel (crevasse rescue gear) and any belayed sections on the steepest ice; the bulk of the face is climbed unroped when conditions are firm and consolidated.
whatToBring: skis/splitboard or snowboard if descending the South Face on snow
bivy gear (an unplanned bivy in Park Creek is a real possibility)
extra layers for variable spring weather
map/GPS for the complex South Face descent route-finding
proTips: Firm, overnight-refrozen snow gives the fastest, safest climbing on the face — plan an early start

Access via the Lake Chelan ferry to Stehekin can shorten the approach compared to the Rainy Pass/PCT route
watchOut: The face can ice up quickly, turning a moderate snow climb into serious front-pointing on hard névé/ice
The South Face descent is long and complex and has led to unplanned bivies when parties ran out of daylight

climate.summer: The face typically melts out to bare rock/scree by summer, favoring the rock Northeast Buttress route instead

routes.corrections: The database lists a distinct 'Northeast Face' route separate from the 'Northeast Buttress.' Research confirms these are indeed different lines: the Northeast Buttress is the well-documented summer rock route (Beckey/Stewart, 1966, 5.5), while the Northeast Face is a separate, far less-documented spring snow/ice mountaineering line up the broader face via the Goode Glacier, typically descended via the South Face into Park Creek. No formal first-ascent record was found for the Northeast Face as a distinct line — it is documented mainly through ski-mountaineering trip reports (e.g., a 2011 Hummel/Miller/McAllister trip, and a separate account climbing what one author called the 'Northeast Couloir').

routes.overview: Goode's classic alpine rock route: a beautiful, glacier-guarded buttress of clean gneiss climbed for over 2,800 feet from the toe near the Goode Glacier straight to the summit, combining a long wilderness approach with sustained, moderate (mostly 3rd-4th class, up to 5.5) climbing.
routes.beta: From a bivy near the Goode Glacier, cross the glacier (often heavily crevassed by late season) to the toe of the buttress, negotiate the moat/bergschrund (frequently the technical crux of the whole route via a jump, downclimb, or short rappel), then climb broad 3rd-4th class terrain onto the buttress crest. The crest gives the most aesthetic climbing; partway up the buttress blends into the face and the climbing stiffens to low-5th and reported unprotectable 5.7 slabs near 8,000 ft, easing again on the narrow, exposed summit ridge.
routes.approach: From the Rainy Pass trailhead on SR 20 (elev. 4,880 ft), hike the PCT south about 10 miles to the North Fork Bridge Creek Trail junction (~2,800 ft), then follow the North Fork of Bridge Creek trail — well-groomed at first, becoming overgrown — to a thigh-deep ford of the creek near Grizzly Creek. Continue up slabs to the right of the leftmost waterfall to a camp near the toe of the Goode Glacier (roughly 12-15 miles one-way to the base of the route, per multiple trip reports).
routes.descent: Three rappels from the summit to Black Tooth Notch, then (after an exposed 4th-class move and simulclimb across the notch's ledge) two rappels down the loose notch itself and three more single-rope rappels into the Southwest Couloir, followed by loose class-3 downclimbing into the basin below and a long hike back out via Bridge Creek/PCT.
routes.descentText: The standard descent used by parties reaching the true summit: rappel from summit anchors toward Black Tooth Notch (roughly 3 raps, the first fairly direct, the next two increasingly traversing skier's right), cross the loose, exposed notch (often 2 more short raps, ~30m then ~15m), then make 3 single-rope rappels into the Southwest Couloir. Where the couloir ends in cliffs, traverse left onto easier terrain and back into the basin below the gully. Rock quality on this descent is notably loose ('garbage' in one trip report) — helmets on, and expect a long, multi-hour exit day (10+ hours reported for the full 20-24 mile hike out).
routes.itinerary: Day 1: hike in (~12-15 miles) to a camp near the Goode Glacier. Day 2: cross the glacier, climb the buttress to the summit (often bivying on or near the summit — a spectacular but exposed spot). Day 3-4: descend via Black Tooth Notch/Southwest Couloir and hike the long approach trail back out.
routes.bail: There is no good bail option once established on the buttress — rappel stations are sparse until near the top, and the terrain and loose rock make downclimbing unattractive. Retreat is easiest before committing to the glacier-to-rock transition at the toe of the buttress.
routes.turnaround: Given the long approach, glacier crossing, and committing nature of the upper buttress, set a turnaround time that keeps the summit ridge and rappel descent from happening after dark or in deteriorating weather.
routes.bestSeason: Late July through September — climbing is generally best after about the third week of July, once the moat/bergschrund at the glacier-to-buttress transition has opened enough to cross safely; accessing the toe of the buttress becomes progressively harder later in the season as the glacier recedes.
routes.fa: Fred Beckey and Tom Stewart, August 6, 1966
routes.rappels: About 5-6 rappels total: 3 from the summit to Black Tooth Notch, 2 short raps through the notch itself (~30m then ~15m), then 3 more single-rope rappels into the Southwest Couloir
objHaz: Crevassed glacier approach with a variable moat/bergschrund
Loose rock on the descent gully
Afternoon serac/rockfall exposure on the Goode Glacier
Sparse bail/rappel options on the buttress itself
routes.detailedRack: A small alpine rack: BD Camalots or equivalent 0.3-1 (doubles in 0.5/0.75 recommended by multiple parties), a set of small-to-mid nuts, a few Aliens/microcams, and one 60m rope (or twin ropes doubled for rappels and simul-climbing). Rock shoes are not required — most climbers wear approach shoes or light trail runners for the whole route.
routes.proNeeds: Gear is mostly for protecting low-5th-class terrain and simul-climbing sections; bring more small-to-mid cams and fewer large pieces — several trip reports specifically note oversized racks going unused.

routes.corrections: Grade is cited variably across sources — Beckey's Cascade Alpine Guide and several trip reports describe it as Grade III-IV, 5.4-5.5, while Mountain Project's structured grade field shows 5.5/IV+; used 5.5 (the most consistently cited YDS grade) with alpineGrade IV and commitment III-IV to reflect the range. Given peak data (name, coordinates, elevation, parent area) was accurate and required no correction.

routes.overview: The original 1936 first-ascent line and the least technical route up Goode: a broad class-4 snow/scree couloir on the southwest side, sometimes finished via a traverse across the upper Northeast Face near the summit block where routes converge.
routes.beta: A broad couloir with easy scrambling when snow-filled; once melted out, a band of solid, stepped darker rock (the 'black stairs') on the left side offers easier passage than the surrounding loose rock. The couloir steepens near the notch between the summit and the West Tower, and the final summit block is class 4 and locally low 5th class depending on the exact line taken — some parties finish by crossing Black Tooth Notch and traversing the exposed upper Northeast Face to reach the true summit.
routes.approach: From the Rainy Pass trailhead on SR 20, hike the PCT south to the North Fork Bridge Creek Trail and up toward the Goode Glacier basin (roughly 15 miles one-way to a high camp per one documented trip); alternatively, approach from the Stehekin side via the Park Creek Trail (about 4 miles) to Goode Creek Bench (~7,200 ft), though this trail has been closed by wildfire in some years (e.g., the 2019 Goode Fire) — verify current NPS trail status before relying on it.
routes.descent: Reverse the couloir; in good snow conditions many parties glissade or ski the apron below the couloir rather than downclimb the loose rock.
routes.descentText: The couloir is generally downclimbed or glissaded/skied the way it was ascended. Once snow has melted out, expect loose rock, particularly in the gully between the summit and West Tower; the 'black stairs' rock band on the left offers a more solid downclimbing option than the surrounding terrain.
routes.itinerary: Day 1: hike in to a high camp (~15 miles from the Rainy Pass trailhead, or shorter via Park Creek from Stehekin when open). Day 2: summit push via the couloir (roughly 11-mile round trip, ~5,300 ft gain from camp per one documented ascent) and return to camp. Day 3-4: hike out.
routes.bail: Retreat down the couloir is straightforward in early season with adequate snow cover; late-season, loose rock and the steepening notch below the summit require more careful downclimbing — the 'black stairs' rock band offers the most solid retreat line if the couloir itself is out of condition.
routes.turnaround: Given the long approach and afternoon cloud/thunderstorm buildup common on the peak, plan to be off the summit block by late morning to early afternoon and set a turnaround time accordingly.
routes.bestSeason: Snow generally fills the couloir into July, giving firmer, faster travel on foot or ski; by late season the couloir can melt out to loose rock, turning the route into more of a class-4/low-5th scramble. A fall ascent can bring verglas/ice on the upper mountain and colder, windier conditions.
routes.fa: Wolf Bauer, Philip Dickert, Joe Halwax, Jack Hossack, and George MacGowan, July 5, 1936 (first ascent of the peak, via this route)
objHaz: Rockfall in the couloir, especially once snow cover thins
Steep snow/ice requiring crampons and axe well into summer
Glacier travel hazards where the approach edges the Goode Glacier
Icy conditions after cold snaps, even in September
routes.detailedRack: A light rack: a few slings and a small selection of rock protection for the occasional class-4/low-5th moves on the summit block; glacier travel gear (rope, crampons, axe) if the approach crosses the edge of the Goode Glacier. Most parties carry a rope more for security and any short rappels than for sustained protection.
routes.proNeeds: Protection needs are minimal — this is primarily a scramble with occasional 4th/low-5th-class moves; a rope is mainly insurance for steep snow sections and any short rappels on descent.
gear: light rack/slings for occasional class 4-5 moves
whatToBring: trekking poles for the long approach
bivy gear for a multi-day trip
sun protection for long snow/glacier slopes
proTips: The couloir is easiest with continuous snow underfoot — once it melts out, look for the 'black stairs,' a band of solid, stepped darker rock on the left that's easier than the surrounding loose rock
Glissading or skiing the apron below the couloir is far more enjoyable than descending on foot
Park Creek Trail is sometimes closed by wildfire — check current NPS trail status before committing to that approach
watchOut: Loose rock throughout the couloir and in the gully between the summit and West Tower
Icy conditions can develop quickly after a cold snap, even in September
Significant wind exposure near the summit ridge
Glacier travel hazards (crevasses, moat) where the approach crosses the edge of Goode Glacier
knownHazards: Rockfall in the couloir, especially once snow cover thins
Steep snow/ice requiring crampons and axe well into summer
Long, remote approach complicates any rescue
Wildfire-related trail closures (e.g., Park Creek Trail) have affected access in some years

pitchDetail.notes: Broad couloir, easy scrambling on snow or the 'black stairs' rock band
Steepens near the notch between the summit and West Tower
Final scramble to the true summit; some parties finish via a traverse across the upper Northeast Face near Black Tooth Notch

road.name: North Cascades Highway (SR 20)
road.status: Open seasonally, typically mid-May through early December (weather-dependent)
road.seasonalGate: Closes for winter between the Ross Dam trailhead (MP 134) and Silver Star Gate (MP 171); closure points can extend to the Newhalem (MP 120) and Early Winters (MP 178) gates as conditions dictate
road.driveNote: Rainy Pass North Trailhead (PCT) is reached via SR 20 about 35 miles west of Winthrop; large paved parking area at 4,880 ft. The Park Creek Trail alternative is reached via the Stehekin shuttle from High Bridge.

climate.typical: Cold, wet winters with heavy snowpack; a short, drier alpine window roughly June-September
climate.spring: Deep, often unstable snowpack fills the couloir; fords and approach trails are swollen with snowmelt
climate.summer: Best travel window; couloir snow cover diminishes as summer progresses, exposing loose rock
climate.fall: Couloir may be melted out to loose rock; cold snaps can bring verglas/ice to the upper mountain
climate.winter: SR 20 closed; approach becomes a major winter mountaineering expedition

access.landManager: North Cascades National Park Service Complex (NPS); the Rainy Pass trailhead itself sits on Okanogan-Wenatchee National Forest land
access.fees: Northwest Forest Pass / Interagency pass required at the Rainy Pass trailhead ($5/day or $30/annual)
access.permit: NPS wilderness/backcountry permit required for overnight stays; peak-season permits reservable via recreation.gov ($26/trip), with free self-issue permits available when the Wilderness Information Center is closed (roughly October-April)
access.passRequired: Northwest Forest Pass at the Rainy Pass trailhead; NPS backcountry permit for camping
access.closures: SR 20 closed in winter (roughly December-May); Park Creek Trail (Stehekin-side approach) has been closed by wildfire in some seasons — check current NPS status

timing.recommendedStart: Early alpine start from high camp to summit before afternoon cloud buildup

emergency.county: Chelan County (summit and most of the peak); the Rainy Pass trailhead approach passes through Okanogan County on SR 20
emergency.sheriffDispatch: Chelan County Sheriff non-emergency (509) 667-6851; RiverCom 911 dispatches Chelan County law/fire/EMS — call 911 for emergencies
emergency.rangerStation: North Cascades NPS Wilderness Information Center, Marblemount, (360) 854-7245; Golden West Visitor Center, Stehekin
emergency.nearestHospital: Lake Chelan Community Hospital, 503 E Highland Ave, Chelan, WA — (509) 682-2531
emergency.notes: No cell coverage in the Bridge Creek or Park Creek basins; this is a remote, multi-day approach — a satellite communicator and self-rescue capability are strongly advised.

routes.corrections: Wikipedia describes this as the combined 'Southwest Couloir/Southeast Ridge' route; one detailed trip report explicitly following 'the Southwest Couloir route' describes it crossing Black Tooth Notch and traversing the upper Northeast Face near the summit, indicating route convergence with other lines near the top of the peak — noted in the beta/pitchDetail rather than treated as a discrepancy.

routes.overview: A serious, rarely repeated 5,000-foot ridge climb up Goode's East Ridge, from the toe of a clean arête above Bridge Creek, over the Southeast Peak, to the true summit — one of the longest and most committing established alpine rock routes in the North Cascades, with 4,000-5,000 feet of exposure to either side.
routes.beta: From an obvious clearing before Grizzly Creek, climb to the toe of a clean-looking arête descending northeast from the main ridge. A 5th-class scramble pitch leads to an initial bivy; from there a roughly two-hour simulclimb (to 5.8) along running belays gains an 8,200 ft tower near the Memaloose Ridge junction. The route continues along the crest with running-belay climbing to 5.8, then two long crux pitches up to 5.9-5.10 crack climbing on the Southeast Peak headwall (easier variations exist to the left). Year-round snow/glacier ice covers the saddle just past the Southeast Peak, and a few more, easier pitches lead to the true summit.
routes.approach: From the Rainy Pass trailhead on SR 20, follow the PCT south about 9 miles, then continue up the North Fork of Bridge Creek about 2 miles. Leave the trail at an obvious clearing before Grizzly Creek and climb to the toe of the ridge's arête — the start of the technical climbing.
routes.descent: From the true summit: 3 rappels to Black Tooth Notch, 2 more short rappels through the loose notch itself, then 3 single-rope rappels into the Southwest Couloir, followed by class-3 downclimbing into the basin below and a long hike out.
routes.descentText: This is the standard descent used by parties reaching Goode's true summit from any of its routes: rappel from summit anchors toward Black Tooth Notch (about 3 raps, the first fairly direct, the next two trending skier's right), negotiate the loose, exposed notch (roughly 2 more short raps, ~30m then ~15m), then make 3 single-rope rappels into the Southwest Couloir before downclimbing loose class-3 terrain into the basin and hiking out via Bridge Creek/PCT.
routes.itinerary: Day 1: approach 9 miles on the PCT plus 2 miles up the North Fork of Bridge Creek to leave the trail, then climb to a bivy on the lower ridge. Day 2: a long day of simul-climbing and pitched climbing along the ridge crest, over the Southeast Peak headwall, to a high bivy near the true summit (multiple bivy options exist below the ridge, past the SE Peak, and on the summit). Day 3: summit and descend via Black Tooth Notch and the Southwest Couloir, then hike out.
routes.bail: Retreat is difficult once established on the ridge crest — there are few natural rappel anchors along most of its length. The sustained, exposed nature of the route and the Southeast Peak headwall crux pitches make early commitment decisions important; the first single-push (car-to-car in a day) ascent by Jens Holsten and Dan Hilden in 2010 shows the route can go fast for a fit, efficient team, but most parties should plan for at least one bivy and treat the route as fully committing once started.
routes.turnaround: Given the route's length, sustained exposure, and lack of easy retreat, plan conservatively for at least one bivy and set decision points (e.g., before the Southeast Peak headwall) for continuing versus retreating the way you came.
routes.bestSeason: Late summer (August-September), after the Southeast Peak headwall pitches and the saddle glacier ice are in reasonable condition; a long, stable weather window is important given the route's length and commitment.
routes.fa: Blake Herrington and Sol Wertkin, September 6, 2007
routes.rappels: 3 raps from the summit to Black Tooth Notch, 2 more short raps through the notch, then 3 single-rope raps into the Southwest Couloir
objHaz: Loose rock on sections of the ridge
Extreme, sustained exposure (4,000-5,000 ft) on both flanks
Year-round snow/glacier ice at the saddle past the Southeast Peak
Very limited rescue feasibility given the remoteness and ridge exposure
routes.detailedRack: A single alpine rack of cams (doubles in small-to-mid sizes) plus a light selection of nuts is typical; many parties simul-climb long sections on a single rope to move efficiently, saving pitched climbing for the Southeast Peak headwall crack pitches and any harder steps.
routes.proNeeds: Protection is spread thin over a very long route — teams typically simul-climb easier terrain and pitch out only the Southeast Peak headwall crux (and any other harder sections); a light single rack with doubles in finger-to-hand sizes covers most of the technical climbing.
gear: rope (single, for pitching cruxes and simul-climbing)
single alpine rack with doubles in small-mid cams
crampons and ice axe (for the saddle glacier ice)
whatToBring: bivy sack or light tent for exposed ridge camps
extra water capacity — limited sources on the ridge
proTips: Carry doubles in small cams if you plan to protect more than the minimum; many parties simul-climb most of the ridge on a single rack
The first single-push (car-to-car in a day) ascent was made by Jens Holsten and Dan Hilden in 2010 — the route can go fast for a fit, efficient team
Multiple bivy options exist below the ridge, just past the Southeast Peak, and on the summit — plan for at least one bivy
Expect minimal beta and traffic; this is one of the most obscure and serious established lines on Goode
watchOut: Loose rock on parts of the ridge, noted by the original ascent party
Long, committing ridge with 4,000-5,000 ft of exposure on either side and few easy retreat options
Route-finding near the crest is nontrivial given limited available beta
Year-round snow/ice at the saddle past the Southeast Peak requires crampons/axe even in summer
knownHazards: Extremely long, sustained, and committing ridge climb — one of the most serious established routes on the peak
Very limited rescue feasibility given the remoteness and ridge exposure
Rockfall/loose rock on sections of the ridge
Sparse traffic means rappel anchors found on the standard descent may warrant backup

pitchDetail.notes: Scramble/climb up the clean arête descending NE from the main ridge to reach the crest; first bivy site partway up

pitchDetail.pitch: Ridge crest to Memaloose Ridge junction
pitchDetail.notes: Roughly 2-hour simulclimb on running belays to an 8,200 ft tower
Crux crack pitches on the SE Peak headwall; easier variations exist to the left
Year-round snow/glacier ice at the saddle just past the Southeast Peak
A few more pitches of easier climbing lead to the true summit

road.name: North Cascades Highway (SR 20)
road.status: Open seasonally, typically mid-May through early December (weather-dependent)
road.seasonalGate: Closes for winter between the Ross Dam trailhead (MP 134) and Silver Star Gate (MP 171); closure points can extend to the Newhalem (MP 120) and Early Winters (MP 178) gates as conditions dictate
road.driveNote: Rainy Pass North Trailhead (PCT) is reached via SR 20 about 35 miles west of Winthrop; large paved parking area at 4,880 ft, Northwest Forest Pass required.

climate.typical: Cold, wet winters with heavy snowpack; a short, drier alpine window roughly late July-September
climate.spring: Heavy snowpack; the route is not practical until much later in the season
climate.summer: Ridge terrain gradually clears of snow; best conditions come later in the season
climate.fall: Best window (Aug-Sep) before shortening daylight and early snow return
climate.winter: SR 20 closed; approach effectively impractical

access.landManager: North Cascades National Park Service Complex (NPS); the Rainy Pass trailhead itself sits on Okanogan-Wenatchee National Forest land
access.fees: Northwest Forest Pass / Interagency pass required at the Rainy Pass trailhead ($5/day or $30/annual)
access.permit: NPS wilderness/backcountry permit required for overnight stays; peak-season permits reservable via recreation.gov ($26/trip), with free self-issue permits available when the Wilderness Information Center is closed (roughly October-April)
access.passRequired: Northwest Forest Pass at the Rainy Pass trailhead; NPS backcountry permit for camping
access.closures: SR 20 closed in winter (roughly December-May); check current NPS/WSDOT road and trail status before an early- or late-season attempt

timing.recommendedStart: Early start to maximize daylight on this long ridge; most parties plan at least one bivy on route

emergency.county: Chelan County (summit and most of the peak); the Rainy Pass trailhead approach passes through Okanogan County on SR 20
emergency.sheriffDispatch: Chelan County Sheriff non-emergency (509) 667-6851; RiverCom 911 dispatches Chelan County law/fire/EMS — call 911 for emergencies
emergency.rangerStation: North Cascades NPS Wilderness Information Center, Marblemount, (360) 854-7245; Golden West Visitor Center, Stehekin
