emergency.notes: No cell coverage on the ridge or in the Bridge Creek basin; this is one of the most remote and committing lines on the peak — a satellite communicator and strong self-rescue capability are strongly advised.

routes.corrections: Grade and length are cited with some variance across sources: Mountain Project's structured field shows 5.9/IV, 3,000 ft, 20 pitches, while theCrag, the AAC Publications article, and the contemporaneous 2007 Alpinist newswire describe the hardest pitches as 5.10/5.10- and the overall alpine grade as IV+. Used Mountain Project's structured values as the primary rockGrade/routeFt/pitches fields and commitment IV+ (AAC/Alpinist) to capture both. First-ascent date: the AAC Publications article text read as '2008,' but this appears to be the AAJ publication year rather than the climb date — the contemporaneous 2007 Alpinist newswire report and Wikipedia both confirm September 6, 2007 as the actual first-ascent date, which was used here.

hierarchyNote: The given name "Mount Pulitzer (Wellesley Peak)" appears to conflate two DIFFERENT Bailey Range summits. Wellesley Peak is a separate, higher peak (~6,758-6,759 ft, ~301 m prominence) located about 14 miles east at approximately 47.791066, -123.291099 (per PeakVisor/SummitPost Olympic Range prominence lists) — it is not an alternate name for Mount Pulitzer. The given coordinates (47.835525, -123.574815) and elevation (6283 ft) match Mount Pulitzer precisely against Wikipedia's sourced data (47.83559N, 123.574217W, 6,283 ft), confirming this DB row genuinely is Mount Pulitzer. Mount Pulitzer's actual documented alternate name is "Snagtooth," not Wellesley Peak. Recommend dropping "(Wellesley Peak)" from the display name or, if Wellesley Peak is meant to exist as its own catalog entry, adding it separately rather than as an alias here. The parent area "Central Olympic Mountains" is geographically plausible in a loose sense (Mount Pulitzer sits in the Bailey Range, a subrange near the center of Olympic National Park), though the precise subrange name used by all sources is "Bailey Range."
blurb: Mount Pulitzer, also known as Snagtooth, is a 6,283-foot summit in the Bailey Range, a subrange of the Olympic Mountains within Olympic National Park, Jefferson County, Washington. It is built of Eocene-age sandstone, turbidite, and basaltic oceanic crust typical of the Olympics' obducted seafloor terrane, and a small unnamed glacier persists on its north slope. The peak was named by the 1889-90 Seattle Press Expedition after newspaper publisher Joseph Pulitzer and was first climbed in 1961 by Doug Waali, Bob Wood, and Kent Heathershaw via the East Ridge.

routes.overview: The standard line follows Mount Pulitzer's East Ridge — the original 1961 first-ascent route — from the Ferry-Pulitzer col up class 3 talus and scree to a false summit; the final stretch from the false summit to the true summit crosses a badly fractured, exposed sedimentary ridge that multiple parties have judged too loose/hazardous and turned back from.
routes.beta: Mount Pulitzer is reached almost exclusively as part of the multi-day Bailey Range Traverse, an off-trail alpine route through Olympic National Park's interior. From a camp near Lone Tree Pass, climbers head north along faint game/boot paths on open ridge terrain to the Ferry-Pulitzer col, then scramble the East Ridge on class 3 rock to Pulitzer's false summit. Trip reports describe this stretch as relaxed, scenic scrambling with time to examine wildflowers and quartz crystals in the rock. The true summit, visible from the false summit as an inviting rock horn, requires crossing a 'horribly shattered sedimentary ridge' with serious exposure that has stopped experienced parties; many groups instead traverse over to nearby Mount Ferry (an easier double-summit peak) from the same camp rather than push for Pulitzer's true top.
routes.approach: From the Sol Duc River Trailhead (end of Sol Duc Road, ~14 mi off US-101), follow the High Divide Trail west past Bogachiel Peak and Heart Lake to the 'Catwalk' beneath Mount Carrie, then leave the trail for cross-country travel through the Northern Bailey Range: down through Eleven Bull Basin, over polished post-glacial slabs and snowfields near Stephen Peak, to a saddle south of Stephen Peak, then on to Lone Tree Pass. This is typically a 2-day approach (roughly 20+ off-trail miles) with the first night at Eleven Bull Basin and the second near Lone Tree Pass (camp on the snowfield-bordered bench south of the pass, since the pass itself has no water). From this camp, climb north to the Ferry-Pulitzer col and up the East Ridge.
routes.descent: Reverse the East Ridge scramble back down to the Ferry-Pulitzer col and return to the Lone Tree Pass camp.
routes.descentText: Downclimb the East Ridge's class 3 talus/scree back to the Ferry-Pulitzer col, taking care on loose rock, then drop back to the Lone Tree Pass camp area. From there, parties either retrace the Northern Baileys route back to the Sol Duc Trailhead (roughly 2 days) or, if continuing the full Bailey Range Traverse, proceed south through the Southern Baileys toward Queets Basin, Humes Glacier, and eventually the Hoh River Trail — often a faster exit than reversing the Northern Baileys once already south of Lone Tree Pass.
routes.itinerary: Typical 5-day Bailey Range Traverse plan that bags Pulitzer: Day 1 - Sol Duc Trailhead to Eleven Bull Basin via High Divide and the Catwalk beneath Mt. Carrie. Day 2 - Eleven Bull Basin to a camp near Lone Tree Pass, passing the Stephen Peak col and the Ferry-Pulitzer saddle (end of the Northern Baileys). Day 3 - climb Mount Pulitzer's East Ridge (and often nearby Mount Ferry) as a side trip from the Lone Tree Pass camp, then continue south into Queets Basin. Day 4 - cross Humes Glacier, Blizzard Pass, and reach a high camp above Hoh Glacier. Day 5 - descend via Hoh Glacier/Blue Glacier toward the Hoh River and out, or continue to summit Mt. Olympus before exiting.
routes.bail: There is no technical retreat required if the shattered upper ridge to the true summit is declined — simply reverse the East Ridge scramble to the Ferry-Pulitzer col. From Lone Tree Pass, the shortest bail-out is back north through the Northern Baileys to the Sol Duc Trailhead (about 2 days); if already south of the pass, continuing the traverse toward the Hoh River Trail can be a faster exit than reversing north.
routes.turnaround: Because the final approach to the true summit crosses an exposed, badly fractured sedimentary ridge, assess rock quality, wetness, and the group's comfort with loose class 3-4 scrambling before committing beyond the false summit; wet rock or afternoon thunderstorm risk are good reasons to stop at the false summit or the Ferry-Pulitzer col instead of pushing on.
routes.bestSeason: Late July through September offers the most favorable weather and driest rock for the scramble itself; most parties time trips for mid-July through mid-August specifically to catch good snow coverage across the Northern and Southern Bailey Range traverse sections (snow bridges, glacier crossings) before it deteriorates later in the season.
routes.comms: No reliable cell coverage in the Bailey Range interior; the area is remote and self-rescue is the default expectation, so a satellite communicator (e.g., inReach) is strongly recommended.
routes.fa: Doug Waali, Bob Wood, and Kent Heathershaw, 1961, via the East Ridge
routes.rockGrade: Class 3 scramble (Class 3-4 exposed on the final ridge to the true summit)
objHaz: loose, shattered sedimentary rock on the upper ridge
sustained exposure on the true-summit ridge
crevasses/snow bridges on nearby Bailey Range glaciers en route (e.g., Humes Glacier)
faint, brushy off-trail route-finding through the Northern Baileys
routes.detailedRack: No fixed technical rock rack is standard for the East Ridge scramble itself. Because Pulitzer is only reached via the Bailey Range Traverse, parties carry full glacier travel kit for the approach: crampons, ice axe, a glacier rope, and basic crevasse-rescue hardware (e.g., Tibloc/Micro Traxion) for crossing Bailey Range snowfields and glaciers such as the one on Pulitzer's own north slope and Humes Glacier further south.
routes.proNeeds: No fixed protection is placed on the standard scramble route; a small nut set has been carried by some parties for the loose, exposed final ridge to the true summit but is not standard gear for most ascents (many parties decline that section entirely).
gear: bear canister (required in Olympic NP backcountry)
whatToBring: bear canister (mandatory for overnight Olympic NP backcountry travel)
topo map and compass or GPS (route is off-trail and faint through the Northern Baileys)
full multi-day backpacking kit for the traverse approach
proTips: Camp south of Lone Tree Pass at the snowfield-bordered bench rather than at the pass itself, since Lone Tree Pass has no water.

Most parties tag Pulitzer's false summit and climb nearby Mount Ferry (an easier double-summit peak) from the same Lone Tree Pass camp, rather than committing to the shattered ridge to Pulitzer's true summit.
Time the trip for mid-July through mid-August for the best snow coverage across the Northern and Southern Bailey Range traverse sections.
watchOut: The ridge from the false summit to Pulitzer's true summit is described by climbers as a 'horribly shattered sedimentary ridge' with serious exposure — multiple parties have turned back here rather than continue.
Route-finding through the Northern Bailey Range to reach the Ferry-Pulitzer col is faint, brushy, and easy to lose in poor visibility.
Snowfields and the peak's own small north-slope glacier require crampons and ice axe well into summer.
knownHazards: Loose, shattered sedimentary rock on the summit ridge creates real rockfall and fall potential.
The area is remote with no maintained trail and no cell coverage, so self-rescue is the realistic expectation if something goes wrong.
Snow bridges and crevasses on Bailey Range glaciers along the broader traverse (e.g., Humes Glacier) present fall-through hazard, especially early season.

pitchDetail.notes: Talus and scree ridge scramble on the 1961 first-ascent line; relaxed but loose in sections.
Badly fractured sedimentary rock ridge; several parties have declined this section due to loose rock and exposure and did not reach the true summit.

road.name: Sol Duc Road
road.status: Open seasonally (reopened late March 2026 per NPS reporting); subject to washouts/winter closure
road.seasonalGate: Typically closed in winter; check NPS road status before driving
road.driveNote: From US-101 west of Lake Crescent, turn onto Sol Duc Road and follow it roughly 14 miles to the Sol Duc River Trailhead at road's end.

climate.typical: Interior Olympic Mountains climate: wet, snowy winters and drier, milder alpine summers; the Bailey Range holds seasonal snowfields and small glaciers well into summer.
climate.summer: Best conditions and driest rock; NPS notes July through September as the most favorable months for viewing and climbing Bailey Range peaks.

access.landManager: Olympic National Park (National Park Service)
access.fees: Wilderness camping fee of $8 per adult (16+) per night plus a non-refundable $6 reservation fee per permit, or a $45 Annual Olympic Wilderness Pass (reservation fee still applies).
access.permit: Backcountry wilderness permit required for any overnight stay, reserved via Recreation.gov or by phone through the Wilderness Information Center (360-565-3100); bear canisters are mandatory in the Olympic NP backcountry.
access.passRequired: Olympic National Park entrance pass plus a wilderness camping permit.
access.closures: Summer wilderness permit reservation window runs roughly mid-May through mid-October; contact the Wilderness Information Center for current route/snow conditions before a Bailey Range trip.

emergency.sheriffDispatch: 911 (emergency); Jefferson County Sheriff's Office non-emergency for search-and-rescue coordination with Jefferson Search and Rescue (JSAR)
emergency.rangerStation: Olympic National Park Wilderness Information Center, Port Angeles: (360) 565-3100
emergency.nearestHospital: Forks Community Hospital (west side, via Hoh/US-101) or Olympic Medical Center, Port Angeles (north side, via Sol Duc/US-101) — both are hours from this remote interior peak.
emergency.notes: The Bailey Range interior has no cell coverage and no maintained trail; NPS advises that emergency help is far away and often impossible to contact, so parties should be fully self-sufficient and carry a satellite communicator.

routes.corrections: The catalog's peak-name alias '(Wellesley Peak)' does not apply to Mount Pulitzer — Wellesley Peak is a separate, higher (~6,758-6,759 ft) Bailey Range summit about 14 miles east. The given elevation (6,283 ft) and coordinates match Mount Pulitzer/Snagtooth exactly, confirming the underlying peak record itself is correct; only the parenthetical alias is wrong. No route-level facts required correction beyond confirming the existing FA and elevation data.

blurb: Sperry Peak (6,120+ ft) is a triangular, glacier-carved summit in the South Stillaguamish Mountains of the North Cascades, rising above the Mountain Loop Highway near Barlow Pass in the Mount Baker-Snoqualmie National Forest, Snohomish County, WA. It is built of 260-370-million-year-old metamorphosed sandstone, shale and conglomerate accreted as part of an ancient terrane, and shares a connecting ridge/saddle with its taller, more popular neighbor Vesper Peak. The peak was first climbed in 1927 by Norval Grigg and Art Winder via its easiest (southwest/west slope) line, which remains the standard scramble route today; its steeper north and east faces hold several rarely-climbed technical ice/mixed and rock lines.

routes.overview: A popular, moderate North Cascades scramble that gains the summit via Headlee Pass, Lake Elan, and Sperry's west ridge/west slope -- mostly Class 2 off-trail travel with an exposed Class 3-4 finish above a talus/heather bench near the top.
routes.beta: Hike the Sunrise Mine Trail from its trailhead through Wirtz Basin to Headlee Pass, traverse below Sperry's south ridge past Lake Elan (Vesper Lake), then scramble up slabs to the broad saddle between Sperry and Vesper Peaks. From the saddle, follow the west ridge/west slope upward -- easy at first, but above a down-sloping talus and heather bench the route steepens and the exposure increases into sustained Class 3-4 terrain to the summit. Many parties combine Sperry with adjacent Vesper Peak from the same saddle in one long day.
routes.approach: From the Verlot Public Service Center (about 11 miles east of Granite Falls on the Mountain Loop Highway/Hwy 530), continue east roughly 17 miles, then turn right (south) onto Sunrise Mine Road (Forest Road 4065) and follow it about 2.2-2.5 miles to its end at the Sunrise Mine Trailhead (2,340 ft; day-use fee or Northwest Forest Pass required). Hike the Sunrise Mine Trail (#707), fording the South Fork Stillaguamish River at about 0.5 mile (can be a difficult crossing in high water), then climbing through forest into the more open Wirtz Basin. At the head of the basin the trail switchbacks steeply up a narrow, rockfall-prone chute to Headlee Pass (roughly 4,600-4,720 ft), about 2.6-2.7 miles from the trailhead. Beyond the pass the trail turns northwest and traverses open talus below Sperry's south ridge; follow the east shore of Lake Elan (Vesper Lake, ~5,030 ft) and scramble up slabs to the saddle between Sperry and Vesper (roughly 5,300 ft).
routes.descent: Reverse the ascent line: down-climb the west ridge/slope back to the Sperry-Vesper saddle, then retrace the traverse past Lake Elan and back over Headlee Pass to the Sunrise Mine Trail.
routes.descentText: From the summit, downclimb the west ridge/slope, staying south of the steeper north and east faces. Below the talus/heather bench, aim back toward the Sperry-Vesper saddle rather than descending directly toward Lake Elan's outlet. From the saddle, drop to Lake Elan and follow its east shore back to the main trail, then retrace the traverse below Sperry's south ridge to Headlee Pass. Descend the steep, loose, rockfall-prone gully below the pass carefully -- it holds hard, steep snow late into the season and has been the site of several accidents, including at least one fatality -- then follow the Sunrise Mine Trail back through Wirtz Basin to the trailhead.
routes.itinerary: Most parties climb Sperry Peak (often paired with neighboring Vesper Peak) as a single long day: a pre-dawn start from the Sunrise Mine Trailhead, roughly 3-3.5 hours to the summit via Headlee Pass, Lake Elan, and the west ridge, and about 2-2.5 hours back down. Parties wanting a more relaxed pace, or linking both summits, sometimes backpack in and camp near Lake Elan or the upper basin, climbing Sperry (and Vesper) on day two before hiking out.
routes.bail: There is no established rappel/bail route independent of the standard line -- retreat by reversing the ascent at any point. The two places to plan a turnaround around are the exposed Class 3-4 terrain above the heather bench near the summit and the steep, rockfall/snow-prone gully below Headlee Pass; back off before committing to either if conditions, weather, or party ability are in doubt.
routes.turnaround: Set a firm turnaround time before starting that accounts for the long approach and the exposed, route-finding-dependent scrambling near the summit; afternoon thunderstorms and lingering snow in the Headlee Pass gully are the main reasons parties get caught out, so don't press on unless you can reach the saddle with enough daylight and margin to descend the chute in good conditions.
routes.bestSeason: Best climbed July through September, once the Headlee Pass gully and upper basin have melted out. The Sunrise Mine Trail is typically snow-covered into midsummer, and hard, steep snow can persist in the pass gully even later in the season, requiring an ice axe (and sometimes crampons) for safe passage.
routes.fa: Norval Grigg and Art Winder, 1927 -- via the peak's southwest/west slope, essentially the same line climbed today as the standard route.
objHaz: Loose rock and rockfall in the Headlee Pass chute
Lingering steep snow/ice in the Headlee Pass gully into early-mid summer
Exposed, route-finding-dependent Class 3-4 terrain on the upper west ridge
Difficult ford of the South Fork Stillaguamish River near the trailhead
routes.detailedRack: No technical rack is needed for the standard route. An ice axe is recommended whenever snow lingers in the Headlee Pass gully or upper basin (common into July), and microspikes or light crampons are worth carrying in early season; trekking poles help on the loose talus and switchbacks. A helmet is a reasonable addition given rockfall in the Headlee Pass chute.
whatToBring: Northwest Forest Pass or day-use recreation pass for the trailhead
GPS/compass -- it's easy to lose the line above the talus/heather bench near the summit
Extra water -- limited reliable sources on the upper mountain
Sun protection for the long exposed talus and scramble sections
proTips: Leave the trail at Lake Elan's outlet and follow its east shore before angling up to the Sperry-Vesper saddle, rather than trying to climb directly from Headlee Pass.
Many parties climb Sperry and Vesper Peak together in the same day since both are reached from the same saddle.
Check recent trip reports for snow conditions in the Headlee Pass gully before going -- it can hold hard, steep snow well into summer.
watchOut: The chute below Headlee Pass is notorious for rockfall and holds hard, steep snow long after surrounding slopes melt out -- several accidents and at least one death have occurred there.
Above the talus/heather bench on the upper west ridge the route becomes exposed and it's easy to wander into unnecessarily hard Class 4 terrain -- stay alert to the correct line.
The ford of the South Fork Stillaguamish near the trail's start can be a difficult, sometimes hazardous crossing in high water.
knownHazards: Rockfall in the steep, narrow chute below Headlee Pass
Hard, steep snow lingering in the Headlee Pass gully into early-mid summer
Exposed Class 3-4 scrambling with route-finding difficulty near the summit
A difficult river ford of the South Fork Stillaguamish about 0.5 mile from the trailhead

pitchDetail.notes: 2.6-2.7 mi via the Sunrise Mine Trail through Wirtz Basin; a difficult river ford near the start, then a steep, rockfall-prone switchbacked chute up to the pass (~4,600-4,720 ft).
Traverse open talus below Sperry's south ridge, follow the east shore of Lake Elan (~5,030 ft), then scramble slabs up to the broad saddle (~5,300 ft) between Sperry and Vesper.
Follow the west ridge/slope; easy at first, then a down-sloping talus/heather bench marks the transition to steeper, more exposed climbing to the top.

road.name: Sunrise Mine Road (Forest Road 4065)
road.status: Gravel Forest Service spur road, seasonally maintained; no reported long-term closures as of research but conditions should be checked before driving
road.driveNote: From the Verlot Public Service Center, drive Mountain Loop Highway (Hwy 530) east roughly 17 miles, turn right (south) onto FR 4065, and continue about 2.2-2.5 miles to its end at the Sunrise Mine Trailhead (2,340 ft).

climate.forecastZone: West Slopes North / Central Cascades (NWS zone forecast; NWAC for avalanche/snow conditions)
climate.typical: Marine west coast climate with heavy winter precipitation and a snowpack that lingers into summer at this elevation.
climate.spring: Trail and upper basin snow-covered into June/July; steep, hard snow persists in the Headlee Pass gully and can pose a fall/slide hazard.
climate.summer: Best climbing window, July-September; generally drier with some afternoon thunderstorm risk.
climate.fall: Shortening days and cooling temperatures; early season snow is possible by October.
climate.winter: Heavy snow and difficult road/trail access; not a recommended season for this route.

blurb: Sofa King Buttress is a small, newly-developed sport-climbing wall within The Citidel, an alpine crag on the NW side of the east wall of Silver Basin just north of Silver Peak near Snoqualmie Pass, Washington. The rock is volcanic metamorphic stone (similar in character to North Bend's World Wall), and as of the routes' 2020 first ascents the area was still described as "new and raw," with loose rock still being cleaned off holds. Development (including stainless-steel hardware, contributed via Martin Volken of Pro Guiding Service/Proski North Bend) was documented and shared on Mountain Project by Wylie Coyote in April 2021.

routes.overview: A single-pitch bolted sport line sitting directly right of Drilling Me Softly on Sofa King Buttress; opens with a cheval-style straddle over a small nose feature low on the route before continuing up bolted face climbing to a chain anchor.
routes.beta: Fully bolted 100 ft (30 m) sport pitch rated 5.10b/c. Mountain Project's route description (by FA party Wylie Moreshead, posted by Wylie Coyote) recommends starting left of the bolt line and straddling the low nose feature cheval-style rather than pulling straight onto it, keeping the rope managed on the inside leg to avoid an awkward twist. 12 bolts lead to chains at the top.
routes.approach: From I-90 Exit 54 (Hyak), take Hyak Drive to Forest Service Road NF-9070. Follow NF-9070 to Ollallie Meadow, where the Pacific Crest Trail crosses the road, then bear left at the fork and follow NF-9070 to its end at a turnaround below Silver Basin. The road has rough, rocky sections and water bars ("oil pan ripping rocks"), so a high-clearance vehicle is recommended. From the turnaround it is roughly a 20-30 minute walk up into Silver Basin to the base of the Citidel/Sofa King Buttress.
routes.descent: Lower off the chain anchor at the top of the route.
routes.descentText: This is a single-pitch, fully bolted sport route with a chain lower-off anchor at its top; there is no walk-off. Thread/clip the chains and lower the full length of the route (a 60 m rope gives ample margin on this ~30 m line) back to the base.
routes.bail: The route is single-pitch and fully bolted, so retreat simply means lowering from the last bolt clipped (leaving a biner/draw if you don't have a way to clean while lowering) rather than a true multi-pitch bail.
routes.turnaround: Not a big-mountain turnaround-time objective — the practical time constraint is the 20-30 minute approach through Silver Basin plus weather: the approach and base of the buttress are exposed, so turn back on the approach if lightning or fast-moving weather is closing in before you're committed to the rock.
routes.bestSeason: Best climbed in summer into early fall (roughly July-September) once Silver Basin and the approach are snow-free; at ~4,650 ft this high basin near the Cascade crest can hold snow into early summer, and the access road can be rough or impassable after heavy rain.
routes.comms: No documented cell coverage in this remote, forested Silver Basin approach off NF-9070; treat the area as a dead zone and carry a satellite messenger for emergencies.
routes.rappels: Single lower-off from chain anchors at the top; a 60 m rope is sufficient for this ~30 m route.
objHaz: loose/sharp rock typical of a newly-developed crag
routes.face: Sofa King Buttress (right-hand line, directly right of Drilling Me Softly)
routes.fa: Wylie Moreshead, 2020
routes.detailedRack: Fully bolted: 12 bolts to a two-bolt chain anchor. No trad gear needed. A 60 m rope is enough to lower off the full ~30 m pitch.
routes.proNeeds: Sport rack only — 12 quickdraws (plus 1-2 extra for the anchor). No natural/trad protection required or possible.
whatToBring: Helmet — rock at this new crag is still loose/sharp in places
Sturdy approach shoes for the talus/brush walk-in
Extra layer — Silver Basin can be cool and windy even in summer
proTips: Start left of the bolt line and straddle the low nose feature cheval-style rather than muscling straight over it, per the first-ascent party's own beta.
Keep the rope run on your inside leg through the straddle move to avoid getting twisted up.
watchOut: This is a newly-developed, "raw" crag — expect some loose or sharp holds and wear a helmet.
The NF-9070 access road is rough with sharp rocks and deep water bars; a high-clearance vehicle is recommended.
knownHazards: Loose and sharp rock is a known issue at this recently-developed alpine sport crag.
The approach and crag sit in a remote basin with no cell coverage, so any injury means a long self-evacuation or SAR response.

pitchDetail.notes: 100 ft (30 m) bolted face pitch; starts with a cheval straddle over a small nose feature low down, 12 bolts to chain anchor.

routes.overview: The furthest-left bolted line on Sofa King Buttress: a single-pitch sport route packed with varied technical movement — 3D stemming, backstepping, flagging, roofs, laybacks, jugs, crimps and underclings — to a chain anchor.
routes.beta: Fully bolted 5.10b/c sport pitch on Sofa King Buttress, positioned as the leftmost route on the wall. Mountain Project's description (FA party Wylie Moreshead) emphasizes the sheer variety of technique packed into one pitch rather than a single hard crux — 3D stemming, backstepping, flagging, a roof, laybacking, jugs, crimps and underclings. Protected by 12 bolts to chain anchors at the top; photos on the route page show the crux section and a rest stance before an upper bulge.

Follow NF-9070 to Ollallie Meadow, where the Pacific Crest Trail crosses the road, then bear left at the fork and follow NF-9070 to its end at a turnaround below Silver Basin. The road has rough, rocky sections and water bars ("oil pan ripping rocks"), so a high-clearance vehicle is recommended. From the turnaround it is roughly a 20-30 minute walk up into Silver Basin to the base of the Citidel/Sofa King Buttress.

routes.bail: Single-pitch and fully bolted — retreat simply means lowering off the last bolt clipped (leave a biner/draw behind if you can't otherwise clean while lowering).
routes.turnaround: Not a big-mountain turnaround-time objective — the real time constraint is the 20-30 minute approach through Silver Basin and weather: the approach and buttress base are exposed, so turn back on the approach if lightning or fast-moving weather threatens before you're committed to the rock.
routes.bestSeason: Best climbed in summer into early fall (roughly July-September) once Silver Basin and the approach are snow-free; at ~4,650 ft this high basin near the Cascade crest can hold snow into early summer, and the access road can be rough or impassable after heavy rain.

blurb: Burgundy Spire is a granite tower in the "Wine Spires" group (with Chianti, Pernod, and Chablis spires) strung along Silver Star Mountain's north ridge above Washington Pass in the North Cascades. First climbed in 1953 by a Fred Beckey-led party using aid and fixed ropes over three days, it was long regarded — per Beckey himself — as one of the hardest principal summits in the Cascades to reach; a later-discovered "tunnel" bypass now lets parties free-climb the historic line at 5.8. The spire's clean alpine granite and multi-pitch trad lines (5.8 to 5.11a) make it one of the most popular objectives in the Washington Pass area.

routes.overview: The historic, easiest line up Burgundy Spire, climbing broken flakes, cracks and a short ridge from Burgundy Col to the summit; a since-found 'tunnel' shortcut lets modern parties free-climb what was originally an aid line.
routes.fa: Fred Beckey party, 1953 (original ascent line, using aid and fixed ropes over three days; a subsequently discovered rock 'tunnel' at Burgundy Ledge now allows the same line to be free-climbed at 5.8)
routes.beta: Considered the standard route up the spire. From Burgundy Col, scramble down/around the north face to the route start (roughly 5-10 minutes below the col). Low-5th/4th-class scrambling links to a broad sandy ledge shared with the Paisano Pinnacle traverse, then two pitches of loose, exfoliating flakes (to about 5.8) lead to a hand-crack/face pitch and a final short, sharp-arete ridge to the summit block; some parties add an optional 5.10 offwidth finish. Rock quality is generally solid on the crux sections but loose in the connecting terrain. LemkeClimbs' and StephAbegg's trip reports describe a similar but 8-pitch/~800 ft version of this same line with 5 rappels — pitch division varies by source depending on rope-length and where climbers choose to break/link sections.
routes.approach: Park at the Highway 20 (North Cascades Highway) pullout about 4 miles east of Washington Pass (elev. ~4,250-4,310 ft, near milepost 166). A boot path drops down talus/scree to cross Early Winters Creek (~4,080 ft), then climbs steeply through forest along a timbered rib to a bench/campsite around 6,460-6,600 ft (roughly 2 hours from the creek). From there, cairned scree gullies lead up to Burgundy Col (~7,720-7,770 ft), about 3 hours total from the car. From the col, scramble down loose rock or steep snow around the north face to the base of the route.
routes.descent: 5 rappels (double ropes) back down the north face to Burgundy Col; a single rope can also work using slings left at stations, though it's slower.
routes.descentText: From the summit, rappel to the only bolted anchor on the route, then continue down: ~40m to a bolted anchor, ~55m to a ledge near a large rock arch, ~55m to a horned anchor, ~40m to scree ledges, and a final ~60m to the base/scree below the north face, regaining Burgundy Col on foot. A 70m rope avoids a short downclimb after the first rappel; 60m double ropes are the standard setup. Some parties report rope-snagging on the pull.
routes.bail: Retreat is possible from most stances via the same rappel line described above back toward Burgundy Col; fixed/bolted anchors exist partway down the route.
routes.turnaround: Given the roughly 3-hour approach to Burgundy Col and the loose, multi-pitch nature of the climbing above, set a firm turnaround time based on remaining daylight and the risk of afternoon thunderstorms, and be willing to retreat down the rappel line rather than push through deteriorating weather or unexpected route-finding delays.
routes.bestSeason: Late June through September is the reliable window; Highway 20 typically closes for the winter (snow/avalanche danger) from roughly mid-November to mid/late-April, making the approach inaccessible outside the summer-fall season. Expect lingering snow on the upper approach into June and possible wildfire smoke in August-September.
routes.detailedRack: A single rack to 3 inches covers most of the route; bring an extra 0.75in and 1in piece if the 5.8 terrain feels near your limit. A helmet is essential given loose, exfoliating rock on several pitches.
routes.proNeeds: Standard single trad rack (nuts + cams to #3) is generally sufficient; the crux sections take small-to-medium cams in flakes and hand/finger cracks.
gear: double ropes for the rappel descent
whatToBring: sturdy boots for the scree gully to Burgundy Col
bivy gear if camping at the ~6,500 ft bench
proTips: A 70m rope avoids a short downclimb after the first rappel
the col-to-base scramble is exposed to rockfall — move efficiently and stay alert for parties above
linking with Paisano Pinnacle's West Ridge makes a longer, popular ~12-13 pitch outing
watchOut: Loose, exfoliating flakes on the connecting/scramble sections
afternoon thunderstorms common in July-August
rope-pull snags reported on the rappel line
knownHazards: Rockfall potential from loose terrain onto parties below, especially with multiple teams on route
route-finding complexity — several variations exist between the Paisano col traverse and the true summit
loose talus/scree on the approach to Burgundy Col

pitchDetail.notes: Scramble up 4th class with occasional mid-5th moves to the broad sandy ledge connecting with Paisano Pinnacle
Loose and exfoliating flakes; multiple lines possible, right side generally more straightforward
Hand crack (or low-5th scramble variant), followed by exposed 5.7-5.8 face climbing
Ridge climbing up a sharp arete with a hand crack; exposed 5.7 downclimbing to reach the summit block
Optional offwidth finish variation; most parties continue scrambling to the true summit instead

road.name: North Cascades Highway (State Route 20)
road.status: Seasonal; open roughly late April/May through November depending on snowpack and plowing
road.seasonalGate: Closes for winter at the Washington Pass/Silver Star gate area (near MP 171) and the Early Winters/Ross Dam gates; typically mid-November to mid/late-April, varies year to year
road.driveNote: Park at the highway pullout about 4 miles east of Washington Pass summit (near milepost 166, elev. ~4,250-4,310 ft); the approach trail drops down talus to cross Early Winters Creek.

climate.forecastZone: NWS zone WAZ568 (West Slopes North Central Cascades and Passes); NWAC's Washington Pass weather station covers mountain weather/avalanche info for the area
climate.typical: Dry, sunny alpine summers (Jul-Sep) with afternoon thunderstorm potential and occasional wildfire smoke
climate.spring: Snow lingers on the approach into June; the highway may still be gated closed
climate.summer: Prime climbing season; warm days, cool nights, brief afternoon storm risk
climate.fall: Good conditions into September/early October; highway closes for the season by mid-November
climate.winter: Highway closed and approach snowed-in; rare technical winter ascents have been made (e.g., a December 1985 winter ascent of this North Face by James Nelson, Kit Lewis, and Kevin Joiner, described in the American Alpine Club's publications).

routes.corrections: Given peak elevation (8,483 ft) falls within the range reported across sources (Mountain Project ~8,400 ft; ListsOfJohn.com, USGS-derived, ~8,492 ft) — left unchanged, but note ~90 ft of disagreement typical for USGS map resolution on an unsurveyed spire. Peakbagger.com's dedicated page (pid=27550) returned HTTP 403 on direct fetch and could not be used directly; ListsOfJohn.com was used as a cross-check instead. Pitch count for this route varies by source: Mountain Project's dedicated route page lists 6 pitches/600 ft (used as the primary figure here), while LemkeClimbs.com and StephAbegg.com describe an 8-pitch/~800 ft version of the same line with 5 rappels, reflecting where different parties choose to break/link the connecting scramble terrain.

routes.overview: An 8-pitch 5.10c trad line up Burgundy Spire's north side, widely considered the standard/easiest technical way up the peak, with sustained flake and crack climbing and route-finding through traverses and down-climbs.
routes.fa: Mark Allen and Mike Layton, July 18-20, 2004
routes.beta: From Burgundy Col, the route starts below a clean splitter flake a few minutes below the col. Eight pitches of flakes and cracks of all sizes — including a fun roof-pull/lieback P1, a finger-crack P2, a steep lichen-covered finger-crack crux P3 (5.10c), 4th-class terrain, a fun chimney P5, a poor-rock corner P6, a heads-up, minimal-gear P7 (5.10), and rambling 5.7 terrain to the summit — make for a varied, tough outing that tests route-finding as much as climbing.
routes.approach: Park at the Highway 20 pullout about 4 miles east of Washington Pass (~4,250-4,310 ft). Descend talus to cross Early Winters Creek, climb through forest to the ~6,500 ft bench, then follow cairned scree to Burgundy Col (~7,720-7,770 ft), about 3 hours from the car. From the col, scramble loose rock or steep snow down and around the north face to the base of a clean splitter flake, about 5-10 minutes below the col.
routes.descent: 5 rappels with double ropes back down toward Burgundy Col; slings are left at most stations so a single-rope party can still descend safely, just more slowly.
routes.bail: Retreat via the established rappel anchors on the route (5 raps with doubles); single-rope parties can lower/rappel using slings left at the stations.
routes.turnaround: This is a long, committing 8-pitch outing above a multi-hour approach — set a turnaround time that accounts for the loose rock, route-finding traverses, and afternoon storm risk, and be ready to retreat down the rap line rather than press on if behind schedule.
routes.bestSeason: July through September is the reliable window (per Mountain Project and area guides); Highway 20 is typically closed by snow/avalanche gates from roughly mid-November to mid/late-April.
routes.detailedRack: Doubles from 0.4 to 3 inches with a few smaller cams and one #4, plus a full set of nuts; 10 alpine draws help on the traversing pitches.
routes.proNeeds: Standard double rack in the small-to-mid range covers the crack/flake climbing; the minimal-gear P7 bulges reward careful, patient placements.
whatToBring: Approach shoes for the talus/scree approach
water treatment for the Early Winters Creek crossing
bivy gear if splitting the approach over two days
proTips: Regarded as the standard/easiest technical route up Burgundy Spire
slings left at rap stations mean a single rope party can still get down safely, though doubles (5 raps) are faster
watch for lichen on the P3 finger crack in wetter years
watchOut: Spooky, loose flakes near the P3 belay
P7's steep bulges have minimal gear ('heads-up' climbing)
rope drag on the traversing pitches
knownHazards: Loose rock and lichen on several pitches (notably P3 and P7)
long, committing outing where retreat from high on the route is slow
route-finding required through multiple traverses and down-climbs

pitchDetail.notes: 150 ft; fun flake system to a roof pull and lieback on the left

routes.corrections: A 2004 CascadeClimbers.com trip-report title refers to this line as being on the 'East Face,' while Mountain Project situates it on/near the North Face; this is treated as informal naming variance rather than a different climb, and 'North Face' is used here per Mountain Project's own routing/area placement.

routes.overview: An 8-pitch 5.11a trad route up Burgundy Spire's Northeast Buttress, a somewhat lower-traffic but high-quality line just left of Action Potential's start, culminating in a bulging, thin-corner crux pitch.
routes.fa: Mark Allen and Tom Smith, July 24, 2004
routes.beta: From Burgundy Col, descend around the north face to a twin-crack system with obvious loose blocks about 60 ft up (Action Potential's start is roughly 15 ft to the right). Eight pitches climb twin cracks, a chimney/right-facing corner, delicate face moves, and a bulging, thin-corner roof-traverse crux (P4, 5.11a) before wide cracks and chimneys and a final hand-crack/offwidth pitch reach the summit. Described by climbers as a good route that would be excellent with more traffic; some sections (notably the P6 corner) were noted as lichen-covered/dirty as of a 2018 ascent.
routes.approach: Park at the Highway 20 pullout about 4 miles east of Washington Pass (~4,250-4,310 ft). Descend talus to cross Early Winters Creek, climb through forest to the ~6,500 ft bench, then follow cairned scree to Burgundy Col (~7,720-7,770 ft), about 3 hours from the car. From the col, descend loose rock or steep snow around the north face to a twin-crack system with obvious loose blocks about 60 ft up.
routes.turnaround: This crux-loaded, 8-pitch route sees less traffic than the North Face or Action Potential, so retreat options and fixed anchors may be less certain — set a conservative turnaround time and be prepared to build your own rappel anchors if descending before the summit.
routes.bestSeason: July through September is the typical window; Highway 20 is generally closed by snow/avalanche gates from roughly mid-November to mid/late-April.
routes.detailedRack: Doubles to #2 plus a single #3 and #4 cover the crack sizes on this route; a standard set of nuts is also recommended for the thinner corners.
routes.proNeeds: The crux P4 roof/thin-corner takes small-to-medium gear in a bulging corner system — inspect placements carefully before committing to the crux moves.
whatToBring: water treatment for the Early Winters Creek crossing
light rock shoes for the delicate face pitch (P3)
proTips: A good, somewhat underused line on the peak's Northeast Buttress
if you're unsure you're on the right crack system at the start, Action Potential begins about 15 ft to the right
expect more lichen/dirt than on the more-traveled North Face or Action Potential
watchOut: Loose blocks about 60 ft up the opening twin-crack pitch
sustained, wandering nature increases rope-drag risk
less-traveled route means less reliable fixed gear/anchors than its neighbors
knownHazards: Loose blocks near the start of P1
lichen/dirt in sections from infrequent ascents (e.g., the P6 corner)
committing crux roof/corner pitch (P4) with a long fall potential

pitchDetail.notes: 30m; twin cracks over loose blocks, step left to a ledge
55m; chimney and right-facing crack to a twin crack system
20m; delicate face moves trending left, belay below a tricky bulge
25m; bulge, thin corner, roof traverse — the route's crux
30m; trend right via the easiest path, seam protection
55m; dirty corner to hand/finger jams, traverse left
35m; wide cracks and chimneys back to the crest
60m; hand crack toward the summit, offwidth finish

routes.corrections: One aggregator source (citing SuperTopo) lists this route at 5.10c/d A0 or 5.11 III under the alternate name 'Z-Man Northeast Buttress'; Mountain Project's dedicated route page (used as primary here) grades it 5.11a free at 8 pitches/900 ft. The first-ascent party and date (Mark Allen and Tom Smith, July 24, 2004) agree across Mountain Project and a contemporaneous CascadeClimbers.com trip report titled around that date. No independently sourced descent/bail beta was found specific to this route (left null rather than assuming it matches the neighboring North Face/Action Potential rappel line).

blurb: Agnes Mountain is a remote, dramatically Matterhorn-shaped peak in the North Cascades of Chelan County, Washington, standing above the Agnes Creek drainage south of Stehekin near the edge of the Glacier Peak Wilderness; its north face drops roughly 1,800 ft to Agnes Creek in only 1.5 miles. It was first climbed in 1936 by W. Ronald Frazier and Dan O'Brien via the West Fork of Agnes Creek, and because of its brutal, largely trail-less approach it remains one of the seldom-climbed summits of the range (only about 14 recorded ascents as of the late 1980s).
hierarchyNote: Peak identity checks out at the coordinate level: the given lat/lng (48.3176, -120.9685) match Wikipedia's cited position for Agnes Mountain (48°19'03"N 120°58'03"W ≈ 48.3175, -120.9675), and the elevation (8,131 ft given) is consistent with the range of values reputable sources report (8,115 ft per WTA/Wikipedia summary, 8,119 ft NAVD88 per Wikipedia infobox, 8,133 ft per ListsOfJohn) — so the peak record itself is sound. However, the assigned parent area "Darrington and Mountain Loop Hwy" appears to be a hierarchy mismatch. Every source describing this peak places it in the Agnes Creek drainage accessed from Stehekin (Lake Chelan), reached only by the Lady of the Lake ferry across Lake Chelan and then a shuttle/hike up the roadless Stehekin Valley to High Bridge — a Chelan County location on the east side of the Cascade crest, within/adjacent to the Glacier Peak Wilderness (Okanogan-Wenatchee National Forest) and the Lake Chelan National Recreation Area (North Cascades National Park Complex). Darrington and the Mountain Loop Highway are on the west side of the range in Snohomish County, roughly 50+ miles away with no shared access corridor. This peak likely belongs under a Stehekin/Lake Chelan/Glacier Peak Wilderness parent area rather than Darrington/Mountain Loop Hwy.

routes.overview: The original 1936 first-ascent line, climbed via the remote, largely trail-less West Fork of Agnes Creek drainage on the west side of the peak; a historic but very rarely repeated line best understood as an approach-defined route rather than a well-documented technical line.
routes.beta: This is the route used on Agnes Mountain's first ascent: W. Ronald Frazier and Dan O'Brien climbed from the West Fork of Agnes Creek in 1936. Unlike the better-documented Beckey-guide line (Northeast Ridge of Asa Peak traversing to Agnes' south ridge, with short 5.6 sections), no detailed pitch-by-pitch account of the original West Fork line is available in current online sources; the peak overall sees roughly one climbing party every 3-4 years, and the second ascent of the mountain didn't occur until decades after the first.
routes.approach: Reach Stehekin (roadless; no outside highway access) via the Lady of the Lake passenger ferry up Lake Chelan from the town of Chelan, WA (or by floatplane). From the boat landing, take the seasonal shuttle bus (about 4 runs/day, roughly $7/adult) 11 miles up Stehekin Valley Road to High Bridge, the trailhead area for Agnes Creek/West Fork Agnes Creek. The West Fork Agnes Creek trail itself has largely disappeared; WTA describes it as 'a rough bushwhack along the West Fork of Agnes Creek' leading to 'no destination except the base of 8115-foot Agnes Mountain' — expect brush, blowdown, and route-finding the entire way in.
routes.descent: Retrace the West Fork Agnes Creek approach; no fixed rappel stations or established descent variant for this specific line are documented in available sources.
routes.itinerary: Multi-day trip: Day 1 - ferry to Stehekin and shuttle to High Bridge, begin hiking/bushwhacking up the West Fork Agnes Creek drainage; Day 2 - continue approach to a high camp near the base of the peak; Day 3 - summit push via the West Route and return to camp; Day 4 - hike out and catch the return ferry from Stehekin. Exact daily mileage/timing for this specific line is not documented in available sources, so treat this as a general framework rather than sourced splits.
routes.bail: Descend back down the West Fork Agnes Creek drainage the way you came; there is no documented alternate bail route or fixed rappel line for this approach.
routes.turnaround: Given the multi-day, trail-less bushwhack approach, no cell coverage, and the difficulty of navigating brushy terrain in the dark, set a firm turnaround time based on remaining daylight and current weather/visibility rather than a fixed clock time, and commit to it.
routes.bestSeason: Best attempted in mid-to-late summer once the Stehekin ferry is running (spring through fall) and seasonal snow has cleared enough to make the West Fork brush and any snow gullies passable; the area's dry-summer climate (rain-shadowed east-of-crest, Koppen Dsb) means June-September is typically driest, per NPS regional weather data.
routes.comms: No cell service in Stehekin or along the Agnes Creek/West Fork corridor; NPS explicitly advises contacting rangers the day before travel since there's no way to reach them from Stehekin itself. Parties should plan for a satellite communicator for this trip.
whatToBring: Wilderness/backcountry permit (self-issued for Glacier Peak Wilderness, or NPS permit if camping within Lake Chelan NRA/North Cascades NP)
Sturdy brush-worthy clothing (long sleeves/pants) for sustained bushwhacking
Bear-aware food storage (bear country per WTA)
Green Trails McGregor Mountain (No. 81) topographic map
proTips: Carry the Green Trails McGregor Mountain (No. 81) map — GPS tracks/beta for this specific historic line are scarce online
Budget substantially more time than the raw mileage suggests; the West Fork Agnes Creek trail has essentially vanished and travel is bushwhack-paced
Coordinate ferry and shuttle schedules in advance — Stehekin has no road access and the shuttle only runs a few times a day
watchOut: The West Fork Agnes Creek trail has largely disappeared; expect no tread, cairns, or signage for long stretches
This is a distinct, much less-repeated line than the Beckey-guide-recommended South Ridge/Northwest Face route from the main Agnes Creek Trail — don't assume beta for one applies to the other
No cell service anywhere on the approach; NPS asks parties to contact rangers a day before arriving in Stehekin
knownHazards: Dense, unmaintained brush and blowdown along the West Fork Agnes Creek drainage making route-finding slow and difficult
Extremely remote location with no cell coverage and multi-day travel time to reach help
Active bear country per Washington Trails Association

road.name: Stehekin Valley Road (High Bridge access)
road.status: Stehekin has no outside road connection — reached only by boat, floatplane, or trail
road.driveNote: From the Stehekin boat landing, a seasonal shuttle bus (about 4 departures/day, roughly $7/adult) covers the 11 miles up Stehekin Valley Road to High Bridge, the jumping-off point for the Agnes Creek and West Fork Agnes Creek trails.

climate.forecastZone: NWS Spokane forecast area, North Cascades East Slopes
climate.typical: Dry-summer continental climate (Koppen Dsb): warm, dry summers and cold, snowy winters, with the Stehekin valley sitting in a rain shadow east of the Cascade crest (1991-2020 normals: ~36.9 in annual precipitation, ~130 in annual snowfall).
climate.summer: Warm and mostly dry, June-September; Stehekin can reach the 90s F on hot days
climate.fall: Cooling with increasing precipitation from October onward
climate.winter: Cold and snowy; majority of the area's precipitation falls October-April

access.landManager: Lower approach (Stehekin Valley) managed by the National Park Service, Lake Chelan National Recreation Area/North Cascades National Park Complex; the upper mountain and West Fork Agnes Creek drainage lie within the Glacier Peak Wilderness, administered by the Okanogan-Wenatchee National Forest.
access.fees: Lady of the Lake ferry fare to Stehekin (schedule/fare varies by boat); optional ~$7/adult shuttle bus from the boat landing to High Bridge; NPS backcountry permit fee of $10 plus $6 if camping within Lake Chelan NRA/North Cascades NP land.
access.permit: Free self-issued wilderness permit at the trailhead for travel within Glacier Peak Wilderness (Forest Service land, no fee); an NPS backcountry permit (reserved on Recreation.gov, or a walk-up permit requested by emailing noca_wilderness@nps.gov a day ahead since there's no cell service in Stehekin) is required for overnight camping within Lake Chelan NRA/North Cascades National Park.

emergency.sheriffDispatch: RiverCom 911 dispatch (Chelan/Douglas Counties): 509-663-9911, or 911
emergency.rangerStation: NPS Stehekin/High Bridge area rangers — email noca_wilderness@nps.gov in advance (no cell service in Stehekin to contact same-day)
emergency.nearestHospital: Lake Chelan Community Hospital, Chelan, WA — Stehekin has no road access, so reaching it requires boat/floatplane; serious emergencies are typically evacuated by helicopter (nearest base in Brewster, WA, roughly 30 minutes flight time)
emergency.notes: No cell service anywhere in the Stehekin valley or on the Agnes Creek/West Fork approach; notify NPS rangers of your itinerary before losing connectivity.

routes.face: West side (West Fork Agnes Creek drainage)
routes.fa: W. Ronald Frazier and Dan O'Brien, 1936, via the West Fork of Agnes Creek
routes.corrections: Sources vary slightly on summit elevation (8,115 ft per WTA/Wikipedia summary text, 8,119 ft NAVD88 per Wikipedia's infobox, 8,133 ft per ListsOfJohn); the existing record's 8,131 ft falls within this cluster and was not overwritten absent one clearly authoritative figure. Also note: the well-documented online trip reports (trailcatjim.com, Out There Outdoors) describe a different, more commonly climbed line — the Beckey-guide-recommended South Ridge/Northwest Face route accessed via the main Agnes Creek Trail to Swamp Creek/Blue Basin — which is NOT the same as this West Route (the 1936 FA line via the West Fork of Agnes Creek). Detailed pitch/gear/timing beta for those reports was intentionally not applied to this route to avoid misattributing a different line's facts.

hierarchyNote: Confirmed as a real, well-documented place: Mountain Project's own GPS for this crag (47.3651, -121.31359) is an exact match to the given coordinates, and several individual route pages (With Love Lie Ancients Nestled Within, Buckets Full of Moonlight Lost, Glacier View Temple) independently list the identical GPS. However, note this is NOT a summit/alpine peak — "East Face" is a climbing sub-sector (one of three: West Wall, Lizard Ledge, East Face) of Keechelus Ridge, a single-pitch/short-multipitch sport-and-trad crag at roughly 4,400-4,700 ft on the forested ridge between Keechelus and Kachess Lakes, just east of Snoqualmie Pass. The parent "Keechelus Ridge" is geographically correct. No elevation figure was given for this node (correctly left null) since it's a crag sector rather than a discrete summit; Mountain Project's crag-base elevation is ~4,418 ft (1,347 m). Peakbagger/SummitPost have essentially no coverage of this location since it isn't a prominence-listed peak — Mountain Project, theCrag/openbeta cross-references, and The Mountaineers are the load-bearing sources here instead.
blurb: Keechelus Ridge's East Face is one of three sport/trad sectors at Keechelus Ridge, a high-elevation (~4,400-4,700 ft) crag on the forested ridge dividing Keechelus Lake and Kachess Lake just east of Snoqualmie Pass in Washington's Central Cascades. The rock is blocky, gas-pocketed andesite (texture compared to Tieton River crags) rising to over 130 ft in places, with commanding views of Mt. Rainier and Mt. Stuart. Most East Face lines were established as bolted sport routes by Leland Windham and partners (Brandon & Heather Kern, David Wolfe, Vincent Bailey, Nicole Flessner, James Mallory) between 2004 and 2007, with a scattering of newer routes added in subsequent years.

routes.overview: A short, powerful sport pitch through overhanging jugs to a mantle crux, a no-hands rest, a second mini-crux, then easier slab to the anchor.
routes.beta: Widely regarded as one of the best and most popular routes at Keechelus Ridge. Climb 30 ft of increasingly overhanging jugs to a crux mantle move, recover on a giant no-hands rest ledge, then work through a second mini-crux (roughly 5.10c difficulty) before easier but sustained slab climbing leads to the chain anchor on a big ledge. The line passes through a band of orange-streaked rock.
routes.approach: Standard Keechelus Ridge approach: from I-90 Exit 54 head north toward Gold Creek, then take FS Road 4832 east ~4 miles (paved to gravel); after a sharp left, continue ~3.25 miles of gravel to a small pullout parking area, then hike the spur road ~15 minutes to a flat area and follow a cairned trail into the forest ~10 more minutes to the wall. Within the East Face sector, this route is reached by walking downhill from Black Systems Solar.
routes.fa: Leland Windham & Brandon Kern, July 2004
routes.detailedRack: Fully bolted sport route: 10 bolts to a chain anchor. 10-12 quickdraws is sufficient; no trad gear needed.
routes.proNeeds: 10 bolts, chain anchor; a 60m rope easily lowers the 75 ft route.
routes.bestSeason: Best from roughly June through September once the access road is snow-free (typically mid-June); the crag's southeast exposure dries quickly after rain but can be hot in direct July sun.
watchOut: Route passes through a band of orange-streaked, sometimes lichen-y rock -- watch hold quality on the upper slab.
Located specifically below/downhill of Black Systems Solar; easy to walk past it on the approach traverse.
proTips: Community consensus rates it one of the best routes at the crag (3.4-star average) -- worth prioritizing on a visit.

pitchDetail.notes: 30 ft of overhanging jugs to a crux mantle, no-hands rest, second mini-crux (~5.10c), easier slab to chain anchor.

access.landManager: Okanogan-Wenatchee National Forest, Cle Elum Ranger District
access.fees: None documented for the Keechelus Ridge climbers' pullout itself.
access.permit: No climbing permit required per Mountain Project; a Northwest Forest Pass may apply at developed Snoqualmie Pass trailheads/sno-parks but not at this roadside pullout.
access.closures: FS Road 4832-142 (the separate spur into Gold Creek Pond/valley, NOT the main 4832 used for this crag) is under a Forest Service restoration-project closure April 1-Nov 30 annually through 2028; the main FSR 4832 approach to Keechelus Ridge remains open.

road.name: FS Road 4832
road.status: Open (gravel, generally decent with shallow potholes)
road.seasonalGate: Typically snow-free and drivable from about mid-June through fall; snowbound/inaccessible in winter.
road.driveNote: From I-90 Exit 54, head north toward Gold Creek, then east on FS Road 4832 ~4 miles (pavement turns to gravel), sharp left, continue ~3.25 miles to a small parking pullout.

climate.typical: High-elevation, rain-shadow crag: notably drier than lower-elevation Snoqualmie/North Bend cliffs.
climate.summer: Primary season; southeast exposure can be hot in July but dries very quickly after rain.
climate.spring: Access road often still snow-covered into late spring.
climate.winter: Road unplowed/snowbound; not a winter climbing venue.

routes.corrections: A Mountain Project trip-report photo caption labels this route 5.11c, but the route's own dedicated MP page (a more authoritative, single-route source) lists 5.11b -- used 5.11b.

routes.overview: A moss-lined, traditionally-protected crack line at the far end of the East Face, notable for heavy moss coverage rather than technical difficulty.
routes.beta: A wiggly crack in mossy rock. Climb through a moss-covered corner ledge, then mossy face climbing to a moss-covered summit jug. Bolts referenced in the Snoqualmie Rock guidebook were not visible to a recent MP contributor ("I think the moss ate them") -- treat as a natural-gear/trad line with a chain anchor at the top.
routes.approach: From the East Face parking/trail area, walk down the talus along the edge of the cliff and turn the corner to the left, passing several bolted sport projects before reaching the moss-filled start of this route.
routes.fa: Rustin Gibson
routes.detailedRack: Gear to 3 inches (single rack). Reported bolts from the older Snoqualmie Rock guidebook were not found/visible on a recent ascent -- come prepared to protect it as a natural line. Chain anchor at the top.
routes.proNeeds: Trad gear to 3"; do not count on bolts despite older guidebook references.
watchOut: Heavy moss throughout the crack, corner, and summit holds reduces friction and can hide protection placements/older bolts.
Low traffic/low star rating (2 of 5 stars, only 3 votes) -- expect a dirtier, less-traveled line than the East Face sport classics.
routes.bestSeason: Same window as the rest of the crag (June-September); being mossy and shaded in spots, it likely stays damp longer after rain than the sunnier sport lines nearby.

access.landManager: Okanogan-Wenatchee National Forest, Cle Elum Ranger District
access.permit: No climbing permit required per Mountain Project.
access.closures: FS Road 4832-142 spur to Gold Creek Pond is seasonally closed for a restoration project (Apr 1-Nov 30, through 2028); the main FSR 4832 approach used to reach the crag is unaffected.

road.name: FS Road 4832
road.status: Open
road.seasonalGate: Snow-free roughly mid-June through fall.
road.driveNote: See main crag approach via I-90 Exit 54 and FS Road 4832.

routes.routeName: With Love Lie Ancients Nestled Within
routes.overview: One of the East Face's best-regarded moderate lines: an easier opening followed by sustained jug-hauling in a spectacular exposed position.
routes.beta: Starts directly above a rest area with trees roughly 30 ft back from the wall, positioned to the left of two other routes that begin on higher ledges. Easier climbing off the deck gives way to sustained jug hauling with a very exposed, spectacular position -- called out by the local guidebook page as possibly the best 5.10c in Washington.
routes.approach: Standard Keechelus Ridge / East Face approach via FS Road 4832 (see crag-level directions); the route starts above a small rest/staging area with trees at the base of the East Face wall.
routes.fa: Leland Windham & David Wolfe, August 2006
routes.detailedRack: Fully bolted sport route: 13 bolts to a chain anchor.
routes.proNeeds: 13 bolts, chain anchor.
proTips: Considered one of the most popular routes at Keechelus Ridge and among the best 5.10c pitches in the state -- a priority tick.
routes.bestSeason: June through September once the access road clears (typically mid-June); dries quickly given the crag's rain-shadow/southeast exposure.

waypoints.name: With Love Lie Ancients Nestled Within (East Face)

access.landManager: Okanogan-Wenatchee National Forest, Cle Elum Ranger District
access.permit: No climbing permit required per Mountain Project.
access.closures: FS Road 4832-142 spur to Gold Creek Pond closed seasonally (Apr 1-Nov 30, through 2028) for restoration; main FSR 4832 crag approach unaffected.

road.name: FS Road 4832
road.status: Open
road.seasonalGate: Snow-free roughly mid-June through fall.
road.driveNote: I-90 Exit 54 -> FS Road 4832 east ~4 mi -> sharp left, ~3.25 mi gravel to parking pullout.

routes.overview: A long, 2-pitch (or single-pitch-with-mid-anchor) 5.9 climb on the lower, west-facing section of the East Face -- regarded as one of the best 5.9s in the state.
routes.beta: Access involves traversing below the wall past Lizard Ledge to the first west-facing section of the lower wall. Can be climbed as one long pitch or split into two via mid-route anchors. If done in one pitch, alpine-sling the lower bolts and back-clean draws you can reach; two closely-spaced bolts protect the crux (clip both within a 6 ft stretch). A 70m rope is NOT long enough to lower from the upper anchors -- rappel or walk off / use the mid anchors instead.
routes.approach: From the East Face trail, traverse below the wall past Lizard Ledge to reach the first (west-facing) section of the lower wall.
routes.fa: Leland Windham & Vincent Bailey, August 2005
gear: 70m rope insufficient for single-pitch lower -- plan accordingly
routes.detailedRack: Fully bolted: 17 bolts total with 2 chain anchors (one mid-route, one at top). Alpine draws recommended to reduce rope drag if linking both pitches.
routes.proNeeds: 17 bolts, 2 chain anchors. Two bolts placed within 6 ft of each other protect the crux.

pitchDetail.notes: Lower pitch to mid-route chain anchor; best belay ledge on the route, shaded from morning through mid-afternoon sun.
Continues from mid anchor to upper chain anchor; crux protected by two bolts placed within 6 feet of each other.

watchOut: A 70-meter rope is not long enough to lower off the upper anchors if climbed as a single 130 ft pitch -- either split it at the mid-route anchors or rappel in two stages.
proTips: Rated one of the best 5.9s in Washington by the local guidebook page.
The mid-route belay ledge shields the belayer from sun morning through mid-afternoon -- useful on a hot day.
routes.bestSeason: June through September; the west-facing lower section and shaded mid-route ledge make it a comparatively good choice on hot afternoons versus the crag's more exposed southeast-facing lines.

access.landManager: Okanogan-Wenatchee National Forest, Cle Elum Ranger District
access.permit: No climbing permit required per Mountain Project.
access.closures: FS Road 4832-142 spur to Gold Creek Pond closed seasonally (Apr 1-Nov 30, through 2028); main FSR 4832 crag approach unaffected.

road.name: FS Road 4832
road.status: Open
road.seasonalGate: Snow-free roughly mid-June through fall.
road.driveNote: I-90 Exit 54 -> FS Road 4832 east ~4 mi -> sharp left, ~3.25 mi gravel to parking pullout.

routes.overview: A brief, easy East Face pitch with an outsized view -- climbers get a glimpse of Mt. Rainier from the crux ledge move.
routes.beta: Short scramble up to a belay area, then a brief but engaging climb; the crux is moving up onto a ledge right before the anchor, with a view toward Mt. Rainier in the distance. Located just past the first set of bolts belonging to the adjacent route Enchanting Puller Blind (5.10c).
routes.approach: Standard East Face approach via FS Road 4832 and the cairned climbers' trail; a short scramble up a ledge reaches the belay.
routes.fa: Leland Windham & Nicole Flessner, 2007
routes.detailedRack: Fully bolted: 4 bolts to a chain anchor.
routes.proNeeds: 4 bolts, chain anchor.
watchOut: Low popularity/low star rating (1.7 stars) relative to its East Face neighbors -- set expectations for a shorter, less-polished pitch.
Starts adjacent to Enchanting Puller Blind; easy to confuse the two starts.

access.landManager: Okanogan-Wenatchee National Forest, Cle Elum Ranger District
access.permit: No climbing permit required per Mountain Project.
access.closures: FS Road 4832-142 spur to Gold Creek Pond closed seasonally (Apr 1-Nov 30, through 2028); main FSR 4832 crag approach unaffected.

road.name: FS Road 4832
road.status: Open
road.seasonalGate: Snow-free roughly mid-June through fall.
road.driveNote: I-90 Exit 54 -> FS Road 4832 east ~4 mi -> sharp left, ~3.25 mi gravel to parking pullout.

routes.overview: The rightmost route on a large black ledge at the far end of the East Face, climbing a detached pillar into a pale, streaked wall.
routes.beta: Scramble up short fourth-class terrain to a belay on a large black ledge; this is the rightmost route on that ledge. Ascend a detached pillar protected by two bolts, transition onto a white-streaked wall, then negotiate numerous sloping holds -- the difficulty here is as much psychological (trusting sparse, questionable bolts) as physical -- through two more bolts to the anchor chains.
routes.approach: Standard East Face approach via FS Road 4832 and the cairned climbers' trail to a large black-rock ledge at the end (rightmost end) of the East Face sector; short 4th-class scramble to the belay.
routes.fa: Leland Windham, James Mallory & David Wolfe, August 2004
routes.detailedRack: Fully bolted: only 5 bolts to a chain anchor, and MP notes those bolts were 'in really bad shape' as of a 2024 report.
routes.proNeeds: 5 bolts (reported in poor condition in 2024), chain anchor. Consider backing up or inspecting bolts closely before committing.
watchOut: Bolts explicitly reported as being in poor/deteriorated condition as of 2024 -- inspect hardware before weighting it.
Sparse protection (only 5 bolts over 65 ft) adds a psychological/run-out character on top of the sloping-hold crux.
Low star rating (1.4 stars, 11 votes) -- a lightly-traveled, harder-to-protect line relative to East Face classics.

access.landManager: Okanogan-Wenatchee National Forest, Cle Elum Ranger District
access.permit: No climbing permit required per Mountain Project.
access.closures: FS Road 4832-142 spur to Gold Creek Pond closed seasonally (Apr 1-Nov 30, through 2028); main FSR 4832 crag approach unaffected.

road.name: FS Road 4832
road.status: Open
road.seasonalGate: Snow-free roughly mid-June through fall.
road.driveNote: I-90 Exit 54 -> FS Road 4832 east ~4 mi -> sharp left, ~3.25 mi gravel to parking pullout.

blurb: South Early Winters Spire (7,807 ft) is the higher of the two Early Winters Spires in the Liberty Bell Group at Washington Pass, North Cascades — a rapakivi-textured granite massif (part of the Golden Horn batholith) split from its twin, North Early Winters Spire, by a deep glacially- and jointing-eroded cleft. It offers excellent granite climbing on every aspect, from the moderate 3-pitch South Arete (Fred & Helmy Beckey, 1942) to sustained 5.11+/A0 big-wall-style lines like the Direct East Buttress, Southern Man, and The Passenger. The peak's true first ascent was via the Southwest Couloir on July 20, 1937 by Kenneth Adam, Raffi Bedayn, and W. Kenneth Davis.
hierarchyNote: Minor coordinate note only — name, elevation, and parent hierarchy all check out cleanly (Liberty Bell Group / Washington Pass / North Cascades; 7,807 ft confirmed by Mountain Project, SummitPost, Peakbagger, and Wikipedia). However, the given peak coordinates (48.512275, -120.655395) sit ~0.4-0.5 mi south of Peakbagger's summit coordinate (48.5167653, -120.6573362), and instead closely match Mountain Project's route-anchor GPS pin (48.51216, -120.65513) used on all of this peak's individual route pages. This suggests the stored lat/lng may be an approach/route-cluster point rather than the true summit — worth a spot-check against Peakbagger if summit-precision coordinates matter downstream.

routes.fa: Fred Beckey and Helmy Beckey, June 19, 1942
routes.season: May-Oct (clears of snow/ice early due to S-SW aspect)
routes.overview: A moderate, popular 3-pitch climb up the south side of SEWS — the peak's easiest technical route and a classic first alpine rock climb, with a short approach and big North Cascades views.
routes.beta: Not truly a knife-edge arete for most of its length — it's a steep ridge where the actual crest is rarely followed directly. The first pitch (5.5) is the hardest; the remainder is mostly 3rd/4th class with occasional 5th-class moves.
routes.approach: From the Blue Lake Trailhead on SR-20 (Washington Pass), hike the Blue Lake Trail roughly 1.5 miles to an obvious clearing/meadow with a wooden trail sign; climber paths continue uphill and right at a stream crossing toward the base of the spire (about 1 hour).
routes.descent: Downclimb/rappel the South Arete route itself.
routes.descentText: From the summit, downclimb the upper South Arete (mostly 3rd/4th class) to a short exposed 5th-class section, then make one or more short rappels from fixed anchors to regain easier ground and the Blue Lake Trail descent.
routes.bail: Retreat is straightforward on this route — downclimb back the way you came from any belay; the line stays close to easy terrain throughout.
routes.turnaround: Start early to beat weekend crowds on the approach and to be off the ridge before afternoon thunderstorms, which are the primary summer hazard on the east-side Cascades.
routes.bestSeason: South-southwest aspect sheds snow quickly; the route can often be climbed shortly after SR-20 reopens for the season in spring, through early fall.
routes.comms: Cell service is unreliable to absent at Washington Pass; do not rely on a phone for emergency communication.

pitchDetail.notes: Short steep crack, then either right and up a short stretch across a slab to a small tree, or left across a short friction slab (5.5 finger traverse).
Gully becoming a chockstone chimney for ~100 feet, with one 5.4 move in the chimney.
Easier ground to the summit.

gear: Approach shoes for the Blue Lake Trail
whatToBring: Light layer for wind on the ridge
proTips: A great introductory alpine rock route with scenic views of Liberty Bell and the North Cascades
Highway 20 usually opens the route to early-season ascents once snow clears off this south-facing line
watchOut: Route is popular and can be crowded on summer weekends
Loose rock in places typical of North Cascades granite ridges
knownHazards: Exposure on the knife-edge sections despite the moderate grade
Afternoon thunderstorms common June-August

road.name: State Route 20 (North Cascades Highway)
road.status: Seasonal — closed roughly Dec-Apr
road.seasonalGate: Gated between Ross Dam (MP134) and Silver Star/Early Winters (MP171-178) each winter due to avalanche hazard
road.driveNote: Approach via SR-20 from Winthrop/Mazama (east) or Marblemount/Newhalem (west); no winter access.

climate.forecastZone: NWAC East Slopes North Cascades / Washington Pass
climate.typical: Dry east-Cascades climate; granite dries fast after rain; afternoon convective thunderstorms are the main summer hazard
climate.spring: Snow lingers in shaded gullies into June; access begins once SR-20 reopens (mid-Apr to May)
climate.summer: Warm, mostly dry June-Aug with afternoon thunderstorm risk
climate.fall: Cool, stable Sept-Oct weather is prime rock season
climate.winter: SR-20 closed; area inaccessible by vehicle

access.landManager: Okanogan-Wenatchee National Forest, Methow Valley Ranger District
access.fees: Northwest Forest Pass (or interagency pass) required for parking at Blue Lake Trailhead
access.passRequired: Northwest Forest Pass at Blue Lake Trailhead
access.closures: Entire area inaccessible when SR-20 over Washington Pass is closed for winter

timing.recommendedStart: Early morning to beat crowds and afternoon thunderstorms

emergency.sheriffDispatch: 911 emergency; 509-422-7232 non-emergency dispatch
emergency.rangerStation: Methow Valley Ranger District, Winthrop, WA — (509) 996-4000
emergency.nearestHospital: Three Rivers Hospital (24/7 ER), Brewster, WA
emergency.notes: Cell coverage is unreliable/absent in the Washington Pass corridor; technical rescues in this area typically require helicopter extraction given remote, exposed terrain.

routes.overview: Historic name for early (1966) partial lines up the lower east buttress of SEWS that did not continue to the summit; the complete buttress route to the top is documented today only as 'Direct East Buttress.'
routes.corrections: No standalone 'East Buttress' route reaching the summit is documented on Mountain Project, SummitPost, or SuperTopo — all three list only 'Direct East Buttress' (Beckey/Leen) as the complete line up this buttress. Per the AAC Journal, two incomplete 1966 lower-buttress lines existed before that: a 'lower east buttress and northeast face' route (Steve Marts, Fred Stanley, Don McPherson) and a 'lower east buttress and southeast face' route (Don Anderson, Jim Richardson, Margaret Young, Paul Myhre) — both stopped below blank sections and did not reach the summit. This DB entry likely duplicates the same physical feature as 'Direct East Buttress' (wa_south_early_winter_spire_direct_east_buttress) rather than representing a distinct, currently-climbed line; recommend checking a current guidebook (Beckey's Cascade Alpine Guide Vol. 3, or Nelson & Potterfield's Selected Climbs in the Cascades) before treating it as separate from that route.

routes.fa: Donald Anderson and Larry Scott, 1964
routes.overview: A classic 7-pitch, 900-foot line up the rib on SEWS's west face, mixing chimneys, offwidths, and slabs on excellent granite; widely regarded as one of the best moderate alpine rock routes at Washington Pass.
routes.beta: Sustained variety of climbing styles makes this route a classic — from the 'Wavy Crack' to the 'Bear Hug' offwidth pitch and the 'Nervous Nelly' slab traverse.
routes.approach: From the Blue Lake Trailhead, hike about 1.25 miles to a clearing, then follow climber trails (staying off fragile alpine meadows) to the base, marked by a Y-shaped larch tree.
routes.descent: Descend via the South Arete route.
routes.descentText: From the summit, descend the South Arete via a combination of downclimbing (3rd/4th class) and short rappels back to the Blue Lake Trail.
routes.bail: Rappel the route from any of several tree/gear anchors on the lower pitches; the upper slab pitches (P6) have minimal fixed gear so downclimbing may be preferable if retreating from high on the route.
routes.turnaround: Plan to summit and begin descent by early-to-mid afternoon to avoid being caught on exposed slab/arete terrain during east-Cascades convective thunderstorms.
routes.bestSeason: West-facing aspect and moderate grade make this a solid June-September objective once snow has cleared from the approach meadows.
routes.comms: Cell service is unreliable to absent at Washington Pass.

pitchDetail.notes: ~160 ft scramble through an awkward bulge.
~80 ft, left-leaning wide crack ('Wavy Crack').
~60 ft traverse.
~100 ft, dual offwidths approach with a delicate, unprotected slab section.
~80 ft, dual offwidth ('Bear Hug' pitch).
~165 ft, slabby arete with minimal protection ('Nervous Nelly').
~80 ft, gully/crack systems to the summit.

routes.detailedRack: One 60m rope; a set of stoppers; cams to #3 Camalot with doubles in the 1-2" range; one large #5 Camalot for the pitch-5 offwidth.
whatToBring: Approach shoes for the meadow approach
proTips: Very good rock overall, with good protection on the harder sections
The Wavy Crack, Bear Hug, and Nervous Nelly pitches are the memorable highlights
watchOut: Spicier slab runouts noted by multiple parties, especially P4 and P6
knownHazards: Unprotected slab moves on P4 and P6
Afternoon thunderstorms common in summer
