routes.fa: Mark Allen, Tom Smith, and Joel Kauffman, 2006
routes.overview: A 9-pitch, 800-foot aid/free route starting via an obvious bolt line 20 feet downhill from the chockstone start of the NW Face route; still awaiting a confirmed full free ascent as of the most recent reporting.
routes.beta: Sustained bolted face climbing low on the route (P1 A0/5.11b) gives way to thinner seam and crack pitches, finishing on easier ground shared with the Southwest Rib.

pitchDetail.notes: ~105 ft bolted face with a runout 5.6 approach to the first bolt.
Thin flaring seam with one bolt.
~85 ft; wide crack, alcove, steep corner crack — tricky stemming boulder problem.
~75 ft, 'Skywalker Traverse' with a tension-traverse option.
~175 ft; awkward seam, detached-block passage, 5.9 corner finger crack.
Blocky terrain through trees to the SW Rib.
Simul-climbed or pitched out via the Southwest Rib to the summit.

routes.detailedRack: 2x cams 0.2-2" (or equivalent), with some offset cams in the smaller range useful for P2; one set of nuts plus extra small brassies; minimum 11 draws.
watchOut: Detached/hollow block on P5 is a real objective hazard
Not confirmed as freed as of the latest Mountain Project reporting — expect aid on the crux pitches
proTips: The first three pitches can be rappelled with a 60m rope if bailing
routes.descent: Continue to the summit via the upper Southwest Rib, then descend the South Arete.

routes.fa: Bryan Burdo and Scott Johnston, 2007
routes.overview: A sustained 9-pitch, 900-foot trad line up the south face of SEWS, starting in a small left-facing dihedral protected by 4 bolts.
routes.descent: Descend via the South Arete.
routes.comms: Cell service is unreliable to absent at Washington Pass.

routes.fa: Blake Herrington and Graham Zimmerman
routes.overview: A 6-pitch, 800-foot free line starting roughly 40 feet down and right of the 'giant chockstone,' finishing on the upper South Arete near the summit.
routes.beta: Stays in shade until roughly 1pm, making it a good choice for hot summer days; features sustained crack climbing through the crux P1-P2, easing into a right-facing corner and easy ground higher up.

pitchDetail.notes: ~100 ft crack climbing to a fixed anchor on a pedestal with small trees.
~82 ft double incipient cracks; flared jams, awkward stances, and fiddly gear.
~40 ft short pitch with an awkward first move.
~164 ft right-facing corner; balancy first move leads to easier climbing.
~262 ft trending right toward the South Arete, past a landmark 'little tree,' traversing into the notch.
~25 ft to the summit.

routes.detailedRack: Triple small cams through yellow aliens; doubles to #1 Camalot; single #2 and #3 Camalots; small wired nuts recommended.
proTips: Route stays shaded until about 1pm — a good pick for hot days
watchOut: Watch for mountain goats near the summit (P6)
Rope drag on P5 — manage rope carefully on the long traverse pitch
routes.descent: Joins the South Arete near the top; descend via the South Arete.

routes.fa: Fred Beckey and Jim Madsen, 1967
routes.face: West face, midway up SEWS, left of the Boving Roof
routes.overview: A short, unique offwidth/chimney pitch through 'the Dolphin' — the unmistakable dark chasm midway up SEWS, directly left of the Boving Roof — with a wild finish rejoining the Southwest Rib's final slabby arete.
routes.beta: An amazing and unusual offwidth/chimneying experience on great rock, but a serious, poorly-protected (R-rated) pitch that has likely seen relatively few ascents because of this.
routes.approach: Traverse left over easy terrain after the 3rd pitch of Northwest Face (Boving-Pollock) or the 2nd pitch of Southwest Rib, roughly 100 feet left of the Boving Roof.
routes.proNeeds: Limited placements throughout; gear to size 5" recommended. A chockstone sits roughly 20 feet up; the chimney narrows to 1-2 feet wide near the top, where good gear placements finally reappear near the roof exit.
watchOut: Rated R for serious/runout protection — a lack of good gear has likely minimized the number of ascents
Narrow, awkward chimneying near the top
proTips: Backpacks can be hauled from the belay rather than carried through the chimney
routes.descent: Rejoins the Southwest Rib's final slabby arete pitch to the summit; descend via the South Arete.

routes.fa: Boving and Kerns, 1977 (per Mountain Project)
routes.overview: A fun, varied 6-pitch route up the shorter west face of SEWS with fairly serious climbing off the ground and several minor cruxes — more of an undertaking than the comparable west-face route on North Early Winters Spire.
routes.approach: A short traverse from the gully between North and South Early Winters Spire leads to a steep corner on the climber's right.

pitchDetail.notes: Initial traverse to a steep corner (~10 moves); belay in a small tree with pitons and cams, or continue.
Right-facing corner with limited feet and hard gear placements — solid 5.11a.
Short chimney continuing right into a vegetated crack past a block to a large ledge belay.
Scramble/rope travel to the base of the overhangs.
Pair of obvious overhangs with committing movement; belay in the gully.
Nice hand crack with a 5.9 crux, then traverse right to the summit.

routes.detailedRack: Standard rack with extra TCUs or aliens.
routes.corrections: Mountain Project's FA data lists 'Boving and Kerns, 1977' for this route despite it being named 'Boving-Pollock' — likely a naming/data discrepancy between the route's namesake pair and the recorded FA party; not independently resolved by other sources checked.
routes.descent: Descend via the South Arete.

routes.fa: Bryan Burdo, Pete Doorish, and Greg White, October 1991
routes.face: South face, right of the South Arete
routes.overview: Widely regarded as one of the best rock climbs in the Cascades — a sustained, technical 7-pitch line featuring roof pulls, campus moves, and an amazing finger crack.

pitchDetail.notes: Roof approach with one bolt to a bolted belay.
Roof pull with campus moves through a flared crack.
Amazing sustained finger crack to a roof/slot.
Bolted face section with a mantle crux past a bolt.
Corner and splitter crack above a dead tree.
Thin-hand crack transitioning to a ramp.
Splitters and blocks up and left to the topout.

routes.detailedRack: Doubles from thin-finger cams to #2 Camalot, one #3 Camalot, one set of nuts.
routes.approach: From the base of the South Arete, descend about 10 minutes of rock-shoe scrambling northeast, crossing the first gully, to double cracks near a fir tree at the right end of a ledge.
routes.descent: Descend via the South Arete.

routes.fa: Fred Beckey and Doug Leen (AAC Journal dates the completed direct ascent to late July 1969, after an early-summer reconnaissance the same year)
routes.aidGrade: A0 (originally more sustained aid; largely freed with modern gear)
routes.overview: The full 1,100-foot direct line up SEWS's East Buttress — originally a heavily-bolted aid route, now commonly free-climbed (with a short A0 bolt-ladder section) in a single day.
routes.beta: Two earlier (1966) partial lines had stalled below blank sections on this buttress; Beckey and Leen's 1969 direct ascent required substantial bolting to link a continuous line to the summit.
routes.approach: From the hairpin turn on Hwy 20, hike up Spire Gully following occasional cairns to the last large tree in the center of the gully; traverse right across talus (or snow early season) to the notch between North and South Early Winters Spire, then traverse left to the base of the route. Crampons and an ice axe are useful early season.
routes.descent: Downclimb the South Arete (mostly 3rd/4th class with a short exposed 5th-class section), then make three single-rope rappels from bolted anchors/chains to the base; hike out via the Blue Lake Trail.
routes.descentText: From the summit, downclimb the upper South Arete to a short, exposed 5th-class section, then rappel three single-rope pitches from bolted anchors/chains to reach the base of the spire, from which the Blue Lake Trail leads back to the trailhead.

pitchDetail.notes: Rambling pitch with moss, loose rock, and vegetation; reasonable gear along the way.
Crack system with various ledges to the base of a large dihedral; some loose rock.
Through a roof, plus ~60 additional feet of 5.8 terrain.
~14-bolt ladder with a finger-crack exit.
Finger crack with good protection.
Bolt ladder with two mandatory 5.9+ mantles.
Wandering cracks to a tree.
Ledges and rambling terrain.
Step-down to the summit.

routes.detailedRack: Standard alpine rack to 4"; several small carabiners for clipping the old bolts on pitches 4 and 6.
watchOut: Loose rock on the lower pitches (P1-P2)
Old, weathered bolt ladders on P4/P6 — inspect fixed gear
routes.corrections: Sources conflict on the FA year: Mountain Project and several trip-report/blog sites cite 1968, while the AAC Journal (the closest contemporary primary account) states the direct ascent was completed in late July 1969, following an early-summer reconnaissance the same year. The AAC Journal account is treated as more authoritative here.

routes.fa: Allen, Falley, and Kauffman, 2008; FFA by B. Matthews and B. Burdo, 2010
routes.face: East buttress area (shares lower pitches with Direct East Buttress)
routes.overview: Shares its first 4-5 pitches with the Direct East Buttress before breaking out left through funky 5.10a climbing into a sustained thin-crack headwall that improves in quality the higher it goes.
routes.beta: Upper headwall pitches feature sustained thin cracks starting in a corner and continuing for a couple of pitches, with belays that are vague and not yet fully fixed.
watchOut: Belays on the upper pitches are vague and unfixed — solid trad anchor-building experience required
routes.descent: Descend via the South Arete.

routes.fa: Paul Boving and Steve Pollock (FA year not documented in sources checked)
routes.face: West face, above the Southwest Rib
routes.overview: Climbs the first three pitches of the Southwest Rib, then breaks left to an incredible double-roof system — the 'money pitch' of the route — before rejoining the upper Southwest Rib.
routes.approach: Climb the first three pitches of the Southwest Rib, then go left at the ledge toward the roofs.

pitchDetail.notes: ~40 ft of loose climbing to a belay stance just below the roofs; watch for a loose block at the start.
~100 ft of powerful climbing going under and pulling up the double roofs — the money pitch.
~80 ft starting in an easy chimney to 5.9 thin hands, stepping right to the top of the SW Rib 'Bear Hug' pitch and an excellent ledge.

routes.detailedRack: Rack for the Southwest Rib's initial pitches, plus doubles of 0.5-3" cams for the roofs.
watchOut: Loose rock on P4 — watch for the loose block at the start
routes.descent: Continue via the remaining Southwest Rib pitches to the summit, then descend the South Arete.

blurb: Mount Stuart (9,415 ft) is the second-highest non-volcanic peak in Washington and the high point of the Stuart Range, rising above Ingalls Creek and the Enchantments in the Alpine Lakes Wilderness on the Chelan/Kittitas county line. It is built of rugged, extensively-jointed Late Cretaceous granodiorite, with a south face that gains roughly 5,000 feet in two horizontal miles and three glaciers (Stuart, Ice Cliff, Sherpa) tucked into its north side. Its North Ridge — one of Steck and Roper's Fifty Classic Climbs of North America — was first climbed with a Great Gendarme bypass by John Rupley and Don Gordon in 1956 and completed direct over the Gendarme by Mead Hargis and Jay Ossiander in 1963.

routes.overview: Climbs the upper ~1,200 feet of Mount Stuart's famous North Ridge from the notch below the Great Gendarme, following exposed 4th-to-mid-5th-class ridge crest to two steep 5.9 pitches on the Gendarme itself, then easier terrain to the summit.
routes.beta: The classic 'short' entry to the North Ridge, joining the ridge at the notch below the Great Gendarme (via Goat Pass/Stuart Glacier from the south, or the hanging valley below Sherpa Peak from the north) rather than the true toe. About 11 pitches of low/mid-5th-class climbing on exposed, solid granite lead to the Gendarme, whose crux is a steep fingers-and-hands dihedral (5.9) followed by an offwidth; most parties instead rappel around the Gendarme.
routes.fa: John Rupley & Don Gordon, 1956 (bypassed the Gendarme); Great Gendarme direct finish and later ascents by James Wickwire & Fred Stanley, 1964
routes.rappels: ~75 ft rappel off the right side of the ridge below the Great Gendarme if bypassing; otherwise walk off via Cascadian Couloir
objHaz: Loose rock in the bypass gully below the Great Gendarme
Cold, shaded Gendarme pitches (can ice up)
routes.face: North Ridge, upper section from the notch
routes.approach: South: Longs Pass/Ingalls Way Trail to Ingalls Lake, along the east shore, over Stuart Pass, down talus to Goat Pass, then across the Stuart Glacier to the notch below the ridge crest. North: Stuart Lake Trailhead via the Mountaineer Creek trail, cross the creek, climb a steep talus/boulder slope to the hanging valley below Sherpa Peak, then to the toe/notch.
routes.descent: Cascadian Couloir
routes.descentText: From the true summit, travel southeast on blocky 3rd/4th-class terrain toward the false summit, follow cairns down into the Cascadian Couloir snowfield, then descend extended 3rd-class scree/talus to Ingalls Creek. Sherpa Glacier or a Sherpa Pass glissade are faster early-season alternatives for parties returning to a north-side camp.
routes.bail: Rappel anchors exist along the lower ridge; below the Great Gendarme, parties commonly rappel ~75 ft off the ridge's right side into a gully rather than commit to the Gendarme pitches.
routes.turnaround: Set a firm turnaround before starting the Gendarme pitches — retreat above them is difficult and the Cascadian Couloir descent from the summit is long; getting benighted above the Gendarme in bad weather is a real hazard.
routes.bestSeason: Prime July through September once approach snow and the moat below the Gendarme have melted; a lingering ice/snow patch in the bypass gully can persist into midsummer.
routes.comms: No reliable cell coverage on the ridge or approach; a satellite messenger is recommended.
routes.detailedRack: Standard alpine trad rack: nuts, cams to roughly 3.5in (a single wide piece for the Gendarme offwidth), doubles in common finger-to-hand sizes, and long slings for wandering ridge belays.
routes.proNeeds: Protection on the ridge pitches is described by climbers as 'pretty subjective' — natural gear supplements a standard rack; the Gendarme offwidth wants a dedicated wide piece.
whatToBring: Extra water for the long approach
proTips: Most parties bypass the Great Gendarme via a short rappel rather than climb it direct — decide before you're below it
The traverse gully below the Gendarme can hold snow/ice into midsummer
watchOut: Descending the Cascadian Couloir is loose and sustained — some parties call it the worst part of the day
Afternoon thunderstorms build quickly over the Stuart Range in summer
knownHazards: Loose rock in the bypass gully
Rockfall from parties above
Long, exposed Cascadian Couloir descent

pitchDetail.notes: Exposed, easy ridge-crest climbing on solid granite to the base of the Great Gendarme
Steep fingers-and-hands dihedral with good rests
Offwidth section; carry a 3.5in piece
Blocky terrain to the summit

road.name: Icicle Rd/FR 7601 (north) or Teanaway Rd N. Fork/FR 9737 (south)
road.status: Seasonal gravel forest roads, high-clearance recommended late season
road.seasonalGate: FR 7601 gated in winter; south-side roads can stay snowbound into June

climate.typical: Dry, continental high-alpine climate; hot days, cold nights, afternoon-storm risk

routes.overview: A moderate 4-5 pitch rock route up the steep (but not vertical) headwall visible just below the summit on the south side, topping out very near the true summit.
routes.beta: A long, loose scree/talus approach leads to a cirque below the headwall at roughly 8,700 ft; from there, 4-5 pitches of mostly 4th and low-5th class climbing, with two short 5.7 cruxes, ascend the center of the wall trending left to the upper South Rib, with ledge belays available at every stance.
routes.fa: Paul Myhre & Darrell Sorenson, 1966
objHaz: Loose scree/choss on the approach gully
Sun exposure/heat on a south-facing route
routes.face: South Headwall, near/southwest of the true summit
routes.approach: From Ingalls Creek Trail via the Longs Pass Trail, hike up the first major clearing west of Ulrich's Couloir; several thousand feet of steep scree, choss, and occasional 3rd-class terrain lead to ~7,000 ft, take the right-hand couloir at the fork, and continue to the cirque below the headwall at ~8,700 ft.
routes.descent: Cascadian Couloir
routes.descentText: The route tops out very close to the true summit; descend south via the Cascadian Couloir.
routes.bail: Retreat down the approach gully is straightforward below the technical pitches; above them, downclimb or build anchors off natural gear/ledges.
routes.turnaround: A sunny, south-facing route that heats up fast — start early to beat both the heat and afternoon storms, and turn around if the technical pitches aren't reached by late morning.
routes.bestSeason: Best July through September once the approach gullies and choss slopes have dried out.
routes.comms: No reliable cell coverage.
routes.detailedRack: Cams and stoppers for mostly 4th/low-5th terrain plus two short 5.7 sections; long slings for wandering belays. No fixed gear reported.
routes.proNeeds: A standard rack of cams and nuts is sufficient; ledges provide belay stations for every pitch.
whatToBring: Approach shoes for the scree gully
proTips: Take the right-hand couloir at the ~7,000 ft fork
Ledges exist for belays at every pitch, so rope lengths are flexible
watchOut: Steep, loose scree and choss on the multi-thousand-foot approach
Hot, sun-exposed south aspect
knownHazards: Loose rock/choss on approach
Rockfall potential in the approach couloir

pitchDetail.notes: Center of the wall, trending left toward the upper South Rib
20-30 ft sections with decent protection

routes.routeName: The Direct North Ridge w/ Gendarme
routes.overview: The full-length version of the North Ridge, starting at the true toe rather than the notch, adding roughly 800 vertical feet and a sustained 5.9+ opening lieback before joining the standard North Ridge line up through the Great Gendarme.
routes.beta: Three opening pitches off a ledge ~150 ft above the toe — including an awkward 5.8 squeeze slot and a striking, sustained 5.9+ lieback crack — join the standard North Ridge; from there roughly 11 more pitches of exposed 4th-to-mid-5th ridge climbing on excellent granite lead to the Great Gendarme (two 5.9 pitches: a fingers/hands dihedral and an offwidth), then blocky 4th/low-5th terrain to the summit.
routes.fa: Mead Hargis & Jay Ossiander, 1963
routes.rappels: ~75 ft rappel around the Great Gendarme if bypassing; otherwise walk off via Cascadian Couloir or Sherpa Glacier/Sherpa Pass
objHaz: Loose, lichen-covered rock on some lower pitches
routes.face: North Ridge, from the true toe
routes.approach: South: from Goat Pass, traverse east below the Stuart Glacier, staying above the large moraine mound, and aim for the toe of the ridge. North: Stuart Lake Trailhead via the Mountaineer Creek trail, cross the creek and follow the south fork upstream, climb a steep talus/boulder slope to the hanging valley below Sherpa Peak/Sherpa Glacier, then head directly to the toe of the ridge, keeping the Ice Cliff Glacier on your left.
routes.descent: Cascadian Couloir (most common) or Sherpa Glacier/Sherpa Pass (faster, early season, north side)
routes.descentText: The Cascadian Couloir is the most common descent — a long traverse south then a loose, sustained scree/talus descent to Ingalls Creek. On the north side, the Sherpa Glacier is quicker and more direct in early season for parties based at a Mountaineer Creek camp; alternatively descend 3rd-class terrain around the south side of Sherpa Peak to Sherpa Pass and glissade a snow slope. A West Ridge downclimb is reported as tedious and not recommended.
routes.bail: Retreat is difficult once established above the first few pitches; the short rappel around the Great Gendarme is the main planned bail/bypass option below the final headwall.
routes.turnaround: A full-value Grade IV outing — recorded ascents range from about 9 hours of pure climbing to 15-40 hours car-to-car including bivies. Set a firm turnaround before the Gendarme and carry bivy gear as a contingency.
routes.bestSeason: July through September; a lingering ice/snow patch in the gully below the Great Gendarme can persist into midsummer.
routes.comms: No reliable cell coverage on the ridge.
routes.detailedRack: Cams 0.3-3in (doubles 0.4-2in, optional #4 for the Gendarme offwidth), one set of nuts (offsets recommended), 8-12 alpine draws; optional ice axe/crampons/helmet for approach snow.
routes.proNeeds: A standard alpine rack sized up for the offwidth crux; natural pro (horns, slung blocks) supplements the rack on the long ridge sections.
whatToBring: Extra food/water for a long day
proTips: The lower 3 pitches are a real technical crux (5.9+ sustained lieback) — don't underestimate them
Decide on the Gendarme bypass vs. direct finish before you're below it
watchOut: Loose, lichen-covered rock on some lower pitches
The Gendarme stays shaded and can be cold or icy
The Cascadian Couloir descent is long and loose
knownHazards: Rockfall
Loose/lichenous rock
Long exposed descent
Afternoon thunderstorms

pitchDetail.notes: Lieback to an awkward 5.8 squeeze slot
Face climbing
Striking, sustained lieback crack; technical crux

~2,400 ft of simulclimbable ridge-crest terrain
Fingers/hands dihedral, good rests
Offwidth
Airy traverse plus final moves to the summit

routes.overview: A long, moderate ridge climb up Mount Stuart's West Ridge — mostly 3rd/4th class scrambling on quality granite with a short 5.6 crux near the summit pyramid — a Cascades classic and one of the peak's most popular technical routes.
routes.beta: From Ingalls Lake, follow the ridge toward Stuart Pass then cut right across talus to the second prominent gully, climbing through Long John Tower via its right side to the notch behind it, then an extended 3rd-class ledge traverse, a moat crossing, and a short 5.6 summit-pyramid crux to the top.
routes.fa: Lex Maxwell, Fred Llewellyn & John Vertrees, 1935
objHaz: Moat at the snow/rock transition near the summit
routes.approach: From the Esmeralda/Longs Pass Trailhead via the Ingalls Way Trail to Ingalls Lake (roughly 3 hours), continuing toward Stuart Pass and cutting across talus to the second gully. Alternatively from Stuart Lake Trailhead via Stuart Pass (longer, ~4.5 hours).
routes.descent: Cascadian Couloir
routes.descentText: Descend the Cascadian Couloir — a steep gully with over 4,000 ft of loose, sustained scree/talus descent — then the Ingalls Creek/Longs Pass trail back to the trailhead.
routes.bail: Retreat is straightforward on most of the route (3rd/4th class terrain); downclimb back the way you came below the summit-pyramid crux.
routes.turnaround: Mostly a long scramble rather than technical climbing, but route-finding on the upper mountain and the long, loose Cascadian descent argue for a conservative turnaround time and daylight margin.
routes.bestSeason: June through September; many parties link it with a bivy for a relaxed 2-3 day trip.
routes.comms: No reliable cell coverage.
routes.detailedRack: Light alpine rack: a set of nuts, small-to-medium cams (roughly #2-#8 Metolius-equivalent), long runners and carabiners for the scrambling terrain and short crux.
routes.proNeeds: Minimal gear needed given mostly 3rd/4th class climbing; a rack is carried mainly for the summit-pyramid crux.
proTips: The north side of the ridge reportedly offers the most fun climbing where route-finding options open up
The southern (Esmeralda) approach is faster than the northern (Stuart Lake) approach
watchOut: Route-finding can be tricky near Long John Tower and the summit pyramid
Cascadian Couloir descent is loose and tedious
knownHazards: Loose rock
Moat/snow near the summit early/late season
Afternoon thunderstorms

pitchDetail.notes: Long, easy ridge scrambling on good granite
Pass via the right side to the notch behind the tower
A couple of short, well-featured steps to the top

routes.overview: The hardest currently-documented free route on Mount Stuart: begins on the original four pitches of Gorillas in the Mist, cuts left on a ledge system, joins two pitches of Gorillas Direct, then finishes independently with a two-pitch direct headwall finish up a striking splitter crack ('Empire State Building', 5.12a).
routes.beta: Combines three existing lines (Gorillas in the Mist's opening pitches, Gorillas Direct's middle pitches, and a new direct finish) into a sustained, mostly-splitter-crack outing on the West Face Wall between Goat and Stuart Pass, topping out with an aesthetic headwall splitter well above the pass.
routes.fa: Sol Wertkin & Tyree Johnson, September 2016 (first free ascent: Sol Wertkin)
routes.rappels: Single-rope rappel from a fixed anchor off the descent bench, then downclimb to Stuart Pass
objHaz: Sparse protection on some traversing sections
Long multi-pitch commitment far from help
routes.face: West Face Wall, between Goat and Stuart Pass
routes.approach: Approach the West Face Wall between Goat Pass and Stuart Pass from the south (Esmeralda/Longs Pass Trailhead via Ingalls Way Trail to Ingalls Lake and Stuart Pass) or from the north (Stuart Lake Trailhead).
routes.descent: Follow the West Ridge to the summit, or descend the main W Ridge gully via scrambling/downclimbing, breaking skier's right onto a large bench, single-rope rappel from a fixed anchor, then downclimb to Stuart Pass bivy sites.
routes.descentText: Quickest descent follows the main gully of the West Ridge via scrambling and downclimbing before breaking skier's right onto a large bench with a single-rope rappel option, then downclimbing to bivy sites at Stuart Pass.
routes.bail: Shared ledge systems with Gorillas in the Mist/Gorillas Direct offer some low-wall retreat options; above the Monkey Traverse, retreat becomes committing.
routes.bestSeason: July through September, dry rock conditions on a west-facing, afternoon-sun wall.
routes.comms: No reliable cell coverage.
routes.detailedRack: Standard alpine trad rack: single blue alien/purple TCU, doubles from green alien to #2, single #3, single #4, one set of nuts.
routes.proNeeds: Sparse protection reported on some traversing sections (notably P6); solid gear on the crack pitches.
gear: Alpine trad rack: alien/TCU to #4 cams
whatToBring: Bivy gear for a Stuart Pass camp
proTips: Rope management matters on the Monkey Traverse (P3) and other horizontal sections
watchOut: Sparse protection on some traversing pitches (P6)
Long multi-pitch commitment far from help
knownHazards: Rockfall potential
Weather exposure high on a west-facing alpine wall

pitchDetail.notes: Hand/finger splitter crack with a chimney, 40m
Hanging corners, arete, crack traverse, 55m
'Monkey Traverse' - exposed horizontal crack, 20m
V-slot with varied stemming, 60m
Corner crack to a belay ledge, 30m
Corner crack, slab, sparse-pro traverse, 55m
'Fifth Avenue' - corner bulge, stem-box, 40m
'Empire State Building' - headwall splitter, 55m

routes.overview: The standard descent and easiest ascent route on Mount Stuart — a long south-facing couloir/scree gully climbed as steep snow in spring or class-3 rock/scree once melted out, gaining the false-summit notch before a final traverse to the true summit.
routes.beta: From Ingalls Creek Trail, ascend access trails around 4,800-5,000 ft just downstream of the Longs Pass trail junction, through trees and brush into a steepening, loose gully, bypassing small cliffs. Around 8,000 ft cross a minor ridge east and continue over loose scree; near 9,000 ft scramble through the notch at the false summit and make a rising traverse to the true summit.
routes.fa: Samuel Gannett, 1895 (first recorded ascent of Mount Stuart via this line)
objHaz: Navigation error at the false summit leading into the wrong gully
routes.approach: Ingalls Creek Trail from the Longs Pass/Esmeralda Trailhead, picking up climbers' access trails near the Longs Pass junction around 4,800-5,000 ft.
routes.descent: Same couloir (the standard descent route on the mountain)
routes.descentText: Descend the same couloir; stay high enough to pass back through the false-summit notch (~9,000 ft) before dropping down — descending too early leads into the wrong gully. Crampons and an axe are recommended when snow-covered.
routes.bail: The couloir itself functions as the bail route from the upper mountain — parties on nearly every other technical route descend via Cascadian regardless of their ascent line.
routes.turnaround: Cornices can form on the summit ridge near the false-summit notch in spring; turn around in whiteout or high-wind conditions rather than risk the notch traverse.
routes.bestSeason: Best as a snow climb/ski descent March-May before it melts to loose scree and rock; also climbable as a scramble June-September.
routes.comms: No reliable cell coverage.
routes.proNeeds: Generally climbed unroped; crampons and an ice axe are the primary protection when snow-covered.
whatToBring: Trekking poles for the scree descent
proTips: This is the standard descent for nearly every technical route on the mountain — know it even if it's not your ascent line
Stay high through the false-summit notch before dropping into the gully
watchOut: Easy to descend into the wrong gully by dropping too early
Loose, sustained scree/rock lower down
Cornices near the notch in spring
knownHazards: Rockfall/loose rock
Cornices (spring)
Navigation error at the false summit

routes.overview: The easiest and most direct of Mount Stuart's three north-side glacier routes — a moderate (~40°) snow/glacier climb up the leftmost gully to simple class-3 ridge scrambling to the summit; also the mountain's best early-season descent.
routes.beta: Glacier travel and a moderately steep snow-gully climb on the NE flank, left of the Ice Cliff arete, topping out on the summit ridge for a short class-3 scramble to the top.
routes.fa: Bill & Gene Prater, Don & Nelson Torrey, 1956
objHaz: Rockfall from the Ice Cliff arete to climber's right
routes.face: Sherpa Glacier, NE flank (leftmost of Stuart's three north-side glaciers)
routes.approach: Branch off the Stuart Lake Trail from the meadow around 4,500 ft and head southwest up Mountaineer Creek to the base of the glacier.
routes.descent: Same glacier (best early-season descent) or continue over the summit to the Cascadian Couloir
routes.descentText: Most parties descend the same glacier back to a Mountaineer Creek camp; commonly used as the descent after climbing the Ice Cliff Glacier.
routes.bail: Retreat down the glacier is straightforward in early season; becomes more broken/crevassed later, complicating retreat.
routes.turnaround: Snow bridges and the glacier surface deteriorate through the summer — plan for a firm, early-morning ascent/descent and reassess if the glacier looks broken up.
routes.bestSeason: Best in April-May as an early-season route/ski descent, before the glacier breaks up; increasingly difficult and crevassed by summer.
routes.comms: No reliable cell coverage.
routes.detailedRack: Most parties solo the route early season; rope, axe, and crampons are standard, with rappel gear and snow/ice protection as a contingency once the glacier opens up later.
routes.proNeeds: Minimal formal protection when climbed early season and solo; carry pickets/screws as the glacier deteriorates.
whatToBring: Ski or snowshoe option for a spring descent
proTips: Climb it early (April-May) while it's still a simple snow slope
Popular as a ski descent from the summit ridge
watchOut: Glacier becomes broken/crevassed as the season progresses
Cornices near the summit ridge
knownHazards: Crevasses (late season)
Rockfall from the Ice Cliff arete

routes.overview: The full toe-to-summit North Ridge climbed as a complete push, over the Great Gendarme direct — the same ~2,800 ft, 20-pitch line documented elsewhere as the 'Direct North Ridge.'
routes.beta: Starts at the true toe of the ridge (south approach via Goat Pass beneath the Stuart Glacier, or north approach via Mountaineer Creek and the hanging valley below Sherpa Peak), climbs roughly 20 pitches of exposed 3rd-to-5.9+ granite ridge crest, and finishes with two pitches directly up the Great Gendarme (5.8+ to 5.9).
routes.fa: Mead Hargis & Jay Ossiander, 1963 (toe-to-summit line over the Great Gendarme)
routes.rappels: Rappel around the Gendarme if bypassing, or walk off via Cascadian Couloir
objHaz: Loose, lichen-covered rock on lower pitches
routes.face: North Ridge, from the true toe
routes.approach: From Esmeralda Trailhead via Lake Ingalls, Stuart Pass, and Goat Pass, beneath the Stuart Glacier to the toe of the ridge — roughly 5 hours on one recorded ascent.
routes.descent: Cascadian Couloir ('the Poop Chute')
routes.descentText: Descend the Cascadian Couloir; one recorded ascent took approximately 4 hours for this descent back to the trailhead area.
routes.bail: Retreat is difficult once established on the ridge; the rappel around the Great Gendarme is the main planned bail option below the final headwall.
routes.turnaround: A committing, all-day-to-multi-day outing; one recorded single-push ascent logged about 9 hours of climbing plus 5 hours approach and 4 hours descent (~18 hours car-to-car) — times vary substantially by party and conditions.
routes.bestSeason: July through September; a lingering ice/snow patch below the Great Gendarme can persist into midsummer.
routes.comms: No reliable cell coverage on the ridge.
routes.detailedRack: Double rack from 0.5-2in, a #3, a 0.3 and 0.4, and a set of nuts; 12 alpine draws and 2 anchors; a doubled 60m twin rope on one recorded ascent.
routes.proNeeds: A standard alpine rack sized up for the offwidth/wide sections at the Gendarme; natural pro supplements the rack on the long ridge sections.
whatToBring: Extra food/water for a long day
proTips: Budget the full day: one recorded ascent took ~5h approach, ~9h climbing, ~4h descent
watchOut: Loose, lichen-covered rock on lower pitches
The Gendarme stays shaded and can be cold or icy
The Cascadian Couloir descent is long and loose
knownHazards: Rockfall
Loose/lichenous rock
Long exposed descent
Afternoon thunderstorms

Long simulclimbable ridge-crest section
Crux of the upper North Ridge, climbed direct

routes.corrections: This 'North Ridge (Complete)' entry describes the same physical line documented elsewhere in this catalog as 'The Direct North Ridge w/ Gendarme' (wa_the_direct_north_ridge_w_gendarme) — both are the toe-to-summit North Ridge over the Great Gendarme direct (~2,800 ft, ~20 pitches, Grade IV, 5.9/5.9+), just under names drawn from different sources (climberkyle.com's 'Complete North Ridge 5.9' trip report vs. Mountain Project's 'Direct North Ridge' 5.9+). The timing figures here (5h approach / 9h climb / 4h descent) come from that single recorded ascent and are not a general guarantee. Recommend catalog maintainers verify whether these two entries should be merged.

routes.overview: A moderate alpine ice/mixed line on the northwest side of Mount Stuart, climbing a snow-and-ice couloir onto the upper Stuart Glacier before finishing up the West Ridge to the summit.
routes.beta: From the top of the Stuart Glacier at ~7,800 ft, the couloir is climbed directly (40-50° snow narrowing to an 80° step with variable ice/snow) before most parties exit right onto the West Ridge for the final pitches to the summit.
routes.fa: Helmy Beckey & Larry Strathdee, June 1944
routes.approach: Begin at Stuart Lake Trailhead via Icicle Road. Hike ~3 miles to the Colchuck Lake turnoff, continue ~1 mile further, then leave the main trail to ascend a steep valley for ~1.5 miles, gaining a ~5,400 ft base camp near Sherpa Glacier. From the basin below the Ice Cliff Glacier, hike west to the base of the North Ridge, then continue onto the Stuart Glacier on the right side of the ridge.
routes.descent: Sherpa Glacier
routes.descentText: Descend via the Sherpa Glacier, traversing around the false summit's southeast face toward the Sherpa Couloir to access the glacier descent route.
routes.bail: Downclimb the couloir below the 80° step is possible early in the route; retreat from the West Ridge section is more committing.
routes.turnaround: Expect freezing temperatures, wind, and difficult route-finding early season; conditions and grade ease later in the season (potentially M3-M4) but with more rockfall risk.
routes.bestSeason: Best in cold, snowy conditions — March, April, and at latest May.
routes.comms: No reliable cell coverage.
routes.detailedRack: A couple of short ice screws for the icy sections plus pickets for early season; standard alpine rack to a BD #3, 8 nuts, 4 pins; consider extra small cams (0.3-0.4in).
routes.proNeeds: Screws/pickets for the couloir; a standard alpine rock rack for the West Ridge finish.
proTips: Climb it early season for the best, most consolidated snow/ice conditions
watchOut: The bergschrund at the couloir entrance (~7,800 ft) may require a step-across or more
Variable, sometimes thin ice/snow on the 80° step
knownHazards: Bergschrund
Rockfall
Difficult early-season route-finding

pitchDetail.notes: Direct line up the couloir from the Stuart Glacier
Variable conditions; crux step
Exit right onto the West Ridge for the final pitches

routes.overview: A striking rock pillar sitting above the Ice Cliff Glacier on the northeast face, reached by ascending the glacier and four easy pitches before three engaging, sustained crack pitches (the route's crux) lead to easier ground near the false summit.
routes.beta: After climbing the Ice Cliff Glacier and four easy pitches to the base of the pillar, three pitches of sustained crack climbing (5.10, 5.11-, 5.10+) ascend the pillar itself, followed by easier climbing to the false summit above the glacier.
routes.fa: Kit Lewis & Jim Nelson, 1983
objHaz: Loose blocks near the top of the pillar
Icefall/rockfall hazard on the Ice Cliff Glacier approach
routes.face: Northeast face, above the Ice Cliff Glacier
routes.approach: Ascend the Ice Cliff Glacier (see Ice Cliff Glacier route) to reach the base of Girth Pillar; four easy pitches lead to the pillar itself.
routes.descent: Sherpa Glacier
routes.descentText: From the false summit above the glacier, descend via the Sherpa Glacier to the east.
routes.bail: Retreat from the pillar's three technical pitches is possible via rappel from gear anchors; the glacier approach itself is committing in poor conditions.
routes.turnaround: This route needs the Ice Cliff Glacier approach to be in climbable condition and the pillar rock to be dry — a narrow, conditions-dependent window; be prepared to bail if the glacier or icefall looks unstable.
routes.bestSeason: Narrow window in July-August once the Ice Cliff Glacier approach is passable and the rock is dry.
routes.comms: No reliable cell coverage.
routes.detailedRack: Ice gear for the glacier approach (screws/tools/crampons) plus a rock rack to 3in for the pillar's crack pitches, including gear to protect the fingerlock crux on pitch 2 (fixed stopper reported in place on that pitch).
routes.proNeeds: Solid crack gear on the three pillar pitches; fixed protection exists at the pitch-2 crux.
whatToBring: Warm layers for the shaded NE face
proTips: The three pillar pitches are the real business — treat the glacier and easy pitches below as approach
watchOut: Loose blocks, particularly large ones near the top of the pillar
Icefall/serac hazard on the Ice Cliff Glacier approach
knownHazards: Loose rock near the pillar top
Icefall on approach
Cold, committing northeast aspect

pitchDetail.notes: 130 ft; steep handcrack that jogs right at the top; crappy ledge belay
100 ft; thin crack/fingerlocks, crux, fixed stopper aid reported
120 ft; perfect hands on steep, somewhat dirty rock to the top of the pillar

routes.overview: A long, moderate ridge climb up Mount Stuart's West Ridge — mostly 3rd/4th class scrambling on quality granite with a short 5.6 crux near the summit pyramid — a Cascades classic and one of the peak's most popular technical routes.
routes.beta: From Ingalls Lake, follow the ridge toward Stuart Pass then cut right across talus to the second prominent gully, climbing through Long John Tower via its right side to the notch behind it, then an extended 3rd-class ledge traverse, a moat crossing, and a short 5.6 summit-pyramid crux to the top.
routes.fa: Lex Maxwell, Fred Llewellyn & John Vertrees, 1935
objHaz: Moat at the snow/rock transition near the summit
routes.approach: From the Esmeralda/Longs Pass Trailhead via the Ingalls Way Trail to Ingalls Lake (roughly 3 hours), continuing toward Stuart Pass and cutting across talus to the second gully. Alternatively from Stuart Lake Trailhead via Stuart Pass (longer, ~4.5 hours).
routes.descent: Cascadian Couloir
routes.descentText: Descend the Cascadian Couloir — a steep gully with over 4,000 ft of loose, sustained scree/talus descent — then the Ingalls Creek/Longs Pass trail back to the trailhead.
routes.bail: Retreat is straightforward on most of the route (3rd/4th class terrain); downclimb back the way you came below the summit-pyramid crux.
routes.turnaround: Mostly a long scramble rather than technical climbing, but route-finding on the upper mountain and the long, loose Cascadian descent argue for a conservative turnaround time and daylight margin.
routes.bestSeason: June through September; many parties link it with a bivy for a relaxed 2-3 day trip.
routes.comms: No reliable cell coverage.
routes.detailedRack: Light alpine rack: a set of nuts, small-to-medium cams (roughly #2-#8 Metolius-equivalent), long runners and carabiners for the scrambling terrain and short crux.
routes.proNeeds: Minimal gear needed given mostly 3rd/4th class climbing; a rack is carried mainly for the summit-pyramid crux.
proTips: The north side of the ridge reportedly offers the most fun climbing where route-finding options open up
The southern (Esmeralda) approach is faster than the northern (Stuart Lake) approach
watchOut: Route-finding can be tricky near Long John Tower and the summit pyramid
Cascadian Couloir descent is loose and tedious
knownHazards: Loose rock
Moat/snow near the summit early/late season
Afternoon thunderstorms

pitchDetail.notes: Long, easy ridge scrambling on good granite
Pass via the right side to the notch behind the tower
A couple of short, well-featured steps to the top

routes.overview: The standard descent and easiest ascent route on Mount Stuart — a long south-facing couloir/scree gully climbed as steep snow in spring or class-3 rock/scree once melted out, gaining the false-summit notch before a final traverse to the true summit.
routes.beta: From Ingalls Creek Trail, ascend access trails around 4,800-5,000 ft just downstream of the Longs Pass trail junction, through trees and brush into a steepening, loose gully, bypassing small cliffs. Around 8,000 ft cross a minor ridge east and continue over loose scree; near 9,000 ft scramble through the notch at the false summit and make a rising traverse to the true summit.
routes.fa: Samuel Gannett, 1895 (first recorded ascent of Mount Stuart via this line)
objHaz: Navigation error at the false summit leading into the wrong gully
routes.approach: Ingalls Creek Trail from the Longs Pass/Esmeralda Trailhead, picking up climbers' access trails near the Longs Pass junction around 4,800-5,000 ft.
routes.descent: Same couloir (the standard descent route on the mountain)
routes.descentText: Descend the same couloir; stay high enough to pass back through the false-summit notch (~9,000 ft) before dropping down — descending too early leads into the wrong gully. Crampons and an axe are recommended when snow-covered.
routes.bail: The couloir itself functions as the bail route from the upper mountain — parties on nearly every other technical route descend via Cascadian regardless of their ascent line.
routes.turnaround: Cornices can form on the summit ridge near the false-summit notch in spring; turn around in whiteout or high-wind conditions rather than risk the notch traverse.
routes.bestSeason: Best as a snow climb/ski descent March-May before it melts to loose scree and rock; also climbable as a scramble June-September.
routes.comms: No reliable cell coverage.
routes.proNeeds: Generally climbed unroped; crampons and an ice axe are the primary protection when snow-covered.
whatToBring: Trekking poles for the scree descent
proTips: This is the standard descent for nearly every technical route on the mountain — know it even if it's not your ascent line
Stay high through the false-summit notch before dropping into the gully
watchOut: Easy to descend into the wrong gully by dropping too early
Loose, sustained scree/rock lower down
Cornices near the notch in spring
knownHazards: Rockfall/loose rock
Cornices (spring)
Navigation error at the false summit

routes.overview: A steep snow/ice climb up the middle of Mount Stuart's three north-side glaciers, ascending an icefall approach and a ~50° upper couloir to the summit ridge just below the false summit.
routes.beta: Ascend the icefall (short AI2 ice sections early season, or a 5.8 slab pitch on the right in late season), traverse through the cirque toward the upper couloir, cross the bergschrund on the climber's right, ascend the ~50° gully forking right near the top, and surmount a cornice to reach the south-facing ridge just below the false summit (~8,600 ft).
routes.fa: Bill & Gene Prater and Dave Mahre, 1957
objHaz: Cornice at the top of the gully
routes.face: North face, between the Sherpa and North Ridge
routes.approach: From Stuart Lake Trailhead, follow the main trail staying right at the first fork. Pick up the climbers' trail around 47.49742°N, 120.86218°W, staying east of Mountaineers Ridge while ascending talus or snow to the upper valley and campsites below Sherpa Glacier at 5,400 ft.
routes.descent: Sherpa Glacier
routes.descentText: Retrace steps back down from the summit, around the false summit, and descend the Sherpa Glacier, located just east of the Ice Cliff Glacier exit, starting around 8,200 ft.
routes.bail: Downclimb the couloir below the cornice/bergschrund is the main retreat option; the icefall approach is more committing in poor conditions.
routes.turnaround: Climb early season while the icefall still has consolidated snow/ice; the late-season 5.8 rock variant adds rockfall exposure — turn back if the bergschrund or cornice looks unstable.
routes.bestSeason: Best April-June while the icefall and couloir hold consolidated snow/ice; deteriorates and grows more broken through summer.
routes.comms: No reliable cell coverage.
routes.detailedRack: 1-4 ice screws, pickets, and a small rock rack for late-season conditions.
routes.proNeeds: Screws/pickets for the icefall and upper couloir; a small rock rack if climbing the late-season 5.8 slab variant.
proTips: Climb it early season for consolidated ice/snow and to avoid the harder late-season rock variant
watchOut: Icefall/serac hazard on the approach
Bergschrund crossing near the top of the couloir
Cornice at the gully exit
knownHazards: Icefall/serac collapse
Bergschrund
Rockfall on the late-season variant

routes.overview: An eight-pitch alpine trad route on Mount Stuart's West Face Wall between Goat and Stuart Pass, following splitter cracks and exposed traverses, including the notable 'Monkey Traverse,' to a headwall finish.
routes.beta: Eight pitches progress from the base through corners and slab sections, across the exposed horizontal 'Monkey Traverse,' and up to the headwall, on sustained crack and corner terrain.
routes.fa: Sol Wertkin, Jens Holsten & Mark Westman, 2011
routes.rappels: Single-rope rappel from a fixed anchor on the descent bench, then downclimb to Stuart Pass
objHaz: Loose rock on the descent gully
routes.face: West Face Wall, between Goat and Stuart Pass
routes.approach: Approach the West Face Wall between Goat Pass and Stuart Pass from the south (Esmeralda/Longs Pass Trailhead via Ingalls Way Trail to Ingalls Lake and Stuart Pass) or from the north (Stuart Lake Trailhead).
routes.descent: Follow the West Ridge to the summit, or descend the main gully with scrambling/downclimbing, breaking right onto a large bench with a single-rope rappel option before reaching Stuart Pass.
routes.descentText: Follow the West Ridge to the summit, or descend via the main gully with scrambling and downclimbing, breaking right on a large bench with a single-rope rappel option, before reaching Stuart Pass bivy sites.
routes.bail: Shared ledge systems on the lower wall offer some retreat options; above the Monkey Traverse, retreat becomes more committing.
routes.bestSeason: July through September, dry rock on a west-facing, afternoon-sun wall.
routes.comms: No reliable cell coverage.
routes.detailedRack: Standard alpine trad rack: single blue alien/purple TCU, doubles from green alien to #2, single #3, single #4, one set of nuts.
routes.proNeeds: Standard crack protection on most pitches; care needed on exposed traverse sections.
gear: Alpine trad rack: alien/TCU to #4 cams
whatToBring: Bivy gear for a Stuart Pass camp
proTips: Aesthetic corner cracks make for sustained, enjoyable climbing — a good introduction to the West Face Wall before attempting the harder King Kong finish
watchOut: Exposed horizontal 'Monkey Traverse'
Loose rock hazards on the final descent gully
knownHazards: Rockfall
Loose rock on descent

pitchDetail.notes: Splitter cracks, corners, slab sections, and the exposed 'Monkey Traverse'

blurb: The Dikes are a cluster of short, fin-like basalt walls (Megadike North, Megadike South, Minidike, and the small Castle spire) protruding from the eastern flank of Chase Mountain in the East Fork Touchet River valley of the Blue Mountains, Umatilla National Forest, south of Dayton, WA. Unlike most basalt crags the rock here fractures into horizontal "cordwood" columns rather than vertical ones, producing short (30-140 ft) bolted sport routes from 5.2-5.11c on rock that needs ongoing loose-rock cleaning. Nearly all ~35 routes were established by Whitman College geology professor Kevin Pogue, making this a small, locally-developed sport crag rather than a mountaineering objective.
hierarchyNote: "The Dikes" is not a summit — it's a cluster of short basalt dike-wall sport crags (Megadike North/South, Minidike, The Castle) on the flank of Chase Mountain in the Blue Mountains, Umatilla National Forest. The given coordinates (46.12344,-117.81893) and name match Mountain Project's "The Dikes" area (id 107487344) and Kevin Pogue's Whitman College guidebook exactly, so the record is correctly identified. Two notes: (1) the county is Columbia County, WA, not Walla Walla County — "Walla Walla Ranger District" is just the USFS administrative unit name; the East Fork Touchet drainage here is south of Dayton, the Columbia County seat. (2) elevationFt is null in the given data; Mountain Project shows ~3,743 ft for the general area pin and ~3,892 ft for the Megadike North subarea specifically, but since this is a multi-wall crag rather than a single summit, no single "peak elevation" cleanly applies — treat any elevation figure here as an approximate crag-location elevation, not a summit height. No PeakBagger/SummitPost entries exist for "The Dikes" because it isn't a baggable summit, which is expected for a crag and not a data problem.

routes.fa: Kevin Pogue
routes.overview: Short bolted sport pitch on the Megadike North wall, starting just uphill of Bachelor Party and sharing its anchor.
routes.beta: A few jugs and a fist jam get you through the crux to a bolted anchor shared with Bachelor Party.
routes.approach: From Dayton, WA drive south on N Touchet Rd (becomes gravel FS-64 near the Bluewood turnoff) about 16-17 mi to the Middle Point Trailhead (MPRT); reset mileage there. Continue ~1.1-1.2 mi further to a dirt-road junction/large pullout on the left. Cross the road and follow the short, steep drainage approach trail (past a downed tree) up to the Megadike North wall, the largest of the dike formations.
routes.descent: Lower from the bolted anchor (shared with Bachelor Party) on a single strand.
routes.descentText: All Megadike North routes are single-pitch and well within a 60m rope's reach (longest route here is 100 ft); lower straight back to the ground from the chain/shut anchor, no walk-off needed.
routes.bestSeason: USFS season of use for the access road/trail is April-November; spring and fall are most comfortable, Megadike North stays shaded most of the day which helps in summer heat.
routes.detailedRack: 4 bolts to shuts; a standard sport rack of ~10 quickdraws and a 60m rope is plenty for this 35 ft route.
routes.proNeeds: Fully bolted (4 bolts to shuts); no trad gear needed.
whatToBring: Helmet (guidebook: it is more dangerous to belay than to climb here due to loose rock)
Brush to clean holds — the basalt sheds loose chunks and this wall is not high-traffic
proTips: Starts just uphill of Bachelor Party and shares its anchor — good to pair the two in one lap
watchOut: Loose basalt chunks are common off the cleaned line; stay on route
Belayers should wear a helmet — the local guidebook specifically warns belaying is more dangerous than climbing here
knownHazards: Fractured, closely-jointed basalt sheds loose rock, especially off the beaten path

road.name: North Touchet Road / FS Road 64
road.status: Paved to the forest boundary, gravel Forest Service road beyond
road.seasonalGate: Not gated but unmaintained and typically inaccessible late fall through spring due to snow; official USFS season of use is April-November
road.driveNote: From Dayton, take S 4th St/North Touchet Rd south toward Bluewood Ski Area following the ski signs; the road becomes gravel FS-64 near the Bluewood turnoff (~23 mi from Dayton). The Middle Point Trailhead (MPRT), ~16-17 mi from the Hwy 12 turn, is the standard mileage reference point for the dike pullouts.

climate.forecastZone: Blue Mountains foothills, eastern WA (NWS Pendleton forecast area)
climate.typical: Dry, hot summers and cold, snowy winters typical of the Blue Mountains foothills at ~3,700-3,900 ft
climate.spring: Snow clears at this elevation by ~April; becomes climbable per the official April-November USFS season of use
climate.summer: Hot and exposed on sun-facing walls, though Megadike North stays shaded most of the day; nearby Joe's Rock is noted as running ~20°F cooler than Walla Walla in summer
climate.fall: Dry and pleasant through October, good climbing conditions
climate.winter: Cold, often snowbound at this elevation; road not maintained. The Castle's routes were established on rare sunny 50°F winter days when Walla Walla was fogged in below freezing

access.landManager: Umatilla National Forest, Walla Walla Ranger District (USFS)
access.closures: Access road/trail unmaintained and generally inaccessible late fall-spring due to snow; official recreation season April-November per USFS

emergency.sheriffDispatch: Columbia County Sheriff's Office — (509) 382-2518; 911 for emergencies
emergency.rangerStation: Walla Walla Ranger District, Umatilla National Forest — (509) 522-6290, 1415 W Rose St, Walla Walla, WA
emergency.nearestHospital: Dayton General Hospital, 1012 S 3rd St, Dayton, WA — 24-hour ER
emergency.notes: Remote gravel forest-road approach; cell coverage in the canyon is not guaranteed. Nearest town/services is Dayton, WA.

routes.overview: Easy bolted sport line on the east side of the Megadike North dike, near the Cordwood formation.
routes.beta: Start on the right (east) side of the dike and follow bolts through loose blocks to the anchor. A left-side variation exists with looser rock and minimal 5th-class climbing; the right side is steeper at the stated grade.
routes.approach: Same drive/approach as the rest of Megadike North (see Face Farce); this route is at the north end of the wall with no established trail — approach from the east is recommended.
routes.descent: Lower from the anchor (left variation uses quicklinks on hangers; right variation uses fixed shuts).
routes.descentText: Single-pitch; lower on a single strand, well within a 60m rope.
routes.bestSeason: April-November per USFS season of use; shaded portions of Megadike North help in summer heat.
routes.detailedRack: ~6-7 bolts of uncertain condition to the anchor; bring extra draws given the route's low traffic.
routes.proNeeds: Fully bolted; inspect bolts/hangers before committing given noted uncertain condition.
whatToBring: Helmet (loose blocks noted directly on this route)
Sturdy shoes for the unmarked east-side approach
proTips: Follow the bolts through the loose blocks rather than freelancing a line — the described path is the cleanest
watchOut: Loose blocks along the bolted line
No established approach trail on this end of the wall
knownHazards: Loose rock encountered directly on-route, not just off-route

routes.fa: Kevin Pogue
routes.overview: Easy bolted introduction route off the trail past the break in the Minidike.
routes.beta: Small holds lead through a little bulge to easier 5.2 ground and the chain anchor.
routes.approach: From Dayton drive N Touchet Rd/FS-64 to Middle Point Trailhead (MPRT), then continue ~1.5 mi further to a pullout on the left (SE) side just after the road crosses a bridge over the river. Cross the road, scramble down the bridge abutment, and follow the trail downstream on the NW side; this route is reached off-trail past the break in the Minidike wall.
routes.descent: Lower from the chain anchor on a single strand.
routes.descentText: Single pitch, 40 ft; well within a 60m rope.
routes.bestSeason: April-November per USFS season of use.
routes.detailedRack: Bolts to anchor chains; standard sport rack.
routes.proNeeds: Fully bolted.
whatToBring: Sturdy shoes for the bridge-abutment scramble access
proTips: Good beginner-grade pitch — described directly as an accessible, easy line
watchOut: Erosion has broken the Minidike into discrete rock fins with loose rock between them; stay on the established approach trail
knownHazards: Loose rock and dirt typical of the fractured Dikes basalt

routes.overview: Harder bolted sport pitch on Megadike South, south of a formation locals call "Way Up."
routes.beta: Crux is low; "holds should hold" per the route description — go up and through the shaded portion on the right.
routes.approach: From Dayton drive N Touchet Rd/FS-64 to Middle Point Trailhead (MPRT), then continue ~1.2 mi further to a pullout on the left just before the road crosses the river on a bridge; scramble up to the Megadike South wall.
routes.descent: Lower from the anchor (9 bolts to chains) on a single strand.
routes.descentText: Single pitch, 45 ft; well within a 60m rope.
routes.bestSeason: April-November per USFS season of use.
routes.detailedRack: 9 bolts to chains; standard sport rack.
routes.proNeeds: Fully bolted (9 bolts).
whatToBring: Chalk — crux is on smaller holds low on the route
proTips: Route beta notes going up and right through the shaded section to stay off the sunnier holds
watchOut: Description flags hold quality uncertainty ("holds should hold") — test holds before committing
knownHazards: Fractured basalt with variable hold quality typical of the Dikes

routes.fa: Kevin Pogue
routes.overview: Longer moderate pitch to the right of Just Enough on Megadike North.
routes.beta: A smooth, mossy start leads to a nice vertical middle section and a mild finish.
routes.approach: Same as Face Farce — Megadike North approach via MPRT + ~1.1-1.2 mi and the steep drainage trail.
routes.descent: Lower from the chain anchor on a single strand.
routes.descentText: Single pitch, 70 ft; within a 60m rope.
routes.bestSeason: April-November per USFS season of use.
routes.detailedRack: Bolted to chains (exact bolt count not published); standard sport rack for a 70 ft pitch.
routes.proNeeds: Fully bolted.
whatToBring: Brush — start is described as mossy
proTips: Located just right of Just Enough — easy to link both in one visit to this section of wall
watchOut: Mossy/slick start, especially early season or after rain
knownHazards: Loose rock and moss typical of the Dikes basalt

routes.fa: Kevin Pogue
routes.overview: A brief two-pitch line that can also be top-roped with a 60m rope; a friendly first taste of multi-pitch climbing.
routes.beta: Bolts lead to a summit anchor; often used as an intro multi-pitch route since it can also be toproped in one long stretch with a 60m rope.
routes.approach: Minidike approach via MPRT + ~1.5 mi, bridge-abutment scramble, and downstream trail.
routes.descent: Lower or rappel from the summit anchor.
routes.descentText: Two short pitches to a summit anchor; can be lowered on a single strand with a 60m rope from the top, or rappelled in two stages.
routes.bestSeason: April-November per USFS season of use.
routes.detailedRack: Bolted line to a summit anchor; standard sport rack, plus a 60m rope if toproping the full 70 ft in one stretch.
routes.proNeeds: Fully bolted, two short pitches.
whatToBring: 60m rope if planning to toprope the whole route in one pull
proTips: A good, low-commitment way to try multi-pitch sport climbing — short and fully bolted
watchOut: Loose rock typical of the Dikes basalt, more relevant on a multi-pitch line with a belay stance mid-route
knownHazards: Fractured basalt with loose-rock potential along a two-pitch line

routes.overview: Climbs through a striking set of rock features resembling stacked firewood (the 'cordwood' basalt jointing the Dikes are named for) — but currently carries a serious active rockfall hazard.
routes.beta: Movement follows the cordwood-jointed features, but as of the most recent posted report the route has severe rockfall potential from separated blocks on the right side capable of causing serious injury or death.
routes.approach: Megadike North approach via MPRT + ~1.1-1.2 mi; this route sits on a separate rock feature about 30 ft west of Monkey On A Woodpile.
routes.descent: Lower from the bolted chain anchor at top — but see hazard warning below before climbing at all.
routes.descentText: Single pitch, 60 ft, chain anchor at top.
routes.bestSeason: April-November per USFS season of use, subject to the active hazard noted below.
routes.detailedRack: Bolted anchor with chains at top.
routes.proNeeds: Fully bolted, but see the active rockfall warning.
whatToBring: Do not climb until you have personally assessed the loose blocks reported on the right side
proTips: The Mountain Project entry for this route was posted specifically as a hazard alert, not as a route recommendation
watchOut: Severe, active rockfall potential from large separated blocks on the right side of the route — described as capable of causing serious injury or death
This is the most acute rockfall hazard documented anywhere in The Dikes route set
knownHazards: Actively separating large basalt blocks on climber's right, posing a lethal rockfall risk — assess in person before attempting

routes.corrections: Route was posted on Mountain Project explicitly as a hazard-warning listing (severe rockfall potential, separated blocks, lethal risk) rather than a standard recommended-climb entry — flagged prominently here rather than presented as a normal moderate 5.8.

routes.fa: Kevin Pogue
routes.overview: The final, longest route before the north face of the dike — a clean two-pitch line with great holds.
routes.beta: Great holds and easy clipping along a clean line; follow the holds without moss for the best climbing. Both pitches share the same grade.
routes.approach: Megadike North approach via MPRT + ~1.1-1.2 mi.
routes.descent: Lower or rappel; pitches can be linked and lowered with a 70m rope, but only barely — tie a knot in the rope end.
routes.descentText: Two pitches totaling 100 ft; a 70m rope can link and lower the whole route in one go, but MP beta specifically warns it's tight ("barely") — tie a stopper/cat knot in the rope end as backup, or lower/rappel in two stages with a standard 60m rope.
routes.bestSeason: April-November per USFS season of use.
routes.detailedRack: Bolted throughout; a 70m rope allows linking both pitches for a single lower, though tightly.
routes.proNeeds: Fully bolted, two pitches.
whatToBring: 70m rope if you want to link and lower in one pull
Stick to the cleanest line — off-line holds carry moss
proTips: Follow the line free of moss for the best (and safest) holds
Also provides top-rope access, via a connection, up to the chains used by the adjacent route Incoming
watchOut: Moss on off-line holds reduces friction and security
Linking both pitches on a 70m rope is tight — back up the rope end with a knot
knownHazards: Loose rock and moss typical of the Dikes basalt, more consequential on a two-pitch line

routes.detailedRack: 7 bolts to chain anchors, shared with Cheap Beer.

proTips: Use the good rest point before the chains to shake out on the overhanging start
watchOut: Overhanging opening moves — pace yourself before the rest
knownHazards: Fractured basalt typical of the Dikes

routes.overview: A long, sustained line on the west side of Megadike North that stays shaded all day, with a crux constriction and small roof.
routes.beta: Face climbing builds to a constriction (crux) and small roof with great holds; hard sections can be bypassed. Sits in the shade all day with excellent views from the anchors. Located west side, left of a large overhanging feature.
routes.approach: Megadike North approach via MPRT + ~1.1-1.2 mi. Beta: can also be reached/top-roped via Lady Slipper to the dike summit to access the chains.
routes.descent: Lower from the 3-bolt chain anchor on a single strand (well within a 60m rope for this 80 ft route).
routes.descentText: Single pitch, 80 ft; 14 bolts to a 3-bolt chain anchor.
routes.bestSeason: April-November per USFS season of use; shaded all day, a good choice on hot summer afternoons.
routes.detailedRack: 14 bolts to a 3-bolt chain anchor — bring a full rack of draws for this longer pitch.
routes.proNeeds: Fully bolted (14 bolts).
whatToBring: Full quickdraw rack given the bolt count (14)
proTips: Shaded all day — a good pick when the rest of the wall is baking
Hard sections can be bypassed if needed
Can also be top-roped by walking up via Lady Slipper to the dike summit and rigging off the chains
watchOut: Crux constriction/small roof partway up — pace your gas tank for it
knownHazards: Fractured basalt typical of the Dikes

routes.overview: The last route on the Minidike face, a little up the talus; juggy start building to a technical crux near the top.

routes.approach: Minidike approach via MPRT + ~1.5 mi, bridge-abutment scramble, downstream trail; a little further up the talus than the other Minidike routes.
routes.descent: Lower from the chain anchor (8 bolts) on a single strand.
routes.descentText: Single pitch, 45 ft.
routes.bestSeason: April-November per USFS season of use.
routes.detailedRack: 8 bolts to chains, set 6 ft above the final bolt behind small trees.
routes.proNeeds: Fully bolted (8 bolts).
proTips: Crux is right before the last bolt — a recently-cleaned left-side slot finish is an alternative to moving right
watchOut: Anchors are partly hidden behind small trees — look carefully above the last bolt
knownHazards: Talus approach and fractured basalt typical of the Dikes

routes.fa: Kevin Pogue
routes.overview: A short initial pitch linked to a second, reaching the top of a 20-ft pillar and through the northeast arete; begins off the trail to Megadike North.
routes.beta: Two parallel bolts partway up allow going right or left around mini-roofs — left is the recommended line. About 20 ft of sustained climbing; most of the rest is easier.
routes.approach: Megadike North approach via MPRT + ~1.1-1.2 mi; route begins just off the trail to Megadike North.
routes.descent: Lower or rappel; a 70m rope is needed to link and lower both pitches at once.
routes.descentText: Two linked pitches to 70 ft total; a 70m rope connects both for a single lower, or lower/rappel each pitch separately with a standard 60m rope.
routes.bestSeason: April-November per USFS season of use.
routes.detailedRack: 3 bolts to wide hangers, then plenty of bolts to a chain anchor on a huge ledge.
routes.proNeeds: Fully bolted, two pitches; ~20 ft of sustained climbing at the grade near the mini-roofs.
whatToBring: 70m rope if linking both pitches for a single lower
proTips: At the parallel-bolt mini-roof section, go left — it's the recommended line
watchOut: ~20 ft of sustained climbing around the mini-roofs is the crux zone; don't underestimate it despite the moderate overall grade
knownHazards: Fractured basalt typical of the Dikes, on a two-pitch line

routes.fa: Kevin Pogue
routes.overview: One of two bolted lines to the top of The Castle, a small ~45-ft basalt spire north of the main Dikes.
routes.beta: The Castle is a spire north of the main Dikes climbing area, with two bolted climbs to its top. Rock quality is poor and the approach is a bushwhack/scramble, but the exposure and summit view are notable.
routes.approach: Continue south on N Touchet Rd/FS-64 past the main Dikes pullouts; park on the road shoulder and bushwhack/scramble up the hillside to the base of the spire. All routes on The Castle are on its south side.
routes.descent: Lower/rappel from the summit bolted anchor.
routes.descentText: Single short pitch (~40 ft) to the ~45 ft spire's summit; lower or rappel from the bolted anchor on a single strand.
routes.bail: Given the short length and single bolted line, retreat is straightforward — lower off any bolt if conditions or rock quality feel unacceptable partway up.
routes.bestSeason: USFS season of use is April-November; per the local guide, all Castle routes were originally established on rare sunny 50°F winter days when Walla Walla itself was fogged in and below freezing, so this south-facing spire can be climbable outside the normal season on the right day.
routes.detailedRack: Fully bolted line up the spire; standard short-route sport rack.
routes.proNeeds: Fully bolted, but rock quality is explicitly noted as poor — test holds carefully.
whatToBring: Sturdy approach shoes for the bushwhack/scramble access
proTips: Established during winter high-pressure days above the valley fog — worth considering on a clear winter day when lower-elevation crags are socked in
Exposure and summit view are the main draw given the spire's small size
watchOut: Rock quality on The Castle is explicitly described as poor
Approach is an unmarked bushwhack/scramble up the hillside, not a maintained trail
knownHazards: Poor rock quality on a small, exposed summit spire
Unmarked scramble approach
