blurb: North Early Winters Spire (7,760 ft) is the more technically demanding of the two Early Winters Spires in the Liberty Bell Group at Washington Pass in the North Cascades, set between Liberty Bell Mountain and Blue Lake Peak. Composed of excellent rapakivi-textured granite, it has routes on every aspect and none easier than 5.7, making it one of the historically hardest "major" summits in the range. It was first climbed May 28, 1950 by Wesley Grande, Pete Schoening, and Dick Widrig via the Southwest Face/Chockstone line.
hierarchyNote: None — name, coordinates (48.51291, -120.65557 per Mountain Project, matching the given 48.512935/-120.655465), elevation (7,760 ft), and the Washington Pass / Liberty Bell Group parent area all cross-confirm across Mountain Project, SummitPost, and Wikipedia. Note the adjacent South Early Winters Spire is actually the taller of the pair (7,807 ft) — do not confuse the two when sourcing.

routes.overview: The most popular route on North Early Winters Spire: a sustained crack-and-corner line up the west side, mixing chimney, corner, and offwidth pitches to a summit crack system.
routes.beta: Four to five pitches of consistently good 5.8-5.9 climbing on excellent granite; several pitches can be strung together by strong parties. Widely regarded as one of the best moderate alpine rock outings at Washington Pass.
routes.approach: From the Blue Lake trailhead on Highway 20 just west of Washington Pass, follow the Blue Lake Trail, then leave it to approach the South Early Winters Spire (SEWS) base and traverse left (north) along the base of the formation to North Early Winters Spire. Most parties take 1-2 hours. The route starts by scrambling up a climbers' trail traversing north along the base of the spire to a corner/crack system.
routes.descent: Scramble/rappel the Southwest Couloir
routes.descentText: From the summit, scramble down south and west, then make several rappels into the Southwest Couloir between North and South Early Winters Spire, followed by additional scrambling and one short rappel to reach the ground and the descent gully back toward the Blue Lake Trail approach.
routes.bail: The route's ledge systems after P1-P2 allow retreat by rappelling the line of ascent with a single 60m rope; fixed gear/slings exist at several belays.
routes.turnaround: Set a turnaround time that leaves a comfortable margin to complete the summit rappels and downclimbing in daylight; afternoon thunderstorms are common at Washington Pass in summer and this west-facing rock route has limited weather escape options once established above the first pitches.
routes.bestSeason: Best climbed in the core summer window (roughly late June through September) once Highway 20 is fully open and the approach and Southwest Couloir descent are free of snow/ice.
routes.comms: No cell phone service at Washington Pass or along Highway 20 for miles in either direction; treat the area as out of contact and plan accordingly.
routes.fa: Paul Boving and Steve Pollack, September 25, 1976
gear: Long slings/runners for rope-drag management on traversing pitches
routes.detailedRack: A standard trad rack to 4-5 inches, with extra long runners recommended to manage rope drag on the traversing alpine pitches (especially P2 and P5's leftward traverses).
routes.proNeeds: Solid protection throughout on good granite; the short chimney on P1 has minimal gear but is manageable, and the offwidth pitch (P4) benefits from large cams.
watchOut: Afternoon thunderstorms are common at Washington Pass in summer with limited retreat once high on the route
Loose rock can be encountered off the established line
Shared descent gully (Southwest Couloir) gets crowded with parties from multiple routes
knownHazards: Standard alpine rockfall and exposure hazards typical of Washington Pass granite spires

The Southwest Couloir descent shared by most routes on this peak has been the site of a fatal fixed-anchor failure (see Early Winter Couloir route) — inspect any fixed pitons/anchors before weighting them

pitchDetail.notes: 45m; crack/corner to a ledge, with a short chimney section that has minimal protection but manageable difficulty.
50m; climb left through some trees and up a corner to the next belay.
30m; left-leading flake/wide crack requiring large gear; belay with multiple slings.
25m; the 'surprisingly secure' offwidth pitch, using face smearing and arm-bar technique.
30m; traverse leftward around a roof (watch rope drag), then a crack system to the summit.

road.name: State Route 20 (North Cascades Highway)
road.status: Seasonally closed in winter
road.seasonalGate: Typically closed early December through mid/late April between the Ross Dam and Silver Star gates (mileposts ~134-171); exact dates vary year to year with snowpack/damage
road.driveNote: Washington Pass Overlook sits at milepost 163 (5,477 ft); Blue Lake trailhead is on the highway just west of the pass.

climate.typical: Alpine granite terrain at ~7,000-7,760 ft; best season is late June through September per Mountain Project, with snow/ice possible into early season
climate.summer: Prime climbing window; afternoon thunderstorms and lightning are a real hazard on exposed rock
climate.spring: Highway typically reopens mid/late April; couloir routes may still be in early-season ice/snow condition well into May
climate.fall: Good conditions into September/early October until the highway's winter closure
climate.winter: Highway 20 closed; peak inaccessible by road

access.landManager: USDA Forest Service, Okanogan-Wenatchee National Forest, Methow Valley Ranger District
access.passRequired: Northwest Forest Pass, America the Beautiful interagency pass, or a $5 Recreation.gov day pass required at the Blue Lake trailhead
access.fees: $5 day-use pass (or annual pass) at Blue Lake trailhead
access.closures: Highway 20 closed seasonally in winter (typically Dec-Apr); no other known route-specific closures

emergency.sheriffDispatch: Okanogan County Sheriff's Office (911 / non-emergency dispatch coordinates SAR call-out)
emergency.rangerStation: Methow Valley Ranger District, Okanogan-Wenatchee National Forest
emergency.nearestHospital: Three Rivers Hospital, Brewster, WA (critical access hospital serving the Methow Valley/Highway 20 corridor)
emergency.notes: No cell service at Washington Pass; the 2025 accident survivor had to drive roughly 42 miles to Newhalem to reach a phone. Rescues in this area typically require helicopter hoist and technical high-angle SAR teams.

routes.overview: A sustained, harder-grade alpine free line between the West Face and Southwest Face routes, with three pitches at the 5.11a limit on excellent rock.
routes.beta: Starts on the Chockstone Route before splitting off up a clean slab, then follows clean cracks, a corner, overhangs and flakes through three consecutive 5.11a pitches. Rock and gear are described as excellent throughout; rated PG13 for occasional runouts.
routes.approach: Approach as for the Chockstone/Southwest Face routes via the Blue Lake Trail, traversing the base of South Early Winters Spire to the base of North Early Winters Spire; the route lies between the West Face and Southwest Face lines.
routes.bail: Retreat by rappelling the line of ascent; the route shares its lower start with the Chockstone Route, which offers an easier bail option low on the route.
routes.turnaround: Because three of the five pitches are sustained 5.11, budget generously for the crux pitches and keep a firm eye on afternoon weather given the limited retreat options high on the route.
routes.bestSeason: Late June through September, once the approach and shared descent gully are clear of snow.
routes.comms: No cell phone service at Washington Pass; treat the area as out of contact.
routes.fa: Steve Risse and Donna McBain, September 1988
routes.face: West side, between West Face and Southwest Face
gear: 1.5 sets of cams (single on #2, #3; no #4 needed)
routes.detailedRack: One and a half sets of cams is sufficient — single on the #2 and #3, no #4 needed — plus tiny cams and a set of stoppers/brassies for thin placements.
routes.proNeeds: Gear is described as adequate to good throughout on excellent rock; the route is rated PG13 for some runout sections between placements.
proTips: Rock and climbing quality are excellent — this is considered one of the better hard free lines at Washington Pass
Bring small gear (tiny cams, brassies) for the thin sections
watchOut: Sustained 5.11 climbing at altitude with limited bail options once past the lower pitches
Afternoon thunderstorms typical of Washington Pass summers
knownHazards: Standard alpine rockfall/exposure hazards; shared Southwest Couloir descent has a documented fixed-anchor failure history (see Early Winter Couloir)

pitchDetail.notes: Starts via the Chockstone Route, then heads left to a ledge with a clean slab above (full 60m).
Full 60m pitch following clean cracks up a slab to a corner with a wide section to a pinch.
A fun, steep pitch up and left through overhangs on flakes.
Fierce and not obviously-lined climbing up to a wild overhang, then left.

routes.overview: The original 1950 first-ascent line of North Early Winters Spire, climbing the gully and chimney system on the west side past a giant namesake chockstone to the summit plateau.
routes.beta: Ascends the left side of enormous chockstones in the gully between North and South Early Winters Spire, then takes easy but slightly loose chimney weaknesses to the summit. Numerous old pitons along the route attest to its long history as a standard line.
routes.approach: From the Blue Lake trailhead on Highway 20, follow the Blue Lake Trail and cut off to traverse the base of South Early Winters Spire to the obvious gully/chasm between the two spires; minimal bushwhacking with a well-established climbers' path.
routes.descent: 4 bolted rappels + 1 tree rappel
routes.descentText: Four bolted rappel stations reach the ground with a 60m rope, plus one tree rappel down a 5.0 slab near the start, located about 30m down and skier's right of the route's top-out. An alternative/faster descent down bolted anchors on the West Face is also used by parties finishing nearby routes.
routes.bail: The gully approach and lower pitches allow straightforward downclimbing/rappel retreat before committing to the crux 5.7 chockstone pitch.
routes.turnaround: Snow/ice can linger in the gully into early summer, adding time and hazard; turn around with enough margin to downclimb wet or icy sections in daylight.
routes.bestSeason: Late June through September; snow/ice can persist in the gully into early summer requiring an ice axe (and possibly crampons).
routes.comms: No cell phone service at Washington Pass.
routes.fa: Dick Widrig, Pete Schoening, and Wes Grande, 1950 (first ascent of the peak)
routes.face: West side, chasm/gully between North and South Early Winters Spire
routes.detailedRack: A single rack to 2 inches plus extra quickdraws for clipping old fixed pitons found along the route; carry an ice axe (and crampons) if snow lingers in the gully.
routes.proNeeds: Protection is generally adequate; the crux 5.7 lieback pitch has some old fixed pitons (some dating to the 1940s-50s) supplementing gear, finishing on a delicate slab with marginal protection.
proTips: The historic fixed pitons are old — back them up with your own gear rather than trusting them fully
The West Face bolted descent is faster/safer than the standard 5-rappel gully descent when accessible
watchOut: Loose rock through the upper pitches
Snow/ice in the gully in early season requiring proper gear
Rubble/loose rock underfoot by mid-late summer
knownHazards: Loose rock throughout the upper mountain
Old fixed pitons of unknown reliability
This gully system connects to the shared Southwest Couloir descent where a fatal fixed-anchor (piton) failure occurred in May 2025

pitchDetail.notes: Low-angle slab with layback crack (often scrambled), followed by walking up the gully (~200 ft, snow possible in early season).
Blocky terrain to a 5.7 lieback bypassing the house-size chockstone on the left; old fixed pitons; finishes on a delicate, marginally-protected slab.
Snow/scree traverse to a second chockstone, then a 5.0 ramp and 5.4 chimney systems.
Unprotected face climbing leading to the summit scramble.

routes.overview: One of the finest alpine rock climbs at Washington Pass: a sustained crack line up the west face with a committing 5.11- thin-crack crux, huge exposure, and great position.
routes.beta: Six pitches of steadily improving quality, from a dirty lower face and chimney start through steep corners and a lieback flake, to a sustained thin-tips crux pitch, finishing on lower-angle crack climbing to the summit ridge.
routes.approach: Via the Blue Lake Trail, traversing the base of South Early Winters Spire to North Early Winters Spire; the route shares its start with the Northwest Corner Route on the west side.
routes.descent: 3 raps + chockstone rap, or 4 bolted raps
routes.descentText: Original descent: three single-rope rappels to a notch, then a free-hanging rappel past a chockstone, followed by scrambling. Alternative: four bolted rappel stations located about 15 feet right of the finishing jam crack, offering a faster, more direct descent.
routes.bail: The lower three pitches allow rappel retreat on the line of ascent; above the crux pitch (P4), commitment increases significantly.
routes.turnaround: The sustained 5.11- crux and thin-gear pitches take real time to lead cleanly — budget conservatively and turn around with a wide daylight margin given the technical retreat.
routes.bestSeason: Late June through September.
routes.comms: No cell phone service at Washington Pass.
routes.fa: Fred Beckey and Dave Beckstad, 1965 (aid); free ascent by Steve Risse and Dave Tower, 1985
routes.detailedRack: Doubles from 0.1 to 3 inches with triples recommended in the finger/tips range for the sustained crux and 5.10 pitches; small wired stoppers are essential on the crux pitch.
routes.proNeeds: Gear is generally good on the crux thin-crack pitch but small sizes are critical; the route is well protected for its grade by Washington Pass standards.
proTips: Ranked among the finest alpine rock climbs in the Washington Pass area for its movement, position, and exposure
The alternate bolted rappel line (4 raps) is faster than the original 3-rap-plus-chockstone descent
watchOut: Sustained, committing 5.11- climbing high on an alpine route with real exposure
Original descent's free-hanging chockstone rappel is exposed and awkward
knownHazards: Standard granite alpine rockfall/exposure hazards typical of the peak

pitchDetail.notes: 160 ft; somewhat dirty face up to a chimney with trees above, then scramble to a sandy ledge at the base of a corner.
60 ft; steep right-facing corner with good cracks and gear.
90 ft; low-angle crack, lieback flake, an airy step right past a bolt, sustained undercling/lieback.
150 ft; thin tips crack that eases and steps left; may continue as a 5.10c finger crack to a 2-inch opening.
150 ft; low-angle crack climbing with a friction slab traverse to the right.

routes.overview: A classic early-season alpine mixed/couloir climb up the deep gully splitting North and South Early Winters Spire, passing three large chockstones and a notorious summit cornice.
routes.beta: Climbs snow/ice between chockstones (AI2-3), drytools or aids past a crux chockstone, then negotiates a well-known cornice crux (mixed, aid, tunneling, or slabby rock variations) before finishing on snow and rock to the summit. Only in condition for a narrow window shortly after Highway 20 reopens each spring.
routes.approach: Park at the Hairpin turn on Highway 20 (east side of the formation) and climb directly up roughly 1,500 feet of steep, often waist-deep snow to the mouth of the couloir; an easy ice/mixed step accesses a tree anchor at the true start.
routes.descent: 4 rappels down the West/Southwest Couloir
routes.descentText: Descend the West (Southwest) Couloir with 4 rappels and some downclimbing. Standard summer bolted anchors are used down to a large chockstone, where a piton-and-nut anchor in the skier's-right wall allows a single-rope rappel past the obstacle. CAUTION: a fixed piton anchor in this descent gully failed catastrophically in May 2025 (see hazards below) — treat any single old fixed piton as suspect and back it up or replace it.
routes.bail: Below the crux cornice, parties can retreat by downclimbing/rappelling the snow and chockstone sections already climbed; above the cornice, commitment is higher and the West Couloir descent becomes the primary way down regardless of direction of travel.
routes.turnaround: This route is highly weather- and condition-dependent; because the standard descent gully has a documented history of instability and at least one fatal anchor failure, begin early, watch for incoming storms, and turn around well before dark to avoid a rushed descent of the couloir.
routes.bestSeason: A narrow window of a few weeks immediately after Highway 20's spring reopening (typically mid/late April into early-mid May), while the couloir still holds continuous snow/ice.
routes.comms: No cell phone service at Washington Pass; in the May 2025 accident here, the injured survivor had to descend and drive roughly 42 miles to Newhalem to reach help.
routes.face: Central gully between North and South Early Winters Spire
routes.detailedRack: One 60m rope is sufficient; no fixed anchors on route. Bring cams .2-3 inches (small sizes most useful), a set of nuts (small sizes most useful), ice screws, 1-2 pickets, and a small rack of pitons (angles and lost arrows) for the mixed/aid sections.
routes.proNeeds: Protection is thin and improvised through much of the couloir — pins and small cams supplement natural rock/ice features; the cornice crux may require aid.
proTips: Climb it in the first weeks after the highway opens, before the snow/ice deteriorates
The cornice crux has multiple variations (aid left, tunnel through, or slabby rock right) — scope options before committing
watchOut: Unstable, deteriorating snow/ice as the season progresses
The summit cornice is a genuine, sometimes dangerous crux requiring aid or delicate mixed climbing
Fragile thin ice on the lower pitches
The shared West/Southwest Couloir descent has a fatal fixed-anchor failure history — inspect and back up all fixed gear
knownHazards: A May 10, 2025 accident on this route's descent gully killed three climbers (Vishnu Irigireddy, Oleksandr Martynenko, Tim Nguyen) and seriously injured a fourth when a single old fixed piton anchor — used by all four climbers on a shared rappel — pulled out of the rock at roughly 6,100 ft, causing an approximately 400-foot fall; official reports attribute this to failure of a pre-existing fixed piton anchor point
Rockfall and icefall are possible in the couloir, especially as the day warms
Waist-deep unstable snow on the lower 1,500 ft approach slope

pitchDetail.notes: ~1,500 ft of steep snow, sometimes waist-deep, to the couloir mouth.
Thin ice smeared across the wall, including a distinctive 3-ft-wide runnel of alpine ice.
Several hundred feet of simul-climbable snow between chockstones.
Vertical climbing on poor-quality ice, overhung to vertical; drytooling or aid past the crux chockstone.
The route's signature obstacle: a summit cornice passed by aid, tunneling, or slabby rock depending on conditions.
60+ meters of mixed climbing to the summit.

road.driveNote: Park at the well-known Hairpin turn on Highway 20 near Washington Pass, on the east side of the spire formation.

routes.overview: A long, ten-pitch alpine buttress climb on the east side of North Early Winters Spire, mixing easy hiking-terrain pitches with cracks, corners, and chimney sections; a lower-traffic alternative to the peak's more popular west-side lines.
routes.beta: Ten pitches ranging from straightforward moderate climbing and near-hiking terrain to a sustained crux around 5.10b, including slabby corners, hand cracks, and a squeeze-chimney-with-chockstones section near the top. Requires solid 5.8 competency from both climbing partners even though the climbing is not continuously sustained; rock quality varies and some anchors are in questionable rock.
routes.approach: From the Hairpin turnout on Highway 20 (the same start used for the Direct East Buttress on South Early Winters Spire), stay right of the gully dividing the two spires and scramble to a flat ledge marking the start of Flycatcher Buttress.
routes.descent: Rappel Chockstone Route or bolted anchors
routes.descentText: Rappel the Chockstone Route with a 60m rope, or use bolted rappel anchors with a 70m rope.
routes.bail: The lower, easier-angled pitches (P1-P3) offer straightforward downclimbing/scrambling retreat before the route steepens.
routes.turnaround: Ten pitches is a long day on a lightly-traveled route — start early and budget generously for route-finding on the less-traveled upper pitches.
routes.bestSeason: Late June through September.
routes.comms: No cell phone service at Washington Pass.
routes.fa: Steve Marts, Don McPherson, and King McPherson, 1965 (aid); first free ascent (onsight solo) by Bryan Burdo, 1990
routes.face: East side (right of the gully between the two spires)
routes.detailedRack: A standard trad rack to #3 with a single #4; SuperTopo notes an optional #5 for wide-crack variations near the top.
routes.proNeeds: Adequate but variable protection; some belay anchors sit in questionable rock, so back them up where possible.
proTips: Route sees minimal traffic compared to other Liberty Bell Group climbs, so expect route-finding and less-cleaned rock
A SuperTopo topo exists for this route and is recommended given the sparse online beta
watchOut: Variable rock quality, particularly at some belay stances
Long day (10 pitches) with limited beta if you get off-route
knownHazards: Standard alpine rockfall/exposure hazards; loose rock in sections given the route's lower traffic

pitchDetail.notes: Straightforward climbing with moderate protection.
Full rope-length traverse right with limited exposure.
Easy terrain, more hiking than climbing.
Awkward step up into a slabby corner; anchors in questionable rock.
Low-angle hand crack option leading to a large ledge.
Target the left crack in a double-crack system.
Relatively direct line to a nice ledge.
Traverse left to a nice-looking wider crack with exposure.
Multiple options: hand crack or a squeeze chimney with chockstones.
Easy terrain to the summit.

blurb: Prusik Peak is an 8,008-ft granite spire at the western end of The Temple, the signature summit of the Enchantments within Washington's Alpine Lakes Wilderness (Stuart Range), Chelan County. Its clean, sound granodiorite hosts some of the Cascades' best moderate-to-hard alpine rock, from the low-fifth-class West Ridge to steep, sustained multi-pitch lines up its south face. Fred Beckey and Art Holben made the peak's first ascent via the East Route in May 1948, using the prusik-knot technique the peak is named for; Beckey returned in 1957 to establish the classic West Ridge, and Fred Stanley and Ron Burgner added the harder south-face line in 1968.

routes.overview: A low-fifth-class ridge scramble/climb up Prusik Peak's west skyline, widely regarded as one of the best moderate alpine rock routes in the Cascades — mostly Class 4/low-5th with one short, exposed 5.7 friction-slab crux near the top.
routes.beta: From the high point of Prusik Pass, blocky low-5th-class climbing gains the ridge crest; a flat trail-like section on the north side leads to a short, highly exposed traverse to the base of an unprotected 5.7 friction slab (the crux), after which easier climbing and a short chimney or hand-crack finish lead to the tiny, airy summit.
routes.approach: Drive Icicle Creek Rd (FR-7601) from Leavenworth to the Stuart Lake Trailhead. Hike the Stuart Lake/Colchuck Lake Trail ~2.5 mi to the Colchuck Lake Trail #1599A junction, then switchback ~1.6 mi up to Colchuck Lake (5,570'). Climb steeply up Aasgard Pass (Colchuck Pass, ~7,841'), gaining roughly 2,200 ft in under a mile, then descend into the Enchantment Lakes basin toward Prusik Peak and traverse west to Prusik Pass at the base of the West Ridge. Most parties backpack in and camp in the Core Zone (overnight permit required) the night before; total approach time is commonly reported around 6 hours one-way. A longer, more gradual alternative follows the Snow Lakes Trail past Nada and Snow Lakes to Lake Vivian (~10 mi, 5,000+ ft gain), then up past Temple Lake to the peak's base.
routes.descent: 4 single-rope rappels down the north side, then walk back over Prusik Pass.
routes.descentText: From the summit, make four single-rope (not full 60m) rappels down the north side of the peak using fixed/slung stations, then walk back over Prusik Pass to rejoin the approach trail toward camp or the trailhead.
routes.bail: The route can be down-climbed or rappelled from slung horns/blocks along the lower ridge in most conditions; retreat is straightforward compared to the south-face lines since the terrain is low-angle Class 4/low-5th for most of its length.
routes.turnaround: Given the multi-hour approach, exposed unprotected slab pitch, and rappels needed to get off, parties should watch the clock and be willing to turn around rather than push through afternoon thunderstorms or arrive at the crux late in the day.
routes.bestSeason: Prime season is roughly July through September, once snow has cleared Aasgard Pass and Prusik Pass and while the Enchantment Permit Area's May 15–Oct 31 overnight-permit window is in effect; early-season parties may still find snow on the pass approaches into June.
routes.comms: Cell coverage is spotty to nonexistent through the Enchantments Core Zone; a satellite messenger is recommended rather than relying on a phone.
routes.fa: Fred Beckey (1957)
routes.rappels: 4 single-rope raps, north side
objHaz: loose blocks on the lower ridge
afternoon thunderstorms typical of North Cascades granite
gear: slings for horn/block anchors (no bolts on route)
routes.detailedRack: A single rack of nuts and cams up to roughly 3 inches is sufficient; there are no bolts on route, though slings back up several belays off horns and blocks.
routes.proNeeds: Mostly moderate face/crack pro on the lower pitches; the 5.7 slab crux on pitch 3 is largely unprotected/runout.
whatToBring: overnight Core Zone permit if camping
light insulation for cool alpine mornings
proTips: Widely cited as one of the best 5.7 alpine rock routes in the country — expect company on nice summer weekends.
Move together/simul-climb the easy sections to keep the long day efficient.
watchOut: The P3 slab crux is unprotected — a fall here is a real ground/ledge-fall risk despite the moderate grade.
Route-finding on the lower pitches wanders; stay close to the ridge crest.
knownHazards: Loose rock in spots on the lower ridge.
Significant exposure on the traverse leading into the crux pitch.

pitchDetail.notes: From the high point of Prusik Pass, a distinct 5.6-ish crack through blocky terrain gains the West Ridge crest.
Follows a flat, trail-like line around the ridge's north side, then climbs ~15 ft to the south side with big exposure to a slingable block below the crux.
Short, unprotected friction-slab crux, then easier climbing/traverse toward the final pitch.
Two finish options to the summit: a hand-jam bulge (5.7) or a layback-crack variation.

road.name: Icicle Creek Rd / FR-7601 to Stuart Lake Trailhead
road.driveNote: Paved Icicle Road runs from Leavenworth to the Stuart Lake Trailhead; no seasonal gate is reported for this access road.

climate.typical: Dry, sunny alpine-granite summers typical of the East Cascades; afternoon thunderstorm buildup is common in July/August.
climate.spring: Snow lingers on Aasgard Pass and around Prusik Pass into June; early-season attempts often require an ice axe/microspikes on the pass.
climate.summer: Warm, mostly stable weather July–August with a real risk of afternoon convective storms.
climate.fall: Cooler, often clear September conditions; days shorten quickly and overnight lows drop near/below freezing.
climate.winter: Not a winter rock objective; deep snow and avalanche terrain on the approach passes.

access.landManager: Okanogan-Wenatchee National Forest — Alpine Lakes Wilderness, Enchantment Permit Area
access.fees: $5/person/night camping fee for overnight Core Zone permits (plus a non-refundable lottery application fee); day climbs carry no fee.
access.permit: Overnight camping in the Enchantment Permit Area's Core Zone (which includes Prusik Peak) requires a permit May 15–Oct 31, allocated via a Recreation.gov lottery (opens mid-February).

emergency.sheriffDispatch: 911 (emergency); RiverCom 911 is the regional dispatch center for Chelan County; Chelan Co. Sheriff's Office non-emergency (509) 667-6851.

emergency.notes: Chelan Co. Sheriff's SAR unit is the primary backcountry rescue responder for the Enchantments; cell coverage is unreliable in the Core Zone.

routes.corrections: Mountain Project's own route page lists the first ascensionist as unknown; several secondary/aggregator sources attribute the West Ridge FA to Fred Beckey in 1957. Included with that caveat.

routes.overview: The classic direct line up Prusik Peak's south face: six sustained pitches of chimneys, cracks, and a squeeze past a chockstone, finishing up an amazing left-facing corner to the summit.
routes.beta: A Grade III alpine rock testpiece climbing straight up the obvious weakness in the south face — wide cracks and chimneys low, a tunnel behind a chockstone at mid-height, and a sustained crux corner high on the route.
routes.approach: Same Enchantments approach as the West Ridge: Stuart Lake Trailhead to Colchuck Lake, up Aasgard Pass, and into the Enchantment Lakes basin to the base of Prusik Peak's south face (most parties camp overnight in the Core Zone on a permit). Start at roughly the middle of the base of the south face.
routes.descent: ~5 rappels down the north face (~1 hr), then walk back to camp/trail.
routes.descentText: Walk east from the summit, then make roughly five rappels down the north face using slung belay stations, with one or two ropes; typically about an hour if no other parties are queued at the anchors.
routes.bail: The chimney/gully system low on the route offers reasonable rappel/downclimb retreat from most stances; higher up, retreat means rappelling the same gear anchors used on the descent.
routes.turnaround: With six sustained pitches, a chockstone tunnel, and a multi-rappel descent, parties should set a firm turnaround time and be ready to bail rather than get caught by afternoon storms high on the face.
routes.bestSeason: Best July–September once the approach snow (Aasgard Pass, Prusik Pass) has melted out and within the May 15–Oct 31 Enchantment Permit Area season.
routes.comms: No reliable cell coverage in the Enchantments Core Zone; carry a satellite communicator.
routes.fa: Fred Stanley and Ron Burgner (1968)
routes.rappels: ~5 raps, north face, ~1 hr
objHaz: loose rock near the chockstone tunnel
runout sections on the upper flare/chimney pitches
party traffic/queuing on a very popular line
routes.detailedRack: A set of doubles through #3 Camalot with TCUs covers the route; little fixed gear exists on route or at the summit.
routes.proNeeds: Solid crack and chimney protection throughout; knee pads help in the chockstone squeeze pitch.
whatToBring: overnight Core Zone permit if camping
proTips: One of the range's finest alpine outings — sustained, varied, high-quality granite throughout.
Combine P1-2 and P3-4 into ~180-ft pitches with a 60m rope to save time.
watchOut: Expect other parties on busy weekends; the chockstone tunnel and squeeze chimney can bottleneck.
The final corner pitch is sustained 5.9+ and is the true crux for most climbers, not the chimney below it.
knownHazards: Loose rock reported near the chockstone.
Exposed, somewhat runout sections on pitches 2 and 5.

pitchDetail.notes: Chimney (5.6/5.7) or wide crack (5.8) start, low fifth-class terrain, scramble to a tree.
Easy ramp to an airy step and a short, slightly runout flare; exit right on knobs to the gully.
Gully climbing to a ledge below the chockstone.
Steep double cracks; tight move under and tunnel behind the chockstone (don't haul a pack).
Flaring squeeze chimney — crux for many climbers; knee pads help.
Sustained left-facing corner with stemming on knobs to the summit — the technical crux.

routes.corrections: This route ID appears to duplicate 'wa_stanley_burgner' — both entries describe the same 1968 Fred Stanley/Ron Burgner south-face route on Prusik Peak, just under different names ('South Face (Burgner-Stanley)' vs 'Stanley-Burgner'). Recommend the hierarchy/dedup team merge these two DB entries. Length/grade also vary by source: Mountain Project lists 5.10a / 500 ft / 6p, while Steph Abegg's trip report and Climbing.com list 5.9+ / 650 ft / 6p; 650 ft / 5.9+ was used here as the better-corroborated figure.

routes.overview: The classic direct line up Prusik Peak's south face: six sustained pitches of chimneys, cracks, and a squeeze past a chockstone, finishing up an amazing left-facing corner to the summit.
routes.beta: A Grade III alpine rock testpiece climbing straight up the obvious weakness in the south face — wide cracks and chimneys low, a tunnel behind a chockstone at mid-height, and a sustained crux corner high on the route.
routes.approach: Same Enchantments approach as the West Ridge: Stuart Lake Trailhead to Colchuck Lake, up Aasgard Pass, and into the Enchantment Lakes basin to the base of Prusik Peak's south face (most parties camp overnight in the Core Zone on a permit). Start at roughly the middle of the base of the south face.
routes.descent: ~5 rappels down the north face (~1 hr), then walk back to camp/trail.
routes.descentText: Walk east from the summit, then make roughly five rappels down the north face using slung belay stations, with one or two ropes; typically about an hour if no other parties are queued at the anchors.
routes.bail: The chimney/gully system low on the route offers reasonable rappel/downclimb retreat from most stances; higher up, retreat means rappelling the same gear anchors used on the descent.
routes.turnaround: With six sustained pitches, a chockstone tunnel, and a multi-rappel descent, parties should set a firm turnaround time and be ready to bail rather than get caught by afternoon storms high on the face.
routes.bestSeason: Best July–September once the approach snow (Aasgard Pass, Prusik Pass) has melted out and within the May 15–Oct 31 Enchantment Permit Area season.
routes.comms: No reliable cell coverage in the Enchantments Core Zone; carry a satellite communicator.
routes.fa: Fred Stanley and Ron Burgner (1968)
routes.rappels: ~5 raps, north face, ~1 hr
objHaz: loose rock near the chockstone tunnel
runout sections on the upper flare/chimney pitches
party traffic/queuing on a very popular line
routes.detailedRack: A set of doubles through #3 Camalot with TCUs covers the route; little fixed gear exists on route or at the summit.
routes.proNeeds: Solid crack and chimney protection throughout; knee pads help in the chockstone squeeze pitch.
whatToBring: overnight Core Zone permit if camping
proTips: One of the range's finest alpine outings — sustained, varied, high-quality granite throughout.
Combine P1-2 and P3-4 into ~180-ft pitches with a 60m rope to save time.
watchOut: Expect other parties on busy weekends; the chockstone tunnel and squeeze chimney can bottleneck.
The final corner pitch is sustained 5.9+ and is the true crux for most climbers, not the chimney below it.
knownHazards: Loose rock reported near the chockstone.
Exposed, somewhat runout sections on pitches 2 and 5.

pitchDetail.notes: Chimney (5.6/5.7) or wide crack (5.8) start, low fifth-class terrain, scramble to a tree.
Easy ramp to an airy step and a short, slightly runout flare; exit right on knobs to the gully.
Gully climbing to a ledge below the chockstone.
Steep double cracks; tight move under and tunnel behind the chockstone (don't haul a pack).
Flaring squeeze chimney — crux for many climbers; knee pads help.
Sustained left-facing corner with stemming on knobs to the summit — the technical crux.

routes.corrections: This route ID appears to duplicate 'wa_prusik_peak_south_face_burgner_stanley' — both entries describe the same 1968 Fred Stanley/Ron Burgner south-face route on Prusik Peak, just under different names. Recommend the hierarchy/dedup team merge these two DB entries. Length/grade also vary by source: Mountain Project lists 5.10a / 500 ft / 6p, while Steph Abegg's trip report and Climbing.com list 5.9+ / 650 ft / 6p; 650 ft / 5.9+ was used here as the better-corroborated figure.

routes.overview: The original 1962 line up Prusik Peak's south face, climbing a chimney/slab/crack system left of the Stanley-Burgner line to a committing 5.9 corner-and-jam-crack crux below the summit.
routes.beta: Seven pitches from 5.5 to 5.9, starting up a chimney left of two prominent V-shaped features on the south face, crossing a long chimney-gully to Snafflehound Ledge, then finishing with a forced rightward dihedral/jam-crack crux and a choice of two summit finishes.
routes.approach: Same Enchantments approach as the other south-face routes: Stuart Lake Trailhead, Colchuck Lake, Aasgard Pass, and into the Enchantment Lakes basin to the base of Prusik Peak's south face.
routes.descent: North Face rappel route from the summit, doable on a single 60m rope.
routes.descentText: From the summit, descend the North Face rappel route, marked by a bolt and tat; can be completed with a single 60m rope.
routes.bail: Snafflehound Ledge (after P2-3) is a natural retreat point with easier downclimbing/rappel options back toward the base; above that, retreat means rappelling the gear placed on lead.
routes.turnaround: Seven pitches plus a multi-rappel descent make this a long day — set a firm turnaround and don't get caught by afternoon storms on the exposed upper corner pitch.
routes.bestSeason: Best July–September, after the approach passes clear of snow and within the Enchantment Permit Area's May 15–Oct 31 season.
routes.comms: No reliable cell coverage in the Core Zone; carry a satellite communicator.
routes.fa: Fred Beckey and Dan Davis, June 29, 1962
routes.rappels: North Face rap route, single 60m rope
objHaz: exposed dihedral/jam-crack crux with a step-across move
loose rock possible in the chimney-gully section
long multi-pitch day with a committing descent
routes.detailedRack: Gear up to a #4 cam with doubles from 0.5–2, plus a full set of nuts/chocks.
routes.proNeeds: Standard crack/chimney protection; the P5 crux dihedral and step-across take small-to-medium cams well.
whatToBring: overnight Core Zone permit if camping
proTips: The historic original line on the face — climbs a different system than the neighboring Stanley-Burgner route, sharing pitch 1 terrain in spots.
Snafflehound Ledge partway up is a good rest/regroup point.
watchOut: The P5 step-across into a flaring jam crack is the crux and is committing.
Choose the P7 finish deliberately: 7A (5.8, north-side traverse) is easier than the 7B (5.10) direct crack.
knownHazards: Some loose rock reported in the chimney-gully sections.
Significant exposure on the upper pitches.

pitchDetail.notes: Chimney left of two prominent V-features on the south face; move left near the top onto knobs to a belay.
Rightward up a steep slab and a long chimney-gully to a large ledge (Snafflehound Ledge).
Traverse right on the ledge to a tree, then a short steep step.
Shallow dihedral forces rightward movement; step across left into a flaring jam crack — the crux.
Crack system/steep gully, bypassing a chockstone on the left.
North-side option: descend slightly, traverse right, follow a ramp to a corner and jam crack to the summit.
Direct option: climb the obvious crack straight up the wall to the summit.

routes.overview: A stiff five-pitch 5.11a face/crack route up the left flank of Prusik Peak's south face, with a hard roof move and a sustained perfect corner-crack finish.
routes.beta: Climbs just right of the outside corner on the face's left flank: a sustained corner and stembox low, a hard roof pull on P2, an off-width/mantle pitch, and a final perfect corner crack to a small tree near the summit.
routes.approach: Same Enchantments approach as the other south-face routes: Stuart Lake Trailhead, Colchuck Lake, Aasgard Pass, into the Enchantment Lakes basin to the base of Prusik Peak's south face; start just right of the outside corner on the face's left flank.
routes.descent: One 60m rappel down the north side, then a ~30-minute scramble back to the base.
routes.descentText: From the top of the route, make one 60-meter rappel down the north side of the peak, then scramble roughly 30 minutes back around to the base of the south face.
routes.turnaround: A sustained, technical route with a hard roof pitch — parties should be efficient and prepared to bail via the single rappel rather than push into worsening weather.
routes.bestSeason: Best July–September once the approach has melted out, within the Enchantment Permit Area's May 15–Oct 31 season.
routes.comms: No reliable cell coverage in the Core Zone; carry a satellite communicator.
routes.fa: Wayne Wallace and Tim Olsen (1989)
routes.rappels: 1 x 60m rap, north side, then ~30 min scramble
objHaz: sustained hard climbing far from the ground
whatToBring: overnight Core Zone permit if camping
proTips: The roof move on P2 is the technical crux — sample it before committing.
Shares its final pitch with the Energizer Bunny variation.
watchOut: Sustained 5.10-5.11 climbing on every pitch — pace yourself for a long day.
knownHazards: Typical Cascades afternoon-storm exposure high on the face.

pitchDetail.notes: ~100 ft: crack to a stembox, sustained corner, belay under a roof.
~100 ft: hard move under the roof, hand crack to a horizontal break — crux.
~185 ft combined: off-width crack, mantle move, arching seam.
~150 ft: perfect corner crack, step right to a small tree.

routes.overview: A four-pitch 5.10 face-and-crack route on the west side of Prusik Peak's south face, with a long, exposed second pitch and a clean bouldery corner crux higher up.
routes.beta: Starts about 100 ft right of Solid Gold on the west side of the south face: a short crack and slab to a corner, a long exposed and somewhat dirty face-crack pitch, a corner with a bouldery move, and a finishing fingers-to-hands pitch to the ridge.
routes.approach: Same Enchantments approach as the other south-face routes: Stuart Lake Trailhead, Colchuck Lake, Aasgard Pass, into the Enchantment Lakes basin to the base of Prusik Peak's south face; start on the west side of the face, ~100 ft right of Solid Gold.
routes.descent: One 60m rappel down the north side, then a ~30-minute scramble back to the base.
routes.descentText: From the top, make one 60-meter rappel down the north side of the peak, then scramble roughly 30 minutes back to the base (same finish as Solid Gold).
routes.turnaround: Four moderate-to-hard pitches plus a long approach — leave enough daylight margin for the descent scramble and don't linger if storms build.
routes.bestSeason: Best July–September once the approach has melted out, within the Enchantment Permit Area's May 15–Oct 31 season.
routes.comms: No reliable cell coverage in the Core Zone; carry a satellite communicator.
routes.fa: Paul Boving and Matt Christensen (1977)
routes.rappels: 1 x 60m rap, north side, then ~30 min scramble
objHaz: long exposed P2 with some dirty/loose rock reported
whatToBring: overnight Core Zone permit if camping
proTips: Pitch 2 is long (~200 ft) — consider a 70m rope or a mid-pitch belay if your rope is shorter.
watchOut: Pitch 2 is described as 'somewhat dirty' in places — test holds.
knownHazards: Some loose/dirty rock reported on the long second pitch.

pitchDetail.notes: ~120 ft: short crack, low-angle slab, corner to a belay.
~200 ft: exposed, somewhat dirty face cracks — long pitch.
~100 ft: corner with a fun bouldery move; clean climbing.
~170 ft: fingers to hands to a flake, finishing on hand cracks to the ridge.

routes.overview: A sustained, sought-after 5.11+ testpiece 50 ft left of the standard South Face route, following a clean arete/crack system with two hard pitches low and a thin, thuggish splitter finish.
routes.beta: Six pitches of steep, splitter crack systems linked by airy, committing sequences on excellent white granite: a stembox crux on P1, a knobby mental crux on P2 protected by a fixed pin, an undercling 'money pitch' on P3, and a flaring thin-hands splitter finish on P6.
routes.approach: Same Enchantments approach as the other south-face routes: Stuart Lake Trailhead, Colchuck Lake, Aasgard Pass, into the Enchantment Lakes basin to the base of Prusik Peak's south face. The route starts about 50 ft left of the standard South Face (Stanley-Burgner) line — look for the obvious stembox.
routes.descent: Rappel the back (north) side of the peak with one rope.
routes.descentText: From the summit, rappel the north (back) side of the peak with a single rope; exact rappel count/anchor spacing not documented in available sources.
routes.turnaround: Six sustained, technical pitches with only one rope to descend on — climb efficiently and don't get caught high on the route by weather.
routes.bestSeason: Best July–September once the approach has melted out, within the Enchantment Permit Area's May 15–Oct 31 season.
routes.comms: No reliable cell coverage in the Core Zone; carry a satellite communicator.
routes.fa: Brooke Sandahl and partners, established over roughly three seasons in the 1990s
routes.rappels: Rap the back (north) side with one rope
objHaz: sustained hard free climbing far above gear
routes.detailedRack: RPs, a full set of draws, doubles from small nuts/cams through #2, and 1-2 #3 cams depending on comfort; a #4 is optional and rarely needed.
routes.proNeeds: Thin, technical protection low (RPs/small cams) transitioning to hand-sized gear on the upper splitter pitches; one fixed pin protects the P2 crux.
whatToBring: overnight Core Zone permit if camping
tape for the finger/hand crack pitches
proTips: Rediscovered decades after its original ascent when a photo of the route appeared in a Metolius catalog.
Look for the obvious stembox to identify the start, 50 ft left of the standard South Face line.
watchOut: P1 and P2 hold the technical/mental cruxes early — pace yourself rather than getting pumped before the upper pitches.
knownHazards: Sustained, sometimes thin protection on the crux pitches.

pitchDetail.notes: Left-slanting crack system to a stembox crux, then twin cracks; lieback/stem to a 2-bolt anchor. Crux.
Corner climbing to a knobby crux protected by a fixed pin, around the arete onto the slab.
'The money pitch' — undercling flake/crack system to a broad ledge.
Obvious corner crack, a dicey crack switch, and wild pillar climbing.
Short corner to a slab with multiple belay options.
Steep, thuggish, flaring thin-hands splitter to a chockstone mantel and chimney.

routes.overview: A five-pitch 5.10+/C1 line on the west face left of Solid Gold, climbing an arching roof beneath the 'Elephant Ear,' a crack between the 'Bunny Ears,' and finishing on Solid Gold's final corner-crack pitch.
routes.beta: A burly, somewhat dirty roof pitch off the deck, an exposed corner to a spectacular ledge, a short aided (C1) section through 15 ft of 5.11+/5.12- terrain, a steep fingery crack between rock features nicknamed the 'Bunny Ears,' and Solid Gold's final perfect corner crack to the summit.
routes.approach: Same Enchantments approach as the other south/west-face routes: Stuart Lake Trailhead, Colchuck Lake, Aasgard Pass, into the Enchantment Lakes basin to the base of Prusik Peak; start on the west face to the left of Solid Gold.
routes.descent: Same as Solid Gold — one 60m rappel down the north side, then a ~30-minute scramble back to the base.
routes.descentText: From the top (shared finish with Solid Gold), make one 60-meter rappel down the north side, then scramble roughly 30 minutes back to the base of the face.
routes.turnaround: A short aid section and sustained free climbing make this a slower route than its neighbors — build in extra time and be ready to bail off the shared rappel line.
routes.bestSeason: Best July–September once the approach has melted out, within the Enchantment Permit Area's May 15–Oct 31 season.
routes.comms: No reliable cell coverage in the Core Zone; carry a satellite communicator.
routes.fa: Jon Pobst and Steph Abegg, June 14, 2015 (documented as a likely first ascent)
routes.rappels: 1 x 60m rap, north side (shared with Solid Gold), then ~30 min scramble
objHaz: burly, dirty roof pitch low on the route
short aid section adds rope-management complexity
whatToBring: aiders/etriers for the short C1 section
overnight Core Zone permit if camping
proTips: A relatively new, less-traveled variation compared to its south-face neighbors — expect a dirtier, more adventurous feel on the lower pitches.
The final pitch shares Solid Gold's beautiful corner-crack finish.
watchOut: The roof pitch (P1) is described as burly and dirty — test holds carefully.
knownHazards: Dirt/lichen reported on the lower pitches given the route's relative obscurity.

pitchDetail.notes: ~100 ft: arching roof under the 'Elephant Ear' feature — burly and dirty.
~100 ft: awesome corner to a super exposed ledge.
~100 ft: crack progression with 15 ft of 5.11+/5.12- terrain, aided at C1.
~100 ft: crack between the 'Bunny Ears' — steep, fingery.
~150 ft: final pitch shared with Solid Gold — perfect corner crack to the summit.

blurb: Pernod Spire is one of the granite Wine Spires (with Burgundy, Chianti, and Chablis) on Silver Star Mountain's north ridge, at the fringe of the glacier just south of Washington Pass in the North Cascades. First climbed in September 1952 by Fred Beckey, Don Wilde, and Dick McGowan via an aid-assisted line, it now offers routes from moderate 5.5-5.7 scrambling terrain up to a sustained 5.10+ R testpiece on its tall west face — the tallest wall of the Wine Spires.
hierarchyNote: The stored coordinates (48.55, -120.595) sit roughly 3 miles northeast of Mountain Project's listed GPS for Pernod Spire's own area page (48.5123, -120.654), which places it just south of Washington Pass — consistent with the AAC's description of the Wine Spires as "at the fringe of the glacier north of Silver Star's North Peak" and with the approach description (Hwy 20 pullout ~0.7 mi past Cutthroat Lake Rd, ~26 mi from Winthrop). The name, elevation range, and parent area (Washington Pass) are otherwise consistent with a real, identifiable peak; recommend re-verifying the stored lat/lng against Mountain Project/topo data.

routes.fa: Tony Bentley and Ross Peritore, July 2006
routes.overview: An 8-pitch alpine rock line taking the steepest, most direct crack-and-corner system up Pernod Spire's tall west face — the tallest wall among the Wine Spires.
routes.beta: Climbs a weakness right of center on the lower west face for two moderate pitches, then a crux corner with an off-fingers crack (P3, 5.10+, 60m, R) around a large wave-like roof. Upper pitches (5.8-5.10) continue via face and crack systems to the summit ridge on solid granite.
routes.approach: From a Highway 20 pullout about 26 miles west of Winthrop (roughly 0.7 mile past Cutthroat Lake Road), follow the climber's trail that drops from the highway, crosses a creek, and climbs steeply to a bench camp around 6,500 ft below the Wine Spires; continue to Burgundy Col and along the base to the Pernod west face.
routes.descent: Rappel the east gully to the Pernod/Chablis notch, then continue rappelling steep slabs to névé; traverse north around Chianti Spire back to Burgundy Col and the climbers' path to the highway.
routes.descentText: From the summit, downclimb/rappel into the gully on the east side of the spire to reach the notch between Pernod and Chablis. From the notch, further rappels down steep slabs lead to névé/snow below. Trend north, skirting around Chianti Spire, to regain Burgundy Col, then follow the climbers' trail back down to the Highway 20 pullout.
routes.bail: Rappel the route itself using trad anchors below the crux; above pitch 3, it is faster to continue to the summit ridge and use the standard east-gully rappel line to the Pernod/Chablis notch than to reverse the harder pitches.
routes.turnaround: This is a full alpine day with a long approach and an 8-pitch technical face; if you are not through the crux (pitch 3) by early-to-mid afternoon, strongly consider retreating so the multi-stage rappel descent isn't finished after dark.
routes.bestSeason: Late June through late September is the recorded season for the Wine Spires; snow and ice linger in approach gullies in early season, so the route is typically driest and safest from mid-July onward.
routes.detailedRack: A light alpine rack works well: 2-3x green Alien (or equivalent small cam) up through a #4 Camalot, plus two 60-meter ropes for the multi-pitch rappel descent.
routes.proNeeds: Small-to-medium cams protect the crux corner and crack systems; the R rating on the crux pitch means some sections run out above gear, so solid technique and route-finding matter more than a heavy rack.
gear: 2-3x green Alien or equivalent small cams
whatToBring: approach shoes for the climber's trail
two 60m ropes for the rappel descent
proTips: Bring 2-3 small cams (green Alien-sized) up to a #4 Camalot and two 60m ropes — the crux corner (P3, 5.10+) is the pitch to be ready for.
Descend via the east gully to the Pernod/Chablis notch rather than trying to reverse the west face.
watchOut: The crux pitch 3 corner carries an R rating for runout climbing above gear.
Route-finding through and around a large wave-like roof feature is part of the challenge.
knownHazards: Runout (R-rated) 5.10+ climbing on the crux pitch (P3)
Long, multi-stage rappel descent (east gully to notch, then further raps down slabs to névé) requiring careful route-finding

pitchDetail.notes: Crux: incredible corner with an off-fingers crack, steep with a bulge, 60m; navigates around a large wave-like roof

road.name: State Route 20 / North Cascades Highway
road.status: seasonal - closed in winter
road.seasonalGate: Gate near Silver Star (milepost 171); closure has begun as early as early December and reopening has occurred as late as mid-to-late April depending on snow/avalanche conditions
road.driveNote: The Wine Spires pullout is on SR 20 about 26 miles west of Winthrop, roughly 0.7 mile past Cutthroat Lake Road.

climate.typical: Best weather is late June through late September; snow and ice should be expected in early season.
climate.summer: Prime climbing window; afternoon thunderstorms can occur in the alpine.
climate.fall: Conditions deteriorate toward late September as snow returns to approach gullies.
climate.winter: Highway 20 is closed for the season; the area is inaccessible by normal means.

access.landManager: Okanogan-Wenatchee National Forest (Methow Valley Ranger District)
access.fees: No fee at the roadside pullout itself; a Northwest Forest Pass (or America the Beautiful interagency pass) is required at developed Forest Service day-use sites/trailheads in the area.
access.passRequired: Northwest Forest Pass (developed FS sites)
access.closures: SR 20 (North Cascades Highway) is closed for winter between roughly the Ross Dam trailhead (MP 134) and the Silver Star gate (MP 171), typically early December into mid-to-late April.

timing.recommendedStart: Early alpine start recommended given the long approach and 8-pitch route

routes.fa: Peter Jewitt, Cliff Leight, and Dan Waters, July 10, 1988
routes.rappels: 2 double-rope (60m) rappels from the Chablis East Face anchors back to the base
objHaz: A0 aid move required at the summit
routes.overview: A short, moderate 2-pitch line up Pernod's true south face to the 'bunny-ear' notch, commonly tagged onto an ascent of neighboring Chablis Spire's East Face.
routes.beta: Follows the obvious weakness — the only reasonable passage — up the south face toward the bunny-ear notch on good cracks with solid protection, through a right-facing corner, finishing on a splitter thin-hands pitch on excellent granite before an A0 aid move off a bolt to reach the summit.
routes.approach: Pernod's true south face is visible from, and reached via, the Chablis Spire East Face route; it is easily added on for parties competent at the grade already climbing Chablis.
routes.descent: Two double-rope (60m) rappels from the Chablis East Face anchors return climbers to the base.
routes.descentText: From the summit, downclimb/scramble (exposed) back to the Chablis East Face anchor system, then make two double-rope rappels with 60m ropes to return to the base. Bring extra webbing for the rappel stations.
routes.bail: From anywhere on the short 2-pitch route, two double-rope (60m) rappels from the Chablis East Face anchors return to the base.
routes.turnaround: Only 150 ft / 2 pitches, but retreat still requires reversing exposed scrambling to the rappel anchors — don't linger if afternoon weather is building.
routes.bestSeason: Late June through late September, matching the broader Wine Spires season; best paired with a Chablis East Face day.
routes.detailedRack: A moderate trad rack: 2x 0.3-2" BD Camalots, one set of stoppers, a 0.2" BD cam, and #3/#4 Camalots recommended for the wider cracks; about 10 alpine draws and two 60m ropes.
routes.proNeeds: Good, solid crack protection throughout; the final summit move is A0 off a fixed bolt rather than free climbable at the stated grade.
proTips: Often linked as an add-on to an ascent of Chablis's East Face, since Pernod's true south face is reached directly from those anchors.
Bring extra webbing for the rappel stations.
watchOut: The final move to the summit is A0 (aid) off a bolt, not free climbable at the stated grade.
Descent involves exposed scrambling before reaching the rappel anchors.
knownHazards: A0 aid move required at the top to reach the summit
Exposed scrambling on the descent traverse back to the rappel anchors

pitchDetail.notes: Good cracks with solid protection through a right-facing corner toward the bunny-ear notch
Splitter thin-hands crack on excellent granite; finishes with an A0 aid move off a bolt to the summit

routes.face: North (approached via the notch between Pernod and Chianti)
routes.overview: The historically easiest way up Pernod Spire: moderate, non-sustained 4th- and 5th-class climbing (5.5-5.7) over roughly 3 pitches, reached via a traverse from Chablis Spire's rappels to the north notch between Pernod and Chianti.
routes.beta: Per Beckey-guide-derived route descriptions, Pernod and neighboring Chablis are considered the easiest of the Wine Spires and can be climbed in an easy day; the climbing is moderate (5.5-5.7) and not sustained.
routes.approach: From Chablis Spire, rappel down (twice) near its NE gully, then traverse obliquely up and around to reach the notch between Pernod and Chianti before climbing the moderate terrain to Pernod's summit.
routes.descent: Likely reverses the approach: downclimb/rappel back to the north notch and retrace the traverse toward Chablis.
routes.bail: Retreat via the same traverse/notch used on the approach; the route is short enough that descending the way you came is generally the fastest option.
routes.turnaround: Though rated moderate, this is not a trivial scramble — the approach traverse to the north notch is non-obvious; don't push on if you can't positively identify the notch and the weather is deteriorating.
routes.bestSeason: Late June through late September, matching the broader Wine Spires season; considered an 'easy day' option relative to Pernod's harder faces.
proTips: Many parties combine this line with a Chablis Spire ascent since the approach shares the same rappels off Chablis.
watchOut: Rated 'easy' relative to Pernod's other routes, but route-finding to the north notch via the Chablis rappels is non-trivial and best attempted with good beta.
knownHazards: Non-obvious approach requiring a rappel/traverse from Chablis Spire to locate the north notch before climbing Pernod

routes.corrections: Pernod Spire's true first ascent (of the peak, not this specific moderate line) was made in September 1952 by Fred Beckey, Don Wilde, and Dick McGowan, via a much harder, aid-assisted route on the peak's NE side (multiple pitons, Rawl-drive bolts for protection, an 8-ft pendulum traverse, and unconventional shoulder-stand belays to place summit bolts) — described by the party as one of the most strenuous climbs in the Cascades at the time. This is a distinctly different, harder line than the moderate 5.5-5.7 'standard'/easiest route via the north notch described by later sources (SummitPost/Beckey guide), so it was NOT attributed as the FA of this 'Standard Rock Route' entry. The FA party of the specific moderate north-notch line is not clearly documented in sources checked.

blurb: Mount Torment (8,120+ ft) is a rugged granite peak on the far west side of Boston Basin in North Cascades National Park, forming the western anchor of the classic Torment-Forbidden Traverse to neighboring Forbidden Peak. It was first climbed August 23, 1946 by Jack Schwabland and Herb Staley, who named it for "the torture of a hot day with only one orange for quenching thirst."

routes.overview: A classic alpine rock traverse that follows the jagged, mile-long granite skyline from Mount Torment's summit to Forbidden Peak, finishing up Forbidden's famous West Ridge — one of the most sought-after moderate alpine outings in the North Cascades.
routes.beta: From Boston Basin, climb Torment's South Ridge or SE Face (loose 4th/low 5th class) to a grassy ledge and V-shaped notch. An overhanging rappel from the notch drops onto a glacier on Forbidden's NE side; bypassing the moat/bergschrund can be tricky. Cross the glacier and follow the exposed knife-edge ridge ('Sidewalk in the Sky'), bypassing the first gendarme left and the second right, to the West Ridge Notch, where the standard Forbidden West Ridge route (4th/5.easy to a 5.6 tower pitch) is followed to Forbidden's summit. The climbing is never technically hard, but there is a large volume of variable terrain to cover efficiently.
routes.fa: Ed Cooper and Walt (Buck) Sellers, July 26, 1958
routes.rappels: Overhanging rap off Torment's notch onto the glacier, then roughly 6-8 rappels down Forbidden's standard descent couloir/gullies back to Boston Basin
objHaz: Exposed knife-edge ridge with serious fall potential
Slick, waterfall-polished slabs below the glacier when wet
routes.face: Torment S Ridge/SE Face to a NE-side glacier crossing, then Forbidden's West Ridge
whatToBring: Approach shoes plus rock shoes or sturdy mountaineering boots
Extra webbing for weathered rappel anchors
routes.detailedRack: Light alpine rack: cams to about #2-#3, a handful of stoppers, 8-10 slings/draws, 60m rope (doubled for rappels), 1-2 snow pickets and/or an ice screw for the glacier crossing and any late-season bergschrund/crevasse issues, plus extra tat/webbing since existing rap anchors on the notch and ridge see heavy traffic.
routes.proNeeds: A moderate rack covers the 5.6 terrain; the real protection needs are for glacier travel (pickets/screws, crevasse rescue kit) and having spare webbing to back up or replace worn rappel slings at the notch and along the ridge.
proTips: Move fast and light — the route's difficulty is in the sheer volume of variable terrain, not technical hardness
Scope the overhanging rappel off Torment's notch carefully; bypassing the moat on the first rap can be tricky
A bivy roughly halfway along the connecting ridge breaks the traverse into two manageable days
Guided itineraries typically budget 3-4 days: approach day, Torment + ridge bivy, Forbidden summit + descent, hike-out
watchOut: Loose rock while rappelling Forbidden's standard descent gullies
Waterfall-polished slabs below the glacier are dangerous when wet
Late-season bergschrund/moat conditions on the glacier crossing can become complex
Bear activity has been reported in Boston Basin — bear canister use is required
knownHazards: Crevasses and a bergschrund on the glacier crossing between Torment and Forbidden
Rockfall on loose gully and gendarme terrain
Sustained exposure along a genuine knife-edge ridge section
Volatile, fast-changing mountain weather typical of the North Cascades

pitchDetail.notes: South Ridge or SE Face of Torment, loose, to a grassy ledge and V-shaped notch; packs can be left here.
Rappel from the notch onto the glacier on Forbidden's NE side; bypassing the moat/bergschrund on this rap can be tricky.
Snow/glacier crossing then the 'Sidewalk in the Sky' knife-edge; bypass the first gendarme on the left, the second on the right, to the West Ridge Notch.
4th/5.easy climbing to a 5.6 tower pitch, then easier terrain to Forbidden's summit.

road.name: Cascade River Road
road.status: Paved to about mile 10, then rough gravel; narrow with steep drop-offs beyond Eldorado (mile 20)
road.seasonalGate: Closed in winter; NPS notes it typically opens to the Cascade Pass trailhead parking area by the end of June
road.driveNote: From Marblemount, drive roughly 22-22.5 miles to the Boston Basin turnout (about 3,200 ft) opposite Johannesburg Mountain. No trailers or vehicles over 22 ft long/8 ft wide are permitted past mile 18.

climate.typical: North Cascades National Park is one of the wettest parks in the US, receiving over 100 inches of precipitation a year; west-slope areas like Boston Basin have a mild, humid, rainy maritime climate.
climate.spring: Snow lingers above about 3,000 ft into May; lower-elevation temps run roughly 35-55°F.
climate.summer: June-September is the best window, with temperatures in the 70s-low 80s°F, mostly clear skies, and occasional afternoon showers.
climate.fall: Cooler temperatures, possible wildfire smoke, and reduced crowds; NPS trip reports note active bear activity in Boston Basin into September.
climate.winter: Heavy snow; nearby SR-20 typically closes mid-November to April, with average highs near 30°F and lows near 18°F.

access.landManager: North Cascades National Park Service Complex (NPS)
access.fees: $10 per person plus a $6 non-refundable reservation fee for the required backcountry/wilderness permit
access.permit: A wilderness permit is required year-round for overnight camping in Boston Basin/Torment Basin (Cross Country Zone). Reserve on Recreation.gov, then pick up in person at the Wilderness Information Center in Marblemount (permits for Cascade River Road trailheads must be picked up in person).
access.passRequired: No park entrance fee; a Northwest Forest Pass/America the Beautiful pass may be needed for some Cascade River Road trailhead parking areas
access.closures: Cascade River Road is subject to seasonal winter gate closure and occasional washout closures; check current NPS road status before driving in.

timing.recommendedStart: Early alpine start on each climbing day to cross the snow/glacier while firm and to leave margin for the notch rappel and ridge traverse

waypoints.name: Boston Basin trailhead turnout, Cascade River Road

emergency.sheriffDispatch: Skagit County Sheriff's Office dispatch: 360-336-9450 (or 911)
emergency.rangerStation: North Cascades NP Wilderness Information Center, Marblemount: 360-854-7200; after-hours NPS Communications Center: 360-873-4590 ext. 37
emergency.nearestHospital: Skagit Valley Hospital, Mount Vernon, WA (Level III trauma center) — 360-424-4111
emergency.notes: Cell coverage is unreliable in Boston/Torment Basin; NPS advises not relying on cell phones for evacuation and recommends carrying a satellite communicator. Rescue response can take hours to days given terrain and weather.

routes.bail: Retreat is straightforward if you turn back before rappelling off Torment's notch — simply reverse the South Ridge/SE Face back to Boston Basin. Once past the notch and onto the connecting ridge, the traverse becomes essentially a one-way commitment through to Forbidden's standard descent gullies.
routes.turnaround: Turn around before committing to the notch rappel or ridge traverse if you can't see a clear weather window and enough daylight/energy margin to complete the crossing and Forbidden's descent — North Cascades weather and rockfall risk both increase quickly once conditions turn.
routes.bestSeason: Prime conditions are mid-summer, roughly July through September, once the connecting glacier and notch have consolidated seasonal snow. Late-season visits face a more complex bergschrund/moat on the glacier crossing, per route reports.
routes.comms: No reliable cell service through most of Boston Basin/Torment Basin and the connecting ridge; NPS notes reception is possible from some higher points but should not be relied on for emergencies — carry a satellite communicator.
routes.itinerary: Guided/typical itinerary: Day 1 - drive and approach to Boston Basin camp (~3-4 hrs, ~3,000 ft gain over ~3 miles). Day 2 - ascend Torment via the South Ridge/SE Face, rappel to the glacier, and bivy below the connecting ridge. Day 3 - traverse the mile-long ridge, climb Forbidden's West Ridge to the summit, and rappel (~6-8 raps) back to Boston Basin. Day 4 - hike out to the trailhead.
routes.descent: Descend Forbidden's standard couloir/gully system back to the glacier and Boston Basin, roughly 6-8 rappels.
routes.descentText: From Forbidden's summit, downclimb/rappel back to the West Ridge Notch, then descend the standard Forbidden couloir/gully system (roughly 6-8 rappels, watching for loose rock) back down to the connecting glacier, retrace the glacier crossing, and return to Boston Basin via the Torment approach.

routes.overview: The standard technical line straight up Mount Torment's summit, climbing a wandering system of gullies and open-book corners on the south ridge to the small summit.
routes.beta: From the notch in the South Ridge, do not start up either of the two obvious dihedral/gully lines to the right — the route follows an open book to the left. Turn the corner right at the top of that open book, gain a ledge, and continue up a second short open book. Some of the route's most solid rock follows through the central section, then easy traversing on ledges leads across broken 4th and easy 5th class terrain to the broad summit area.
routes.fa: Ed Cooper and Walt (Buck) Sellers, 1958
routes.detailedRack: A light rack of small-to-mid cams and a few stoppers, plus long slings for the wandering gully/dihedral system; a rope for the occasional short rappel. Ice axe and crampons are useful for the Taboo Glacier crossing, especially early season.
routes.proNeeds: A moderate rack covers the sustained 4th/low 5th terrain; replace weathered webbing found at existing rappel anchors rather than trusting it outright.
proTips: Cross the Taboo Glacier early while the snow is firm — it softens quickly in the afternoon sun
Don't start up either of the two obvious dihedral/gully lines right of the true start; the route follows the open book to the left
Carry long slings since the route wanders and belay/rap stations are spaced for that terrain
watchOut: Route-finding: the correct start is easy to miss among several similar-looking gully/dihedral options nearby
Biting flies can be intense in late summer
Rappel anchors along the route may have weathered webbing — inspect and back up as needed
knownHazards: Loose 4th/low-5th rock on the initial gully/open-book sections
Glacier travel hazards (crevasses) crossing the Taboo Glacier, especially early season
Sustained no-fall exposure on some pitches

pitchDetail.notes: Open book (dihedral) climbing up and left from the notch in the South Ridge.
Turn the corner to the right at the top of the first open book, reach a ledge, continue up a second short open book.
Some of the most solid rock on the route, through the central section of the peak.
Easy traversing on ledges to the large summit area; broken 4th and easy 5th class to Torment's summit.

road.status: Paved to about mile 10, then rough gravel; narrow with steep drop-offs beyond Eldorado (mile 20)

road.driveNote: From Marblemount, drive roughly 22-22.5 miles to the Boston Basin turnout (about 3,200 ft) opposite Johannesburg Mountain. No trailers or vehicles over 22 ft long/8 ft wide are permitted past mile 18.

access.fees: $10 per person plus a $6 non-refundable reservation fee for the required backcountry/wilderness permit
access.permit: A wilderness permit is required year-round for overnight camping in Boston Basin/Torment Basin (Cross Country Zone). Reserve on Recreation.gov, then pick up in person at the Wilderness Information Center in Marblemount.
