timing.recommendedStart: Early start to cross the Taboo Glacier while the snow is firm, before afternoon softening and rockfall risk increase

waypoints.name: Boston Basin trailhead turnout, Cascade River Road

emergency.sheriffDispatch: Skagit County Sheriff's Office dispatch: 360-336-9450 (or 911)
emergency.rangerStation: North Cascades NP Wilderness Information Center, Marblemount: 360-854-7200; after-hours NPS Communications Center: 360-873-4590 ext. 37
emergency.nearestHospital: Skagit Valley Hospital, Mount Vernon, WA (Level III trauma center) — 360-424-4111
emergency.notes: Cell coverage is unreliable in Boston/Torment Basin; NPS advises not relying on cell phones for evacuation and recommends carrying a satellite communicator. Rescue response can take hours to days given terrain and weather.

routes.bail: Numerous downclimb/rappel options exist along the gully/open-book system below the summit, allowing retreat back toward the notch and Boston Basin at most points on the route.
routes.turnaround: Turn around if deteriorating weather, a slow pace, or an overly soft/complex Taboo Glacier crossing threatens your ability to finish the ridge and get back down (either by continuing the Traverse, descending the SE Face, or reversing the ridge) with a safe daylight margin.
routes.bestSeason: Best climbed June through September once the Taboo Glacier approach has enough consolidated snow to cross easily but before it becomes too broken up; MP notes the peak's rugged terrain and short season keep it quieter than the neighboring Traverse.
routes.comms: No reliable cell service through most of Boston Basin/Torment Basin; NPS notes reception is possible from some higher points but should not be relied on for emergencies — carry a satellite communicator.
routes.itinerary: Typically climbed in a day from a high camp in Boston/Torment Basin: cross the Taboo Glacier at first light, climb the South Ridge to Torment's summit, then descend via the SE Face or by downclimbing/rappelling the ridge back to camp.
routes.descent: Continue the Forbidden Traverse, descend the full SE Face, or downclimb/rappel back down the South Ridge via traverse-y rappels.
routes.descentText: From the summit, parties either continue onto the Torment-Forbidden Traverse (5.6), reverse down the full Southeast Face, or downclimb and make a series of traverse-y rappels back down the South Ridge itself to the notch and the Taboo Glacier, then return to Boston/Torment Basin.
routes.corrections: Mountain Project attributes the South Ridge's first ascent to Cooper & Sellers, 1958 — the same party and year as the Torment-Forbidden Traverse FA. The primary 1958 American Alpine Journal account of that climb describes their line up Torment as the 'southwest and south face' rather than explicitly the 'South Ridge,' so this should be read as the earliest recorded ascent of this general line rather than a confirmed, separately-documented South Ridge first ascent.

blurb: Mount Lago (8,745-8,748 ft depending on source) is the highest summit of the Okanogan Range and the second-highest peak in Washington's Pasayten Wilderness after Jack Mountain, rising above Eureka Creek on the east side of the Cascade crest deep in Okanogan County. The mountain is built of North Cascades metamorphic and granitic rock, capped near its summit by a distinctive band of dark hornfels, and its easiest line is a scree/talus scramble up the south slopes reached only via a remote, 16-plus-mile wilderness approach. It was first climbed in 1933 by Hermann Ulrichs and Dick Alt during a summer campaign that also claimed neighboring Mount Carru; the peak's name honors Forest Service surveyor Lage Wernstedt, who mapped (and by some accounts may have earlier summited) many of the same Pasayten peaks in the mid-1920s.

routes.overview: The standard and easiest line up Mount Lago: an unroped scree-and-talus scramble up the peak's open south-facing slopes to the summit ridge. The climbing itself is straightforward Class 2-3, but the route is defined by the very long, off-trail Pasayten Wilderness approach required to reach its base.
routes.beta: Most parties reach Lago via Slate Pass and the Middle Fork Pasayten River drainage, crossing Freds Pass and upper Eureka Creek into Shellrock Basin or the Lago-Carru col, then climbing directly up the south-facing scree/talus slopes to the summit ridge and following it east to the high point. A steep drop-off and cliff bands guard the north side of the ridge, so route-finding stays on the south-facing slopes throughout. Many parties link Lago with Mount Carru, Osceola Peak, and/or Ptarmigan Peak on a multi-day loop, since the approach effort dwarfs the technical difficulty of any single summit.
routes.rockGrade: Class 2-3 scramble (occasional optional Class 4 on ridge variations)
objHaz: Rockfall/loose rock on scree and talus
Cliff bands on the north side of the summit ridge
Lingering snow/ice on upper slopes into early summer
Whiteout/fog complicating route-finding on featureless scree
Lightning exposure on open alpine terrain
Extremely long, remote approach with no quick evacuation
routes.season: Jul-Sep for a dry scramble; the access road (Harts Pass/FR 5400) is typically snow-closed from roughly December into late June or early July
routes.approach: Drive Harts Pass Road (Forest Road 5400) from the North Cascades Highway (SR-20) near Mazama, past Harts Pass, to the Slate Pass/Buckskin Ridge trailhead (~6,900 ft) near the road's last hairpin turn. From Slate Pass, drop into and descend the Middle Fork Pasayten River valley on good trail, then take the signed Freds Lake Trail (#474) at the Robinson Creek/Berk Creek junction up to Freds Lake and Freds Pass. From there, cross the upper reaches of Eureka Creek into Shellrock Basin (via Shellrock Pass or Rolo Pass) to gain the base of Lago's south slopes, or continue toward the Lago-Carru col. SummitPost describes this as the shortest road-to-peak approach, at roughly 16 miles one-way; a longer ~18-mile alternative starts from the Monument Creek trailhead and climbs via the Carru-Lago col.
routes.descent: Reverse the south-slope scree/talus ascent route back toward Shellrock Basin or the Lago-Carru col; no rappels are needed on the standard line.
routes.descentText: Descend the same broad south-facing scree and talus slopes used on the ascent, staying well back from the cliff bands that fall away on the north side of the ridge. Most parties drop back to a high camp at Shellrock Basin, the ~7,400-ft tarn near the Lago-Carru col, or Freds Lake before retracing the multi-day trail approach out via Freds Pass and the Middle Fork Pasayten River to Slate Pass.
routes.itinerary: A typical trip is 3-4 days: Day 1 hike in from Slate Pass to a camp near Freds Lake or lower Eureka Creek; Day 2 continue to a high camp in Shellrock Basin or at the Lago-Carru col tarn (~7,400 ft); Day 3 climb Lago's south slopes to the summit (often combined with Carru and/or Osceola from the same camp) and return to high camp; Day 4 hike out to Slate Pass.
routes.bail: There is no fixed gear or technical descent to worry about — retreat simply means reversing the ascent slopes back down toward Shellrock Basin/the Lago-Carru col rather than continuing along the ridge toward the cliffy north side in deteriorating weather or visibility.
routes.turnaround: Because the summit slopes offer no shelter and the approach back to camp or the trailhead is long and off-trail, set a turnaround time that leaves ample daylight margin for slow talus/scree travel and deteriorating weather, and be willing to retreat before crossing onto any ridge terrain with cliffs close by in poor visibility.
routes.bestSeason: Best climbed once the Harts Pass access road has opened for the season (typically late June or July) and the scree slopes have shed most of their snow, roughly July through September; several trip reports describe residual snowfields on the upper mountain persisting into late June/early July, and fall (late September-early October) ascents are popular for larch color but coincide with the road's season-ending closure window.
routes.comms: This is one of the most remote standard routes in the Cascades; expect no reliable cell coverage for the vast majority of the approach and climb, and carry a satellite communicator or PLB.
routes.detailedRack: No technical rock rack is used on the standard route — it is an unroped Class 2-3 scramble on scree, talus, and occasional short rock steps. Carry an ice axe and crampons or microspikes for residual snow/ice on the upper slopes, which multiple parties have encountered even in late June and early summer.
routes.proNeeds: None — this is a non-technical scramble with no fixed protection or rappel anchors reported on the standard line.
gear: Topo map, compass, and/or GPS for off-trail navigation
Bear-resistant food storage (canister, Ursack, or proper hang)
whatToBring: Water treatment — water sources are limited once off the main creek drainages
Sun protection for extended time on open slopes
Extra insulating/wind layers for the exposed summit ridge
Satellite communicator or PLB (no cell coverage)
Sturdy boots suited to loose scree and talus
proTips: Most parties combine Mount Lago with neighboring Mount Carru, Osceola Peak, and/or Ptarmigan Peak in a single multi-day loop, since the approach effort is so large relative to any one summit.
A helmet is worth carrying given the loose rock underfoot and overhead on the scree slopes.
Camping at Shellrock Basin, the ~7,400-ft tarn near the Lago-Carru col, or Freds Lake sets up a much shorter summit push than attempting it from the trailhead in a single day.

Confirm current Harts Pass Road (FR 5400) conditions with the Methow Valley Ranger District before driving in — the road has both a routine winter closure and, in the 2025-26 season, an extended flood-damage closure that only reopened July 2, 2026.
Carry an ice axe and traction even for a mid-summer trip; multiple parties have needed them on residual snow on the upper mountain.
watchOut: The north side of the summit ridge drops away in sheer cliffs — trip reports specifically warn against approaching the edge in poor visibility.
Loose talus and scree (one report describes 6-12 inch rock) make for slow, ankle-twisting travel, especially on descent.
Snowfields on the upper slopes can linger into late June/early July and have required crampons and an ice axe outside of winter conditions.
Cloud and fog frequently move into the Eureka Creek/Shellrock Basin area, complicating route-finding on the largely featureless scree.
This is one of the most remote standard routes in the Cascades — an injury or weather event far from any road is a serious, multi-day self-rescue problem.
knownHazards: Cliff bands along the north aspect of the summit ridge.
Rockfall and loose rock across nearly the entire scree/talus route.
Lingering snow and ice on north- and shade-facing sections into early summer.
An extremely long, off-trail wilderness approach (roughly 16-18 miles one-way) with no quick evacuation option.
No cell phone coverage for most of the approach and climb.

pitchDetail.notes: Slate Pass down the Middle Fork Pasayten River, up to Freds Lake/Freds Pass, and across upper Eureka Creek to Shellrock Basin or the Lago-Carru col; ~16 mi one-way (SummitPost's shortest documented approach).
Via the Monument Creek trailhead and the Carru-Lago col; ~18 mi one-way (SummitPost).
Forest and talus rising from Shellrock Basin/Eureka Creek toward the Lago-Carru saddle or directly up Lago's south-facing scree.
Scree and talus with occasional short rock steps; several trip reports note a distinctive dark hornfels band near the summit.
Follow the ridge crest to the true summit, staying clear of cliffs on the north side; a short class-4 step can reportedly be bypassed.

road.name: Harts Pass Road (Forest Road 5400) to Slate Pass
road.status: Seasonal high-clearance gravel/dirt road; closed for extensive flood/storm-damage repairs from winter 2025-26 and reopened July 2, 2026 — confirm current status before a trip
road.seasonalGate: Typically snow-closed roughly December 1 through late June/early July; also closed to trailers June 1 - December 31 year-round
road.driveNote: Narrow, steep, one-lane cliff-edge sections beyond Harts Pass toward Slate Pass and Slate Peak; high-clearance vehicle recommended, not advisable for trailers or drivers uneasy with exposure.

climate.typical: East-of-crest rain-shadow climate — drier and sunnier on average than the western Cascades, but still subject to fast-moving alpine storms, wind, and lingering snow into summer.
climate.spring: Harts Pass Road is normally still snow-closed through spring, making this a non-objective season.
climate.summer: Primary climbing season; scree/talus slopes are typically snow-free by mid-to-late July, though isolated snow patches have been reported into early July.
climate.fall: Clear, cool weather and a popular window for larch color in late September/early October, but the access road can see season-ending snow by late fall.
climate.winter: Road closed by snow; the peak has been reached in winter only via a multi-day ski/snowmobile expedition from a sno-park.

access.landManager: Okanogan-Wenatchee National Forest, Methow Valley Ranger District
access.fees: No wilderness permit fee; a Northwest Forest Pass or America the Beautiful pass may be required for parking at some trailheads.
access.permit: Free, non-quota, self-issued wilderness permit available at the trailhead.
access.passRequired: Northwest Forest Pass / America the Beautiful Pass at trailheads that require one.
access.closures: Harts Pass Road (FR 5400) has a standing summer trailer restriction, a typical winter closure (roughly December-June), and was closed for extensive flood-damage repairs from December 2025 until reopening July 2, 2026 — verify current conditions with the Methow Valley Ranger District (509-996-4000) before departing.

waypoints.name: Mount Carru summit (adjacent peak, near the Lago-Carru col used on approach/descent)

emergency.sheriffDispatch: Okanogan County Sheriff's Office dispatch: (509) 422-7232 (non-emergency); dial 911 for emergencies
emergency.rangerStation: Methow Valley Ranger District, 24 West Chewuch Road, Winthrop, WA 98862 — (509) 996-4000
emergency.nearestHospital: Three Rivers Hospital, Brewster, WA (509-689-2517) or Mid-Valley Hospital, Omak, WA (509-826-1760) — both roughly 60+ road miles from the Harts Pass area, in addition to the multi-day wilderness approach out
emergency.notes: Okanogan County Search & Rescue is an all-volunteer team that responds only when tasked by the Sheriff's Office; this is a very remote part of the Pasayten Wilderness with no cell coverage, so a satellite communicator/PLB and a filed trip plan are strongly advised.

blurb: Johannesburg Mountain (8,200+ ft) is a dramatic granite/gneiss peak in the North Cascades of Skagit County, Washington, rising directly above the Cascade River near the end of the Cascade River Road. Its Northeast Face is one of the most striking escarpments in the range, dropping roughly 5,000 feet in under a mile and hung with active glaciers that make every route on the peak subject to serious rockfall and icefall. The mountain was first climbed July 26, 1938 by Calder Bressler, Bill Cox, Ray W. Clough, and Tom Myers via the East Ridge/Cascade-Johannesburg Couloir; its harder technical lines on the Northeast Buttress/Rib and Northeast Ridge followed in 1951, 1957, and 1963, and the peak still sees only a handful of ascents per year.

routes.fa: 1951 (left/original rib, Tom Miller and partner); Western Rib (right-hand) variation added 1957 — the two lines together are climbed today as "the Northeast Buttress"
routes.iceGrade: AI2 (short ice step high on the route, when present)
routes.rappels: Up to 7 rappels on East Ridge descent (extra cord/webbing needed to back up anchors)
objHaz: icefall from hanging glaciers above the northeast face
routes.overview: A long, sustained line up Johannesburg's massive Northeast Buttress, combining brush, heather, low-5th-class rock and a summit snow arete over nearly 5,000 continuous feet directly above the Cascade Pass parking lot — one of the biggest, least-relenting moderate alpine routes in the range.
routes.beta: Often described as by far the hardest sustained 3rd/4th-class terrain in the Cascades, the buttress climbs almost the full 5,000 ft of relief from the valley floor to the summit with little easy ground. Modern parties typically follow a blend of the 1951 (left rib) and 1957 (right/Western rib) lines through a loose "chossy" start, vegetated slabs, a 5.5-5.8 chimney/slab pitch (common bivy at ~7,100 ft nearby), and a final steep snow arete, occasionally with a short ice pitch, to the summit.
routes.approach: From the Cascade Pass trailhead parking lot, walk back down the road roughly 0.2 miles, scramble down a dirt embankment, ford the outlet stream/Cascade River, and cross talus, moraine and a snowfield to gain the base of the buttress just south of the main Cascade-Johannesburg Couloir — about 20-30 minutes from the car.
routes.descent: Standard descent is "Doug's Direct": traverse beneath the south faces of Cascade Peak and the Triplets, then climb the North Ridge of Mixup Peak to reach Cache Glacier and the Cascade Pass trail, roughly 4-6 hours back to the parking lot. Some parties instead rappel the East Ridge (up to 7 raps) down to the Cascade-Johannesburg col and descend the couloir.
routes.descentText: From the summit, parties down-climb and/or rappel the upper ridge (up to seven rappels, backing up marginal anchors with extra cord) toward the Cascade-Johannesburg col. From the ridge/col area, sidehill across steep 45-60° wildflower and heather slopes — the most dangerous section, with serious fall consequences and poor protection — gain roughly 1,600 ft up Mixup Peak's north ridge, cross Cache Glacier, and rejoin the Cascade Pass trail back to the parking lot. Total descent commonly runs 4-6+ hours and this stage has produced multiple benightments.
routes.itinerary: Strong parties do it car-to-car in a single long day (roughly 11-15 hours), starting by 4-5 AM. Slower parties bivy on a ledge near 7,100 ft below the crux chimney and finish plus descend the following day.
routes.bail: Once above the lower ~3,000 ft of brush and heather, retreat is difficult and the terrain is loose; most parties commit to reaching the ridge crest or summit rather than reversing the buttress, then descend via Doug's Direct or the East Ridge rappels.
routes.turnaround: Set a firm turnaround time before committing above the lower buttress — down-climbing this loose, sustained terrain is slow, and the standard descent (Doug's Direct) itself takes 4-6+ hours across exposed heather, so parties need enough daylight remaining to finish the climb and complete the descent before dark.
routes.bestSeason: Late June through September, once the road is open and the couloir/heather approach has melted out enough to be climbable; conditions vary significantly year to year with the health of the hanging glaciers, so recent trip reports should be checked before committing.
routes.comms: Cell coverage in the Cascade Pass/Johannesburg cirque is unreliable; NPS advises never relying on a phone as a sole evacuation plan, though reception is occasionally possible from higher, more open terrain.
routes.detailedRack: A light alpine rack to about a #2 Camelot, a handful of knifeblade pitons for the loose 5.5 start where cam/nut placements are scarce, 2-3 ice screws and 1-2 snow pickets for the summit snow arete/ice step, plus a 60m rope, helmet, ice axe and crampons.
routes.proNeeds: Protection is thin and marginal for much of the route (loose rock, vegetation belays); come prepared to run it out and to back up rappel anchors on the descent with extra cord/webbing.
gear: light alpine rock rack to #2 Camelot
whatToBring: bivy gear if planning two days
plenty of water (little available on route)
proTips: Start at first light or earlier — car-to-car parties commonly leave the trailhead by 4-5 AM to be off the loose lower buttress and heather before afternoon thunderstorms build.
Carry a few knifeblade pitons for the loose 5.5 start section where cam/nut placements are scarce.
The bivy ledge near 7,100 ft below the crux chimney pitch is the standard overnight stop for two-day parties.
watchOut: The lower 3,000 feet is officially only 3rd/4th class but is sustained, loose, and largely unprotected — a fall here would be serious.
Vegetation holds (heather, moss, alder) fail without warning; test everything.
The final snow arete drops away steeply on both sides with minimal protection.
knownHazards: Rockfall and occasional icefall from the hanging glaciers above the northeast face.
Loose, rotten rock throughout much of the route limits protection quality.
Moats and snow bridges near the summit can be unstable late season.

pitchDetail.notes: Chossy/loose slabs; knifeblade pitons useful for protection.
Tree and brush climbing through dense vegetation.
Marginal protection roughly every 200 feet.
Common bivy site at ~7,100 ft near this section.
Steep, drops away on both sides; crampons required.
Roughly 200 ft when present; 2-3 ice screws.
Final scramble to the true summit.

road.name: Cascade River Road
road.status: Paved to mile 10, then narrow gravel to the Cascade Pass trailhead at mile 23.1
road.seasonalGate: Gated in winter; typically opens to the Cascade Pass trailhead by late June, sometimes not until early July after heavy snow years
road.driveNote: From SR 20 at Marblemount, drive 23.1 miles (~1 hr) up Cascade River Road to the trailhead parking lot beneath Johannesburg Mountain; vehicles over 22 ft long/8 ft wide or towing trailers are prohibited within the park boundary.

climate.forecastZone: NWS/NWAC "West Slopes North Cascades and Passes" zone (WAZ567)
climate.typical: Wet, snowy winters and warmer, drier summers typical of the west-slope North Cascades; the Johannesburg cirque holds hanging glaciers year-round, so cold microclimates and active icefall persist even in summer.
climate.spring: Deep snowpack and the access road often still gated; avalanche and wet-slide hazard is high.
climate.summer: Primary climbing season (Jul-Sep); frequent afternoon thunderstorms and ongoing icefall off the hanging glaciers.
climate.fall: Shortening weather windows with early-season snow possible by late September/October.
climate.winter: Road closed at a lower gate; extreme avalanche and icefall hazard, essentially unclimbed in this season.

access.landManager: North Cascades National Park (NPS); lower Cascade River Road is Mt. Baker-Snoqualmie National Forest
access.fees: No park entrance fee; Northwest Forest Pass/America the Beautiful Pass required for parking on the National Forest segment of the road
access.permit: Overnight backcountry/climbing permit mandatory year-round, obtained via recreation.gov or the Wilderness Information Center in Marblemount
access.passRequired: Northwest Forest Pass or America the Beautiful Pass
access.closures: Cascade River Road is gated in winter; also subject to temporary washout or wildfire closures — check the current NPS road-conditions page before driving up.

timing.recommendedStart: Pre-dawn, 4-5 AM trailhead departure

emergency.sheriffDispatch: Skagit County Sheriff dispatch (360) 336-9450; North Cascades NP dispatch (360) 856-5700 ext. 515, after-hours (360) 873-4590 ext. 37
emergency.rangerStation: North Cascades National Park Wilderness Information Center, Marblemount, WA
emergency.nearestHospital: Skagit Valley Hospital, 300 Hospital Pkwy, Mount Vernon, WA — Level III ED, (360) 424-4111
emergency.notes: Cell coverage is unreliable in the Cascade Pass/Johannesburg cirque; rescue response can take hours given the remote, technical terrain.

routes.fa: July 26, 1938 — Calder Bressler, Bill Cox, Ray W. Clough, Tom Myers (first ascent of the mountain, via this line)
routes.rockGrade: 3rd-4th class on the East Ridge (mossy, broken gneiss), scattered low 5th class
routes.iceGrade: AI2 (short water-ice bottleneck in the couloir)
routes.rappels: Variable; several rappels (2-3+) commonly used descending the upper ridge/east face, backed up where anchors are marginal
objHaz: icefall from the Sill Glacier/hanging glaciers overhanging the couloir
routes.season: Jun-Sep; the couloir climbs best pre-dawn/early season when snow and ice are firm and rockfall/icefall risk is lower
routes.overview: The mountain's original and most popular technical line: a steep snow/ice couloir splitting the peak from neighboring Cascade Peak, gained directly from the Cascade Pass trailhead, followed by the loose, broken East Ridge to the summit.
routes.beta: One of the shorter approaches in the range but with serious objective hazard: the couloir sits beneath overhanging seracs of the Sill Glacier, so most parties climb it in the dark to catch firm snow/ice and minimize rockfall/icefall exposure. A roughly 45-ft belayed water-ice step narrows the couloir to 20-50 ft wide where it steepens to about 50°. Above the Cascade-Johannesburg col, the East Ridge continues about 1,400 ft on mossy, broken gneiss with a mix of 3rd-4th class rock, gullies and ribs to the summit; rock quality is best low on the ridge and deteriorates higher.
routes.approach: From the Cascade Pass trailhead (~3,600 ft) at the end of Cascade River Road, walk back down the road about 0.2-0.3 mile, descend a dirt embankment to the valley floor, and ford the outlet creek/Cascade River (best crossed via a faint climbers' trail on the south side of the creek to minimize brush). Cross talus and the terminal moraine to the base of the hanging snowfield beneath the Cascade-Johannesburg col — one of the shortest approaches in the Cascades, roughly 45-60 minutes to the base of the couloir. Some parties instead use a longer approach via the standard Cascade Pass trail, Cache Glacier, and Mixup Peak's north ridge ("Doug's Direct") to reach the col from the west side, avoiding the couloir's rockfall/icefall exposure at the cost of extra distance (up to ~14 miles round trip via that variation).
routes.descent: Most parties reverse the route: down-climb/rappel the East Ridge back to the Cascade-Johannesburg col, then down-climb the couloir (best done early while still firm). Others exit via "Doug's Direct" — traversing beneath the south faces of Cascade Peak and the Triplets and up over Mixup Peak's north ridge to Cache Glacier and the Cascade Pass trail — which avoids re-exposure to the couloir's icefall but takes roughly 4-6 hours back to the parking lot.
routes.descentText: From the true summit, down-climb/scramble to the false summit and follow class 2-3 south-face ledges for roughly 1-1.5 hours back toward the ridge crest. Several rappels are typically made off pinnacles and through a heather gully near the ridge crest — there are multiple similar-looking gullies, and misidentifying the correct one has led to benighted parties. A final rappel onto moderate-angle slabs leads back down toward the Cascade-Johannesburg col. From the col, either reverse the couloir (safest in early morning) or take "Doug's Direct": sidehill across 45-60° heather and wildflower slopes (the most hazardous section), gain roughly 1,600 ft up Mixup Peak's north ridge, cross Cache Glacier, and rejoin the Cascade Pass trail back to the parking lot.
routes.itinerary: Typically a single long day: alpine start (1-3 AM) up the couloir, reaching the col around dawn, then the ridge to the summit by mid-morning. Fast parties climbing and descending the couloir directly report roughly 6 hours car-to-summit and 8 hours car-to-car; parties using the Cache Glacier approach and Doug's Direct descent should budget a full day (up to ~14 miles round trip).
routes.bail: Below the col, retreat down the couloir is possible but exposes climbers to the same icefall danger as the ascent, so it's safest done early/pre-dawn. Above the col on the East Ridge, fixed rappel slings at several points allow retreat back toward the col.
routes.turnaround: Plan to be back at or below the Cascade-Johannesburg col by midday — afternoon sun destabilizes the Sill Glacier icefall and softens the couloir snow, sharply increasing rockfall/icefall danger later in the day.
routes.bestSeason: Late June through September; the couloir is more heavily crevasse-bridged and easier early season but carries greater rock/ice-fall risk, while late-season ascents find more open crevasses and firmer, more predictable ice.
routes.comms: Cell coverage in the Cascade Pass/Johannesburg cirque is unreliable; NPS advises never relying on a phone as a sole evacuation plan, though reception is occasionally possible from higher, more open terrain.
routes.detailedRack: Ice tools (one or two per climber) and crampons for the couloir, about 3 ice screws and one snow picket per climber for the bottleneck/upper snowfield, a single 60m rope, a light rock rack (small-to-medium cams/nuts) and rock shoes for the ridge, plus a helmet.
routes.proNeeds: Protection is mostly snow/ice gear low on the route (screws, pickets) and a light rack on the upper ridge where rock quality is only fair to poor; carry gear for both.
whatToBring: extra layers for pre-dawn cold in the couloir
approach shoes to swap at the col
proTips: Climb the couloir at night or pre-dawn (parties commonly start 1-3 AM) so the Sill Glacier icefall above is still frozen and less prone to shedding.
Some parties bypass the lower couloir's rockfall exposure entirely by approaching via Cache Glacier and Mixup Notch to reach the Cascade-Johannesburg col directly.
Plan to be back at or below the col by midday to avoid afternoon rockfall/icefall as the sun softens the glacier.
watchOut: The couloir sits directly beneath overhanging seracs of the Sill Glacier — rockfall and icefall are the defining hazard of this route.
The bottleneck section narrows to 20-50 ft with a 50-degree slope and a belayed water-ice step.
The East Ridge has several similar-looking descent gullies; misidentifying the correct one has caused benightments.
knownHazards: Continuous rockfall/icefall danger from hanging glaciers overhanging the couloir.
Crevasses and moated snow on the couloir and upper snowfields.
Loose, mossy gneiss on the ridge limits protection quality.

pitchDetail.notes: Crevasse negotiation; climb pre-dawn for firm conditions.
~45 ft belayed water ice; couloir narrows to 20-50 ft, slope to ~50°.
Continues to the Cascade-Johannesburg col.
Mossy, broken gneiss; best rock quality is low on the ridge.
Several fixed rappel slings; route-finding among similar gullies.
Final short pitch to the true summit.

routes.fa: 1963 (first-ascent party not documented in available sources)
objHaz: icefall from hanging glaciers on the peak's northeast side
routes.season: Summer, when the low couloir approach still holds climbable snow
routes.overview: A more direct, all-rock alternative to the neighboring East Ridge and Northeast Buttress lines: climbs partway up the Cascade-Johannesburg Couloir to a hanging glacier, then follows the leftmost rib to the summit, deliberately avoiding the brush that plagues the other routes.
routes.beta: Described by the route's documenters with the instruction "AND NO BUSH CLIMBING!", this 12-pitch, roughly 4,000-ft line climbs partway up the C-J Couloir to the hanging glacier on the right, then ascends the leftmost rib on the northeast side of the mountain to the summit — a steep-snow-and-rock line graded 5.7+ / Alpine IV.
routes.approach: Shares the Cascade Pass trailhead approach used for the Cascade-Johannesburg Couloir: descend to and ford the Cascade River/outlet creek, then ascend talus and snow toward the hanging glacier below the Cascade-Johannesburg col, climbing partway up the couloir before cutting onto the route's leftmost rib.
routes.descent: Not specifically documented for this route; climbers on Johannesburg's other technical lines typically descend via "Doug's Direct" (traversing beneath Cascade Peak/the Triplets and over Mixup Peak's north ridge to Cache Glacier) or by rappelling the East Ridge back to the Cascade-Johannesburg col.
routes.bail: Retreat from the upper rib is difficult on loose rock; parties are effectively committed once established above the C-J Couloir, generally continuing to the summit and descending via Doug's Direct or the East Ridge rappels.
routes.turnaround: As with the mountain's other technical routes, set a firm turnaround time before committing above the couloir — the loose upper rib and long descent leave little margin if benighted.
routes.bestSeason: Late June through September, once the road is open and the lower couloir approach has consolidated snow.
routes.comms: Cell coverage in the Cascade Pass/Johannesburg cirque is unreliable; NPS advises never relying on a phone as a sole evacuation plan, though reception is occasionally possible from higher, more open terrain.
routes.detailedRack: A small-to-medium alpine rock rack up to about 3 inches, 10+ runners/slings, plus ice axe and crampons for the couloir/glacier portion of the approach.
routes.proNeeds: Standard alpine rock rack to 3 inches with plenty of runners for the 12-pitch rib; supplement with snow/ice protection for the lower couloir approach.
gear: small-medium alpine rock rack to 3 inches
ice axe and crampons for the couloir/glacier approach
whatToBring: light bivy gear as a contingency
proTips: The route stays on more solid, direct rock and avoids the brush of the neighboring Northeast Buttress lines by climbing partway up the C-J Couloir before cutting onto the leftmost rib.
watchOut: Like all of Johannesburg's northeast-side routes, the lower section shares exposure to icefall from the hanging glaciers above the couloir.
knownHazards: Rockfall and icefall hazard shared with the adjacent Cascade-Johannesburg Couloir on the lower approach.

emergency.sheriffDispatch: Skagit County Sheriff dispatch (360) 336-9450; North Cascades NP dispatch (360) 856-5700 ext. 515, after-hours (360) 873-4590 ext. 37
emergency.rangerStation: North Cascades National Park Wilderness Information Center, Marblemount, WA
emergency.nearestHospital: Skagit Valley Hospital, 300 Hospital Pkwy, Mount Vernon, WA — Level III ED, (360) 424-4111
emergency.notes: Cell coverage is unreliable in the Cascade Pass/Johannesburg cirque; rescue response can take hours given the remote, technical terrain.

blurb: Sharkfin Tower is an 8,120+ ft granite spire in North Cascades National Park's Boston Basin, perched midway on the connecting ridge between Forbidden Peak and Boston Peak, with the Quien Sabe Glacier below its south side and the Boston Glacier below its north side. Its solid alpine granite is widely regarded as among the best rock in Washington State. Two established routes exist: the moderate Southeast Ridge (5.0), first climbed August 1947 by Wesley Grande, Jay Todd, and Joseph Vance, and the harder two-pitch Southeast Face (5.10-), first climbed September 1990 by Jim Nelson, Jim Martin, Kit Lewis, Bill Liddell, and Dave Bale.

routes.overview: A short, classic low-fifth-class ridge scramble/climb up moderate, solid granite directly to the summit — the standard, popular line on the tower.
routes.beta: From the notch at the base of the rock, the route climbs near or directly on the crest of the Southeast Ridge for three short pitches of low fifth-class granite, with exposure increasing near the top; the final section eases to 4th/easy-5th-class scrambling to the summit block. Rated 5.0 overall but with a couple of stiffer, more exposed moves that several parties describe as feeling closer to 5.4.
routes.approach: From the Boston Basin trailhead (~3,200 ft, a small pullout on Cascade River Road roughly 21.7 miles from Marblemount, just past mile marker 22 opposite Johannesburg Mountain), follow an abandoned mining road for about a mile, then the steep Boston Basin climbers' trail (~3.2 mi, ~2,500 ft gain) to the low camp (~5,300-5,700 ft) or high camp (~6,200-6,400 ft). From camp, follow the standard Quien Sabe Glacier approach used for Sahale Peak: skirt climber's left of the glacier until gaining it relatively high, then traverse climber's right until directly beneath Sharkfin Tower. A gully leads from the glacier up to a sloped terrace and the notch at the base of the ridge — snow-filled and requiring axe/crampons early season, loose 3rd/4th-class scree and rock by late summer. Correctly identifying this gully is critical: a 2005 accident on the tower stemmed in part from a party ascending the wrong gully.
routes.descent: Downclimb/rappel the Southeast Ridge back to the notch, then reverse the approach gully down to the glacier.
routes.descentText: From the summit, scramble back down to the top of Pitch 2, make a rappel down that pitch, then scramble over to a large block above the notch and make a second rappel to the notch. A single 60m rope is sufficient for both rappels. From the notch, reverse the approach gully back down onto the Quien Sabe Glacier and traverse back to camp; loose rock in the gully is more pronounced late-season.
routes.itinerary: Typical 2-3 day plan: Day 1 - hike the Boston Basin Trail (~3.2 mi, ~2,500 ft gain) from the trailhead (~3,200 ft) to low camp (~5,700 ft) or high camp (~6,200-6,400 ft); Day 2 - pre-dawn start across the Quien Sabe Glacier to the base of Sharkfin Tower, climb the Southeast Ridge, and return to camp (strong parties sometimes combine this with Sahale Peak via the shared glacier approach); Day 3 - hike out.
routes.bail: Retreat by downclimbing/rappelling back to the notch from any stance on the ridge; from the notch the approach gully leads back down to the Quien Sabe Glacier and camp.
routes.turnaround: Because the route sits above a glacier approach with real crevasse hazard and a gully that has been fatally misidentified before, turn around if the correct approach gully cannot be positively confirmed, if the party is not moving efficiently enough to be off the glacier and past the creek crossings before afternoon meltwater peaks, or if weather is closing in — the ridge is fully exposed with no quick escape once established on the rock.
routes.bestSeason: Prime conditions run roughly July through September, after the approach gully has melted out enough for 3rd/4th-class scrambling rather than steep snow; the Quien Sabe Glacier crossing is most straightforward in mid-to-late summer, and creek crossings on the Boston Basin approach are easiest earlier in the day before afternoon meltwater peaks.
routes.comms: Cell coverage in Boston Basin and on Sharkfin Tower is unreliable and inconsistent, as it is through most of North Cascades National Park backcountry. The park's own dispatch line, (360) 873-4500 ext. 37, is generally faster to reach than 911 or the county sheriff if a signal is found; a satellite communicator is recommended given the marginal coverage.
routes.rappels: 2 rappels (top of P2, then near the notch) using a single 60m rope
objHaz: rockfall / loose rock in the approach gully (worse late-season)
crevasses on the Quien Sabe Glacier approach
afternoon meltwater raising creek-crossing difficulty on the hike in
wrong-gully route-finding error (fatal 2005 incident on this peak)
routes.fa: Wesley Grande, Jay Todd, Joseph Vance (August 1947)
routes.detailedRack: Light alpine rack: a set of nuts and cams up to about 2.5 inches, a handful of slings/runners, 2 locking carabiners for building rappel anchors, and a single 60m rope for the two rappels back to the notch.
routes.proNeeds: Protection is generally good on solid granite; a light rack to 2.5" covers the crack systems on P1-P2. The exposed P2, though rated 5.0, feels stiffer to many parties and still takes good gear when placed carefully.
whatToBring: bear canister (required in Boston Basin zone Jun 1-Nov 15)
overnight camp gear (tent, sleeping bag, stove) for low/high camp
extra layers for the exposed ridge
proTips: The route is rated 5.0 overall but has a couple of moves several parties grade closer to 5.4 — don't be surprised by the exposure on P2.
Fit parties camped in Boston Basin sometimes combine this with Sahale Peak via the shared Quien Sabe Glacier approach.
Consider the loose-rock/scree state of the approach gully by season: snow and an axe help early season, but late-season scree underfoot needs care.
watchOut: Positively identify the correct approach gully before committing — a 2005 party's wrong-gully ascent contributed to a fatal accident on this peak.
Do not build anchors on detached or loose-looking blocks; a dislodged boulder killed three climbers on Sharkfin Tower in 2005.
Afternoon meltwater can make Boston Creek crossings on the approach markedly harder later in the day.
knownHazards: Loose rock and rockfall in the approach gully, worsening as it melts out through the season.
Crevasse hazard while crossing the upper Quien Sabe Glacier en route to the tower.
A 2005 anchor-failure accident (a boulder dislodged by climber weight) killed three members of a Mountaineers party on this peak, the park's worst multi-fatality climbing incident at the time.
Frequent bear activity at Boston Basin camps; food storage canisters are required.

pitchDetail.notes: From the notch, skirt the ridge to climber's right on large blocks, then follow a short dihedral up to the ridge crest and belay.
Follow solid rock on the ridge crest proper with good gear; notably exposed position.
Scramble at or slightly right of the ridge crest to the summit.

road.name: Cascade River Road
road.status: Open (seasonal; gravel and narrow past roughly mile 10)
road.seasonalGate: Gated in winter; opening date each spring depends on snowpack and washout repairs
road.driveNote: From Marblemount, drive about 21.7 miles on Cascade River Road (paved to about mile 10, then gravel with potholes/washboard) to a small pullout at roughly 3,200 ft, just past mile marker 22 and directly opposite Johannesburg Mountain. Unsuitable for sedans, low-clearance vehicles, RVs, or vehicles over 22 ft. The road has had washouts near Eldorado Creek in recent years requiring periodic closures/repairs — check current NPS road status before driving.

climate.forecastZone: NWAC West Slopes North (North Cascades)
climate.typical: Wet, maritime-influenced winters and springs with deep snowpack; drier, more stable summers.
climate.spring: Heavy snowpack, elevated avalanche and creek-crossing hazard; glacier travel favors firm early-morning snow.
climate.summer: Prime climbing window (Jul-Sep); afternoon thunderstorm risk, still-present glacier crevasses, and bug activity at camp.
climate.fall: Shortening weather windows and possible early snow, but fewer crowds.
climate.winter: Heavy snow; the access road is typically gated/inaccessible.

access.landManager: North Cascades National Park (National Park Service)
access.fees: No park entrance fee; an overnight backcountry/wilderness permit costs $10 per person plus a $6 non-refundable reservation fee if booked in advance on Recreation.gov.
access.permit: A backcountry/wilderness permit is required for any overnight stay in Boston Basin, obtained at the Wilderness Information Center in Marblemount or via Recreation.gov (about 60% of permits reservable online, 40% held for walk-ups). Boston Basin is a high-demand, quota-limited cross-country zone that can sell out on summer weekends.
access.closures: Camping is restricted to the designated low camp (~5,300-5,700 ft) and high camp (~6,200-6,400 ft) sites; approved bear canisters are required in the Boston Basin zone June 1-November 15.

timing.recommendedStart: Pre-dawn (headlamp) departure from camp to cross the Quien Sabe Glacier and complete the climb before afternoon thunderstorms or softening snow.

emergency.sheriffDispatch: 911 for emergencies; Skagit County Sheriff non-emergency (360) 416-1911
emergency.rangerStation: North Cascades National Park Dispatch (360) 873-4500 ext. 37; Wilderness Information Center, Marblemount (360) 854-7245
emergency.nearestHospital: PeaceHealth United General Medical Center, Sedro-Woolley (360) 856-6021 (nearest); Skagit Valley Hospital (Level III trauma center), Mount Vernon (360) 424-4111
emergency.notes: Cell coverage in Boston Basin is unreliable; rescues typically require NPS climbing rangers and/or helicopter response. State your exact location and callback number early in any call in case the connection drops.

routes.overview: A short, sustained two-pitch face route just left of the Southeast Ridge's arete, on solid granite with a fun, exposed position and mostly thin, sparse protection.
routes.beta: Pitch 1 steps onto a detached block, then works up a widening finger-to-ring-lock seam that zig-zags just left of the arete to a two-bolt anchor in a notch (no chains/rings). Pitch 2 traverses left from the belay past two bolts, then follows a 'choose your own adventure' line up the face, with gear mostly limited to thin cracks and horizontal seams; the upper section has some lichen.
routes.approach: From the Boston Basin trailhead (~3,200 ft, a pullout on Cascade River Road roughly 21.7 miles from Marblemount, just past mile marker 22 opposite Johannesburg Mountain), follow the climbers' trail (~3.2 mi, ~2,500 ft gain) to the low camp (~5,300-5,700 ft) or high camp (~6,200-6,400 ft). From camp, follow the Quien Sabe Glacier approach used for Sahale Peak, skirting climber's left of the glacier until gaining it relatively high, then traversing climber's right until directly below the tower. A gully leads from the glacier up to a sloped terrace beneath the face — snow-filled early season, loose scree by late season but manageable.
routes.descent: Rappel the route (two 75-ft rappels) to the notch, then reverse the Southeast Ridge/approach-gully descent to the glacier.
routes.descentText: From the top of the Southeast Face, make two 75-foot rappels from fixed anchors down the route to the notch (the P1 two-bolt anchor lacks rings/chains, so back it up or plan to leave gear/a sling). From the notch, descend as for the Southeast Ridge: down the approach gully to the Quien Sabe Glacier and back to camp; a single rope of at least 150 ft (a 60m works for the individual raps) is needed.
routes.itinerary: Typical 2-3 day plan: Day 1 - hike in to low or high camp in Boston Basin; Day 2 - cross the Quien Sabe Glacier at first light, climb the Southeast Face, rappel back to the notch and return to camp; Day 3 - hike out.
routes.bail: From either pitch, rappel to the P1 bolted anchor and then to the notch; the P1 anchor has no chains/rings, so be prepared to leave a nut, sling, or rap ring to retreat.
routes.turnaround: Because the approach crosses glacier terrain with crevasse hazard and the descent gully has been fatally misidentified by a party on this peak before, turn back if the correct gully/notch cannot be confirmed, if afternoon storms are building, or if the party can't be off the face and back at the notch with margin before dark.
routes.bestSeason: Prime conditions run roughly July through September, once the approach gully is largely snow-free; earlier-season ascents should expect a snow-filled gully requiring an axe and crampons.
routes.comms: Cell coverage in Boston Basin and on Sharkfin Tower is unreliable and inconsistent, as through most of North Cascades National Park backcountry. The park dispatch line, (360) 873-4500 ext. 37, is generally faster to reach than 911 or the county sheriff if a signal is found; a satellite communicator is recommended.
routes.rappels: 2 rappels of 75 ft from a fixed anchor at the top of the route
objHaz: rockfall / loose rock in the approach gully (worse late-season)
crevasses on the Quien Sabe Glacier approach
unhangered/no-chain fixed anchors requiring backup gear
wrong-gully route-finding error (fatal 2005 incident on this peak)
routes.fa: Jim Nelson, Jim Martin, Kit Lewis, Bill Liddell, Dave Bale (September 1990)
routes.detailedRack: Single light rack to about 2 inches (thin-to-mid cams and small nuts) plus extra slings; the route relies on four fixed bolts (two at the P1 belay/notch anchor, which has no chains or rings, and two on the P2 face traverse) supplemented by natural gear in seams and horizontal breaks.
routes.proNeeds: Good protection low on P1 once the seam widens past fingers to ring-locks, but sparse and thin higher on P2's face — look for horizontal seams and thin cracks. The P1 bolted anchor lacks chains/rings, so plan to leave a nut or sling to retreat, and the unhangered bolt at the P1 base needs a supplemental gear anchor for the belayer.
gear: single light rack to 2" (thin-to-mid cams, small nuts)
whatToBring: bear canister (required in Boston Basin zone Jun 1-Nov 15)
overnight camp gear for low/high camp
a rap ring or two/spare gear to back up or replace the chainless P1 anchor
proTips: The P1 anchor is 2 bolts with no chains or rings — bring a rap ring, quicklink, or be prepared to leave a biner/sling.
There's an unhangered bolt at the base of P1; the belayer should build a supplemental gear anchor rather than rely on it alone.
Solid granite and a great, exposed position make this a highly regarded moderate-hard outing despite its short length.
watchOut: Do not rely solely on the P1 two-bolt anchor for rappelling without a backup — it has no chains or rings.
Protection thins out significantly on the P2 face traverse; be comfortable running it out between horizontal seams.
Lichen on the upper part of P2 can make holds slicker than they look.
Positively identify the correct approach gully — a 2005 wrong-gully ascent contributed to a fatal accident on this peak.
knownHazards: Loose rock and rockfall in the approach gully, worsening as the season progresses.
Crevasse hazard on the upper Quien Sabe Glacier during the approach.
A 2005 anchor-failure accident (a dislodged boulder) killed three climbers on Sharkfin Tower, underscoring the need for solid anchor-building anywhere on the peak.
Frequent bear activity at Boston Basin camps; food storage canisters are required.

pitchDetail.notes: Step onto a detached block, then work up a widening seam (fingers to ring-locks) zig-zagging just left of the arete to a 2-bolt anchor in a notch (no chains/rings); a bolt without a hanger sits at the base — build a gear anchor for the belayer.
From the bolted belay, traverse left past two bolts, then choose your own line up the face; gear is sparse and best found in thin cracks/horizontal seams; some lichen on the upper section.
