blurb: Chianti Spire (~8,400 ft) is one of four granite pinnacles collectively known as the Wine Spires — with Chablis, Pernod, and Burgundy — strung along Silver Star Mountain's north ridge above Washington Pass in the North Cascades. Its clean granite was first climbed via the north face by Joe Hieb and Art Maki in September 1952, during a season of first ascents on the Wine Spires by Beckey-era Cascades climbers. The spire's modern classic, the East Face (Rebel Yell), was put up in 1986 by Mark Bebie and Jim Nelson and is now regarded as one of the finest moderate alpine crack climbs at Washington Pass.

routes.overview: A striking 6-pitch, roughly 800-foot splitter crack line up the East Face of Chianti Spire, widely regarded as one of the best moderate alpine rock climbs at Washington Pass — nearly every pitch offers a different crack size, from thin liebacks to full-body offwidth, capped by an airy, exposed summit block.
routes.beta: Rebel Yell (also called East Face) climbs continuous, high-quality granite cracks up Chianti Spire's east aspect. MountainProject documents it as 6 pitches/800 ft (III 5.10); guidebook-style sources (Steph Abegg, NC Mountain Guides, Wenatchee Outdoors) describe a 7-pitch version because of an optional harder start. Two start options exist: a wider, easier right start (5.8-5.9), and a thinner, harder left start up a clean corner/chimney (5.10b) that rejoins the main line by the second pitch. The crux pitch follows a splitter crack (0.75"-4") through thin hands/fingers before widening toward an offwidth; the route finishes atop a small, exposed summit block reached by 5.7 moves.
routes.fa: Mark Bebie and Jim Nelson, 1986 (first ascent of this East Face/Rebel Yell line; note the peak itself was first climbed via its north face by Joe Hieb and Art Maki in 1952 — a different route)
routes.rappels: 4 double-rope rappels on fixed anchors, using two 60m ropes (roughly 30-40 min), staying generally to climber's right of the route
objHaz: Loose, eroded ('kitty litter') scree/talus on the upper approach trail to Burgundy Col
Rockfall/icefall potential on the snowy traverse beneath Burgundy Spire's East Face
Persistent snow on the col traverse can require an ice axe well into summer
Afternoon thunderstorms, common in North Cascades summers, are a lightning/exposure hazard high on the spire
Offwidth/wide-crack terrain on the crux pitch raises fall consequence
routes.approach: From SR-20 (North Cascades Highway), park at the informal pullout on the north side of the highway roughly 4 miles east of Washington Pass, about 0.7 mile past the Cutthroat Lake Road turnoff (near milepost 166), just east of the hairpin turn. Descend through talus/timber to cross the creek on logs, then pick up the steep climbers' trail on the timbered rib that switchbacks up toward Burgundy Col, passing a flat bench (roughly 6,400-6,500 ft) that makes a good campsite. Above the bench the trail becomes loose, eroded Class 2 terrain up to Burgundy Col (reported between about 7,720 and 7,950 ft across different sources). From the col, descend a short snow step and traverse south beneath Burgundy Spire's East Face, crossing a small snowy rib, to reach the obvious east side of Chianti Spire and the base of the route. Total time to the col/route area is commonly reported as 3-4 hours.
routes.descent: Rappel the route: 4 double-rope raps on fixed anchors using two 60m ropes, generally down and to climber's right.
routes.descentText: From the summit block, downclimb/scramble to the first rappel station and retrace the route via 4 double-rope rappels (two 60m ropes recommended; a single rope may not reach some stations) on fixed anchors, staying generally to climber's right. Trip reports put the rappels themselves at roughly 30-40 minutes in good conditions. From the base, re-cross the snowy rib and traverse north back beneath Burgundy Spire's East Face to regain Burgundy Col, then descend the climbers' trail to the highway; multiple parties note the hike back to the car feels long after a full climbing day.
routes.itinerary: Day 1: Drive to the informal pullout near milepost 166 on SR-20 and hike in via the Burgundy Col climbers' trail to camp at Larch Bench or Burgundy Col (water can be scarce at the col late in the season). Day 2: From camp, descend toward Burgundy Spire's East Face, traverse to Chianti's east side, climb Rebel Yell (6-7 pitches), rappel the route, and either hike out or stay a second night to add another Wine Spires objective (e.g., Silver Star Glacier or Burgundy Spire's North Face) from the same camp before hiking out.
routes.bail: The route is largely self-rescuing: nearly any belay station can be used to begin rappelling the line already climbed via the fixed anchors used for the standard descent, so retreat is more straightforward than on many Cascades alpine routes.
routes.turnaround: Set a firm turnaround time before starting that accounts for the multi-hour approach to Burgundy Col, 6-7 pitches of climbing, and a rappel descent plus hike out. Start early enough to finish the rappels and regain the approach trail well before dark, and be willing to retreat down the route's own rappel anchors if afternoon thunderstorms build, a common summer hazard in the North Cascades.
routes.bestSeason: Typically climbed July through September, once seasonal snow has cleared from the approach gullies and Burgundy Col; an ice axe (and sometimes crampons) is still commonly carried for lingering snow on the approach even in midsummer. SR-20/North Cascades Highway is itself only open seasonally (roughly mid-spring to late fall, avalanche- and weather-dependent, with additional storm/rockslide-related closures reported in 2026), so the route is completely inaccessible whenever the highway is closed.
routes.detailedRack: Standard alpine rock rack plus doubles of mid-size cams: doubles from a yellow Alien/0.75" up through a #3 Camalot, with a single #3.5 and #4 Camalot for the wider/offwidth sections; a standard set of nuts/stoppers and slings; two 60m ropes for the rappel descent; ice axe (and consider crampons) for the approach.
routes.proNeeds: Protection is plentiful in continuous crack systems throughout the route; it rewards extra mid-to-large cams (roughly 0.75"-4") for the sustained hands/fists/offwidth pitches rather than small gear.
whatToBring: Approach shoes or light boots for scree and snow
Crampons for early/late-season or persistent snow on the approach
Bivy gear if camping at Burgundy Col or Larch Bench
Extra water/filter (water reported scarce at the col camp in late summer)
Sun protection for the long approach
proTips: The right-side start is easier and wider; the left variation is thinner and harder (5.10b) but rejoins the main line by the second pitch.
Water can be very hard to find at the Burgundy Col camp late in the season — plan accordingly or carry extra from lower on the approach.
Strong parties often link Rebel Yell with Silver Star Glacier or Burgundy Spire's North Face from the same camp.
watchOut: The crux pitch widens from thin hands/fingers into an offwidth section — bring the larger cams and be ready for strenuous, sustained climbing.
The approach trail above the flat bench turns to loose, eroded ('kitty litter') scree/talus on the way to Burgundy Col.
Persistent snow on the col traverse and approach gully can require an ice axe well into summer.
knownHazards: Loose rock and scree on the upper approach trail near Burgundy Col.
Rockfall/icefall potential on the snowy traverse beneath Burgundy Spire's East Face.
Afternoon thunderstorms are common in North Cascades summers, a lightning and exposure hazard high on the spire.
The approach and descent hike is long and physically demanding, adding fatigue-related risk on the way out.

pitchDetail.notes: Right start: large left-facing dihedral, flakes and cracks with wide protection to the base of the obvious offwidth. (Left variation start, if taken instead: clean white left-facing corner, finger crack into a chimney, 5.10b.)
Fist crack widening to offwidth; left-variation parties do chimney moves and a horizontal break here to swing right and rejoin the original line.
Lower-angle dihedral with face climbing and flakes; easier, quicker pitch.
Dihedral leading to a blind, airy leftward step-across/frictiony move; described by some parties as an awkward step-around.
Crux pitch: obvious splitter crack (0.75"-4"), thin hands/short fingers widening through tight hands into wider sizes; bolted belay at the top.
Continues in a wide crack/dihedral (right side) up to the base of the summit block.
Short (~15-20 ft) summit block climbed at 5.7 to the tiny, exposed summit; some parties combine this into the top of P6, giving the 6-pitch count.

road.name: North Cascades Highway (SR-20)
road.status: Paved highway, seasonally open only
road.seasonalGate: WSDOT closes SR-20 for winter between the Ross Dam Trailhead (MP 134) and Silver Star gate (MP 171) area — most recently closed Dec 4, 2025; typical reopening is mid-to-late spring, avalanche- and weather-dependent. As of the 2026 season, additional closures/restrictions were in effect (MP 134-156) due to winter storm damage and a spring rockslide — check WSDOT before traveling.
road.driveNote: The Wine Spires/Burgundy Col approach starts at an informal roadside pullout on the north side of SR-20 about 4 miles east of Washington Pass, roughly 0.7 mile past the Cutthroat Lake Road turnoff, near milepost 166, just east of the highway's hairpin turn.

climate.forecastZone: East Slopes Washington Cascades (NWAC avalanche forecast zone; relevant primarily for shoulder-season snow on the approach)
climate.typical: High alpine granite climate with a short reliable climbing window; cold mornings, warm afternoons, and a real risk of afternoon thunderstorms typical of North Cascades summers.
climate.spring: Still winter/spring conditions in the cirque; SR-20 is often still closed and approach gullies typically remain snow-covered.
climate.summer: Primary climbing season (roughly July-September); afternoon thunderstorms are a recurring hazard, and some snow can linger in the approach gully/col traverse into midsummer.
climate.fall: Often good, stable rock conditions with fewer parties, but shortening daylight and the possibility of early season snow by October.
climate.winter: Highway closed and area inaccessible; full winter alpine/avalanche conditions.

access.landManager: Okanogan-Wenatchee National Forest, Methow Valley Ranger District (US Forest Service)
access.fees: No fee at the informal roadside pullout used for the Wine Spires/Burgundy Col approach; developed trailheads in the SR-20 corridor (e.g., Blue Lake) require a Northwest Forest Pass or day-use fee (~$5/day, $30/year).
access.permit: No permit required for climbing or backcountry camping in this National Forest corridor.
access.passRequired: Northwest Forest Pass / Interagency Pass if parking at a developed trailhead; not required at the informal roadside pullout itself, though a pass is a reasonable precaution.
access.closures: Entire area is inaccessible whenever SR-20/North Cascades Highway is under seasonal winter closure (typically mid-Nov to mid/late-April); additional storm- or rockslide-driven closures have occurred (e.g., reported restrictions in the 2026 season).

timing.recommendedStart: Pre-dawn from camp (or very early from the car if attempting car-to-car) to finish the climb and rappel descent before afternoon thunderstorms build

waypoints.name: Chianti Spire / East Face (Rebel Yell) area

emergency.sheriffDispatch: Okanogan County Sheriff non-emergency dispatch: 509-422-7232 (or 800-572-6604 within the 509 area code); 911 for emergencies
emergency.rangerStation: Methow Valley Ranger District (Okanogan-Wenatchee National Forest), 24 West Chewuch Rd, Winthrop, WA — (509) 996-4000
emergency.nearestHospital: Three Rivers Hospital (critical access hospital), 507 Hospital Way, Brewster, WA
emergency.notes: Remote alpine cirque; cell service is unreliable to absent in the immediate Wine Spires/Washington Pass area. Backcountry rescues are handled by the volunteer Okanogan County Search and Rescue Association, dispatched through the Sheriff's Office.

routes.corrections: Given peak elevation was listed as 8,459 ft; MountainProject's GPS/area data and a USGS-sourced reference both put Chianti Spire at approximately 8,400 ft, and this route's highPointFt has been set to 8,400 ft accordingly (minor discrepancy, likely rounding/survey-method difference — not a hierarchy problem). Also note a pitch-count discrepancy across sources: MountainProject documents the route as 6 pitches/800 ft, while several guidebook-style sources (Steph Abegg, NC Mountain Guides, Wenatchee Outdoors) describe a 7-pitch version reflecting an optional harder start pitch that MountainProject folds into pitch 1/2 of its main line; both are documented above.

blurb: Luna Peak (8,311 ft) is the highest summit in the Picket Range, a famously rugged and remote subrange of the North Cascades in North Cascades National Park, Whatcom County, WA. Built of solid but locally loose metamorphic rock (gneiss), it sits on an outlying eastern ridge of the Pickets and rises over 6,500 ft above McMillan Creek to the south, giving it what many climbers call one of the best summit panoramas in the Cascades — the glaciated Northern and Southern Picket cirques laid out on either side. Unlike its jagged, heavily glaciated neighbors, Luna is a non-technical Class 3-4 scramble, first climbed in early September 1938 by Bill Cox and Will F. Thompson; the true difficulty of the peak is the multi-day, bushwhack-heavy approach rather than the climbing itself.

routes.overview: The standard and, per Mountain Project/SummitPost, essentially only established route on Luna Peak: a long alpine scramble that gains the Luna(-Fury) Col via the Access Creek drainage, then follows the broad south ridge crest north to the lower false (south) summit before a short, exposed Class 3-4 traverse to the true (north) summit. No roped pitches are required in good summer conditions, though the route is graded 4th class overall for the final ridge crossing.
routes.beta: From a high camp near Luna(-Fury) Col, climb the broad Class 2 talus ridge north for roughly 1,100 ft to the false (south) summit. From there the ridge narrows; stay on or just below the crest (typically on the east side) across a short, exposed Class 3-4 section of solid but loose rock to the true (north) summit, roughly 15-20 minutes of scrambling. Some parties rope up for this final section; in dry summer conditions it is regularly soloed. The route is reached only after a full day (or more) of approach hiking and off-trail travel — the technical climbing is brief, the logistics are not.
routes.approach: From the Ross Dam Trailhead on SR-20 (MP 134), descend ~1 mile to Ross Lake, cross Ross Dam, and follow the Ross Lake / Big Beaver Trail roughly 9-10 miles (or take the Ross Lake Resort water taxi to shortcut the lakeshore miles) to Luna Camp on the Big Beaver Trail. From Luna Camp, ford Big Beaver Creek and locate the Access Creek drainage; follow it (open forest along the north/right bank at first, then brushier terrain and slide alder higher up) to the Access Creek headwaters basin (~4,400 ft). From the basin, ascend one of several steep talus/scree/snow gullies (climbers commonly use the middle of three gullies leading to the SE shoulder) roughly 1,600 ft to the ridge crest, then traverse across a broad basin on the SE side of the peak toward Luna(-Fury) Col, the obvious notch between Luna Peak and a small tower to its west. Most parties camp at or near the col before the summit push.
routes.descent: Reverse the ascent route back to Luna(-Fury) Col and down the Access Creek gully/basin system to Luna Camp.
routes.descentText: From the true summit, downclimb the Class 3-4 ridge back to the false (south) summit, then descend the broad Class 2 south ridge/talus to Luna(-Fury) Col. From the col, re-descend the steep scree/snow gully system into the Access Creek headwaters basin and follow Access Creek back down to Big Beaver Creek, fording it to regain the Big Beaver Trail toward Luna Camp and, eventually, the Ross Dam Trailhead. Early in the season the descent gully can be glissaded with an ice axe, but it typically runs out onto talus lower down, so self-arrest readiness and careful runout assessment are essential. No fixed rappels exist on the route; cautious parties occasionally rig a short handline off a rock horn for the exposed Class 4 step near the true summit.
routes.itinerary: Day 1: Ross Dam Trailhead to Luna Camp via the Big Beaver Trail (~10 mi, mostly flat, optional water-taxi shortcut across Ross Lake). Day 2: Luna Camp, ford Big Beaver Creek, ascend Access Creek and the SE-shoulder gully system to a high camp at or near Luna(-Fury) Col. Day 3: Summit day — climb the south ridge to the false and true summits and return to the col camp, then descend partway or fully back toward Luna Camp. Day 4: Hike out to the trailhead. Fit parties sometimes compress this to 3 days.
routes.bail: Retreat is a straightforward reversal of the ascent line at any point; there is no shorter bailout given the peak's remoteness. The Class 3-4 step between the false and true summits is easily downclimbed, or a short handline/rappel can be rigged from a horn for less confident parties. Below Luna Col, the only way out is back down the access gullies to Access Creek and the Big Beaver Trail.
routes.turnaround: Set a firm turnaround time before leaving high camp based on your slowest team member's pace on the approach gullies and ridge scramble — this is a long, physically demanding day with route-finding challenges, and descending the loose gully system or the exposed summit ridge in the dark or in deteriorating weather meaningfully raises the accident risk. Build in a safety margin for the multi-hour, multi-mile hike back to camp.
routes.bestSeason: Late July through September is the most reliable window, once the Access Creek approach gullies have mostly melted out and stream fords are lower; some parties have completed dry, snow-free ascents into October on the south-facing slopes. Earlier-season attempts (June-early July) commonly encounter firm, steep snow in the access gullies requiring an ice axe and crampons.
routes.comms: No reliable cell service in the Big Beaver/Access Creek drainages or on Luna Peak itself; this is deep, remote North Cascades National Park backcountry. A satellite messenger (inReach/SPOT) is strongly recommended given the multi-day approach and lack of any quick bailout.
routes.detailedRack: No fixed protection or bolted anchors exist on the route. Most parties climb it unroped as a scramble; a light rack (a few slings, a couple of small-to-mid cams/nuts, and a 30 m rope) is a reasonable option for parties wanting to protect the exposed Class 4 step between the false and true summits, but it is not standard practice in dry summer conditions.
routes.proNeeds: Protection needs are minimal and situational — mainly for the short, exposed Class 3-4 ridge traverse near the true summit, where loose rock and exposure (not sustained difficulty) are the main concerns.
gear: Crampons (early/late season or if snow lingers in the gullies)
Helmet (loose rock on the ridge and gullies)
Light rack + 30m rope (optional, for the exposed class 4 step)
Trekking poles (for the brush and creek fords)
whatToBring: Bear spray / awareness (bear activity reported near Luna Pass/Access Creek)
Insect repellent and head net (mosquitoes/biting flies in the lower valleys)
Water treatment (running water available below Luna Col, less reliable above)
Extra socks / footwear for repeated creek fords
Sun protection for the exposed upper basin and ridge
proTips: Camp at or near Luna(-Fury) Col if possible — it sets up a much shorter, earlier summit push than camping lower in the Access Creek basin.
Of the three gullies leading to the SE shoulder, the middle one is the most commonly used — narrower and steeper on the right, broader and mellower on the left.
A water-taxi shuttle from Ross Lake Resort across Ross Lake meaningfully cuts the approach mileage and is worth arranging in advance.
The Class 3-4 traverse between the false and true summits is easier than it looks from below — there are good ledges for feet and handholds, but stay alert to loose blocks.
watchOut: Loose, blocky rock on the summit ridge and in the access gullies — parties below other climbers are at rockfall risk.
Firm, steep snow can persist in the access gullies well into summer, especially in the morning before it softens.
Access Creek and Big Beaver Creek fords can be knee-deep or higher and are more serious after rain or during peak snowmelt.
Dense slide-alder brush on the upper approach is disorienting; route-finding through it is a common source of delay.
Weather can close in quickly on the exposed upper basin and ridge, with no quick escape once committed.
knownHazards: Sustained off-trail travel and route-finding through brush, talus, and slide alder on the Access Creek approach.
Exposed Class 3-4 scrambling with loose rock on the final ridge between the false and true summits.
Steep snow/ice in the access gullies, particularly early and late season.
Creek fords at Big Beaver and Access Creek that vary with recent weather and snowmelt.
Extremely remote location with no cell coverage and no realistic quick rescue access — self-sufficiency is essential.
Documented black bear activity in the Access Creek/Luna Pass area.

pitchDetail.notes: Broad talus ridge, roughly 1,100 ft of gain; some loose rock underfoot.
Narrow, exposed ridge traverse (~15-20 min), staying on or just below the crest, typically on the east side; solid rock but with loose blocks — the crux of the route.

road.name: North Cascades Highway (State Route 20)
road.status: Seasonal — closed to vehicles in winter
road.seasonalGate: Typically closes in early December and reopens between roughly May and June depending on snowpack and any needed repairs; closure points can shift to lower-elevation gates (e.g. Newhalem) later in the season.
road.driveNote: Ross Dam Trailhead (MP 134) sits only about 0.5 mile past the western winter closure gate, so it is one of the last trailheads reachable by car before the highway shuts for the season and one of the first to reopen.

climate.forecastZone: NWS zone WAZ567 (West Slopes North Cascades and Passes); NWAC's West Slopes North zone is the nearest avalanche-forecast reference, though the Pickets themselves are outside NWAC's core forecast area
climate.typical: Wet, maritime-influenced North Cascades climate with a short, drier climbing window in mid-to-late summer; the range generates its own weather and cirques like Luna's hold snow late.
climate.spring: Heavy lingering snowpack in the approach gullies and on the ridge; stream fords are at their highest.
climate.summer: The main climbing window (Jul-Sep); still subject to sudden North Cascades convective storms and afternoon buildups.
climate.fall: Dry, stable stretches possible into October on south-facing terrain, but shortening days and the first snow can arrive with little warning.
climate.winter: Effectively inaccessible — SR-20 is gated and the approach is a serious winter mountaineering undertaking.

access.landManager: North Cascades National Park Service Complex / Ross Lake National Recreation Area (NPS)
access.fees: No park entrance fee. Backcountry camping requires a permit; summer-season (mid-May to early Oct) permit fees are $10 per person plus a $6 non-refundable reservation fee, free the rest of the year.
access.permit: Wilderness/backcountry permit required for all overnight stays, issued by the Wilderness Information Center in Marblemount or reserved in advance via recreation.gov.
access.passRequired: None for the Ross Dam Trailhead itself — it lies within the NPS/Ross Lake NRA boundary, not National Forest land, so no Northwest Forest Pass is needed there.
access.closures: SR-20 winter road closure (roughly Dec-May) cuts off vehicle access to the trailhead entirely during that period; backcountry permit quotas can also limit camp availability at Luna Camp in peak summer season.

timing.recommendedStart: Early/pre-dawn departure from a high camp at or near Luna(-Fury) Col for the summit push

emergency.sheriffDispatch: 911 (Whatcom County Sheriff's Office coordinates county SAR; NPS rangers lead wilderness rescue within the park)
emergency.rangerStation: North Cascades National Park Wilderness Information Center, 7280 Ranger Station Rd, Marblemount, WA — (360) 854-7245
emergency.nearestHospital: PeaceHealth United General Medical Center, 2000 Hospital Dr, Sedro-Woolley, WA (24-hour ER)
emergency.notes: This is one of the most remote climbs in the North Cascades — no cell coverage, multi-day walk-out, and any rescue would likely require NPS/county SAR helicopter or ground team mobilization taking many hours at minimum. Carry a satellite communicator and file a trip plan.

routes.season: Jul-Sep (best); early/late season needs axe & crampons for the access gullies
routes.face: South/Southwest ridge, from Luna(-Fury) Col to the summit
routes.fa: Peak first ascent (via this line): Bill Cox and Will F. Thompson, early September 1938
routes.corrections: Mountain Project's scraped stats for this route ('4th class, 1 pitch, 2 ice, 2 mixed, 1b aid') appear to be a data/scraping artifact and are contradicted by every other primary source (SummitPost, Wikipedia, WTA, multiple trip reports), all of which describe a non-technical Class 3-4 scramble with no sustained ice/mixed/aid climbing; that MP grade string was not used here. Reported approach mileage and elevation gain vary substantially between sources (roughly 33-48 mi round trip, 6,200-12,200 ft gain) depending mainly on whether the Ross Lake water-taxi shortcut is used and how camps are counted; the WTA figures (36.5 mi RT / 8,009 ft gain) were used here as the most consistently citable official trail-organization numbers. The given peak elevation (8,311 ft) and coordinates were confirmed correct against Wikipedia/USGS data.

routes.overview: The Southeast Slopes describe the broad, non-technical basin and open talus/scree/heather/snow terrain on Luna Peak's SE aspect that the standard approach crosses between the upper Access Creek basin and Luna(-Fury) Col/the south ridge crest — used by parties as the easier, walk-up line onto the mountain rather than the more exposed South Ridge crest scramble. It is not documented as a separately named, first-ascended technical line in Mountain Project, SummitPost, or Peakbagger; it is best understood as the standard broad SE-facing ascent slopes/basin, distinct from the crest-line 'South Ridge' route description.
routes.beta: From the Access Creek headwaters basin (~4,400 ft), climbers ascend broad talus, heather, or lingering snow slopes on the SE side of the peak (multiple parallel gully/slope lines exist) to gain the ridge system near Point 6,730, then cross a large open basin on Luna's SE side at roughly the 6,600 ft level toward Luna(-Fury) Col. This SE basin/slope terrain is consistently Class 2 walking/scrambling on talus, heather, or snow with no sustained exposure, in contrast to the Class 3-4 crest scramble of the South Ridge route above the col.
routes.approach: Identical to the South Ridge route as far as the Access Creek headwaters basin: Ross Dam Trailhead -> Big Beaver Trail to Luna Camp -> ford Big Beaver Creek -> ascend Access Creek to its headwater basin (~4,400 ft). From there, rather than committing directly to one of the steeper gully lines to the SE shoulder, parties ascend the broader, lower-angle SE slopes/basin toward the ridge and Luna(-Fury) Col.
routes.descent: Reverse the SE slopes/basin line back to the Access Creek basin and out via Luna Camp; snow sections can sometimes be glissaded early season.
routes.descentText: Downclimb or plunge-step back down the SE basin/slope terrain to the Access Creek headwaters basin, then follow Access Creek down to Big Beaver Creek, ford it, and rejoin the Big Beaver Trail toward Luna Camp and the Ross Dam Trailhead. Where snow lingers, this line can offer a faster glissade descent than the steeper gully options, but always assess runout onto talus before committing.
routes.bail: As with the South Ridge route, retreat is a straightforward reversal of the ascent line — there is no shortcut out of the Access Creek/Luna Peak drainage given its remoteness.
routes.turnaround: Set a turnaround time before departing camp with margin for slow travel across talus, heather, and any lingering snow in the SE basin, and for the long hike back out; this terrain has no technical crux but is easy to underestimate given its length and remoteness.
routes.bestSeason: Late July through September, once the SE basin has substantially melted out; in early season the slopes can hold continuous snow well above 1,900 m (~6,200 ft), which some parties use to their advantage for a faster ascent/glissade with an ice axe.
routes.comms: No reliable cell coverage anywhere in this drainage; a satellite messenger is strongly recommended.
gear: Ice axe (useful on lingering snow in the SE basin)
proTips: This SE basin/slope terrain is a good option for parties wanting to avoid the more exposed Class 3-4 crest sections of the South Ridge route above Luna Col — it rejoins the standard line at the col.
Snow-covered in early season, the SE slopes can offer quicker, lower-effort travel (and a glissade descent) compared to dry-season talus.
watchOut: Loose talus and scree underfoot across the open basin.
Lingering snow can obscure the talus and create postholing or slip hazard into early summer.
Route-finding across the broad, featureless basin can be disorienting in poor visibility.
knownHazards: Long, sustained off-trail talus/scree/snow travel with no technical crux but real remoteness and self-rescue burden.
Lingering snow and potential glissade runout hazards onto rock in the SE basin.
Same brush, ford, and bear-activity hazards as the Access Creek approach shared with the South Ridge route.

road.name: North Cascades Highway (State Route 20)
road.status: Seasonal — closed to vehicles in winter
road.seasonalGate: Typically closes early December, reopens roughly May-June depending on conditions.
road.driveNote: Ross Dam Trailhead (MP 134) is just past the western winter closure gate.

climate.forecastZone: NWS zone WAZ567 (West Slopes North Cascades and Passes)
climate.typical: Wet maritime North Cascades climate with a short mid-to-late summer climbing window.
climate.spring: Deep lingering snow across the SE basin.
climate.summer: Primary climbing window (Jul-Sep), talus mostly snow-free by August in a normal year.
climate.fall: Can remain dry into October on south/SE aspects.
climate.winter: Inaccessible; SR-20 gated.

access.landManager: North Cascades National Park Service Complex / Ross Lake National Recreation Area (NPS)
access.fees: No entrance fee; backcountry permit fee $10/person + $6 reservation fee in summer season, free off-season.
access.permit: Wilderness/backcountry permit from the Marblemount Wilderness Information Center or recreation.gov.
access.passRequired: None at Ross Dam Trailhead (inside NPS/NRA boundary).
access.closures: SR-20 winter closure blocks vehicle access to the trailhead; backcountry permit quotas apply at Luna Camp in summer.

timing.recommendedStart: Early departure from Access Creek basin or Luna Col camp

emergency.sheriffDispatch: 911 (Whatcom County Sheriff's Office / NPS SAR)
emergency.rangerStation: North Cascades National Park Wilderness Information Center, Marblemount — (360) 854-7245
emergency.nearestHospital: PeaceHealth United General Medical Center, Sedro-Woolley, WA (24-hour ER)
emergency.notes: Remote basin terrain with no cell coverage; carry a satellite communicator.

routes.corrections: No primary source (Mountain Project, SummitPost, Peakbagger) documents 'Southeast Slopes' as a distinct, separately first-ascended named route on Luna Peak — only the South Ridge/crest line appears as a formally cataloged route. This entry is built from consistent, multi-source trip-report descriptions (countryhighpoints.com, havetent.com, and others) of the broad SE-facing basin/slope terrain climbers use as the standard, easier ascent line to Luna(-Fury) Col, as distinct from the exposed crest scramble described under South Ridge. Numeric approach stats (gain/distance) were left null rather than estimated, since sources do not cleanly separate them from the shared Access Creek approach figures used for South Ridge.

blurb: Red Mountain (5,890 ft) is a peak in the Snoqualmie Pass area of the Alpine Lakes Wilderness, Cascade Range, about three miles north of I-90's Snoqualmie Pass summit in King County. It is composed of reddish, fractured rock (often loose, especially near its east and west ridges) and is most often climbed as a class 2-3 scramble via Commonwealth Basin, though it also holds short technical routes on its south and east flanks; its first recorded ascent was by W.C. Mendenhall in 1898.
hierarchyNote: Peak identity confirmed: Wikipedia's coordinates for Red Mountain (King County, WA), 47°27'22"N 121°23'21"W, convert to 47.4561,-121.3892 — an essentially exact match to the given lat/lng (47.456135,-121.389105), and its elevation (5,890 ft) and parent area (Snoqualmie Pass / Alpine Lakes Wilderness) are correct and well-documented. HOWEVER: one listed route, "Ragged Ridge" (wa_ragged_ridge), does not belong to this peak. Ragged Ridge is a well-documented, geographically distinct North Cascades ridge/traverse (Katsuk, Kimtah, Cosho Peaks) near Easy Pass/Thunder Creek in North Cascades National Park, roughly 50+ miles north of Snoqualmie Pass — no source ties it to this Red Mountain, and Mountain Project's "Red Mountain" area page (the authoritative local route catalog) lists only three climbs: East Ridge, South Face, and Southwest Ridge (Winter). This route entry appears to be a data-entry/attribution error and should be reviewed for removal or reassignment to the correct North Cascades peak. Separately, minor note: Mountain Project's "East Ridge" route page (filed under the Red Mountain area, reached via the same Red Pass approach) is itself titled "East Ridge, Lundin Peak" — Lundin Peak is a distinct, slightly lower neighboring summit "halfway between Snoqualmie Mountain and Red Mountain." Sources are not fully consistent on whether this specific technical line tops out on Red Mountain proper or on Lundin Peak; I've filled the route as documented under the Red Mountain area/approach but flagged this ambiguity in that route's corrections field.

routes.overview: A short, single-pitch easy 5th-class scramble/climb up broken, featured rock on the ridge above Red Pass, reached via the saddle between Lundin and East Lundin peaks.
routes.beta: Popular short technical add-on for parties already scrambling in the Red Pass/Commonwealth Basin area. Climbs broken rock past a slab section and a short featured section to the top; several minor variations exist. Three fixed bolts are installed and can be used for protection/anchor with just quickdraws, or a light trad rack with an emphasis on small gear can be carried instead.
routes.approach: From the PCT trailhead at Exit 52 (Snoqualmie Pass), hike the Pacific Crest Trail north about 2-2.2 miles to the Commonwealth Creek/Red Mountain Trail (1033) junction. Follow this trail, crossing Commonwealth Creek, about 1.7 miles further to Red Mountain Saddle/Red Pass (approx. 5,320+ ft), passing the Red Mountain scramble boot path along the way. The route begins at the saddle between Lundin and East Lundin peaks; follow a climber's path west/up to the base of the route.
routes.descent: Rappel off the top of the route (anchor material needed) or downclimb/reverse the approach ridge.
routes.descentText: Most parties rappel from anchors near the top of the East Ridge back to the saddle, then reverse the approach path down to Red Pass and out via the Commonwealth Creek/PCT trail system.
routes.rappels: 1 rappel from top anchors (or fixed bolts) back to the saddle
routes.bestSeason: Best in mid-summer through early fall once snow has cleared from the Red Pass approach; a dry approach trail and dry rock are needed for the class-5 moves.
gear: 3 quickdraws (for the 3 fixed bolts, as an alternative to trad gear)
routes.detailedRack: A light alpine rack emphasizing smaller protection (small cams and stoppers) is sufficient for the broken-rock climbing; alternatively, three installed bolts allow the route to be protected and rappelled with just three quickdraws plus anchor/rappel material.
routes.proNeeds: Minimal - small gear only; three fixed bolts provide an easier protection/rappel option.
proTips: Bring a helmet given the generally loose rock in this part of the Red Mountain/Red Pass ridge system.
The three installed bolts let you climb light with just quickdraws instead of a full trad rack.
watchOut: Rock in this area (east and west ridges near the summit) is noted as looser than the solid south-face slabs.
Confirm which summit (Red Mountain vs. neighboring Lundin Peak) you intend to top out on before committing — see route notes.
knownHazards: Loose, fractured red rock typical of this ridge system, more pronounced than on the south face.
Short but real exposure on the summit block.
routes.corrections: Mountain Project's own route page for this climb (filed under its 'Red Mountain' area, reached via the identical Red Pass approach) is titled 'East Ridge, Lundin Peak' — Lundin Peak is described independently as a distinct, slightly lower neighboring summit between Snoqualmie Mountain and Red Mountain. Sources are inconsistent on whether this specific line's summit is Red Mountain proper or Lundin Peak; data is presented here as documented in the local (Red Mountain-area) route catalog, but this ambiguity should be verified against a guidebook before treating the summit point as Red Mountain's true summit.

routes.overview: The solid, class 3-4 south ramp of Red Mountain — the most commonly climbed technical line on the peak, with an optional steeper class-4/low-5 direct finish through the summit block.
routes.beta: Ascends the broad, comparatively solid south-facing ramp/slabs of the mountain via several easy, enjoyable class-3 pitches; most parties climb it unroped. Near the top the direct summit block steepens to class 4 or low 5th and becomes 'roofy' overhead — most parties instead traverse left onto the class-2 hiking finish on the west side of the south ridge rather than continue straight up.
routes.approach: From the PCT trailhead at Exit 52, hike the Pacific Crest Trail north to the Commonwealth Creek Trail junction, then follow it roughly 2 miles until Red Mountain comes into view. Traverse on heather across the base of the mountain until you reach the bright red rock gully/ramp that marks the start of the route.
routes.descent: Descend via the class-2 scrambling/hiking route on the mountain's west side.
routes.descentText: From the summit, downclimb the standard class-2 west-slope/southwest-ridge scramble route rather than reversing the technical south-face pitches; this is the same descent used for the Standard Scramble route.
objHaz: exposure on the direct summit-block finish
routes.bestSeason: Snow-free summer and early fall (June-October) once the approach heather bands and rock are dry.
gear: webbing/rap rings (optional, for tree rappels on descent variations)
routes.detailedRack: Typically climbed with no protection on the standard class 3-4 ramp; a rope and light rack are only useful if taking the class-4/low-5 direct summit-block finish.
routes.proNeeds: None required for the standard class 3-4 line; rope up only for the harder direct finish.
proTips: Most parties climb the ramp unroped and simply walk off via the class-2 west-side descent rather than finishing directly through the roofy summit block.
watchOut: The direct summit-block finish steepens abruptly to class 4/low 5th with real exposure — know the walk-off option before committing above it.
Rock is 'solid' relative to Red Mountain's other faces but still red, fractured Cascade rock — test holds.
knownHazards: Rockfall potential onto parties below, especially where the route funnels through gullies.
Loose rock typical of Red Mountain's composition, though the south ramp is noted as the most solid aspect of the peak.

routes.corrections: No reputable source (Mountain Project's Red Mountain area page, SummitPost, Peakbagger, or any Snoqualmie Pass guide/trip-report site) documents a 'Ragged Ridge' route or feature on this Red Mountain. 'Ragged Ridge' is a real but geographically distinct North Cascades landform — a rugged ridge with peaks including Katsuk, Kimtah, and Cosho, located near Easy Pass and Thunder Creek in North Cascades National Park, roughly 50+ miles north of Snoqualmie Pass — with no documented connection to this peak. This route entry is very likely a data-attribution error; I could not source any facts for it as a Red Mountain (Snoqualmie) route and recommend verifying/removing or reassigning it rather than publishing fabricated route content.

routes.overview: The standard, non-technical route up Red Mountain: an approach hike through Commonwealth Basin followed by a class 2-3 rock scramble up the mountain's west slope/southwest ridge to the summit.
routes.beta: The most-climbed of Washington's several 'Red Mountains.' After a gradual, forested approach through Commonwealth Basin, the final half-mile up the west slope gains about 900 ft at a 30-40 degree angle, alternating between class 2 and easy class 3 terrain on characteristically loose, fractured red rock; hands are needed occasionally but there is no sustained exposure. An alternate line follows the abandoned Commonwealth Basin trail to a junction, then climbs the south ridge, staying climber's-left (south) of the ridgeline.
routes.approach: From I-90, take Exit 52 (West Summit, Snoqualmie Pass), turn north and cross under the freeway; a paved spur road on the right leads to the Pacific Crest Trail (PCT) trailhead parking lot. Hike the PCT north about 2-2.5 miles to the junction with the Commonwealth Creek Trail (No. 1033); follow it toward Red Pass, passing beneath the mountain and a small pond ('Red Pond'/'Commonwealth Pond'). A cairn near the pond marks the start of a climber's boot path that turns off-trail and ascends the west slope; when the boot track fades, scramble directly to the summit.
routes.descent: Reverse the ascent route down the west slope/south ridge boot path back to Red Pond and the Commonwealth Creek Trail.
routes.descentText: Downclimb the same class 2-3 west-slope/southwest-ridge line used on the ascent back to the boot-path cairn near Red Pond, then follow the Commonwealth Creek Trail and PCT back to the trailhead. Loose rock makes downclimbing slower and requires care to avoid kicking debris onto climbers below.
routes.bestSeason: June through October, matching most Washington Cascades summits; the final scramble section can hold snow into early summer and becomes wet/slick during fall rains.
gear: trekking poles (optional, for the approach trail)
whatToBring: Navigation (map/GPS) for the unmarked upper boot path
Northwest Forest Pass or equivalent (required at the PCT trailhead)
Extra layers - the summit ridge is exposed to wind
routes.detailedRack: No technical rack needed; this is an unroped scramble for competent parties in good conditions.
routes.proNeeds: None - class 2-3 scrambling terrain.
proTips: Watch for the cairn just before the small pond in Commonwealth Basin — it marks the unsigned boot path that leaves the trail for the summit scramble.
Wear a helmet even on this 'easy' route: the red rock is notably loose and rockfall between members of the same party is a documented hazard.
Staying close to (climber's-left/south of) the ridgeline on the upper mountain is reported to give better rock and route-finding than wandering onto the open slope.
watchOut: Red-colored Cascades rock is a known indicator of poor rock quality with multiple intersecting fracture lines - test every hold.
The boot path is unofficial and easy to lose in the upper scramble section, especially in cloud or early-season snow.
Rockfall triggered by parties above has caused close calls between climbers on the same route.
knownHazards: Loose rock throughout the upper 900+ ft of scrambling, worst on the east and west ridges near the summit.
Rockfall risk when multiple parties are on route simultaneously.
Residual snow/ice on the upper slope into early summer, which can make the class 2-3 terrain considerably more serious.

road.name: I-90 to PCT Trailhead access road (Exit 52)
road.status: Paved, open year-round (subject to normal I-90 winter conditions/chain requirements over Snoqualmie Pass)
road.driveNote: Take I-90 Exit 52 (West Summit), turn north and cross under the freeway, then follow the short paved spur road to the PCT trailhead parking lot.

climate.forecastZone: Washington Cascades West Slopes South / Snoqualmie Pass area
climate.typical: Wet, maritime Cascades climate; the pass itself averages some of the heaviest annual snowfall of any highway pass in the continental US.
climate.spring: Significant lingering snowpack on the upper mountain into June; avalanche/snow-travel skills often still needed on the approach.
climate.summer: Warm, mostly dry July-August; the primary climbing/scrambling season with dry rock.
climate.fall: Cooling, increasingly wet by October; rock becomes slick in rain.
climate.winter: Heavy snowfall and high avalanche danger on the mountain's flanks; becomes a snow/ski mountaineering objective rather than a scramble.

access.landManager: Mt. Baker-Snoqualmie National Forest (Snoqualmie Ranger District), within the Alpine Lakes Wilderness
access.fees: Northwest Forest Pass (or America the Beautiful/National Park interagency pass) required at the PCT trailhead
access.permit: Free self-issue wilderness permit available at the trailhead for day use
access.passRequired: Northwest Forest Pass

timing.recommendedStart: Early morning start recommended given the roughly 8-mile round trip and loose scrambling terrain

emergency.sheriffDispatch: 911 (emergencies); King County Sheriff's Office coordinates backcountry SAR response via the King County Search & Rescue Association)
emergency.rangerStation: Snoqualmie Ranger District, Mt. Baker-Snoqualmie National Forest, 902 SE North Bend Way Bldg 1, North Bend, WA 98045 - (425) 888-1421
emergency.nearestHospital: Snoqualmie Valley Hospital, 9801 Frontier Ave SE, Snoqualmie, WA - 24/7 ER, (425) 831-2300 (Level 5 trauma center; higher-level trauma cases are transferred toward Seattle/Overlake)
emergency.notes: Cell coverage is inconsistent in Commonwealth Basin and largely absent on the upper mountain; I-90 corridor itself generally has coverage. SAR response is coordinated by King County Sheriff's Office with volunteer units (Seattle Mountain Rescue, King County Explorer SAR, Ski Patrol Rescue Team).

blurb: Lexington Tower is a 7,560-ft granite pinnacle in the North Cascades, the middle spire of the five-tower Liberty Bell Group (with Liberty Bell Mountain, Concord Tower, and the Early Winters Spires) rising directly above Washington Pass on the North Cascades Highway (SR-20). The rock is high-quality Golden Horn batholith granite, and routes exist on nearly every aspect, ranging from the moderate 1954 North Face to the bolt-protected 5.12a testpiece Tooth and Claw. Its roadside proximity and short approaches make it one of the most accessible alpine rock objectives in the North Cascades.

routes.overview: A sustained, mostly bolt-protected alpine rock testpiece climbing a vague right-facing corner and slab system just right of (south of) the East Face, finishing with sections of hand/finger cracks; regarded as one of the harder free routes at Washington Pass.
routes.beta: Eight-pitch, 800-ft Grade IV 5.12a on the east side of the tower. The route alternates bolt-protected technical slab pitches (P1, P2, P5) with crack pitches (P3, P4, P6-P7) that need a full trad rack. Rock quality is noted as inconsistent, with looser sections on P1 and the P4 roof traverse. Descent is either a rappel of the route with two 70m ropes, or an exposed 4th-class traverse west from the East Shoulder to the same gully used by East Face parties.
routes.approach: Shares the East Face approach: park at pullouts near the pond about 1.2 miles east of Washington Pass along SR-20 (near the hairpin curve), then hike roughly 30-45 minutes through forest and talus to the base of the east side of the tower.
routes.descent: Rappel the route (2x70m ropes) or traverse west from the East Shoulder to the Blue Lake Trail gully.
routes.descentText: Two options: (1) From the East Shoulder, a very exposed 4th-class (treat as 5th-class) traverse leads west to a gully descending toward the Blue Lake Trail — the same west-side exit used by East Face parties, which means a car shuttle or a road walk/hitch back to an east-side vehicle; or (2) rappel the route using two 70m ropes from the top anchor down to the bolted P5 station, then continue down via a mix of bolted and tree anchors. Two 60m ropes are workable if the rappels are started from the P5 station rather than the true top.
routes.itinerary: Single, full alpine-rock day: approach ~45 min-1 hr from the east-side pond pullout, climb the 8-pitch route (a sustained 5.12a day, budget most of the daylight hours), then either rappel the route or descend west via the East Shoulder traverse and Blue Lake Trail exit.
routes.bail: Bolted anchors exist through the lower/middle pitches (notably P2 and the P5 crux station), so the route can be rappelled from partway up using two ropes without needing to reach the top.
routes.turnaround: Start early enough to be off the exposed, technical upper pitches before typical North Cascades summer afternoon thunderstorms build; bail via the bolted rappel line rather than pushing into deteriorating weather.
routes.bestSeason: Best from roughly late June through September, once SR-20 has reopened for the season and approach gullies/ledges have shed snow; conditions are driest in July-August.
routes.comms: Cell coverage is inconsistent to absent on the route and in the approach gullies; the SR-20 corridor itself sees regular summer traffic that can be flagged down in an emergency.
routes.fa: Steven Risse, Dave Tower (June 1989)
routes.rappels: Route can be rappelled top-to-bottom with 2x70m ropes via bolted/tree anchors; 2x60m ropes work if starting from the P5 bolted station
objHaz: Loose rock on the pitch 4 roof traverse
Shared rockfall-prone approach gullies (same as Liberty Crack approach)
routes.detailedRack: Doubles to 0.75 Camalot plus single #1, #2, and #3 Camalots cover the crack pitches (P3, P4, P6-P7); bring a full set of quickdraws for the well-bolted technical slab pitches (copperhead + 6 bolts on P1, bolted slab on P2 and the P5 crux). Two 70m ropes are ideal for the rappel descent; two 60m ropes work if stopping the rappels above P5.
routes.proNeeds: Well-bolted on the technical slab pitches, but the crack pitches need a full complement of cams; loose rock on P1 and the P4 roof calls for careful placement and rope management to avoid dislodging blocks.
whatToBring: Extra water/food for a long alpine day
Approach shoes for the walk-off descent option
proTips: A well-bolted, sustained alpine slab testpiece — the grade is stiff for 5.12a with alpine rock character, not a sport-crag 5.12a.
Rack doubles through 0.75in; the bolted sections still benefit from gear backup between bolts.
Verify fixed hardware before weighting it; condition varies pitch to pitch.
watchOut: Pitch 1 rock quality is notably poor/crumbly for a technical alpine slab pitch.
Loose rock on the pitch 4 roof traverse.
Rockfall risk in the shared approach gullies below Washington Pass.
knownHazards: Loose granite blocks typical of North Cascades spires, concentrated on P1 and the P4 roof.
Afternoon thunderstorms are common in summer at Washington Pass.
Approach gullies see rockfall from parties above, including on adjacent Liberty Crack.

pitchDetail.notes: Vague right-facing corner right of the East Face; cracks trend rightward across a slab past a copperhead and six bolts; sustained, alpine rock quality issues.
Shorter, slightly exposed slab section to a bolted anchor.
Crack climbing with a crux traverse left over a small roof, then slab to a tree belay.
Left-facing corner past three pins, meeting a roof via a bolt; hand-and-finger crack above.
Crux pitch: steep cracks above the belay go flared, transitioning to a difficult move on thin slab past bolts.
Wild hand-and-finger crack with varied jamming past a fixed nut to a ledge or bolted anchor.

waypoints.name: Lexington Tower / Tooth and Claw (Mountain Project marker)

routes.overview: The classic long alpine rock route on Lexington Tower: an 800-ft, 8-10 pitch crack-and-chimney line splitting the steep east face, topping out at a notch on the shoulder rather than the true summit.
routes.beta: Grade III 5.9+ trad route following a crack/corner system to a dead-end chimney, then an offwidth pitch with a fixed jammed 2x4 chockstone, before easier terrain leads to a 4th-class traverse to the shoulder notch. Mountain Project lists it as 10 pitches/Grade III; other trip reports (StephAbegg, SummitPost, Chossclimbers) describe it as 8-9 pitches depending on how sections are combined — the climbing itself is consistent across sources. Car-to-car time is typically 6-8 hours.
routes.approach: Park at pullouts near the pond about 1.2 miles east of Washington Pass along SR-20 (near the hairpin curve); hike roughly 30-45 minutes through forest and talus fields beneath Liberty Bell to the base of the east face (shared approach with Liberty Crack).
routes.descent: Walk-off: 4th-class traverse to a notch, then descend the gully on the west side of the tower to the Blue Lake Trail.
routes.descentText: From the top of the route (a notch on Lexington's shoulder, not the true summit), make a 4th-class traverse to the descent notch, then head west/down a gully on the west side of the tower. Traverse right/north to pick up a climbers'/cattle trail that intersects the Blue Lake Trail, then follow it out to the highway. Because the standard descent exits to the west side of Washington Pass, parties who parked on the east side (pond pullout) need a car shuttle or a roadside walk/hitch back to their vehicle. Rappelling is avoided on this route to reduce rope-snag risk.
routes.itinerary: Single, long day: approach ~30-45 min from the east-side pond pullout, climb the 8-10 pitch East Face (several hours of technical climbing), then descend west via the walk-off gully and Blue Lake Trail climbers' path; Mountain Project notes the full car-to-car outing typically runs 6-8 hours, plus any shuttle/road-walk time back to an east-side vehicle.
routes.bail: No fixed rappel stations on route; retreat is by down-climbing/traversing back toward the class-3 ledge system low on the route, so committing parties should have solid stable-weather conditions before starting.
routes.turnaround: Given the lack of fixed retreat anchors, start early enough to be off the upper pitches well before afternoon thunderstorms typical of North Cascades summers build.
routes.bestSeason: Best late June through September once SR-20 has reopened and the approach gullies/ledges are free of snow; driest conditions in July-August.
routes.comms: Cell coverage is inconsistent to absent on the face and in the approach gullies; the SR-20 corridor sees regular summer traffic that can be flagged down in an emergency.
routes.fa: Steve Marts, Don McPherson (1966)
routes.rappels: No rappels on the standard descent — walk-off via the west-side gully
objHaz: Runouts, especially on pitch 2 and other slab sections
Loose, 'chunky' rock on the upper pitches
Rockfall in the shared approach gullies below Washington Pass (also used by Liberty Crack parties)
Route ends at a shoulder notch, not the true summit
routes.detailedRack: Standard alpine trad rack with cams to 4 inches, doubled through the finger-to-hand range; a #4 Camalot sees frequent use, and a #5-#6 is useful for the offwidth pitch (a fixed 2x4 chockstone also protects part of that section). A single 60m rope is sufficient since the descent is a walk-off, not a rappel route.
routes.proNeeds: Generally good protection on the crack pitches but thinner/insecure gear on the slabby traverse sections; wide gear (#4-#6) is essential for the offwidth and dead-end chimney pitches.
gear: Standard trad rack to 4in, doubled mid-size
whatToBring: Helmet (rockfall shared with Liberty Crack approach)
Approach shoes for the walk-off descent
Water/food for a 6-8 hour car-to-car outing
proTips: The middle three pitches (crack/corner system plus the dead-end chimney) are widely cited as the route's highlight.
Bring a #4 and #6 Camalot — the offwidth and chimney pitches eat wide gear.
Plan for the west-side walk-off exit: shuttle a car to the Blue Lake trailhead or be ready for a road walk back to an east-side car.
watchOut: Significant runouts on pitch 2 and other slab sections.
Loose, 'chunky' rock on the upper pitches.
The route tops out at a shoulder notch, not the true 7,560-ft summit.
knownHazards: Rockfall in the shared approach gullies, a popular zone also used to access Liberty Crack.
Afternoon thunderstorms are common in summer at Washington Pass.
Loose granite blocks typical of North Cascades alpine rock.

pitchDetail.notes: Crack system, traverse left into slab climbing with difficult protection.
Continue left to a right-facing (or left-facing per some accounts) corner; belay at a tree; noted for significant runouts.
Climb the corner to a ledge, tree belay.
Hand-to-finger cracks to a stance below a roof/dead-end chimney.
Crux: traverse right on an exposed, no-hands ledge for ~30 ft, then lieback/jam a flake-to-finger crack.
Offwidth with a fixed jammed 2x4 and two bolts to an alcove; stemming/chimney technique reduces the need for armbarring.
Short chimney leading to blockier terrain and another offwidth.
Easier terrain to a tree belay, then a 4th-class traverse to the descent notch on Lexington's shoulder (does not reach the true summit).

routes.corrections: Pitch count varies by source (8-10); Mountain Project's structured listing (10 pitches, Grade III, 800 ft) was used as primary, consistent with StephAbegg (9p) and SummitPost/Chossclimbers (8p) describing the same climbing with different pitch-combining conventions.

routes.overview: The original first-ascent route on Lexington Tower: a short, enjoyable 3-pitch line with an awkward 5.7 crux right off the ground, followed by easier climbing to the notch and an exposed 4th-class ridge finish to the true summit.
routes.beta: Grade II 5.7, 300 ft, 3 pitches. The crux is an awkward hand-crack move low on pitch 1; after that the climbing eases to 5.4-5.6 territory. A popular half-day objective, often combined with neighboring Concord Tower via the connecting gully.
routes.approach: From the Blue Lake Trailhead, follow the Blue Lake Trail about 1.5 miles to a well-worn climbers' trail (marked by a large cairn) branching left into the basin below the Liberty Bell Group, then climb the Concord/Lexington gully to the base of the tower.
routes.descent: Two single-rope rappels down the face, with the final pitch downclimbed rather than rappelled.
routes.descentText: From the high point, downclimb pitch 3 back to the belay at the notch (leave gear placed on the lead and re-clip on the way down, since the traversing terrain doesn't rappel cleanly). A single-rope rappel from the notch anchor reaches the first rap station on the pitch 2 line; a second single-rope rappel from there returns to the base of the route. Retrace the approach gully back to the Blue Lake Trail climbers' path and out to the trailhead.
routes.itinerary: Half-day outing: drive to the Blue Lake Trailhead, approach roughly 1-1.5 hours via the Blue Lake Trail and Concord/Lexington gully climbers' trail, climb the 3-pitch North Face (about 40 minutes for a fast party per trip reports), descend via downclimbing and two rappels (roughly 20-30 minutes), and hike out.
routes.bail: Rappel anchors exist after pitches 1 and 2, so the route can be bailed from either station with a single rope.
routes.turnaround: A short route, but start with enough daylight margin to avoid afternoon thunderstorms common to Washington Pass summers, particularly on the exposed pitch 3 ridge traverse.
routes.bestSeason: Best late June through September once SR-20 has reopened and the Concord/Lexington gully has shed its snow; a good option even later in the season given its short length.
routes.comms: Cell coverage is inconsistent to absent in the approach basin and on route; the SR-20 corridor below sees regular summer traffic.
routes.fa: Tim Kelley, Dick McGowan (July 5, 1954) — first ascent of Lexington Tower
routes.rappels: 2 single-rope rappels down the face; pitch 3 is downclimbed rather than rappelled
objHaz: Awkward, unwarmed-up 5.7 crux right off the belay
Exposed 4th-class ridge traverse on pitch 3 that cannot be rappelled
routes.detailedRack: A single rack of cams to 2 inches plus a handful of slings covers the crux hand crack and the wandering, blocky second pitch; minimal gear is needed for the 4th-class ridge finish, though a rack should still be carried if leading it roped.
routes.proNeeds: Small-to-medium cams protect the opening crux crack adequately; runners help manage rope drag on the wandering, blocky second pitch.
proTips: A short, quick objective — pairs well as a half-day with a second route or Concord Tower via the connecting gully.
The crux hand crack comes right off the ground with no warm-up, so rack up ready to climb.
watchOut: Awkward 5.7 crux right off the belay with no warm-up.
Loose/mossy blocks on pitch 2 — test holds before committing.
Pitch 3's traversing terrain can't be rappelled cleanly; plan to downclimb or re-lead it.
knownHazards: Loose granite blocks typical of North Cascades alpine terrain, notably on pitch 2.
Exposed, unprotected traverse moves on the pitch 3 ridge finish.
Afternoon thunderstorms are common in summer at Washington Pass.

pitchDetail.notes: 120 ft. Awkward rightward-trending hand crack off the ground (crux), then easier climbing to a belay block with an existing anchor.
80 ft. Traverse blocky, sometimes mossy terrain, moving left and right to find passage to the notch between the true and west summits; rap anchor present.

Cross through the notch to the south side and traverse below the crest to the true summit; can be scrambled unroped or belayed — gear is needed if roped since the traverse can't be rappelled on descent.
