blurb: Liberty Bell Mountain (7,720 ft) is the northernmost spire of the Liberty Bell Group in Washington's North Cascades, about one mile south of Washington Pass on the North Cascades Highway (SR-20). Carved from granite of the Golden Horn batholith, it offers some of the shortest-approach, highest-quality alpine rock climbing in the range, from a moderate 4-pitch summit scramble-climb to sustained 5.13 big-wall free routes. The peak was first climbed September 27, 1946 by Fred Beckey, Jerry O'Neil, and Charles Welsh via the line now called the Beckey Route, and its 1965 east-face testpiece, Liberty Crack, is featured in "Fifty Classic Climbs of North America."
hierarchyNote: None. Name, coordinates (48.515435, -120.658365), and elevation (~7,720 ft) all match the real Liberty Bell Mountain at Washington Pass in the North Cascades (confirmed against Wikipedia, Mountain Project, and peak search results), and "Liberty Bell Group" is the correct parent grouping used by Mountain Project/SummitPost (Liberty Bell, Concord Tower, Lexington Tower, North Early Winters Spire, South Early Winters Spire).

routes.fa: Steve Marts and Don McPherson
routes.overview: A 10-pitch line up the East Face proper, right of the Liberty Crack/Thin Red Line/Independence cluster, with an offwidth crux protected by a jammed 2x4.
routes.beta: Ten pitches of varied crack, chimney and face climbing on the East Face, with a difficult-to-protect slopey pitch 1 traverse and an offwidth crux (pitch 6) passing a fixed 2x4 and two bolts to an alcove.
routes.approach: Park at the pond turnout about 1.2 miles east of Washington Pass (south side of SR-20). A cairned trail leaves the east end of the pond into the woods (30-45 min) to the East Face base; traverse left on 3rd/4th class ledges to reach the start.
routes.descent: Gully west of summit, then traverse to a cattle trail down to Blue Lake Trail.
routes.descentText: From the notch west of the true summit, descend the gully staying skiers left, then traverse right to pick up a cattle trail that leads down to the Blue Lake Trail and back to the highway.
routes.detailedRack: Standard rack with gear to 6 inches; a #4 cam is useful and a 6-inch piece is optional if comfortable running it out in wide sections.

pitchDetail.notes: Crack system, traverse left into slopey climbing with difficult protection
Ascend right to a left-facing corner; belay from a small tree
Climb the corner to a ledge with tree belay
Cracks (hands to fingers) to a stance below a roof/dead-end chimney
Traverse right on a no-hands ledge for 30 ft (exposed), then a difficult lieback up a hand/finger crack
Offwidth above, clipping a jammed 2x4 for pro, then two bolts to an alcove
Short chimney to blockier terrain and another offwidth
Easier ground trending left to a tree belay, then to the summit formation via a 4th class traverse

watchOut: Pitch 1's slopey traverse has difficult, sparse protection
Pitch 5's 30-ft no-hands traverse is exposed before the crux lieback
knownHazards: Loose rock in sections of the gully descent

routes.fa: Mikey Schaefer and Shanjean Lee (free ascent August 19, 2019; developed top-down over the 2018-2019 seasons)
routes.overview: A steep, clean, physical free route up Liberty Bell's untouched northeast side, perpetually shaded except for a few hours in high summer.
routes.beta: Ten pitches climbing steeper, cleaner, more physical rock than the other East Face free routes, starting atop a car-sized chockstone in the gully at the base of the east face. The money pitch (P4) is a 45-ft finger crack into a V9/V10 boulder crux; P1-P2 hold a bouldery 5.12+ start and a sustained 5.13- roof pitch.
routes.descent: 150 m of 4th class to the true summit, or rappel the route.
routes.descentText: From the top of pitch 10, either scramble roughly 150 m of 4th class terrain to the true summit and descend via a standard route (e.g. Overexposure), or rappel the line directly using a single 80 m rope.
routes.detailedRack: Doubles from green C3 to #2 Camalot, a single #3, small to medium nuts, 8 quickdraws, 4 shoulder-length slings, and an 80 m rope for the single-rope rappel descent.

pitchDetail.notes: Funky rock improving after the 3rd bolt; finishes with a V6 boulder move
Sustained climbing around an orange dike into a roof with blind reaches
Hand crack and arete; funky moves
The 'money pitch' - 45 ft finger crack into a V9-V10 crux on small feet/crimps, mantel finish
Slab to corner, off-width roof section
4th class approach into funky jams on a flake
Small ledges, open corner laybacking
Overhanging chimney stemming/jamming
Wide stems, arete face climbing
Loose blocks; linkable with pitch 9

proTips: The route is in shade nearly all day, making it a good hot-weather option but colder than the sunny East Face lines - bring an extra layer
watchOut: Loose blocks on pitch 10
Sustained 5.11-5.13 climbing with a bouldery, high-commitment crux far off the deck

routes.fa: Brooke Sandahl (first free ascent of the line, 1991); current standard free variation established by Mikey Schaefer, Shanjean Lee, Blake Herrington, and Nathan Hadley (2017)
routes.overview: The all-free version of the classic Liberty Crack line, keyed by the powerful 'Lithuanian Lip' traverse and roof on pitch 2.
routes.beta: Follows Liberty Crack's original aid line but climbed entirely free; pitch 1 (5.11-) leads to the crux pitch 2 'Lithuanian Lip' (5.13b) - a scrunchy, powerful traverse to finger locks above the lip and committing moves to a small stance. A slab variation on pitch 3 (5.12) avoids the original bolt ladder.
routes.detailedRack: Single rack from purple C3 to #3 Camalot, extra stoppers in small sizes, 8 long slings plus 1 triple-length sling for the slab belay; all belays are fixed.

pitchDetail.notes: Original aid-line climbing, freed
'Lithuanian Lip' - corner into a scrunchy powerful traverse to finger locks above the lip
Slab variation moving right across sloping ledges with cruxy foot-step-ups, bypassing the original bolt ladder
Thin crux before a small roof

routes.approach: Park at the hairpin-turn turnout east of Washington Pass on SR-20; a cairned trail from the east end of the pond leads into the woods to the East Face; traverse left on 3rd/4th class ledges about 40 ft to the base.
routes.descent: Rappel bolted anchors to Beckey Gully (two ~30 m raps), or downclimb the final slab of the Beckey Route.

routes.fa: Alex Bertulis and Don McPherson (1966, wall-style, 180 pitons and 5 bolts placed over 4 days)
routes.overview: A sustained 12-pitch East Face testpiece with a storied ascent history spanning three generations of Cascade climbers.
routes.beta: First climbed wall-style in 1966 by Bertulis and McPherson, freed in 1991 by Steve Risse and Keith Hertel without adding fixed gear (partly yo-yo style over 2 days), and given its first ground-up redpoint in 2009 by Jens Holsten and Max Hasson. Sustained 5.10-5.12 climbing through named features like the Baer Ceiling, Cascade Ceilings, and Well Hung Ceiling.
routes.detailedRack: Doubles from tips to #1, a single #2 and #3, and a single set of small offset wires.

pitchDetail.notes: Horizontal breaks and cracks on the east face right edge
Bolted climbing with a pendulum traverse to a corner
Left-facing quartz corner; 2-bolt anchor
Chimney/off-width to the Baer Ceiling alcove
Ramp to corner with face moves; crux pitch
Thin crack through the Cascade Ceilings
Traverse with flake climbing to a corner
Technical granite to the Well Hung Ceiling corner
Belay move to M&M ledge
Chimney and blocky corner
Splitter 2-3 inch crack
Slab traverse to the ridge and summit

routes.fa: Ron Burgner and Don McPherson (1966)
routes.face: Upper Southwest Face (above the Liberty-Concord notch)
routes.overview: A quick 2-pitch line of clean 5.8 finger crack and laybacking to the summit; also the standard rappel/descent route for climbs on Liberty Bell.
routes.beta: From the Liberty-Concord notch, either a runout 5.8R left variation (poor pro for ~30 ft) or the original line up the top of the notch ramp reach a shared anchor; pitch 2 climbs up and right to a clean white dihedral to a sloping ledge with chain anchors.

pitchDetail.notes: Original line from top of the notch ramp, or a left variation with ~30 ft of runout, poorly protected rock, to a shared chain anchor
Up and right to a clean white dihedral, to a sloping ledge with chain anchor

routes.proNeeds: Gear to 2 inches; consider several small RPs for the runout left variation.
routes.approach: Via the Blue Lake Trail, same approach as the Beckey Route, to the Liberty-Concord notch.
routes.descent: Two rappels with one rope back to the notch.
watchOut: Frequently crossed by rappel ropes from other parties, since this is the standard descent line off Liberty Bell
Left-variation pitch 1 is runout and poorly protected (5.8R)

routes.fa: Steve Marts, Don McPherson, and Fred Stanley (July 1965)
routes.overview: The famous East Face line featured in Fifty Classic Climbs of North America, climbable full-aid, hybrid, or free with a fixed rope; keyed by the 'Lithuanian Lip' roof on pitch 2.
routes.beta: A 12-pitch, Grade V east-face route that accommodates full aid, hybrid, or free climbing styles. Most parties take two days, often fixing the first three pitches and returning the next day to climb to the summit.
routes.itinerary: Most climbers take two days: fix and rappel the first three pitches on day one, then jug/climb to the high point and continue to the summit on day two.
routes.detailedRack: One 60 m rope, a micro-stopper set, doubles in TCUs/C3s (#00-3), doubles in cams 0.5-3 inches, 12-16 trad draws, 3x 48-inch slings, 2 alpine etriers, and a jumar setup or equivalent.
routes.approach: Park at the hairpin-turn turnout east of Washington Pass on SR-20. A cairned trail from the pond's east end leads into the woods to Liberty Bell's East Face; traverse left on 3rd/4th class ledges about 40 ft to the base.
routes.descent: Rappel bolted anchors to Beckey Gully (two ~30 m raps), or downclimb the sheltered final slab of the Beckey Route.

pitchDetail.notes: Low-angle traversing approach pitch, 5th class
Right-facing corner and splitter crack to a clean bolted belay below the Lithuanian Lip
The 'Lithuanian Lip' roof with a bolt ladder
Face climbing with fixed anchors
Crack jamming, increasing difficulty
Corner climbing
Block surmounting; crux
Steep moves, ramp/corner with an overlap
Quartz features and chimneys
Excellent pitch on a left-facing corner
Easy low-angle grooves for 160 ft; belay from a tree, summit ~60 ft above on the right
200+ ft of mostly flat, gravel-covered terrain heading west, away from the route

watchOut: The Lithuanian Lip has no recorded repeats of its only completely free ascent (done with a pre-placed static rope) - most parties aid or hybrid this pitch

routes.fa: Bryan Burdo
routes.face: Southwest Face (shares start with the Beckey Route)
routes.overview: A variation off the Beckey Route's first pitch, climbing an open-book corner system of 5.7 finger and hand cracks back to rejoin the Beckey line.
routes.beta: After the Beckey Route's first pitch, traverse right along the 'Beckey ledge' past a few trees and climb an open-book corner system to the top of the buttress, rejoining the Beckey Route. A direct start begins a few feet right of a red blocky overhang and trends left to rejoin the original line; another variation continues past the Beckey ledge to an overhanging leaning block with a tree belay before moving up and left to rejoin the route. Includes a 10-15 ft 5.8 crack with an old piton and finishes on a 10-ft 5.6 slab to the summit.
routes.proNeeds: Pro to 4 inches.

routes.fa: Fred Beckey, Jerry O'Neil, and Charles Welsh (September 27, 1946) - the first ascent of Liberty Bell Mountain
routes.overview: The classic, most popular route on Liberty Bell: 3 pitches of clean class-5 climbing plus a short 4th class finish to the summit.
routes.beta: The standard easiest way up Liberty Bell and the most-climbed route at Washington Pass, with frequent weekend traffic jams. Chimney and face climbing lead to a 6-ft finger-crack traverse past a large roof (clipping a fixed pin), then a dihedral to the summit shoulder and a short 5.6 boulder problem before easy scrambling to the top.
routes.approach: Hike the Blue Lake Trail (~2.5 miles) to a marked climbers' trail branching left; stay on the climbers' trail as it climbs (even if it feels like heading too far climber's right). At a fork near Concord's west side, bear left and traverse under Concord toward the Beckey Gully separating it from Liberty Bell; the loose gully is best climbed staying left low, then right near the top, to the notch between the peaks.

pitchDetail.notes: Climb the chimney or the face to its left to a large ledge with a tree
Continue up the chimney ~30 m past chockstones and blocky slabs to 3rd/4th class terrain; often considered the hardest section
Ascend 4th class terrain to a 6 ft finger-crack traverse past a large roof (clip a fixed pin), then a dihedral back right to the summit shoulder
A slabby ~10 ft boulder problem, then easy 3rd/4th class terrain to the summit

routes.detailedRack: A single set of stoppers and cams to 2 inches is more than enough; carry an axe and/or crampons depending on snow conditions in the gully, and bring bug repellent.
routes.descent: Two single-rope rappels (60 m) from bolted anchors below the summit, or downclimb the ridge/boulder problem; many parties instead descend via Overexposure.
routes.descentText: From the summit, either downclimb the ridge and boulder problem to reach rappel bolts for two single-rope (60 m) rappels, or use the rappel station reached via a left-trending groove with tree slings below the true summit.
proTips: Start early - this is the most popular route at Washington Pass and gets crowded/backed up on weekends in peak season
watchOut: Weekend crowding causes bottlenecks at belays

routes.fa: Hans Kraus and John Rupley (1956, original ascent); first free ascent by Sandy Bill, Ron Burgner, Ian Martin, and Frank Tarver (1966)
routes.overview: Five pitches up the northwest side of Liberty Bell to its west summit, with a signature dihedral crux pitch of interesting features.
routes.beta: A left-trending gully/crack system leads to a crack system left of a chimney (traversing a horn feature or bypassing via a 4th class gully), then a left-facing corner of large thin flakes, the signature dihedral pitch 4 crux (some runout near the top), and mid-5th class terrain to the west summit.

pitchDetail.notes: Left-trending gully/crack system past a couple of trees to a good belay ledge
Crack system left of a chimney; traverse over a horn feature or bypass left via a 4th class gully
Left-facing corner system of large thin flakes; crux ~25 ft off the belay; ~100 ft
Signature dihedral pitch with interesting features; runout 20-30 ft near the top
Easy terrain to the west summit of Liberty Bell

routes.detailedRack: Standard trad rack with doubles in finger sizes, narrow cams (TCUs, C3s); small nuts/offsets helpful; a 60 m rope is sufficient.
routes.approach: From the Blue Lake Trailhead, follow the trail through forest to a meadow; after a stream crossing, a cairn marks the climbers' trail toward the Liberty Bell-Concord Tower notch. Scramble up the rock wall left of the notch (4th class) onto the western slab, traverse leftward along a bench, then ascend rightward about 60 ft to the crack/gully start.
routes.descent: Two single-rope rappels from fixed anchors on the shoulder beneath the Beckey Route's friction pitch, reaching the Liberty-Concord notch.

routes.fa: Mikey Schaefer (August 16, 2016)
routes.overview: An 11-pitch East Face free climb sharing its lower pitches with Freedom or Death or Thin Red Line before branching into sustained 5.12-5.13 climbing including a 'mega slab' pitch.
routes.beta: Climbs the first three pitches of Freedom or Death (5.10, 5.11, 5.11), then a right-leaning overlap (P4, 5.12-) into a traverse with layback flakes and a mantle (P5, 5.12- crux), an arete with a big hand-rail traverse (P6, 5.12), a 165 ft 'mega slab' pitch with 14 bolts and 4 crux moves (P7, 5.12+), and a razor-thin slab crux (P8, 5.13-) before easing off through P9-P11 (5.10-5.10+).
routes.detailedRack: 2x #0 C3 to #.75, 1x #1 to #3, one set of small to medium nuts, 8 quickdraws, 6 shoulder-length slings, and 2 ropes if rappelling.
routes.approach: Starts on either Freedom or Death or Thin Red Line.
routes.descent: Possible to scramble to the top staying roped up, or rappel the route using two ropes.

pitchDetail.notes: Shared with Freedom or Death
Right-leaning overlap; tricky locks and smearing
Traverse left with layback flakes and a mantle move; crux
Arete climbing with a big hand-rail traverse; 165 ft
'Mega slab' pitch, 165 ft, 14 bolts, 4 crux moves
Starts easy, turns vicious with a razor-like crux slab sequence
Varied climbing on decent rock
Low-angle terrain with an optional flair-climbing line
Left-facing corner with finger cracks transitioning to low-angle terrain

blurb: Sahale Mountain (8,680+ ft) rises at the head of Boston Basin in North Cascades National Park, on the Skagit/Chelan county line, its summit crowned by the small Sahale Glacier and bordered by the Quien Sabe and Davenport Glaciers. A glaciated granite peak first climbed in August 1897 by John Charlton and Albert H. Sylvester, it is regarded as one of the North Cascades' classic introductory glacier climbs, reached either by the popular Sahale Arm trail or the steeper, more committing Quien Sabe Glacier out of Boston Basin.

routes.rappels: No rappel required to descend; an optional single rappel from a slung block atop the summit is used by some parties instead of down-climbing the final block.
objHaz: lingering steep snow on the Arm into July
routes.face: Southwest side of the summit pyramid, above Sahale Glacier
routes.overview: The standard and most popular route up Sahale Mountain: a long trail approach up Sahale Arm to Sahale Glacier Camp, followed by a rope-team ascent of the small Sahale Glacier to a short 4th-class/5.0 rock step at the true summit.
routes.beta: Considered one of the best introductory glacier climbs in the North Cascades, combining a spectacular alpine trail approach with a short, straightforward glacier and a brief technical finish. Most parties camp at Sahale Glacier Camp to split the trip over two days; strong parties do it car-to-car in a long day.
routes.approach: From Marblemount, drive Cascade River Road (paved to about mile 10, then gravel with potholes/washboard, no RVs or vehicles over 22 ft) about 23 miles to its end at the Cascade Pass Trailhead (3,660 ft). Hike the Cascade Pass Trail 3.7 miles and 1,700 ft to Cascade Pass, then continue north on the Sahale Arm Trail through alpine meadows and up the Arm, reaching Sahale Glacier Camp at about 7,400 ft (5.9 miles and 3,940 ft of gain total from the trailhead).
routes.descent: Descend by reversing the ascent route down the Sahale Glacier and the Sahale Arm/Cascade Pass Trail.
routes.descentText: From the summit, down-climb (or rappel) the short summit block back to the glacier, then descend the Sahale Glacier on the ascent track (glissading firm snow where safe and free of crevasses) back to Sahale Glacier Camp. From camp, follow the Sahale Arm Trail back down to Cascade Pass and the Cascade Pass Trail to the trailhead.
routes.itinerary: Day 1: Drive to Cascade Pass Trailhead (3,660 ft), hike ~5.9 mi / 3,940 ft to Sahale Glacier Camp, set up camp. Day 2: Early start, rope up for glacier travel, ascend the Sahale Glacier to the southwest side of the summit pyramid, climb the short 5.0 summit block, then reverse the route back to camp and out to the trailhead.
routes.bail: Retreat by reversing the glacier descent and Sahale Arm trail; no rappels are required except an optional single rappel from the slung block below the true summit if down-climbing the final block feels unsafe.
routes.turnaround: Set a firm turnaround time based on your pace to camp and back before dark; deteriorating weather, softening glacier snow, or afternoon thunderstorm buildup are all valid reasons to turn back before the summit block.
routes.bestSeason: Late July through September offers the most consistent conditions, once Cascade River Road has opened (typically late June, sometimes later in heavy-snow years) and the Sahale Arm trail has melted out. Snow can still cover sections of the Arm into July, requiring an ice axe; by early fall the glacier can be more crevassed and camp water sources may dwindle.
routes.comms: No reliable cell coverage in the Cascade Pass/Boston Basin backcountry of North Cascades National Park; a satellite communicator or PLB is recommended for emergency contact.
routes.detailedRack: A light glacier kit is sufficient: a rope for the rope team plus crevasse-rescue gear (prusiks, pulley, a couple of slings, maybe a picket or two for steeper snow), and a few small pieces of rock protection or slings to back up the short 5.0 chimney move to the true summit, where a slung block also serves as a rappel anchor.
routes.proNeeds: Minimal rock protection needed — a few slings or small cams for the final summit block; the rest of the route is glacier travel protected by roped travel and occasional pickets on steeper snow.
gear: A few small cams/nuts or slings for the summit block
proTips: Camp at Sahale Glacier Camp (7,400 ft) to break the long approach into two days and catch sunrise on the glacier.
The Sahale Glacier is small and not very active, but roping up is still recommended, especially later in the season as crevasses open.
An early start avoids softening snow on the glacier and the afternoon thunderstorm risk common in North Cascades summers.
watchOut: Snow can linger on the Sahale Arm trail into July, requiring an ice axe for safe travel on steep sections.
Backcountry permits for Sahale Glacier Camp are in very high demand and often need to be reserved well in advance.
Mountain goats habituated to hikers frequent the Arm — keep dogs and urine away from them and give them distance.
knownHazards: Crevasse hazard exists on the Sahale Glacier despite its small size, particularly late in the season.
The final summit block involves exposed 4th-class/5.0 rock moves above steep terrain.
Afternoon thunderstorms are common in North Cascades summers, posing lightning risk on the open glacier and ridge.

pitchDetail.notes: 3.7 mi, 1,700 ft gain on maintained trail

pitchDetail.pitch: Cascade Pass to Sahale Glacier Camp
pitchDetail.notes: Continue north on the Sahale Arm climbers' trail; totals 5.9 mi / 3,940 ft gain from the trailhead to camp (7,400 ft)
Rope up and ascend the Sahale Glacier to the southwest side of the summit pyramid; watch for crevasses, especially late season
Short chimney-like feature on the south side; a few pieces of rock pro protect the final moves; slung block on top serves as a rappel anchor

road.name: Cascade River Road
road.status: Open (seasonal)
road.seasonalGate: Typically opens to the Cascade Pass parking lot by end of June (sometimes later in heavy-snow years); closed by snow in winter
road.driveNote: Paved to about mile 10 from Marblemount, then gravel with potholes and washboard to road's end at 3,660 ft; no RVs, large trailers, or vehicles over 22 ft.

climate.forecastZone: NWAC West Slopes North zone
climate.typical: Wet, maritime-influenced North Cascades climate with a short dry-ish summer window and heavy snow accumulation the rest of the year.
climate.spring: Deep lingering snowpack; road and trail often still snow-covered or gated into June, with avalanche/cornice concerns on approach slopes.
climate.summer: Brief reliable climbing window, roughly July-September; afternoon thunderstorms and occasional wildfire smoke are the main summer hazards.
climate.fall: Snow returns by October in most years; road and trail access closes for the season by late fall.
climate.winter: Heavy snowfall, road closed/gated, access is a ski-mountaineering undertaking.

access.landManager: North Cascades National Park Service Complex (NPS)
access.fees: No park entrance fee; a free backcountry/wilderness permit is required for any overnight stay.
access.permit: Free wilderness permit required for overnight camping (e.g. at Sahale Glacier Camp); a portion of sites are reservable in advance via Recreation.gov, the rest first-come/first-served at the Wilderness Information Center in Marblemount. Day climbs to the summit do not require a permit.
access.closures: Road and trail are generally accessible late June through October; closed by snow in winter and can wash out or be gated after storms.

timing.recommendedStart: Alpine/early-morning start from camp to travel the glacier on firmer snow and beat afternoon thunderstorm buildup

emergency.rangerStation: North Cascades Wilderness Information Center, Marblemount, WA — (360) 854-7200
emergency.nearestHospital: Skagit Valley Hospital, 300 Hospital Pkwy, Mount Vernon, WA (Level III Emergency Department, 24 hours)
emergency.notes: No reliable cell coverage in the backcountry; report trip plans via wilderness permit and carry a satellite communicator/PLB. Dial 911 for search and rescue; the park coordinates SAR response.

routes.rappels: No fixed rappel stations; descent is by down-climbing/reversing the glacier and ridge.
objHaz: loose rock on the summit ridge
exposed unmaintained approach trail with stream crossings
routes.face: West side of Sahale, via the Boston-Sahale col below Sharkfin Tower
routes.overview: A steeper, more glaciated alternative to the Sahale Arm route, climbing the Quien Sabe Glacier from Boston Basin to the Boston-Sahale col before finishing on an exposed rock-and-snow summit ridge.
routes.beta: The less-traveled and more committing of Sahale's two standard routes, often combined with a Boston Basin camp and sometimes linked with Boston Peak or Sharkfin Tower objectives. It involves more sustained glacier travel and looser rock than the Sahale Arm/Sahale Glacier route.
routes.approach: From Marblemount, drive Cascade River Road about 21.7-22 miles to a small pullout (room for roughly 6-8 cars) opposite Johannesburg Mountain at about 3,200 ft, near the old Diamond Mine Road fork. Follow the abandoned mine road about 1 mile to where an unmaintained climbers' trail breaks off steeply to the right; follow it up through four stream crossings to treeline and Boston Basin low camp (~5,300 ft), continuing on to high camp (~6,400 ft). Approach takes roughly 3-4 hours with a full overnight pack.
routes.descent: Reverse the Quien Sabe Glacier ascent and the Boston Basin climbers' trail back to the trailhead.
routes.descentText: From the summit, reverse the exposed ridge traverse back to the Boston-Sahale col, then descend the north side of the Quien Sabe Glacier (down-climbing or glissading firm snow where safe) toward Boston Basin high camp. From camp, follow the climbers' trail back down through the stream crossings and the abandoned mine road to the Cascade River Road pullout.
routes.itinerary: Day 1: Drive to the Boston Basin trailhead pullout on Cascade River Road (~3,200 ft), hike the climbers' trail to Boston Basin low camp (5,300 ft) or high camp (6,400 ft), 3-4 hours. Day 2: Alpine start, ascend the Quien Sabe Glacier's north side to the Boston-Sahale col, traverse the exposed summit ridge and climb the loose class 3-4 summit block (4-5 hours up, 7-10 hours round trip from camp). Day 3: Hike out to the trailhead.
routes.bail: Retreat down the Quien Sabe Glacier the way you ascended; there are no fixed rappel stations on this route, so descent is by down-climbing/reversing the glacier and ridge.
routes.turnaround: Establish a turnaround time from camp that accounts for the 4-5 hour ascent and a comparable descent; cornice collapse, whiteout, or afternoon thunderstorms near the col are all reasons to retreat before the summit ridge.
routes.bestSeason: June through September, with route character shifting through the season: more continuous snow cover (easier travel but more avalanche/cornice concern) in June-July, and a more broken, crevassed glacier with looser rock exposed by August-September.
routes.comms: No reliable cell coverage in Boston Basin or on Sahale Mountain; carry a satellite communicator or PLB for emergencies.
routes.detailedRack: A standard glacier travel kit: rope, harness, crevasse-rescue hardware (prusiks/pulley), a couple of pickets for the steeper upper glacier, and a handful of slings for protecting the loose 4th-class summit scramble — no dedicated rock rack needed.
routes.proNeeds: Light protection only — slings for the final 4th-class summit block and pickets for the steeper glacier sections; the route is otherwise glacier travel and scrambling.
proTips: Camp at Boston Basin high camp (6,400 ft) to shorten summit day and climb on firmer early-morning snow.
Stay on the left (north) side of the Quien Sabe Glacier on the ascent to minimize crevasse exposure.

Conditions vary significantly year to year — check recent trip reports (CascadeClimbers.com, WTA, The Mountaineers) before committing, as cornices near the Boston-Sahale col can persist into early summer.
watchOut: The Boston Basin climbers' trail is unmaintained, steep, and crosses several streams — expect a demanding 3-4 hour approach with full overnight packs.
Cornices on the ridge near the Boston-Sahale col can persist into early summer and pose a serious fall/collapse hazard.
The final summit ridge is exposed, loose class 3 terrain.
knownHazards: Crevasse danger increases on the steeper upper sections of the Quien Sabe Glacier, especially later in the season.
Rockfall is possible below Sharkfin Tower and along the loose summit ridge.
Boston Basin's approach trail involves exposed, unmaintained tread and stream crossings that can be hazardous in high water or early season.

pitchDetail.pitch: Trailhead to Boston Basin low camp
pitchDetail.notes: Abandoned mine road for 1 mi, then steep unmaintained trail with 4 stream crossings, to ~5,300 ft
Continue to high camp at ~6,400 ft
Ascend the left (north) side of the glacier to the Boston-Sahale col below Sharkfin Tower; crevasse hazard, cornices can persist into early summer
Traverse exposed rock/snow ridge; a few slings protect the 4th-class summit block

road.name: Cascade River Road
road.status: Open (seasonal)
road.seasonalGate: Typically opens by end of June (later in heavy-snow years); closed by snow in winter
road.driveNote: Paved to about mile 10 from Marblemount, then gravel with potholes and washboard; trailhead pullout is near mile 21.7-22, before the road's official end.

climate.forecastZone: NWAC West Slopes North zone
climate.typical: Wet, maritime-influenced North Cascades climate with a short dry-ish summer window.
climate.spring: Deep snowpack lingers on the approach and glacier; avalanche and cornice hazard into early summer.
climate.summer: Primary climbing window, roughly June-September; glacier becomes more broken and crevassed as summer progresses.
climate.fall: Snow returns by October in most years; access closes for the season by late fall.
climate.winter: Heavy snowfall, road closed/gated.

access.landManager: North Cascades National Park Service Complex (NPS)
access.fees: No park entrance fee; a free backcountry/wilderness permit is required for any overnight stay in Boston Basin.
access.permit: Free wilderness permit required for overnight camping at Boston Basin low or high camp; some sites reservable in advance via Recreation.gov, remainder first-come/first-served at the Wilderness Information Center in Marblemount.
access.closures: Road and trail generally accessible late June through October; closed by snow in winter.

timing.recommendedStart: Alpine start from camp to climb the glacier on firm snow and clear the col/summit ridge before afternoon weather

emergency.rangerStation: North Cascades Wilderness Information Center, Marblemount, WA — (360) 854-7200
emergency.nearestHospital: Skagit Valley Hospital, 300 Hospital Pkwy, Mount Vernon, WA (Level III Emergency Department, 24 hours)
emergency.notes: No reliable cell coverage in the backcountry; report trip plans via wilderness permit and carry a satellite communicator/PLB. Dial 911 for search and rescue; the park coordinates SAR response.

blurb: The Obelisk is a small, recently developed sport-climbing formation on the shoulder of Silver Peak, in Silver Basin above Snoqualmie Pass in Washington's central Cascades. It is composed of volcanic-metamorphic rock similar to other North Bend-area crags, sits beside a larger neighboring formation called "The Citidel" and its own neighbor Sofa King Buttress, and is reached via a rough Forest Service road and a short scree/talus approach. As of 2026 it hosts a single documented bolted route, Lone Wolf (5.10a), established in 2020 by Wylie Moreshead.
hierarchyNote: Verified via an exact GPS match (47.36859, -121.45406) to Mountain Project's "The Obelisk" sub-area page, itself nested under an area literally named "The Citidel" (matching this DB's misspelled parentArea) in the Snoqualmie Pass Area of the Central-West Cascades. Flag for future researchers: Washington has two unrelated features both called "Citadel" — (1) this small, non-wilderness sport-climbing formation (~4,585 ft) on Silver Peak's shoulder near Snoqualmie Pass, reached by FS road NF-9070, and (2) a separate, much better-known 7,120-ft alpine summit named "The Citadel" near Cathedral Rock deep in the Alpine Lakes Wilderness (accessed via Tucquala Meadows/Circle Lake), which has its own SummitPost page and is NOT this peak. The coordinate match confirms the given data correctly refers to formation (1), the Snoqualmie Pass crag — no correction needed, just noting the ambiguity so it isn't conflated later.

routes.fa: Wylie Moreshead, 2020
routes.rappels: Bolted chain anchors at the top of each of the 2 pitches; rappel or lower pitch-by-pitch back to the base.
objHaz: Loose, sharp rock typical of a newly developed/unweathered route
Rockfall risk on approach and route — helmet recommended
routes.season: Roughly Jul-Oct (approach road/trail typically snow-free only July through October/November)
routes.overview: A 2-pitch, 200-ft bolted sport line straight up the main face of The Obelisk, a distinctive pinnacle-like rock embossment protruding from the mountainside in Silver Basin near Snoqualmie Pass.
routes.beta: Lone Wolf follows the obvious, continuous bolt line up The Obelisk's main face for two pitches of 5.10a climbing to bolted chain anchors. It is currently the only documented route on this formation, established in 2020 as part of a small, still-developing crag alongside the neighboring Sofa King Buttress.
routes.approach: From I-90 exit 54 (Hyak/Gold Creek), follow Hyak Drive NE east; pavement ends after about 0.6 mi and the road becomes Forest Road NF-9070 (high-clearance vehicle recommended — potholes and washouts are common). Continue on NF-9070 roughly 4.7 miles to its junction with the Pacific Crest Trail, then take the left fork of NF-9070 past that junction and follow it to its end at a large turnaround below Silver Basin. From the turnaround, pick up the climbers' trail up into the basin, cross the creek, and cross talus/scree to the base of The Obelisk — about 20-30 minutes from the road-end turnaround.
routes.descent: Rappel the route from bolted chain anchors atop each pitch.
routes.descentText: From the top of pitch 2, rappel (or lower with a sufficiently long rope) to the bolted chain anchor atop pitch 1, then rappel again to the base of the face. No walk-off descent has been documented for this route.
routes.bail: Retreat is straightforward at any point: lower or rappel from either pitch's bolted chain anchor.
routes.turnaround: Because the approach crosses unmaintained talus/scree and a creek with no formal trail, start early enough to reverse the basin approach and the rough forest-road drive before dark, and turn around if weather deteriorates enough to make the loose approach terrain or rock unsafe.
routes.bestSeason: The access road (NF-9070) and basin approach are typically only snow-free from around July through October/November; the road is unplowed and often impassable with snow outside that window, and washouts have made high-clearance vehicles necessary in recent years. As a newly bolted, low-traffic formation (established 2020) with very little online trip-report history, expect variable rock cleanliness and minimal current-conditions beta.
routes.detailedRack: A standard sport rack of quickdraws sized for a 2-pitch bolted line; bring enough draws to cover the longer pitch plus the anchor. Bolted chain anchors at the top of each pitch allow rappelling or lowering — no trad gear is needed for protection.
routes.proNeeds: Fully bolted sport protection throughout; no gear placements required.
whatToBring: Sturdy approach shoes for talus/scree and creek crossing
Water — no reliable water source noted at the crag itself
proTips: Wear a helmet — Mountain Project's area description specifically flags sharp, loose rock and falling-rock hazard on this newly developed formation.
This is a very low-traffic, recently bolted crag (FA 2020) with almost no online trip reports or comments — expect to route-find the approach yourself and climb with minimal beta.
watchOut: Loose and sharp rock is characteristic of this new route/area — test holds carefully.
Forest Road NF-9070 is rough with potholes and washouts; a high-clearance vehicle is recommended to reach the road-end turnaround.
No maintained trail beyond the turnaround — the approach crosses scree/talus and a creek crossing.
knownHazards: Rockfall from loose, largely unweathered rock typical of a newly developed climb.
Rough, pothole- and washout-prone approach road requiring a high-clearance vehicle.

pitchDetail.notes: Follows the obvious bolt line up the lower main face to a bolted chain anchor.
Continues the bolt line up the remainder of the main face to a bolted chain anchor at the top.

road.name: Forest Road NF-9070
road.status: Rough, native-surface Forest Service road with reported potholes and washouts; high-clearance vehicle recommended
road.seasonalGate: Unplowed and typically snow-bound outside roughly June/July through October/November
road.driveNote: From I-90 exit 54 (Hyak/Gold Creek), take Hyak Drive NE, which becomes NF-9070 after pavement ends (~0.6 mi); continue ~4.7 miles to the PCT junction, then follow the left fork of NF-9070 to the road-end turnaround below Silver Basin.

climate.forecastZone: Snoqualmie Pass / Central Cascades crest
climate.typical: Typical Washington Cascade-crest pattern: wet, heavy-snow winters and drier, milder summers
climate.spring: Deep, lingering snowpack on the road and approach into June
climate.summer: Warmest and driest window (roughly Jul-Sep) — best conditions for the approach and climbing
climate.fall: Cooling with increasing rain by October/November; early snow can make the road/approach impassable
climate.winter: Deep unplowed snowpack; NF-9070 not accessible

access.landManager: Mount Baker-Snoqualmie National Forest (Snoqualmie Ranger District)
access.fees: Northwest Forest Pass (or America the Beautiful interagency pass) required to park at the trailhead/road-end
access.passRequired: Northwest Forest Pass / America the Beautiful Pass
access.closures: NF-9070 is unplowed and generally impassable with snow outside roughly June/July-October/November

emergency.sheriffDispatch: King County Sheriff's Office non-emergency: (206) 296-3311 (dial 911 for emergencies)
emergency.rangerStation: Snoqualmie Ranger District - North Bend Office, 902 SE North Bend Way Bldg 1, North Bend, WA 98045, (425) 888-1421
emergency.nearestHospital: Snoqualmie Valley Hospital, 712 SW Mt Si Blvd, North Bend, WA 98045, (425) 831-2313
emergency.notes: Search and rescue in this area is coordinated by the King County Sheriff's Office with volunteer King County SAR units (including Seattle Mountain Rescue, based in North Bend). Cell coverage in Silver Basin is unreliable — carry a backup means of emergency communication.

blurb: Mossy Loaf is not a mountain summit but a small, two-tiered, moss- and fern-covered rock wall directly beside the paved Mountain Loop Highway, roughly a quarter mile before the Barlow Pass trailhead in the historic Monte Cristo mining district, Mount Baker-Snoqualmie National Forest (Darrington Ranger District), Snohomish County, WA. The wall shares the friable, "choss"-prone rock character typical of the broader Monte Cristo climbing area and was developed as a short 2-route trad crag by Josh Glantz and Amber Michaelis on September 5, 2020.
hierarchyNote: Mossy Loaf is correctly filed under "Monte Cristo" (matches Mountain Project's own area hierarchy), and the given coordinates (48.03015, -121.44552) match Mountain Project's Mossy Loaf listing exactly, so placement is verified. However, this is NOT a distinct mountain peak/summit — it is a small (35-45 ft) two-tiered roadside rock wall sitting essentially at Barlow Pass elevation (~2,300-2,360 ft), about 0.4 km (a quarter mile) before the Barlow Pass trailhead parking area on the paved Mountain Loop Highway. It does NOT require the well-known 4-mile hike down the gated Monte Cristo road to the historic ghost-town site — that hike leads to an entirely different, more remote location roughly 4 miles further southeast (Monte Cristo townsite proper, ~47.986, -121.394, elev. ~2,762 ft per Wikipedia). "Summit"-style fields (highPointFt, etc.) are not meaningfully applicable to this crag.

routes.fa: Josh Glantz and Amber Michaelis, September 5, 2020
routes.rockGrade: 5.7 (also 5a French / 15 Ewbanks / V+ UIAA / MVS 4b British)
routes.overview: A one-pitch 5.7 trad line up Mossy Loaf's lower tier, climbing an open-book past a distinctive 'monkey face' rock feature to a roof, then a steep lieback flake crux before easing off on slab.
routes.beta: Follows an open book formation through moss and ferns. Climbers pull a feature nicknamed 'Harambe's nose' to reach a block above and right of a monkey-face visible in the rock, then proceed under a roof overhang (placing gear), traverse left into an 'awesome lieback flake' — the 5.7 crux — and finish up easier slab. The route is named for the monkey-face feature in the wall.
routes.approach: No real hike is needed: park at or near the Barlow Pass trailhead on the (paved) Mountain Loop Highway, about 31 miles east of Granite Falls / roughly 15 miles past the Verlot Public Service Center. Mossy Loaf is the first wall along the road on the left (north) side, about a quarter mile (roughly 0.4 km) before the Barlow Pass parking lot — a short roadside walk, not a backcountry approach.
routes.descent: Walk off along the base of the tiers back to the roadside; no fixed rappel anchor is documented for this single-pitch line.
routes.descentText: From the top of the route, downclimb the easy slab above the crux or scramble off along the wall's edge back to road level. Mountain Project notes the standard walk-off between tiers involves some bushwhacking, which is one reason the neighboring route You Moss Be Joking exists as a lower-impact link.
routes.bail: Single pitch, only 45 ft: retreat by downclimbing the easier slab above the lieback crux, or build a gear anchor and lower/rappel on trad protection — no fixed hardware is documented on this route.
routes.turnaround: Not an alpine turnaround-time situation given the roadside single-pitch length, but the wall is shaded and moss/fern-covered — if holds are wet or slick after rain, back off before committing to the lieback crux rather than pushing through greasy rock.
routes.bestSeason: Developers describe the wall as naturally mossy even after cleaning; drier summer-into-early-fall conditions (once accumulated winter/spring moisture has had time to dry out) will generally offer the cleanest holds, though no specific climbing-season window is published for this crag.
routes.detailedRack: Standard trad rack to 5 inches; a single Black Diamond #4 cam is useful near the top and could allow running out the final easier ground.
routes.proNeeds: Gear to 5 inches; solid finger-to-hand-size placements protect the lieback-flake crux and the roof traverse best.
whatToBring: Approach/hiking shoes are unnecessary beyond a short roadside walk
A stiff brush for lingering moss on holds
Helmet — Monte Cristo-area rock is generally chossy
proTips: The route's name comes from a monkey-face feature in the rock passed partway up, near the 'Harambe's nose' hold.
Route developers cleaned the line but note it remains naturally mossy — expect some residual moss/lichen off the main hold line.
watchOut: Holds can be damp or moss-covered after rain given the shaded, fern-covered character of the wall.
General Monte Cristo-area rock is described as chossy/friable — test holds before committing.
knownHazards: Loose or friable ('choss') rock typical of the broader Monte Cristo climbing area.
Wet or moss-covered holds in the open-book and roof sections after rain.

access.landManager: USDA Forest Service, Mount Baker-Snoqualmie National Forest, Darrington Ranger District
access.fees: Standard Mount Baker-Snoqualmie NF trailhead fee applies at Barlow Pass (day-use fee, or pass in lieu of fee)
access.passRequired: Northwest Forest Pass or America the Beautiful/Interagency Pass required for parking at the Barlow Pass trailhead
access.closures: None specific to the roadside pull-off itself; the onward Monte Cristo road beyond Barlow Pass has been permanently gated to motor vehicles since flood damage in 2003 and 2006 (irrelevant to reaching Mossy Loaf, which sits before the gate).

road.name: Mountain Loop Highway to Barlow Pass
road.status: Paved and open to vehicles as far as Barlow Pass (weather permitting); Mossy Loaf is passed directly along this stretch, just before the Barlow Pass trailhead lot — no need to continue onto the gated Monte Cristo road beyond.
road.seasonalGate: No seasonal gate on the highway itself near Barlow Pass, though snow/ice can affect winter access; the separate Monte Cristo road past Barlow Pass is permanently closed to vehicles (not seasonal) due to flood damage.
road.driveNote: From Granite Falls, drive Mountain Loop Highway east about 31 miles (roughly 15 miles past the Verlot Public Service Center) toward Barlow Pass; Mossy Loaf's two tiers are on the left/north side of the road just before the Barlow Pass parking area.

climate.typical: Low-elevation (~2,300-2,360 ft), forested, shaded valley-bottom setting on the west slope of the Cascades near Barlow Pass — consistent with the persistent moss and fern growth on the wall noted by the route developers.

emergency.sheriffDispatch: 911 for emergencies; Snohomish County Sheriff non-emergency (425) 407-3999 (or (425) 775-3000 in southwest county)
emergency.rangerStation: Verlot Public Service Center, Darrington Ranger District, (360) 691-7791, 33515 Mountain Loop Hwy, Granite Falls, WA
emergency.nearestHospital: Cascade Valley Hospital (Skagit Regional Health), Arlington, WA — nearest full-service ER to the Mountain Loop Highway corridor
emergency.notes: Mossy Loaf sits directly off a paved, drive-to highway near Barlow Pass, so evacuation/access logistics are far simpler here than for the backcountry alpine routes elsewhere in the Monte Cristo area.

routes.corrections: Given peak elevation was null; sourced from Mountain Project as 2,301 ft (701 m). Important context correction: this crag is a short roadside pull-off near Barlow Pass, NOT reached via the well-documented 4-mile gated-road hike to the historic Monte Cristo townsite — those are two different, non-overlapping approaches that happen to share the 'Monte Cristo' area name.

routes.fa: Josh Glantz and Amber Michaelis, September 5, 2020
routes.rockGrade: 5.5 (also 4b French / 13 Ewbanks / IV+ UIAA / MS 4a British)
routes.overview: A short, easy one-pitch 5.5 that traverses under a small roof to a good crimp then eases up slab; used by some parties mainly as a lower-impact way to reach Mossy Loaf's upper tier.
routes.beta: Follows the obvious scrub line traversing left under a small roof. Pull the roof on a good crimp, step right, and walk up the slab. Belay at a large dead tree just before the hiking trail. Mountain Project describes the climbing itself as 'crappy' but notes it offers a way up to the upper tier with less bushwhacking than the standard walk-off.
routes.approach: No real hike is needed: park at or near the Barlow Pass trailhead on the (paved) Mountain Loop Highway, about 31 miles east of Granite Falls / roughly 15 miles past the Verlot Public Service Center. Mossy Loaf is the only wall directly off the road (left side), about 100 yards before the Barlow Pass/Monte Cristo trailhead — a short roadside walk, not a backcountry approach.
routes.descent: Walk off from the large dead-tree belay near the hiking trail back down to road level.
routes.descentText: The route tops out at a belay beside a large dead tree just below the hiking trail; from there, walk off along the trail/wall base back to the roadside rather than descending technical ground.
routes.bail: Single pitch, only 35 ft at easy 5.5: simply downclimb the traverse or roof-pull, or walk off; no fixed rappel anchor is documented.
routes.turnaround: Not an alpine turnaround-time situation given the short roadside length, but treat the roof/crimp move with care if wet — the wall is shaded and prone to moss and dampness.
routes.bestSeason: No specific season window is published; as with the wall generally, drier summer-into-early-fall conditions likely minimize moss/dampness on the holds.
routes.detailedRack: Single rack, nuts through small cams up to 2 inches; a small nut placed above the roof is worthwhile, though the climbing above is easy.
routes.proNeeds: Light rack to 2 inches is sufficient; protection becomes largely optional once past the roof given the easy slab above.
gear: Single rack to 2 inches (nuts + small cams)
whatToBring: Approach shoes unnecessary beyond a short roadside walk
Awareness this line is often climbed as an access/link pitch rather than for quality
proTips: Mountain Project frames this route as a practical way to reach the upper tier with less bushwhacking than the standard walk-off, rather than as a quality climb in its own right.
Aim for the large dead tree near the top — it's the described belay/finish landmark, right before the hiking trail.
watchOut: Described on Mountain Project as 'crappy' climbing on a scrubby line — go in with expectations set accordingly.
Loose or vegetated rock in the traverse under the roof, typical of an under-cleaned line.
knownHazards: Loose or vegetated rock typical of a lightly traveled line.
General Monte Cristo-area choss.

climate.typical: Low-elevation (~2,300-2,360 ft), forested, shaded valley-bottom setting on the west slope of the Cascades near Barlow Pass — consistent with the persistent moss growth noted in the route's own name and description.

emergency.notes: Mossy Loaf sits directly off a paved, drive-to highway near Barlow Pass, so evacuation/access logistics are far simpler here than for the backcountry alpine routes elsewhere in the Monte Cristo area.

routes.corrections: Given peak elevation was null; sourced from Mountain Project as 2,301 ft (701 m). Important context correction: this crag is a short roadside pull-off near Barlow Pass, NOT reached via the well-documented 4-mile gated-road hike to the historic Monte Cristo townsite — those are two different, non-overlapping approaches that happen to share the 'Monte Cristo' area name.

blurb: Cutthroat Peak (8,066 ft) is a striking granite spire in the North Cascades, about two miles west of Washington Pass along the North Cascades Highway (SR 20), within the Okanogan-Wenatchee National Forest near the Chelan/Skagit county line. Built of solid granite with a broad, sunny South Buttress clearly visible from the highway, it offers several accessible moderate alpine rock routes (5.6-5.8) that rank among the most popular objectives at Washington Pass. The peak was first climbed via its West Ridge on July 22, 1937 by Sierra Club climbers Kenneth Adam, Raffi Bedayn, and W. Kenneth Davis; Fred Beckey and Donald Gordon added the now-classic South Buttress route in 1958.

routes.fa: Fred Beckey and Donald Gordon, 1958
routes.rappels: Route can be rappelled with a single 60m rope (~12 raps documented by some parties); a 70m rope allows linking pitches and fewer rap stations
objHaz: marmots chewing gear left at the base
early-season snow/ice in the approach gully
routes.overview: The classic, most-traveled line on Cutthroat Peak: a long, wandering 850-ft, 12-pitch buttress climb up clean cracks, slabs and short chimneys on the broad, sunny south face, finishing on an airy summit ridge.
routes.beta: Grade III 5.8, 12 pitches. Moderate, low-to-mid 5th-class climbing on generally solid granite broken by looser, 'kitty litter' sections; route-finding follows the path of least resistance near the buttress crest and variations are common. Crux terrain includes a 5.8 hand crack/lieback and a short offwidth near the summit.
routes.approach: Park at the long pullout on the north side of Hwy 20 about 1.5 miles west of Washington Pass. A climbers' trail leaves the east end of the pullout, drops through woods, crosses a creek (look for a log crossing), and climbs through meadows and a boulder field to the base of the leftmost gully below the buttress. Rope up where the gully forks and steepens, roughly 100 ft up. Approach takes about 1.5-2 hours.
routes.descent: Descend via a series of rappels down the route, or traverse off to descend the West Ridge.
routes.descentText: Most parties rappel back down the South Buttress itself — a single 60m rope suffices, or link raps with a 70m rope for fewer stops. An alternate is to traverse toward the West Ridge for a mixed rappel/downclimb descent. Expect loose rock and 'kitty litter' in the descent gully, and firm snow lingering into early summer that may require care or an axe.
routes.itinerary: Single long day: alpine start (5-6 AM) from the highway pullout, 1.5-2 hr approach to the base, several hours of climbing across 12 pitches, and a multi-rappel descent back to the trailhead. MountainProject cites a typical 9-10 hour car-to-car time for an efficient party, though 13-15 hours has been reported by slower parties.
routes.bail: Retreat by rappelling the route on gear anchors/fixed tat where established; below the crux pitches, parties can generally walk off or downclimb easier terrain to the sides of the buttress crest.
routes.turnaround: Given the long, multi-pitch rappel descent, start early enough to be off the summit ridge well before afternoon thunderstorms typically build over the Cascade crest in summer; don't commit to the upper pitches if a storm is visibly building to the west.
routes.bestSeason: Best climbed June through September once the approach gully and ledges have shed most snow; the south-facing aspect means the rock dries and warms quickly after rain, but it can also be uncomfortably hot on sunny summer afternoons.
routes.comms: Remote North Cascades terrain; do not count on cell coverage on the approach or route.
routes.detailedRack: A single rack of cams from 0.3-3 in (or doubles from 0.5-2 in for more complete protection) plus a set of nuts covers the crux pitches; several parties add an optional #4 cam, which protects the short offwidth near the summit especially well. Bring multiple single- and double-length runners for wandering belays.
routes.proNeeds: Standard trad rack sized for finger-to-hand cracks with occasional offwidth moves; placements are generally good on the solid sections but sparser on the looser, blockier terrain.
whatToBring: approach shoes for the hike, rock shoes for the climb
extra water — approach and route are dry/sun-exposed
headlamp for an early start or late finish
layers for the exposed summit ridge and descent
proTips: Simul-climb the easier low-5th-class sections to save time on this long route.
Stay near the crest of the buttress for the most solid rock and clearest route-finding.
A 70m rope lets you link pitches and reduces the pitch count on the easier terrain.
Cache trekking poles/gear well out of reach at the base — marmots have chewed through gear left unattended.
watchOut: Loose blocks and 'kitty litter' sections require careful testing of holds, especially low on the route.
Afternoon thunderstorms are a real hazard on the exposed upper buttress and summit ridge in summer.
Early-season parties may find firm snow or ice in the approach gully.
knownHazards: Rockfall and loose rock throughout, particularly on ledgy, sandy sections.
Long multi-rappel descent with some fixed anchors of variable age/quality.
No reliable cell coverage on approach or route.

pitchDetail.notes: Simul-climbed low-angle terrain via clean cracks with good protection.
Slab pitch leading toward the steeper upper buttress.
Offwidth section; a #4 cam protects it well.
Overhanging lieback/hand-crack crux section, sustained and sporty.
Short ~4in offwidth/splitter crack finishes at the true summit.

road.name: North Cascades Highway (SR 20)
road.status: Paved 2-lane highway, open seasonally
road.seasonalGate: Closes roughly early-mid December (Ross Dam TH / MP134 west gate, Silver Star gate / MP171 east gate, sometimes moved to Newhalem MP120 / Early Winters MP178 later in the season); reopens mid-to-late April depending on avalanche/snow conditions.
road.driveNote: Trailhead pullout is on Hwy 20 about 1.5 miles west of Washington Pass, roughly a 2-2.5 hour drive from Seattle via I-5 and Hwy 20.

climate.typical: Short alpine season with a dry, sunny summer window; the south-facing granite dries and warms quickly after rain, while shaded aspects nearby hold snow longer.
climate.spring: Snow lingers in gullies and on ledges into June; the approach trail can be snow-covered.
climate.summer: Prime climbing season (roughly June/July through September); hot, sun-exposed face, and afternoon thunderstorms are a real hazard.
climate.fall: Cooler, often-stable weather persists into September/October until the highway closes for winter.
climate.winter: Highway 20 is gated closed; the peak is essentially inaccessible by vehicle.

access.landManager: Okanogan-Wenatchee National Forest, Methow Valley Ranger District
access.fees: No fee at the roadside pullout used for this route; a Northwest Forest Pass is required only at the developed Blue Lake Trailhead nearby.
access.permit: No climbing permit required.
access.passRequired: Northwest Forest Pass (only if parking at the Blue Lake TH area)
access.closures: SR 20 (North Cascades Highway) closes seasonally for winter, typically early December to mid/late April, cutting off all vehicle access.

timing.recommendedStart: Alpine start, roughly 5-6 AM

waypoints.name: South Buttress base / area reference point

emergency.county: Okanogan County (trailhead/access side of Washington Pass); the summit sits on the Chelan-Skagit county line
emergency.sheriffDispatch: Okanogan County Sheriff's Office non-emergency: 509-422-7232 (800-572-6604 in-state)
emergency.rangerStation: Methow Valley Ranger District, Okanogan-Wenatchee NF, Winthrop WA: 509-996-4000
emergency.nearestHospital: Three Rivers Hospital, Brewster WA — 24-hr ER, Level IV trauma, 509-689-2517; roughly 60+ miles / 1.5+ hrs drive from Washington Pass
emergency.notes: Call 911 first for backcountry rescue. Okanogan County Search & Rescue and Aero Methow Rescue Service (Winthrop-based) have responded to prior climbing accidents in this immediate Washington Pass corridor.

routes.corrections: Older sources (MountainProject area page, some SummitPost text) list the summit at ~8,050 ft; more recent survey-based sources (Wikipedia, Peakbagger) list 8,066 ft — the latter is used here for highPointFt.

routes.fa: Kenneth Adam, Raffi Bedayn, W. Kenneth Davis — July 22, 1937 (also the first ascent of the peak)
routes.rappels: Multiple single-rope rappels off bolted anchors, mainly used on descent by parties coming off other routes
objHaz: loose rock, especially high on the ridge
routes.overview: The easiest technical route to the true summit and the standard descent line for other Cutthroat routes: a moderate ridge scramble/climb gaining the crest via the Southwest Gully and finishing up the summit block. This was the line of the peak's 1937 first ascent.
routes.beta: Grade II, 5.6, 3 pitches over roughly 3,200 ft of ridge terrain (mostly scrambling with short roped sections).
routes.approach: From the State Creek pullout on Hwy 20 (about a half mile west of the Blue Lake Trailhead, roughly 1.5-2 miles west of Washington Pass), bushwhack briefly then follow a climbers' trail dropping to about 5,000 ft, crossing State Creek, and climbing through meadows to a bowl southwest of the summit at about 6,200 ft.
routes.descent: Downclimb the ascent route, or use fixed rappel anchors lower on the ridge.
routes.descentText: From the summit, downclimb the exposed ridge crest back toward the saddle, then move to 3rd-class ledges on the south side for a series of rappels on bolted anchors. Avoid dropping into either of the two southwest-facing gullies below the ridge — many parties get pulled into them by mistake; stay on the correct line back down to the State Creek drainage.
routes.itinerary: Half-day to full-day: MountainProject reports roughly 6 hours trailhead-to-summit and about 4 hours back down, for about 10 hours car-to-car on a direct ascent/descent.
routes.bail: The route can generally be downclimbed or rappelled from most points on the ridge using the bolted descent anchors.
routes.turnaround: Because this is also the standard descent line for other Cutthroat routes, keep daylight in reserve to navigate the correct gully back to State Creek — parties have gotten cliffed out or benighted after dropping into the wrong southwest gully.
routes.bestSeason: June through September; snow can linger in the approach bowl and summit gully into early summer.
routes.comms: Remote North Cascades terrain; do not count on cell coverage.
routes.detailedRack: A light rack suffices: small stoppers and a range of cam sizes for running protection on the ridge crest, plus slings for the belayed pitch near the ridge crest (historically protected by two fixed pitons).
routes.proNeeds: Minimal-gear route — mostly used for running belays and short roped sections rather than sustained protection.
gear: rope for short roped sections and rappels
whatToBring: sturdy approach shoes for the bushwhack/meadow approach
map/GPS track — descent gully route-finding error is a known trap
proTips: Used as the standard descent for the South Buttress, North Ridge, and Complete South Buttress — learn this line even if it's not your objective.
Don't get pulled into the two southwest-facing gullies below the ridge on descent — that's the classic mistake here.
watchOut: Loose rock increases toward the summit block.
The correct descent gully is easy to miss; two decoy gullies nearby have funneled many parties astray.
knownHazards: Route-finding error on descent is the most commonly reported hazard on this line.
Exposed, unroped scrambling on some sections.

pitchDetail.notes: Scramble the initial gully section, then belay up to the ridge crest past two fixed pitons (~50 ft roped pitch).
Follow the exposed ridge crest to the summit block; the final moves to the true summit are the most exposed on the route.

road.name: North Cascades Highway (SR 20)
road.status: Paved 2-lane highway, open seasonally
road.seasonalGate: Closes roughly early-mid December, reopens mid-to-late April depending on conditions (see WSDOT).
road.driveNote: State Creek pullout is on Hwy 20 about a half mile west of the Blue Lake Trailhead, roughly a 2-2.5 hour drive from Seattle.

climate.typical: Short alpine season, dry sunny summer window typical of the Washington Pass corridor.
climate.spring: Snow lingers into June in the approach bowl and summit gully.
climate.summer: Prime season, June/July-September; afternoon thunderstorms possible.
climate.fall: Often stable into September/October until the highway closes.
climate.winter: Highway gated closed; essentially inaccessible.

access.fees: No fee at the State Creek roadside pullout; Northwest Forest Pass required only at the developed Blue Lake Trailhead.

routes.fa: Fred Beckey, Jim Crooks, Ed Kenney — August 19, 1940
routes.rappels: Not typically rappelled on-route; descent is usually via the West Ridge (~5 rappels on fixed anchors plus downclimbing)
objHaz: route-finding onto the correct descent line
routes.overview: A moderate 8-pitch alpine ridge route gaining the North Ridge notch and following mostly 2nd/3rd-class ramps and corners up the east side of the ridge, with a runout 5.7 crux low on the route and a final 5.4 headwall pitch to the summit.
routes.beta: Grade III, 5.7, 8 pitches, 600 ft. Two approach options exist. The lower crux pitch (5.7, loose and runout, described as 'PG13') is the technical and psychological crux; above it, rock quality improves to clean white granite.
routes.approach: Option A: from Rainy Pass, follow the Pacific Crest Trail into Porcupine Creek, then climb 2-3 short pitches of low 5th class to reach the North Ridge Notch. Option B: follow the South Buttress approach, then veer right/east into the southeast basin below the peak and climb an unprotected 5.6 slab pitch to the notch.
routes.descent: Descend via the West Ridge route.
routes.descentText: From the summit, downclimb a short (5.0) section across a flat part of the ridge, then join the West Ridge descent: roughly 5 rappels on fixed anchors interspersed with exposed easy ridge climbing and some easy downclimbing back toward State Creek.
routes.itinerary: Full day: approach via Porcupine Creek/PCT or the South Buttress trail, climb the 8-pitch ridge, then descend via the West Ridge — a long day given the two different route corridors involved.
routes.bail: The lower slab/crux pitches are runout with marginal protection, so retreat from low on the route can be difficult; above the crux, the ridge's easier 3rd/4th-class terrain allows downclimbing or simul-rappelling back to the notch.
routes.turnaround: Start early — this route combines a sometimes-unprotected crux low on the route with a full traverse to the West Ridge for descent, leaving little margin for a late start.
routes.bestSeason: June through September; the crux slab and approach snow patches are best climbed once fully melted out.
routes.comms: Remote North Cascades terrain; do not count on cell coverage.
routes.detailedRack: A single rack from 0.4-3 in plus a set of nuts; narrow, small cams are particularly useful for thin placements on the crux/approach pitch.
routes.proNeeds: Protection is marginal on the crux pitch (loose, runout 5.7) — not a route for climbers uncomfortable running it out on suspect rock.
whatToBring: helmet (loose rock on the crux pitch)
layers for an exposed ridge and long day
headlamp — this is a long linkup day
proTips: Rock quality improves dramatically above the crux pitch — grind through the loose lower section and the climbing gets much better on clean granite.
Simul-climb the 3rd/4th-class sections above the crux to keep the long day moving.
watchOut: The crux pitch is loose and runout ('PG13') — not bolt-protected terrain.
The slab traverse pitch is essentially unprotected 5.6.
knownHazards: Loose rock and marginal protection on the crux (lower) pitch.
Long, involved descent via a different route (West Ridge) adds route-finding complexity.

pitchDetail.notes: Crux pitch: loose, runout terrain requiring delicate stemming on flexing holds ('PG13').
Unprotected slab traverse.
Rock quality improves to clean white granite; mostly simul-climbed ramps and corners on the east side of the ridge.
Final headwall pitch to the summit, after a short 5.0 downclimb across a flat section of ridge.

objHaz: limited/uncertain fixed protection on the direct face
routes.face: South Face (direct line up the South Buttress face)
routes.overview: A more direct, sustained line up the smooth central face of the South Buttress, referenced in older guidebook-style sources as the 'South Face of South Buttress' — climbing the narrow, smooth face directly to its top rather than following the wandering, easier-angled crest line of the standard South Buttress route.
routes.beta: Grade III, 5.7 with some aid, per historical guidebook description on SummitPost's Cutthroat Peak overview page. Distinct from — and more sustained than — the standard South Buttress, which weaves around the crest at an easier grade.
routes.approach: Shares the same trailhead and lower approach as the standard South Buttress route: park at the pullout on Hwy 20 about 1.5 miles west of Washington Pass and follow the climbers' trail toward the base of the buttress.
routes.turnaround: As with the standard South Buttress, start early enough to be off the exposed buttress before afternoon thunderstorms build.
routes.bestSeason: June through September, in line with the standard South Buttress season.
routes.comms: Remote North Cascades terrain; do not count on cell coverage.
routes.proNeeds: Historical sources describe some aid climbing on this direct face line, implying thinner or more sparse protection than the standard South Buttress.
watchOut: This line is far less documented/traveled than the standard South Buttress — expect route-finding uncertainty and possibly aging or absent fixed gear.
knownHazards: Sparse modern documentation means the condition of any fixed protection or the exact line is uncertain.

routes.corrections: Could not find a route specifically named 'South Buttress Direct' on Mountain Project, SummitPost, or in modern trip-report literature — Mountain Project's current Cutthroat Peak area page lists only six named routes (Cauthorn-Wilson, Complete South Buttress, North Ridge, South Buttress, The Swarm, West Ridge), none titled 'Direct.' The closest documented match is an older guidebook-style route mentioned on SummitPost's Cutthroat Peak overview page, the 'South Face of South Buttress' (Grade III, 5.7 & aid) — a more direct, sustained line up the smooth face vs. the wandering standard South Buttress. Data here reflects that likely match; pitch count, length, and gear are left null as unconfirmed until a primary source specifically documents this line under either name.

routes.rappels: Two half-rope rappels to gaps/cols on the descent; some fixed anchors present but reported as potentially needing replacement
routes.overview: The full, extended version of the South Buttress: starts much lower on the SW edge of the buttress and climbs the entire feature from near its base, roughly doubling the pitch count of the standard South Buttress route (22 vs. 12 pitches).
routes.beta: Grade III 5.8, 22 pitches, 1,200 ft. Predominantly 3rd/4th class and easy 5th-class climbing on the lower two-thirds, joining the standard South Buttress line higher up for the same 5.7-5.8 crux climbing near the top.
routes.approach: Hike the standard South Buttress trail to about 6,500 ft, then contour SE off-trail around to the SW edge of the buttress and start up easy, ledgy slabs low on the feature.
routes.descent: Same rack/approach as the South Buttress; descend via a mix of rappels (some at fixed but aging anchors) to gaps/cols, and/or the West Ridge.
routes.descentText: Two half-rope rappels lead down to gaps/cols on the descent; some fixed anchors are present but described as potentially needing replacement (worn tat/slings) — inspect before weighting or back them up.
routes.itinerary: A long full day given the extra pitch count (22 vs. 12 for the standard line) — expect a longer car-to-car time than the standard South Buttress' 9-10 hours.
routes.bail: Escape options are similar to the standard South Buttress once the two lines join higher on the buttress; low on the route, easy 3rd/4th-class terrain allows walking off to the sides.
routes.turnaround: With roughly double the pitch count of the standard route, start earlier still and budget extra time for the descent — don't commit to the upper pitches without daylight margin.
routes.bestSeason: June through September, matching the standard South Buttress season.
routes.comms: Remote North Cascades terrain; do not count on cell coverage.
routes.detailedRack: Same rack as for the standard South Buttress route: a single rack 0.3-3 in (or doubles 0.5-2 in) plus nuts.
routes.proNeeds: Protection is generally adequate on the upper, shared pitches with the standard South Buttress; lower, easier terrain is often simul-climbed or soloed with minimal gear.
whatToBring: approach shoes for the long lower section
extra water/food for the longer day
proTips: Simul-climb or solo the lower 3rd/4th-class sections to keep pace on this much longer version of the route.
Expect to re-sling or back up old rappel anchors on the descent — carry extra webbing/cord.
watchOut: Fixed rappel anchors on the descent are reported as potentially old/worn — inspect before weighting.
This is a significantly longer day than the standard South Buttress; plan accordingly.
knownHazards: Loose rock on lower, less-traveled pitches.
Aging fixed gear at some rappel stations.

pitchDetail.notes: Ledgy slabs on the SW edge of the buttress, largely simul-climbed or scrambled.
Joins the standard South Buttress line for the crux climbing near the top.
