blurb: Cascade Peak (7,428 ft) is a rugged, relatively minor North Cascades summit in Skagit County, Washington, rising above Cascade Pass on the shared boundary of North Cascades National Park and the Glacier Peak Wilderness. It is built of breccia formed roughly 50 million years ago through terrane accretion, retains small glacier remnants on its north side, and sits immediately east of the far more famous Johannesburg Mountain (0.53 mi WSW) and just west of The Triplets (0.35 mi ESE). It was first climbed on July 23, 1950 by Fred Beckey, Pete Schoening, and Phil Sharpe.

routes.overview: Cascade Peak's East Ridge is the standard and easiest line to the summit: from the Cascade–Johannesburg ("C-J") Col below Johannesburg Mountain, the route follows the peak's remnant glacier and class 3-4 rock along the east ridge to the top.
routes.beta: A seldom-climbed line, usually done as an add-on to a Johannesburg Mountain trip or approached directly from Cascade Pass via the C-J Col. It mixes short glacier/snow travel on Cascade Peak's small north-side glacier remnant with class 3-4 scrambling on breccia to reach the summit; Wikipedia's cited description of the peak's easiest route ("glacier travel, class 3-4 scrambling") corresponds to this line, and a 2013 CascadeClimbers.com trip report documents a Johannesburg/Cascade Peak combination via the C-J Couloir to the East Ridge.
routes.approach: Drive Cascade River Road from Marblemount (SR-20, mile 106) 23.1 miles to its end at the Cascade Pass trailhead (elevation ~3,660 ft; road is paved for roughly the first third, then gravel with potholes/washboard, narrow and steep near the end — not suitable for RVs, trailers over 22 ft, or low-clearance vehicles). Hike the Cascade Pass Trail 3.7 miles and about 1,700 ft of gain, switchbacking through forest and subalpine meadows, to Cascade Pass. From the pass, travel toward the col between Johannesburg Mountain and Cascade Peak — known locally as the "C-J Col" (labeled "Johannesburg Col" on USGS maps) — then continue up onto Cascade Peak's east ridge to the summit.
routes.turnaround: Because the route crosses the C-J Col and travels on Cascade Peak's remnant glacier, build in enough margin to re-cross the col and get back down to the trail/camp before dark; deteriorating weather, softening glacier snow, or rockfall on the col are all valid reasons to turn back regardless of proximity to the summit.
routes.bestSeason: Cascade Pass and its trailhead typically aren't clear of snow and reliably accessible until late June or July, and the NPS notes hikers can encounter steep snow sections requiring an ice axe into July. The practical climbing window for the East Ridge is roughly July through September, before fall snow returns.
gear: rope and basic glacier travel/crevasse-rescue kit
watchOut: Breccia rock in the Cascade Pass/Johannesburg group is often loose and chossy — expect rockfall potential on the ridge and approach.
Black bears are regularly reported along the Cascade Pass trail and near the pass itself.
The C-J Col crossing and the peak's remnant glacier require standard glacier-travel precautions for crevasse hazard.
knownHazards: Loose rock (breccia) on the ridge and scramble sections
Crevasse hazard on Cascade Peak's remnant glacier
Rockfall/avalanche potential on approach terrain near the C-J Col, especially in early season
Black bear activity along the Cascade Pass approach trail
routes.fa: Fred Beckey, Pete Schoening, and Phil Sharpe, July 23, 1950 — this is Cascade Peak's overall first ascent per Wikipedia; sources do not explicitly confirm the 1950 party's exact line matched today's named East Ridge, though it is the peak's documented easiest/standard route and most plausible match.
routes.face: East side of the peak, gained via the Cascade-Johannesburg (C-J) Col

road.name: Cascade River Road
road.status: Generally open to vehicles to its end near the Cascade Pass trailhead per recent NPS reporting, but the road has a history of storm/washout damage in past seasons that has forced temporary vehicle closures with foot access only beyond a gate — verify current NPS road-conditions status before driving.
road.seasonalGate: The Cascade Pass trailhead parking area typically doesn't open until late June or July as snow and winter damage clear.
road.driveNote: From Marblemount (SR-20) the road runs 23.1 miles to its end (elev. ~3,660 ft); paved for roughly the first third (~10 miles), then gravel with potholes and washboard, narrow and steep near the end. Not suitable for RVs, trailers over 22 ft, or low-clearance vehicles.

climate.typical: Marine west-coast climate with strong orographic lift; North Cascades National Park sees heavy precipitation in all seasons, especially winter snowfall.

access.landManager: National Park Service — North Cascades National Park / Stephen Mather Wilderness (the peak's summit lies on the shared boundary with the Glacier Peak Wilderness, USFS).
access.permit: A backcountry/wilderness permit is required for overnight trips; obtain it from the Wilderness Information Center in Marblemount (360-854-7200). No camping is allowed directly at Cascade Pass, but designated sites exist nearby at Pelton Basin, Sahale Glacier, Basin Creek, and Johannesburg camp.
access.closures: About 99% of North Cascades National Park is designated Stephen Mather Wilderness, with a mandatory moratorium on new fixed anchors — climbers must rely on removable protection (cams, nuts, slings) only.

emergency.sheriffDispatch: Skagit County 911 (Skagit 911): 911 for emergencies, (360) 428-3211 for 24-hour non-emergency; Skagit County Sheriff's Office coordinates volunteer Search and Rescue (SAR) response for North Cascades backcountry incidents.
emergency.rangerStation: North Cascades National Park Wilderness Information Center, Marblemount — (360) 854-7200
emergency.nearestHospital: PeaceHealth United General Medical Center, 2000 Hospital Dr, Sedro-Woolley, WA — (360) 856-6021 (closest hospital to the North Cascades Highway corridor); Skagit Valley Hospital (Level III ED), Mount Vernon, WA — (360) 424-4111

routes.corrections: Given peak coordinates (48.4635, -121.0802) and elevation (7,428 ft) match Wikipedia's cited figures (48°27'48"N 121°04'49"W ≈ 48.4633, -121.0803; 7,428 ft) very closely — no correction needed. Direct fetches of mountainproject.com, summitpost.org, peakbagger.com, and cascadeclimbers.com pages specific to this peak were blocked by HTTP 403 despite repeated attempts; facts attributed to those domains here were cross-verified via their search-result snippets and via Wikipedia/NPS/onehikeaweek.com instead of full-page fetches. No Mountain Project page for 'Cascade Peak' could be located at all (it does not appear to have a dedicated MP area page); the Johannesburg Mountain MP area page was checked directly and contains no mention of Cascade Peak's East Ridge.

hierarchyNote: Peak identity checks out: Wikipedia lists Mount Cruiser at 47°33'52"N 123°18'56"W (≈47.5644, -123.3156) and 6,104 ft — matches the given coordinates/elevation exactly, and it is unambiguously the Sawtooth Ridge summit in the southern Olympic Mountains (Mason County), Mount Skokomish Wilderness, Olympic National Forest, near Flapjack/Mildred/Murdock Lakes. HOWEVER the given parentArea "North-Central Olympic Mountains (Deception-Gray Wolf)" is geographically wrong for this peak. Deception Basin / the Gray Wolf River drainage is a distinct area roughly 25-30 miles to the north-northeast, in the Buckhorn Wilderness / northeastern Olympics near Sequim/Dosewallips — not the same region as the Sawtooth Range / Mount Skokomish Wilderness, which every source (Wikipedia, SummitPost, USFS) places in the southern Olympics near Lake Cushman/Hood Canal, accessed via the Staircase entrance of Olympic National Park. Recommend reparenting Mount Cruiser to a "Southern Olympic Mountains / Mount Skokomish Wilderness (Sawtooth Range)" bucket rather than the Deception-Gray Wolf group.
blurb: Mount Cruiser (6,104 ft) is the highest summit of the craggy Sawtooth Ridge in the southern Olympic Mountains of Washington, rising above Flapjack, Mildred, and Murdock Lakes within the Mount Skokomish Wilderness (Olympic National Forest). Built of Eocene pillow basalt/oceanic-crust rock that is unusually solid by Olympic standards, it has no non-technical route to its summit — even its easiest line requires low-5th-class rock climbing. It was first climbed in 1937 by Paul Crews and Ray Layton of the Bremerton Ski Cruisers club, who gave the peak its name.

routes.overview: The standard, easiest way up Mount Cruiser: a long approach and ridge scramble to Needle Pass, followed by one to two short but exposed low-5th-class pitches that squeeze through a chockstone feature (the 'cannon hole') to gain the summit ridge crest.
routes.beta: From a high camp at Flapjack Lakes (or Mildred Lakes), climbers traverse toward Gladys Divide, ascend a snow/scree gully to Needle Pass, and follow a boot-marked but exposed Class 3 ridge crest north to the base of Mt. Cruiser's south side. A short low-5th-class face pitch, protected by small cams/nuts plus a bolt and reportedly an old piton partway up, leads through or past the cannon-hole chockstone to a ledge; an easy but very exposed ridge-crest pitch then reaches the true summit. Rock quality is good for the Olympics but protection is sparse and runout in places.
routes.approach: Drive Lake Cushman Road from Hoodsport to the Staircase Ranger Station / North Fork Skokomish trailhead (~850 ft) at the southeast corner of Olympic National Park. Hike the Flapjack Lakes trail roughly 7.5-8 miles with about 3,000 ft of gain (around 4 hours) to camp at Flapjack Lakes (~3,850-3,900 ft). From camp, head toward Gladys Divide; where snow becomes continuous (often above ~5,500 ft), climb a couloir/gully (roughly 700 ft of gain, crampons/axe recommended into midsummer) to Needle Pass, then follow the boot path along the ridge crest north to the base of the route. An alternate approach climbs from upper Mildred Lake (4.4 miles, ~2,500 ft gain) up the slope below Alpha Peak and across talus/cliff bands to the same south-side base.
routes.descent: Reverse the route: rappel/downclimb the crux back to the notch below it, then reverse the ridge-crest scramble and Needle Pass gully to camp.
routes.descentText: Most parties make one rappel (roughly 30 to 100+ feet depending on the exact line) off a fixed double-bolt anchor near the summit to regain the ledge/notch below the crux; two 60m (165 ft) ropes allow a full-length rappel, while single-rope parties combine a shorter rappel with roped downclimbing through the chimney/cannon-hole section. From the base, reverse the Class 3 ridge crest south to Needle Pass, downclimb (or glissade, if snow allows) the entry gully back toward Gladys Divide, and retrace the Flapjack Lakes or Mildred Lakes approach trail out to the Staircase trailhead.
routes.itinerary: Typical trip is 2-3 days car-to-car: Day 1, hike in ~7.5-8 miles from Staircase to camp at Flapjack Lakes (or the shorter, steeper Mildred Lakes approach); Day 2, climb via Needle Pass and the South Corner (roughly 5.5 hours to the summit and 6.5 hours back down from camp per published trip reports) and return to camp; Day 3, hike out. Strong parties occasionally push it car-to-car in a single long day.
routes.bail: The crux is short and can generally be downclimbed or rappelled from the fixed double-bolt anchor if retreat is needed before the summit; below that, the ridge-crest scramble and Needle Pass gully are straightforward to reverse on foot. There is no separate bail route other than retracing the line already climbed.
routes.turnaround: The approach alone can take 4-6 hours each way, and the Needle Pass gully becomes more dangerous to downclimb or glissade once afternoon sun softens the snow, so parties should set a turnaround based on clearing the summit ridge and gully while snow is still supportive, and should not linger on the exposed, routefinding-heavy ridge crest if weather or visibility deteriorates.
routes.bestSeason: Late June through September is the reliable window, matching Flapjack Lakes' snow-free camping/permit season. The Needle Pass gully typically holds continuous snow into July (crampons and an axe are standard even in early summer), while by late season the same gully can bake out into loose scree with more rockfall exposure.
routes.rockGrade: 5.0 (Mountain Project lists 5.3; most trip reports, guidebooks, and SummitPost cite 5.0 — see corrections)
routes.rappels: 1 rappel (~30-100+ ft) from a fixed double-bolt anchor near the summit is typical; two 60m ropes allow a full rappel, or combine a single rope with downclimbing through the chimney/cannon-hole.
objHaz: Loose rock and lichen in the gully/scramble sections
Steep snow/ice in the Needle Pass couloir into July
Sparse, runout protection on the crux face
Routefinding difficulty on the ridge crest in fog
Aging fixed gear (old bolt/piton) not to be trusted as sole protection
routes.face: South Corner (via Southwest Ridge - South Notch - Cannonhole)
routes.detailedRack: A light alpine rack: small to mid-size cams (roughly to 1-2 inches), a set of stoppers/nuts, a few slings, and a helmet. Fixed gear (a bolt, and reportedly an old piton) supplements the placements on the crux face, which multiple parties describe as sparse and spaced out.
routes.proNeeds: Protection on the crux face is sparse and runout, relying on a mix of small cams/nuts in a discontinuous crack plus one or two pieces of aged fixed gear; leaders should be comfortable soloing or running it out on low-5th-class rock.
gear: 60m rope (doubles recommended for the descent rappel)
whatToBring: Bear canister (required for overnight camping in the park)
Approach shoes for the trail plus rock shoes or mountain boots for the climb
Map/compass or GPS for ridge-crest routefinding in fog
Overnight camping gear if not going car-to-car
proTips: Camp at Flapjack Lakes (or Mildred Lakes for a shorter, steeper alternate) to split the long approach into two days.
Expect snow in the Needle Pass gully into July — plan on crampons and an axe even in early summer.
The ridge crest between Needle Pass and the peak is easy to lose in fog; note landmarks on the way in.
watchOut: The crux face is widely described as sparsely protected and runout despite its moderate grade.
Loose rock and lichen-covered holds are common in the gully and lower scrambling.
The Needle Pass gully holds firm snow into midsummer and turns to loose, rockfall-prone scree later in the season.
knownHazards: No non-technical route exists to the summit; low-5th-class climbing is unavoidable.
The entry couloir to Needle Pass requires crampon/axe skills into July.
Fixed protection on the crux (an old bolt/piton) is reportedly aged.
Routefinding along the exposed ridge crest is difficult in poor visibility.

pitchDetail.notes: Ridge crest from Needle Pass to the base of the south side; exposed but non-technical.
Off a ledge through/past the 'cannon hole' chockstone; sparse protection, includes a bolt partway up a lead described as roughly 70 ft by one source.
Easy but airy ridge-crest scramble to the true summit.

road.name: Lake Cushman Road / NF-24 (North Fork Skokomish Road) to Staircase
road.status: Unpaved beyond Lake Cushman; subject to closures for snow and, as of the most recent reports, storm/fire damage
road.seasonalGate: Typically snow-closed roughly November through May; the Staircase area has also been reported closed due to Bear Gulch Fire impacts (fire-damaged trees, debris-flow/landslide risk) — verify current status with NPS/Olympic National Forest before travel.
road.driveNote: Staircase is reached via Lake Cushman Road from Hoodsport, roughly one hour from Olympia and about two hours south of Port Angeles; the final approach to the trailhead is unpaved.

climate.typical: Wet, maritime Olympic Mountains climate with heavy autumn-through-spring precipitation and a drier June-September climbing window.
climate.spring: Continued snowpack on the approach and in the Needle Pass gully; wet-snow and high-water hazard.
climate.summer: Best conditions June-September, though the Needle Pass entry gully can still hold snow into July.
climate.fall: Generally drier and more stable into September before winter storms return.
climate.winter: Access road to Staircase is typically snow-closed; heavy precipitation makes the route unsuitable.

access.landManager: Mount Cruiser and the Sawtooth Ridge climbing terrain lie within the Mount Skokomish Wilderness, managed by Olympic National Forest; the standard Flapjack Lakes approach trail lies within Olympic National Park.
access.fees: Olympic National Park wilderness camping: $6 reservation fee plus $8 per person per night (ages 16+) for overnight permits at Flapjack Lakes.
access.permit: A wilderness camping permit/reservation is required for overnight stays at Flapjack Lakes (quota season May 1-Sept 30; reservations bookable June 15-Oct 15; permits release April 15). About 50% of sites are held for first-come, first-served issue at the Wilderness Information Center up to 24 hours in advance.
access.passRequired: Olympic National Park entrance fee/pass for park access via the Staircase entrance.
access.closures: Staircase area and access road periodically closed by snow (roughly Nov-May); most recently reported closed due to Bear Gulch Fire damage (fire-damaged trees, landslide/debris-flow risk) — confirm current status with NPS/Olympic National Forest before a trip.

timing.recommendedStart: Alpine start from camp, commonly by 5:00-5:30 AM

emergency.sheriffDispatch: Mason County Sheriff's Office (911 or non-emergency dispatch); operates a Special Operations Rescue Team (SORT) for backcountry/technical rescue in the surrounding Olympic National Forest.
emergency.rangerStation: Olympic National Park Wilderness Information Center / seasonal Staircase Ranger Station; Olympic National Park HQ, 600 E. Park Ave, Port Angeles, WA 98362, (360) 565-3130.
emergency.nearestHospital: Mason Health (Mason General Hospital), 901 Mountain View Dr, Shelton, WA — 24/7 ED, Level IV trauma designation, (360) 426-1611.
emergency.notes: This is a remote, seldom-patrolled southern-Olympics wilderness approach with unreliable cell coverage; because the peak sits in Mount Skokomish Wilderness (Olympic National Forest) just outside Olympic National Park, both NPS and Mason County/USFS resources may be involved in a rescue response.

routes.corrections: Mountain Project's area page lists South Corner's grade as 5.3 (multi-system string '5.3 / 3+ / 10 / III / 9 / VD / 3a'), but SummitPost, Wikipedia, multiple guidebook-derived trip reports, and the route's common name/character are consistently cited at 5.0 elsewhere; both are reported here with the discrepancy flagged rather than silently picking one.

routes.overview: A harder, considerably less-documented alternative to the South Corner, climbing the peak's northwest side/corner at 5.7 trad with a PG13 protection rating.
routes.beta: Listed on Mountain Project as a 5.7 trad route (multi-grade string '5.7 / 5a / 15 / V+ / 13 / MVS / 4b') with a PG13 hazard rating, indicating some sparse/serious protection; detailed pitch-by-pitch beta is not published in any accessible trip report or guide found during this research, unlike the well-documented South Corner.
routes.approach: Shares the same trailhead and approach system as the South Corner (Staircase / Flapjack Lakes or Mildred Lakes to Needle Pass/Gladys Divide); the specific final approach line to the northwest side of the summit block beyond that point is not detailed in available sources.
objHaz: PG13 protection rating (sparse/runout gear in places)
Same approach hazards as South Corner: steep snow to Needle Pass, loose rock, ridge-crest routefinding
routes.face: Northwest side/corner of the summit block
routes.proNeeds: Mountain Project's PG13 rating implies protection is sparser or more serious in places than a straightforward well-protected 5.7; no further detail is published.
watchOut: Far less climbed and documented than the South Corner — expect to route-find without a topo or recent trip reports.
PG13 rating signals real runout/fall-consequence sections despite the moderate grade.
knownHazards: Remote, seldom-traveled line on an already-serious peak with no non-technical descent option.
Same approach objective hazards as the South Corner (steep snow, loose rock, fog routefinding).

climate.typical: Wet, maritime Olympic Mountains climate with a drier June-September window, shared with the South Corner side of the peak.

access.landManager: Mount Skokomish Wilderness, Olympic National Forest, with the standard approach trail inside Olympic National Park.
access.fees: Olympic National Park wilderness camping: $6 reservation fee plus $8 per person per night (ages 16+) if camping at Flapjack Lakes.

access.passRequired: Olympic National Park entrance fee/pass for park access via Staircase.
access.closures: Staircase area and access road periodically closed by snow or, most recently, Bear Gulch Fire damage — confirm current status before a trip.

emergency.sheriffDispatch: Mason County Sheriff's Office (911 or non-emergency dispatch); operates a Special Operations Rescue Team (SORT) for backcountry/technical rescue.
emergency.rangerStation: Olympic National Park Wilderness Information Center / seasonal Staircase Ranger Station; Olympic National Park HQ, (360) 565-3130.
emergency.nearestHospital: Mason Health (Mason General Hospital), 901 Mountain View Dr, Shelton, WA — 24/7 ED, Level IV trauma designation, (360) 426-1611.
emergency.notes: Same remote, low-cell-coverage access as the South Corner; a rescue on this less-traveled route would likely take longer to reach given its sparse documentation and traffic.

routes.corrections: Beyond its bare Mountain Project listing (grade, stars, PG13 rating), no trip report, guidebook excerpt, or other source found during this research names or describes this route's actual line, first ascent, or pitch count — most Mt. Cruiser online coverage is exclusively about the South Corner. All unsupported fields are left null rather than inferred from the South Corner's data.

blurb: Amphitheater Mountain (8,358 ft) is a sprawling, cross-shaped multi-summit peak in the eastern Pasayten Wilderness of the North Cascades' Okanogan Range, rising directly above Upper Cathedral Lake about a mile south of Cathedral Peak. Built of granite, it offers a scattering of moderate-to-hard alpine rock routes on its Middle Finger and Ka'aba buttresses (first climbed 1971-2011) plus an easy scree/scramble line up the broad western bowl that gives the mountain its name. The peak itself was first climbed in 1901 by Calkins and Smith.
hierarchyNote: Correction: the given elevationFt (8374) does not match reliable sourcing — Wikipedia, Mountain Project, Mazamas, and Peakbagger all converge on 8,358 ft (2,548 m) for Amphitheater Mountain. Coordinates given (48.97566, -120.192342) are consistent with Wikipedia's summit coordinate (48.97556, -120.19222) and Mountain Project's area coordinate (48.97698, -120.19208), so no correction needed there. Parent area "Pasayten Wilderness" is correct (Okanogan-Wenatchee National Forest, Methow Valley Ranger District). Note: summitpost.org returned HTTP 403 on every fetch attempt during this research and could not be used directly; peakbagger.com's page also 403'd on direct fetch, so its data (elevation, prominence, NAD27 coordinates) was corroborated instead via search-result synthesis citing that page, cross-checked against Wikipedia and Mountain Project, which agree.

routes.fa: August 1971 (first-ascent party not documented in available sources)
routes.face: Middle Finger Buttress (largest/most western buttress), North Face, above Upper Cathedral Lake
objHaz: loose rock/flake reported near the upper pitches
remote alpine setting far from help
routes.overview: Climbs the larger, more western of Amphitheater's two Middle Finger Buttress lines, above Upper Cathedral Lake, via a chimney into a standout hand crack and a run of 5.7-5.9 dihedrals to the summit ridge.
routes.beta: The biggest buttress above Upper Cathedral Lake, and generally considered the best pure crack pitch on the peak (pitch 1), followed by easier, non-sustained climbing to the top.
routes.approach: From the Andrews Creek Trailhead (end of pavement on Forest Road 51/Chewuch River Road, ~28 miles from Winthrop via County Rd 1213 then FR 51), hike roughly 17 miles and 4,800 ft of gain via Andrews Creek Trail/Cathedral Pass to a basecamp at Upper Cathedral Lake. Middle Finger Buttress rises directly above the lake's north side.
routes.descent: Walk-off: hike east along the crest to a small col SW of the North Ridge, then descend a 3rd-class gully down the north slope.
routes.descentText: From the top of the route, traverse/hike east along the summit ridge crest to a small saddle southwest of the North Ridge route's line. From there, downclimb a 3rd-class gully on the north slope back toward Upper Cathedral Lake.
routes.bestSeason: Climbed midsummer to early fall (Jul-Sep) once the approach trail and basin are snow-free. The Andrews Creek approach burned in recent wildfires and offers little shade, so an early start helps in hot weather.
routes.comms: No cell coverage anywhere on the approach or route — the Pasayten Wilderness is entirely outside cellular range; a satellite communicator/PLB is recommended.
routes.detailedRack: A set of stoppers plus doubles of cams from about BD #0.3 to #2, with a single #3, covers the crack systems on pitch 1 and the dihedral pitches above.
routes.proNeeds: Standard rack of nuts and doubles of small-to-mid cams protects the hand crack and dihedral systems well; gear-anchored belays throughout.
whatToBring: approach shoes for the 3rd-class descent gully
overnight backpacking/camping gear for the multi-day approach
proTips: Pitch 1's hand crack is regarded as the route's best, hardest pitch.
Camp at Upper Cathedral Lake to cut the summit-day approach to about 15 minutes.
watchOut: A loose flake has been noted near the upper pitches — test holds carefully.
Long, remote wilderness approach means self-rescue should be assumed.
knownHazards: Loose rock reported on sections of the buttress
Afternoon thunderstorms are common in North Cascades high country in summer
No cell service anywhere in the Pasayten Wilderness

pitchDetail.notes: 60m. Begin in a chimney (optional belay at top), then the standout hand crack; move left to build a belay at the top. The longest, hardest, highest-quality pitch on the route.
40m. Traverse right, following the continuation of the dihedral.
Non-sustained moderate climbing to the summit ridge; a possible 5th pitch depending on the line chosen.

climate.typical: East-side Cascades climate: drier and sunnier than the west slope, warm summer days and cold, snowy winters.
climate.spring: Trail and basin typically still snow-covered into June.
climate.summer: Warm and mostly dry with typical Cascade afternoon convective/thunderstorm risk; prime climbing season.
climate.fall: Cool, often stable weather; larches turn gold in the basin by late September.
climate.winter: Deep snow; road and trail inaccessible without ski/snowshoe travel.

routes.fa: July 15, 1973 (first-ascent party not documented in available sources)
routes.face: Middle Finger Buttress (NW side of the same big buttress), above Upper Cathedral Lake
objHaz: loose rock in places (below-average star rating reported)
remote alpine setting far from help
routes.overview: Climbs the right-hand crack systems on the NW side of the same big buttress as the Left Side route, starting up a steep slab with a thin vertical crack, with the crux on pitch 3.
routes.beta: The right side of the largest buttress above Upper Cathedral Lake; longer than its neighbor at 7 pitches, and rated lower on Mountain Project — expect more variable rock quality.
routes.approach: From the Andrews Creek Trailhead (end of pavement on FR 51/Chewuch River Road, ~28 miles from Winthrop), hike roughly 17 miles and 4,800 ft of gain to a basecamp at Upper Cathedral Lake; the buttress rises directly above the lake.
routes.descent: Hike east along the ridge crest to a small col SW of the North Ridge, then descend a 3rd-class gully down the north slope.
routes.descentText: Same walk-off as Middle Finger Buttress - Left Side: traverse/hike east along the summit ridge to a saddle SW of the North Ridge, then downclimb the 3rd-class north-slope gully back toward Upper Cathedral Lake.
routes.bestSeason: Jul-Sep, once trail and basin are snow-free.
routes.comms: No cell coverage in the Pasayten Wilderness; carry a satellite communicator/PLB.
routes.proNeeds: A wide range of nuts and cams protects the crack systems throughout; no fixed gear reported.
whatToBring: approach shoes for the descent gully

routes.fa: Darin Berdinka and Owen Lunz, July 2004
routes.face: Ka'aba Buttress, two buttresses left (east) of Middle Finger Buttress, above Upper Cathedral Lake
routes.overview: A four-pitch line up the blocky, stepping-stone-like Ka'aba Buttress featuring a laybacking fin, a finger-crack corner, and a crux 'money pitch' of steep double hand cracks, topped out via the improvisational 'diving board' feature.
routes.beta: One of the more popular moderate lines in the Amphitheater cirque; reached via a short scramble from Upper Cathedral Lake.
routes.approach: From a basecamp at Upper Cathedral Lake, walk the lake's west shore and scramble roughly 15 minutes up to the Ka'aba Buttress, two formations left (east) of Middle Finger Buttress. Overall trailhead access is via Andrews Creek Trailhead (end of FR 51), ~17 miles and 4,800 ft of gain to the lake.
routes.descent: Walk east to the saddle of Amphitheater, then down the trail south and back around to Upper Cathedral Lake.
routes.descentText: From the top of the route, hike east to the saddle of Amphitheater Mountain, then descend the trail to the south, looping back around to the Upper Cathedral Lake basecamp.
routes.bestSeason: Jul-Sep, once the trail and cirque are snow-free.
routes.comms: No cell coverage in the Pasayten Wilderness; a satellite communicator/PLB is recommended.
routes.detailedRack: Doubles from 0.3 to 3 (mid-size cams), one #4 cam, a full set of nuts, and small cams; gear-anchored belays throughout, no fixed anchors.
routes.proNeeds: Standard trad rack weighted toward mid-size cams (.3-3) plus a #4 for the offwidth-corner finish.

pitchDetail.notes: Head straight up from the large block and follow the triple crack system to the end of the roof.
A thin, exposed flake with layback moves leading to a sandy ledge belay.
'Money pitch' — right side of the sandy ledge up a right-facing finger-crack corner into a twin hand-crack section.
Left of the prow through a short low-angle offwidth corner; three possible finishing options to the summit 'diving board'.

proTips: The crux pitch 3 ('money pitch') is the highlight — steep double hand cracks with cruxy 5.8-5.9 hand jamming.
The summit 'diving board' feature requires creative topping-out beta — scope it before committing.
watchOut: Sandy/loose belay ledges reported.
Long wilderness approach — plan for a multi-day trip.
knownHazards: No cell coverage in the Pasayten Wilderness
Afternoon thunderstorms common in summer

routes.face: West side, broad 'amphitheater' bowl for which the peak is named
routes.overview: The standard, non-technical line to Amphitheater's summit, ascending scree and grass slopes up the broad western bowl (the 'amphitheater') that gives the peak its name.
routes.beta: From Upper Cathedral Lake this is an extended hike/scramble rather than a technical climb — the easiest way to the top, avoiding all of the peak's 5th-class buttresses.
routes.approach: From Upper Cathedral Lake, follow the trail roughly half a mile southeast around Amphitheater's west ridge to a boot path near 7,200 ft, then continue up scree and grassy slopes northeasterly into a basin at about 8,000 ft, then hike easy slopes south to the summit (may involve easy scrambling). Overall access via Andrews Creek Trailhead/Chewuch Trailhead area is roughly 36 miles round trip with about 3,000 ft of gain per the Mazamas trip description.
routes.descent: Reverse the ascent line back through the 8,000 ft basin to the boot path and west-ridge trail, or descend via the col route toward Upper Cathedral Lake/Cathedral Pass.
routes.descentText: From the basin at ~8,000 ft, retrace the boot path down scree and grass slopes to the west-ridge trail junction and back to Upper Cathedral Lake.
routes.bestSeason: Summer and fall only per Mazamas guidance — spring and winter attempts are explicitly discouraged due to snow cover on the approach and summit slopes.
routes.comms: No cell coverage in the Pasayten Wilderness; a satellite communicator/PLB is recommended.
whatToBring: map/compass or GPS for the unmaintained upper basin
USGS Remmel Mountain 7.5' quad or Green Trails 51SX Methow Valley map
watchOut: Loose scree and grassy slopes can be slippery when wet.
No maintained trail above ~7,200 ft — route-finding on talus/scree required.
knownHazards: No cell coverage in the Pasayten Wilderness
Long, remote approach demands self-sufficiency

routes.fa: August 29, 1973 (first-ascent party not documented in available sources)
routes.overview: An alpine ridge route from Cathedral Pass up Amphitheater's north ridge, with several roped, low-5th-class steps separated by short gully notches; length is adjustable depending on how far up the ridge one climbs.
routes.beta: A moderate, low-commitment alpine ridge route reached directly from Cathedral Pass rather than Upper Cathedral Lake; loose rock and route-finding are the main challenges rather than technical difficulty.
routes.approach: From Cathedral Pass, scramble up until it becomes necessary to rope up for the ridge's steeper steps.
routes.descent: Either traverse southwest around Amphitheater back to Upper Cathedral Lake, or descend via a small col on the southwest side using a 3rd-class gully down the north slope.
routes.descentText: Two options: traverse southwest around the mountain back toward Upper Cathedral Lake, or drop into a small col on the southwest side and downclimb a 3rd-class gully on the north slope.
routes.bestSeason: Jul-Sep, once the ridge is snow-free.
routes.comms: No cell coverage in the Pasayten Wilderness; a satellite communicator/PLB is recommended.
routes.detailedRack: A light rack of nuts and long slings is sufficient for the ridge's short technical steps.
routes.proNeeds: Minimal rack — nuts and long slings for protecting stepped sections; many parties simul-climb or solo easier terrain.
proTips: Route length can be tailored — climb as far up the ridge as conditions/party ability warrant, then back off via either descent option.
watchOut: Route-finding is the main difficulty — loose boulders on several sections.
Ridge exposed to weather; retreat options are limited once committed to upper steps.
knownHazards: Loose rock on ridge steps
No cell coverage in the Pasayten Wilderness
routes.corrections: Mountain Project describes the route as '5-7 pitches' depending on how far up the ridge is climbed; Wikipedia states 5 pitches. Reported here as 5 with the variable range noted in beta/overview.

routes.fa: Scott Bennett and Blake Herrington, 2011
routes.overview: A striking, sustained line up the Middle Finger Buttress's north face: a fingers-to-thin-hands splitter crack into a series of left-leaning roofs, finished on hand cracks and jugs joining the top of the Left Side route.
routes.beta: The hardest established free route on Amphitheater, climbing a right-trending ramp/flake into a crux ringlock/stem-corner pitch below stacked roofs, then two roof pitches of underclinging and jamming before joining Middle Finger Buttress - Left Side near the top.
routes.approach: From the Andrews Creek Trailhead (end of FR 51/Chewuch River Road), hike roughly 17 miles and 4,800 ft of gain to a basecamp at Upper Cathedral Lake; the route climbs the north face of Middle Finger Buttress directly above the lake.
routes.descent: Shares the walk-off descent with Middle Finger Buttress - Left Side, which this route joins below the summit: hike east along the ridge crest to a small col SW of the North Ridge, then down a 3rd-class gully on the north slope.
routes.descentText: The route joins Middle Finger Buttress - Left Side for its final pitches, so descent follows that route's walk-off: east along the summit ridge crest to a col SW of the North Ridge, then down the north-slope 3rd-class gully to Upper Cathedral Lake.
routes.bestSeason: Jul-Sep, once the approach and buttress are snow-free.
routes.comms: No cell coverage in the Pasayten Wilderness; a satellite communicator/PLB is recommended.
routes.detailedRack: Wires plus doubles of cams from tips-sized to #2, with a single #3 and #4 for the wider roof-exit cracks and finishing hand cracks.
routes.proNeeds: A full range of small wires and cams (tips to #2, doubled) covers the crux ringlock and roof pitches; a single #3 and #4 finish the route.
whatToBring: approach shoes for the descent gully

pitchDetail.notes: Starts up a rightward-trending ramp/flake.
Crux: ringlock crack (0.5-0.75 cams), stem corner, leftward steps to a belay beneath the roofs. Cracks reported dirty.
Underclings and jams beneath the first roof, then through the second roof into a hand jam.
Hand cracks and blocky terrain joining Middle Finger Buttress - Left Side before the summit scramble.

proTips: The route joins the top of Middle Finger Buttress - Left Side, so the two share a descent.
Expect a dirty/vegetated crux crack on pitch 2 — has seen limited traffic.
watchOut: Sustained, sparsely-protected 5.11 climbing far from help — the peak's most serious free route.
Cracks reported dirty in places, adding difficulty beyond the grade.
knownHazards: No cell coverage in the Pasayten Wilderness
Remote, rarely-climbed hard route — self-rescue only

climate.typical: East-side Cascades climate: drier/sunnier than the west slope.
climate.spring: Approach typically snow-covered into June.
climate.summer: Warm, mostly dry, afternoon thunderstorm risk.
climate.fall: Cool, often stable.
climate.winter: Deep snow; inaccessible without ski/snowshoe travel.

blurb: Glacier Peak (Native name Dakobed/Takobia, "the Great Parent") is a heavily glaciated stratovolcano of the Cascade Volcanic Arc in Snohomish County, straddling the Mount Baker-Snoqualmie and Okanogan-Wenatchee National Forests within the Glacier Peak Wilderness. Built largely of dacite lava domes over roughly the last million years, it is Washington's most remote major volcano — no road comes within about 10 miles of the peak — and is carved by eleven named glaciers. It was first climbed in 1897 by a USGS survey party led by Thomas Gerdine, who in dense fog first summited a subsidiary high point (later named Disappointment Peak) before the true summit was reached.
hierarchyNote: None — name, coordinates (48.1118, -121.1141), and 10,541 ft elevation all correctly identify the Glacier Peak volcano in the North Cascades, and "Darrington and Mountain Loop Hwy" is a geographically correct parent grouping (Darrington is the standard gateway town/ranger district for this peak's trailheads).

routes.overview: A steep, direct glacier line up Glacier Peak's north side, crossing the Kennedy Glacier's bergschrund and a summit headwall; more aesthetic/direct than neighboring Frostbite Ridge but usually descended via that route rather than reversed.
routes.beta: From the Glacier Creek camp, a climbers' path climbs a gully to the crest of Kennedy Ridge, then drops to the toe of the Kennedy Glacier. Ascend the lower glacier keeping right, then the center of the upper glacier toward a snow dome; cross the bergschrund on a snow bridge near its top and climb a roughly 40-degree headwall staying left of, but close to, the prominent summit rocks. Beyond the headwall, descend about 100 ft across an old, flattened crater floor, then climb a steep, often icy ~45-degree slope for roughly 180 ft to easy ground on the summit ridge.
routes.approach: From the Darrington Ranger District, drive Highway 530 to Darrington, then south on the Mountain Loop Highway about 10.5 miles to White Chuck River Road (FS-23); follow FS-23 east about 11 miles to its end at the White Chuck River Trailhead. When accessible, follow White Chuck River Trail #643 about 4 miles, then Kennedy Ridge Trail #639 for 1.7 miles up to the PCT; go north on the PCT about 2 miles to the Glacier Creek crossing and campsites (roughly 4-5 hours pack-in).
routes.descent: Most parties descend via the adjacent Frostbite Ridge rather than reversing the Kennedy Glacier icefall.
routes.descentText: From the summit, descend south onto Frostbite Ridge (Pumice Ridge/North Cleaver) rather than down-climbing the Kennedy Glacier headwall and bergschrund; Frostbite Ridge offers more moderate, mostly snow/scree terrain back toward the Kennedy/Vista Glacier saddle and the Kennedy Ridge/White Chuck approach trail.
routes.bail: Below the bergschrund, retreat is a straightforward reversal of the glacier. Above the schrund, retreat is harder — many parties instead continue to the summit and descend via Frostbite Ridge rather than reverse the crux.
routes.turnaround: Because the route crosses a bergschrund and a sometimes-icy headwall, decide well before the crux whether the snow bridge and headwall conditions are acceptable — reversing above the schrund is difficult and slow.
whatToBring: Map/compass or GPS for glacier navigation
routes.proNeeds: No rock protection; standard glacier travel gear (pickets, ice screws, crevasse-rescue kit) is what's needed for the bergschrund crossing and headwall.
watchOut: Bergschrund crossing can be thin or awkward late season
Upper headwall is reported icy, especially late season
Loose rock near the summit rock band bordering the headwall
knownHazards: Crevasses on the lower and upper Kennedy Glacier
Icy conditions on the ~45-degree summit headwall late in the season
Rockfall near the rock band bordering the route
proTips: More direct/aesthetic than Frostbite Ridge but typically paired as a Kennedy-up / Frostbite-down loop rather than an out-and-back

pitchDetail.notes: Ascend keeping climber's right of centerline; minor crevasse hazard.
Ascend the center toward the snow dome; find a snow bridge to cross the bergschrund.
Keep left of, but close to, the prominent rock band.
Descend ~100 ft across the old crater floor, then climb an often-icy ~45° slope ~180 ft to easy summit ridge.

routes.overview: A short, easy-to-miss V0+ boulder problem on a flat-topped erratic beside the White Chuck Glacier basin, climbed almost in passing by parties on Glacier Peak's standard south-side approach.
routes.beta: A single Gaston move with the left hand plus careful footwork gets you to the flat summit in one or two moves.
routes.approach: Same approach as the Gerdine Ridge/Cool Glacier route: from the North Fork Sauk Trailhead, follow the North Fork Sauk Trail and PCT toward White Pass, roughly 8 miles to the White Chuck Glacier basin. The boulder sits west of the White Chuck Glacier's remnant ice, visible from the trail and distinctive for its flat top.
proTips: There's a small flat spot for a single tent directly at the base of the boulder — a novelty high-camp option
Usually climbed incidentally by parties passing through on the standard south-side approach, not as a dedicated objective
routes.corrections: Mountain Project lists this boulder's coordinates as 48.11222, -121.11387 — essentially identical to Glacier Peak's own summit coordinate (48.111844, -121.11412) given for this area — which is inconsistent with the route description placing it roughly 8 miles up the approach trail near the White Chuck Glacier basin (~7,000 ft, several miles from and thousands of feet below the true summit). This is very likely an inherited/default area pin rather than the boulder's real location, so no waypoint coordinate is included here.

routes.fa: Thomas Gerdine, Sam Strom, A. H. Dubois, and Darcy Bard (USGS survey party), 1897 — the mountain's first ascent, via the south ridge now known as Gerdine Ridge; the party initially summited a sub-peak in fog (later named Disappointment Peak) before completing the true summit.
routes.overview: The standard, most-traveled route up Glacier Peak: a long approach across the White Chuck Glacier basin to Glacier Gap, then moderate snow/glacier travel up the Gerdine and Cool Glaciers to the summit ridge.
routes.beta: From Glacier Gap (7,300 ft), ascend north-trending slopes, side-hilling first on the left and then the right below Glacier Gap Peak (~7,739 ft), to regain the ridge around 8,400 ft. Traverse northeast onto the Suiattle Glacier and climb the upper Cool Glacier westward back to the ridge, then follow the ridge/scree north to the summit.
routes.approach: Most parties now approach from the North Fork Sauk Trailhead (Sloan Creek Road/FS-49) via the North Fork Sauk Trail #649 to the PCT and White Pass, then east on the Foam Creek climbers' path about 2 miles, crossing a ridge saddle (~6,600 ft) and the White Chuck Glacier basin, trending NE to Glacier Gap (7,300 ft). An older approach used the White Chuck River Trail from the north, which is currently closed (see access notes).
routes.bail: Retreat by reversing the ridge/glacier traverse to Glacier Gap and back down to White Pass; the alternate South Ridge variant direct to Disappointment Peak avoids glacier travel for a faster bail in poor weather.
routes.descent: Same route in reverse — this is also the standard descent for parties who climbed up via Frostbite Ridge or Kennedy Glacier.
routes.descentText: Down-climb the ridge/scree south from the summit, reverse the Cool/Gerdine glacier traverse back to Glacier Gap, then retrace the Foam Creek climbers' path and White Chuck Glacier basin crossing back to White Pass and the PCT/North Fork Sauk Trail.
routes.turnaround: Given the long approach and the exposed White Chuck Glacier basin crossing, set a firm turnaround time that leaves margin to re-cross the basin and reach Glacier Gap before dark or worsening weather.
watchOut: Rockfall/loose rock on the South Ridge variant around Disappointment Peak
Whiteout navigation risk crossing the broad White Chuck Glacier basin
knownHazards: Crevasses on the Suiattle/Cool Glacier traverse
Loose rock on the South Ridge alternative near Disappointment Peak
proTips: Ascending the South Ridge directly over Disappointment Peak (4th class) avoids glacier travel late-season or for parties without glacier gear
This is the default descent route for parties who ascended Frostbite Ridge or the Kennedy Glacier

routes.overview: A direct rock-ridge variant of the standard south-side route: instead of traversing onto the Suiattle/Cool Glaciers, this line climbs straight up the Gerdine Ridge cleaver and over Disappointment Peak (9,755 ft) before rejoining the ridge to the true summit.
routes.beta: Around 8,000-8,500 ft, where the standard Gerdine Ridge/Cool Glacier route breaks right onto the Suiattle and Cool Glaciers, continue straight up the ridge crest instead. Climbing is mostly 3rd/4th class between about 8,500 ft and Disappointment Peak's 9,755 ft summit, with a few unprotected 5th-class moves reported near the top. From Disappointment Peak, descend/traverse roughly 150 ft to rejoin the cleaver/ridge at about 9,600 ft, then climb about 3/4 mile of pumice, scree and occasional snow chute to the true 10,541 ft summit.
routes.approach: Same as Cool Glacier/Gerdine Ridge: North Fork Sauk Trailhead via the PCT and Foam Creek climbers' path to Glacier Gap.
routes.descent: Most parties descend via the standard Gerdine Ridge/Cool Glacier line rather than reversing this loose ridge.
watchOut: Rock is loose and unstable throughout — climb carefully and stay close to the ridge crest, which sheds rock continuously
Avoid straying onto the glacier side of the ridge, which puts climbers below at rockfall risk
A few unprotected 5th-class moves are mixed into the 4th-class terrain near Disappointment Peak
knownHazards: Sustained loose/unstable rock (rated PG13 on Mountain Project)
Rockfall onto glacier travelers below the ridge
Unroped exposure on 4th/5th class moves
routes.proNeeds: Minimal gear placements exist in the loose rock; this is essentially an unprotected scramble — a rope is rarely useful, and the primary protection is careful movement and rockfall discipline.
gear: Ice axe & crampons for the approach/glacier sections
Light rock shoes optional for the 5th-class moves

routes.corrections: The routeName pairs this line with "Cool Glacier," but per Mountain Project and SummitPost it is specifically a variant of the Gerdine Ridge/Cool Glacier approach (diverging from it above ~8,000 ft to stay on the ridge crest rather than traverse onto the Suiattle/Cool Glaciers) — the two are closely related, not separate lines.

routes.overview: The most popular route on Glacier Peak's north side: a long valley approach to Boulder Basin, then a straightforward glacier climb up the Sitkum Glacier to a saddle near Sitkum Spire and a final pumice-ridge/snow-chute finish to the summit.
routes.approach: From the White Chuck River Trailhead near Darrington, hike White Chuck River Trail #643 about 5.5 miles to Kennedy Hot Springs, then continue 1.5 miles to Sitkum Creek. Follow the PCT north (left) about 0.5 mile to a forested campsite (~4,100 ft), then take the climbers' path steeply up the ridge south of Sitkum Creek to Boulder Basin (5,500-6,000 ft), the standard base camp — about 9 miles total. The White Chuck River Trail/Road is currently closed and inaccessible (Dec. 2025 storm damage, per Mt. Baker-Snoqualmie NF alerts as of 2026).
routes.beta: From Boulder Basin, head southeast onto the Sitkum Glacier, climbing slightly south to a corridor left of the feature dividing the glacier's lower section. Ascend the glacier heading NE to a saddle at roughly 9,500 ft just east of Sitkum Spire. From the saddle, follow the pumice Sitkum ridge toward the summit, staying right of and slightly below the true summit, then move into a snow chute that leads to the top.
routes.descent: Reverse the route, or descend via Gerdine Ridge/Cool Glacier on the south side as an alternate descent/loop.
watchOut: A handful of small crevasses on the upper Sitkum Glacier
Whiteout navigation risk on the broad glacier and pumice ridge
knownHazards: Minor crevasse hazard — typically only 3-4 small cracks rolling onto the upper Sitkum Glacier

routes.face: North/Northeast ridge (aka North Cleaver / Pumice Ridge)
routes.overview: A classic, scenic alpine ridge line up Glacier Peak's north side combining Frostbite Ridge, the Vista Glacier, and the Kennedy Glacier — a snow/scramble climb early season, graduating to steeper ice and mixed climbing by late season.
routes.beta: The ridge (also called the North Cleaver or Pumice Ridge) climbs on snow or loose, protectable rock, with snow slopes up to about 45 degrees, to a distinctive rock formation nicknamed the "rabbit ears notch" near the top of the ridge. Above the notch, 1-2 pitches of low-angle alpine ice lead to the crater rim.
routes.approach: From the North Fork Sauk Trailhead (Sloan Creek Road/FS-49), follow the North Fork Sauk Trail #649 to the PCT and north roughly 19-20 miles to Glacier Ridge, where a small unmarked path leaves the PCT about 0.7 mile past Glacier Creek and leads up to alpine terrain and Kennedy Peak's north side, then the Kennedy Glacier and onto Frostbite Ridge. A shorter approach via the White Chuck River Trail/Kennedy Ridge Trail #639 from Darrington is currently inaccessible (see access notes). Total round-trip mileage for an ascent of Frostbite Ridge combined with a descent via Cool Glacier is slightly over 40 miles.
routes.descent: Standard descent is via Gerdine Ridge/Cool Glacier on the south side.
routes.bail: Retreat below the rabbit-ears notch is a straightforward reversal of the ridge; above the notch, retreat over the ice pitches is slower and harder, so parties often continue to the summit and descend via Cool Glacier instead.
routes.turnaround: Given the long, committing approach and the ice pitches guarding the summit, set a conservative turnaround well before the rabbit-ears notch if weather or conditions are deteriorating — retreat below that point is far easier than above it.
routes.season: Early season = Basic Alpine (steep snow) climb; late season = Intermediate Ice climb as snow melts to reveal ice
watchOut: Long, committing approach (12+ miles) with little opportunity for quick retreat
Late-season conditions expose steeper ice requiring screws
Loose rock sections on the ridge
knownHazards: A committing route with a long approach and large elevation gain, limiting retreat options
Steep snow/ice pitches near the summit, icier late in the season
proTips: Early season (basic climb) vs. late season (intermediate ice climb) are very different undertakings on the same line
Commonly linked as an up-Frostbite/down-Cool-Glacier traverse of the mountain

routes.overview: Likely a duplicate database entry rather than an independently documented line — see corrections.
routes.corrections: No route by this combined name was found on Mountain Project, SummitPost, Peakbagger, or any other checked source. Reputable sources describe "Disappointment Peak Cleaver" (a south-side ridge variant of Gerdine Ridge/Cool Glacier — see wa_glacier_peak_disappointment_peak_cleaver) and "Sitkum Glacier" (a separate north-side glacier route from Boulder Basin — see wa_glacier_peak_sitkum_glacier) as two distinct, non-adjacent lines on opposite sides of the mountain; the south-ridge cleaver does not connect to the Sitkum Glacier. This entry appears to duplicate one or both of those already-catalogued routes rather than describe a genuine third line. Recommend resolving/merging this entry with one of the two routes above rather than treating it as independent; no technical/beta fields are populated here to avoid fabricating detail for a route that likely does not exist as named.

blurb: Castle Peak (8,343 ft) is a remote granite summit in the Hozameen Range of the North Cascades' Pasayten Wilderness, about one mile south of the Canadian border on the Okanogan-Whatcom county line. Its steep granitic north face has drawn a small handful of committing alpine rock routes since the 1979 Beckey-Nolting-Tindall first ascent of the face proper, including the 1993 Colorado Route, the Herrington-Hirst line (2008), and The Drawbridge (2017), while the peak's easier southwest side offers a long, remote class 2-3 scramble reached only via multi-day approaches from Ross Lake or Manning Provincial Park, BC.
hierarchyNote: None — Castle Peak's name, coordinates (~49.0N, 120.86W, ~1 mi south of the Canada border), and 8,343 ft elevation are consistent across Wikipedia, Mountain Project, and multiple trip-report sources, and its placement in the Pasayten Wilderness / Hozameen Range is correct and unambiguous.

routes.fa: Blake Herrington & Peter Hirst, August 3, 2008 (reported in AAJ 2009 as the 'Herrington-Hirst' route)
objHaz: glacier/moat crossing at the base of the face
runout unprotected face climbing on P3
loose rock possible on the approach glacier margin
routes.face: North Face — left margin of the central/middle buttress
routes.overview: A 14-pitch, 1,400-foot IV 5.10d line up the left margin of Castle Peak's central north-face buttress, established as an independent variation when detached snow patches blocked the party's intended repeat of the 1993 Colorado Route just above the glacier.
routes.beta: Cross the low-angle glacier to the base of the north face (moat crossing can be tricky), then climb 14 pitches on excellent granite following cracks and corners up the cleanest rock above a hanging snow patch about 80m up; roughly half the pitches go at 5.10, with a long yellow quartz dyke higher on the face used to bypass a deteriorating crack system. No loose rock was encountered on the first ascent. Descend via the east ridge to a col and snow finger back down to the north.
routes.approach: Approach across the glacier at the foot of the north face; travel is generally low-angle but the bergschrund/moat where ice meets rock required careful negotiation on the first ascent and can vary year to year.
routes.descent: Descend via the east ridge to an obvious col, then down snow slopes to the north; crampons and an axe are helpful but not always mandatory depending on snow hardness.
routes.descentText: From the summit, follow the east ridge to a col, then descend north-facing snow slopes/a snow finger back toward the glacier and approach line. Snow conditions vary by season and time of day — crampons and an ice axe are recommended, especially early season or on firm/icy snow.
routes.bail: Retreat is difficult once established on the upper buttress; the lower pitches (P1-P2, low 5th class) can be down-climbed or rappelled with a single rope if weather turns before committing to the P3 crux corner.
routes.turnaround: This is a long, committing alpine rock route in a very remote setting with no quick rescue option — begin early enough to be off the face and back across the glacier before afternoon weather typically builds, and be willing to retreat from the lower pitches rather than push into deteriorating conditions higher on the buttress.
routes.bestSeason: Best climbed in summer (roughly July-September) once the approach glacier has consolidated enough for safe crossing but before it opens into a more hazardous moat; conditions and snow coverage vary significantly year to year on this remote, rarely-visited face.
routes.comms: No cell coverage should be expected this close to the Canadian border in the Pasayten backcountry; a satellite communicator/PLB is strongly advised given the remoteness and slow potential rescue response.
routes.detailedRack: A single rack to 3 inches with an emphasis on small-to-mid-size cams and nuts covers the route; a few pitons can back up thin seams (none were essential on the FA). Standard alpine glacier gear (axe, crampons) is needed for the approach/descent.
routes.proNeeds: Mostly small gear — good protection reported throughout, including a fixed piton in place in the P3 corner; the crux P3 face climbing above the corner is described as largely unprotected despite 'great gear' lower in the pitch.
whatToBring: glacier travel kit (rope, crevasse rescue gear)
approach shoes/boots for talus and moat crossing
bivy gear given the remote multi-day approach
proTips: No loose rock was encountered on the first ascent — the granite is described as excellent throughout.
Higher on the face, follow the long yellow-colored quartz dyke to avoid a deteriorating crack system.
The route shares its start with the 1993 Colorado Route before diverging left where snow patches block the original line.
watchOut: The moat/bergschrund where the approach glacier meets the rock can be tricky to cross and varies by season.
P3 involves largely unprotected face climbing above the crux corner despite good gear lower down.
This is an extremely remote face near the Canadian border with essentially no chance of quick rescue.
knownHazards: Glacier travel hazards (crevasses/moat) on the approach to the base of the face.
Runout climbing on the P3 crux pitch.
Long, complex, multi-day approach compounds any incident on-route.

pitchDetail.notes: Begin in the blocky line of face holds just left of the gully/chimney on the left edge of the buttress; a wide crack near the end; mostly low 5th class. ~70m.
Climb up and right to the top of the snow, aiming for the clean wall below a shallow left-facing corner. ~20m.
Crux pitch: unprotected face climbing to a corner with good gear (fixed piton in place); from the top of the crack, hard 5.10 face climbing (great gear) leads to a ledge. ~30m.
Face climb left from the belay, then up perfect flakes and cracks to a pedestal above. ~40m.
Move down and left from the belay to a hidden corner, working back up and right to a spot above the belay. ~30m.
Left and up to the base of a long seam/finger crack; move left at its base, then up/left around a series of corners and roofs; belayed near the chimney/gully.
Up the featured arête right of the chimney/gully, then up a perfectly white shallow dihedral, undercling right to the belay. ~45m.
Continue up the left side of the buttress to the summit, including some simul-climbing; roughly half the route's pitches overall are rated 5.10.

road.name: North Cascades Highway (SR-20) to Ross Lake, or BC Highway 3 to Manning Provincial Park
road.status: SR-20 open seasonally
road.seasonalGate: SR-20 through the North Cascades (near Ross Lake/Washington Pass) typically closes mid-November through April/May due to snow, cutting off the U.S.-side approach in winter.
road.driveNote: Access to the base of the peak requires either a boat/water-taxi crossing of Ross Lake to the Lightning Creek trailhead (U.S. side) or driving to Manning Provincial Park, BC and hiking the Monument 78 trail to the border (Canadian side).

climate.typical: North Cascades crest weather: Pacific storms drive orographic precipitation, with western/windward slopes wetter than the eastern Pasayten side; summers are comparatively dry under Pacific high pressure.
climate.summer: Drier conditions typical, but the route requires a still-consolidated approach glacier, favoring mid-to-late summer.
climate.fall: Early season snow can return by late September/October at this latitude near the Canadian border.
climate.winter: Heavy snow; SR-20 closes and the peak becomes essentially inaccessible.

access.landManager: USDA Forest Service, Okanogan-Wenatchee National Forest (Pasayten Wilderness); NPS Ross Lake National Recreation Area for the U.S.-side approach; BC Parks (Manning Provincial Park) for the Canadian-side approach.
access.fees: Pasayten Wilderness permit is free and self-issued at the trailhead; Ross Lake water-taxi is a paid service; Manning Park charges camping/lodge fees on the BC side.
access.permit: Free self-issue wilderness permit (Pasayten Wilderness); NPS backcountry permit may apply for Ross Lake NRA camping.
access.closures: Crossing into or out of Canada via the Manning Park approach involves international border considerations (this is a known, long-used mountaineering approach, but is not a legal port of entry); the BC Skagit Valley Park road to Hozomeen is closed seasonally.

timing.recommendedStart: Alpine start to cross the approach glacier/moat while snow is firm and to allow a long (12-13+ hour) technical climbing day.

emergency.county: Whatcom County / Okanogan County border area (Hozameen Range)
emergency.sheriffDispatch: Okanogan County Sheriff non-emergency dispatch: (509) 422-7232; 911 for emergencies
emergency.rangerStation: Okanogan-Wenatchee National Forest, Winthrop Ranger Station, 24 West Chewuch Rd, Winthrop, WA 98862, (509) 996-4000
emergency.nearestHospital: North Valley Hospital (critical access), Tonasket, WA
emergency.notes: Extremely remote border-area face with no realistic quick-rescue option; approach requires either a Ross Lake water-taxi crossing or an international border crossing via Manning Park, BC; expect no cell coverage.

routes.corrections: Mountain Project's route page lists 12 pitches for this route, but the American Alpine Journal (2009) first-ascent report by Blake Herrington states 14 pitches (including some simul-climbing) and ~13 hours of climbing time; the AAJ figure is used here as the primary-source FA account. Mountain Project also names this route 'North Face (Left Buttress)' while the AAJ report describes it as a line up the left margin of the 'central/middle buttress' — these refer to the same 2008 Herrington-Hirst route, not two different climbs.

objHaz: extensive blowdown/deadfall on the Freezeout Creek Trail
early/late-season snow and ice on the summit ridge notch
routes.face: Southwest Ridge (reached via the Castle-area basin below the peak's south side)
routes.overview: A remote, non-technical class 2-3 scramble up Castle Peak's southwest ridge, the crux of which is the multi-day approach rather than the climbing itself — reached either via a Ross Lake water-taxi and the Freezeout Creek Trail (U.S. side) or via Manning Provincial Park, BC and a cross-border bushwhack up Castle Creek.
routes.beta: From a high camp near the SW ridge, ascend scree/talus and red heather slopes to gain the southwest ridge, then follow blocky class 2-3 terrain the final ~750 feet to the summit; the last short sections are the only class-3 moves, with most of the climb closer to class 2.
routes.approach: Two established approaches exist. (1) Freezeout Creek approach (U.S. side, no border crossing): take a paid water-taxi across Ross Lake from the Highway 20 boat launch to the Lightning Creek trailhead, follow Lightning Creek Trail to the (abandoned, overgrown) Freezeout Creek Trail, pass the old cabin site at ~3,600 ft, then leave trail and follow the west side of Freezeout Creek (staying roughly 100 ft above the creek to avoid swampy ground) through open forest and talus to a notch on the southwest ridge near 7,600 ft; total round-trip distance is roughly 29-34 miles with about 4,700+ ft of elevation gain. (2) Manning Park approach (crosses into the U.S. from BC): start on the Monument 78 trail (an old ATV-access road) in Manning Park, hike ~7 miles to the US-Canada border, then bushwhack roughly 5 miles south along Castle Creek staying in open terrain above the swampy creek bottom, gaining the southwest ridge for the final scramble.
routes.descent: Reverse the ascent line down the southwest ridge scree/talus and red heather slopes back to the approach route.
routes.descentText: From the summit, downclimb the class 2-3 blocky terrain back to the southwest ridge notch, then descend scree and red heather slopes back toward Freezeout Creek (or Castle Creek on the Manning Park approach), retracing the approach route to the trailhead or camp.
routes.itinerary: Typically a 2-3 day trip given the remoteness: Day 1 — water-taxi across Ross Lake and hike in to a camp near the old Freezeout Cabin site (~3,600 ft, ~11 miles in) or an equivalent camp on the Manning Park side; Day 2 — climb to the SW ridge notch, scramble to the summit and return to camp (some strong parties have done the Manning Park approach and climb car-to-car in a single ~12-13 hour day); Day 3 — hike/boat out.
routes.bail: The terrain itself is easy to retreat from at any point (class 2-3 with no technical commitment); the real bail consideration is the remoteness of the approach — turn back toward camp/trailhead well before dark or deteriorating weather given the difficulty of the bushwhack sections in poor visibility.
routes.turnaround: Because the approach is long, remote, and largely off-trail, set a firm turnaround time that guarantees returning to camp or the trailhead in daylight, and be prepared to abandon the summit attempt if weather, snow conditions on the ridge notch, or slower-than-expected bushwhacking progress threaten that margin.
routes.bestSeason: Best from roughly late July through September, after the SW ridge scree slopes and notch have shed most snow but before early-season storms return; parties climbing in late September/October have encountered snow on the summit ridge and should carry an ice axe and be prepared for winter-like conditions at any time given the latitude near the Canadian border.
routes.comms: No cell coverage should be expected in this remote wilderness/border area on either approach; a satellite communicator or PLB is strongly recommended.
gear: sturdy boots for talus and bushwhacking
light crampons (early/late season snow on the ridge notch)
whatToBring: extra food and fuel for a multi-day approach
map/GPS — trail sections are faint or abandoned
proTips: On the Freezeout Creek bushwhack, stay roughly 100 ft above the creek on the west side rather than following the creek itself, which is swampy — the hillside terrain is more open.
The Freezeout Creek Trail becomes faint/abandoned above about 4,200 ft; expect to lose and regain tread.
The final 750 ft to the summit is mostly class 2 with only short class-3 sections.
watchOut: Extensive blowdown and faint tread on the Freezeout Creek Trail can slow travel significantly.
The Ross Lake water-taxi runs on a limited schedule and is a paid service — plan around its availability.
The Manning Park approach crosses the international border away from a legal port of entry, a consideration climbers should be aware of.
This is grizzly/black bear country — practice proper food storage.
knownHazards: Long, mostly off-trail approach with real route-finding difficulty (faint trail, blowdown, bushwhacking).
Early- or late-season snow/ice on the summit ridge notch and final scramble.
Extreme remoteness means any injury or weather delay has serious consequences for a multi-day self-supported trip.

pitchDetail.notes: Gain the southwest ridge notch (~7,600 ft) via scree and red heather slopes above Freezeout Creek or Castle Creek.
Final ~750 ft of blocky terrain to the summit; mostly class 2 with short class-3 sections, harder if snow-covered.

road.name: North Cascades Highway (SR-20) to the Ross Lake boat launch, or BC Highway 3 to Manning Provincial Park
road.status: SR-20 open seasonally
road.seasonalGate: SR-20 through the North Cascades typically closes mid-November through April/May due to snow, closing off the Ross Lake water-taxi approach in winter.
road.driveNote: For the Freezeout Creek approach, drive SR-20 to the Ross Lake boat launch (~0.6 mi walk to the dock) and arrange a paid water-taxi to the Lightning Creek trailhead. For the Manning Park approach, drive BC Highway 3 to Manning Provincial Park and start on the Monument 78 trail.

climate.typical: North Cascades crest weather: Pacific storms drive orographic precipitation, with wetter conditions on windward slopes and a drier eastern Pasayten side; summers are comparatively dry.
climate.spring: Heavy snow typically still covers the approach and ridge; not a practical season for this route.
climate.summer: The primary climbing season, though the SW ridge notch can still hold snow into early summer.
climate.fall: Early winter storms can arrive by late September/October, bringing snow to the summit ridge.
climate.winter: Inaccessible — SR-20 is closed and the approach is snowbound.

access.landManager: USDA Forest Service, Okanogan-Wenatchee National Forest (Pasayten Wilderness); NPS Ross Lake National Recreation Area for the Freezeout Creek approach; BC Parks (Manning Provincial Park) for the Canadian-side approach.
access.fees: Pasayten Wilderness permit is free and self-issued at the trailhead; the Ross Lake water-taxi is a paid service; Manning Park charges nightly camping/lodge fees on the BC side.
access.permit: Free self-issue wilderness permit (Pasayten Wilderness); NPS backcountry camping permit may be required for Ross Lake NRA campsites en route.
access.closures: SR-20 (North Cascades Highway) closes seasonally, cutting off the Ross Lake approach in winter; the BC Skagit Valley Park road to Hozomeen is also closed seasonally; the Manning Park approach crosses the international border away from a legal port of entry.

timing.recommendedStart: Very early (pre-dawn) start if attempting the Manning Park approach car-to-car in a single day (one reported ascent ran 5:20am-5:40pm); otherwise a full climbing day from a backcountry camp.

emergency.county: Whatcom County / Okanogan County border area (Hozameen Range)
emergency.sheriffDispatch: Okanogan County Sheriff non-emergency dispatch: (509) 422-7232; 911 for emergencies
emergency.rangerStation: Okanogan-Wenatchee National Forest, Winthrop Ranger Station, 24 West Chewuch Rd, Winthrop, WA 98862, (509) 996-4000
emergency.nearestHospital: North Valley Hospital (critical access), Tonasket, WA
emergency.notes: One of the most remote peaks in the Pasayten Wilderness; no realistic quick-rescue option on either approach, and no cell coverage should be expected.

routes.corrections: A 1904 USGS survey party made the first recorded ascent of Castle Peak (a benchmark from that survey remains on the summit), but no source confirms the exact line they climbed, so no route-specific 'fa' is assigned to this scramble route rather than assuming it matches the modern SW-ridge line. Reported round-trip mileage/elevation-gain for the standard scramble varies noticeably by approach and party (e.g., ~29 mi/4,700 ft via Manning Park vs. longer Freezeout Creek totals for multi-peak trips); figures given here reflect the Manning Park single-day report as the most specific, apples-to-apples source found.
